Before this list existed, formulary coverage of these drugs was a major differentiator between plans. Now it's a floor. The differentiation shifted to: tier placement, cost-sharing requirements, and whether the plan has any additional management requirements (prior auth, step therapy) on top of the mandatory coverage.

Some plans are being creative here — technically covering the drug but loading it with administrative requirements that slow access. That's a real thing to watch for. If a plan covers Eliquis but requires prior authorization, your pharmacy experience in January is going to be rough while that auth request sits in queue.

Bottom line: The mandatory coverage list is a floor, not a ceiling. Plans that cover these drugs with no PA, no step therapy, and favorable tier placement are the winners for people on these medications.

sections.heading: How to Actually Pick Your Plan in 15 Minutes
sections.content:
I know people who agonize over this for weeks. Here's what I actually do when helping someone choose.

Step 1: Pull your current drug list. Every medication, every dose, every fill frequency. Don't guess.

Step 2: Go to Medicare.gov/plan-compare. Enter your drugs and your pharmacy. Let the tool calculate total estimated annual drug cost for every available plan in your zip code.

Step 3: Sort by total estimated annual cost — not premium. The plan with the lowest total annual cost is almost always your answer. The only reason to deviate: if the plan has terrible reviews, a history of prior auth games, or a network pharmacy that's inconvenient to you.

Step 4: Check that your drugs aren't subject to step therapy or quantity limits on the winning plan. The Plan Finder shows this. If your drug has a restriction, factor in the hassle of getting that managed.

Step 5: Look at the plan's star rating (1-5 stars from CMS). 4+ stars is the target. Sub-3 star plans have documented quality problems. It's not worth saving $8/month on a 2.5-star plan.

Total time: honestly 15-20 minutes if you have your drug list ready. The tool does the hard math. Your job is to give it accurate inputs.

One last thing: enrollment periods matter. If you're new to Medicare, you have a 7-month window around your 65th birthday. If you're switching plans, Annual Enrollment Period is October 15 – December 7, with coverage starting January 1. Don't miss it. The late enrollment penalty (separate article coming) is permanent and brutal.

sections.heading: Plans to Watch in 2026 by Category
sections.content:
I'm hesitant to publish a 'ranked list' that ages out in 90 days, but here are the plan families worth looking at in 2026 based on carrier positioning, formulary depth, and member satisfaction data.

Humana remains a major player in standalone PDPs. Their portfolio ranges from the low-cost Humana Walmart Value Rx (generic-focused) up to more comprehensive options with better brand coverage. They have geographic breadth that many smaller carriers can't match.

Aetna SilverScript is the largest standalone PDP insurer by enrollment and for good reason — they've historically had competitive formularies and consistent service. SilverScript Choice and SilverScript Plus are the two main variants to compare depending on your drug complexity.

WellCare has aggressively repriced entering 2026 and has some of the most competitive premiums in several state markets. Their Value Script plan is drawing comparison-shoppers. The tradeoff: pharmacy network restrictions in some regions.

UnitedHealthcare (AARP MedicareRx) plans tend to carry higher premiums but often have broader pharmacy networks and better preferred brand coverage. For people who prioritize convenience and comprehensive formularies over low premium, they're consistently competitive.

CVS Health/Aetna through the SilverScript brand continues to dominate enrollment numbers. They also benefit from the CVS preferred pharmacy network, which lowers cost-sharing significantly for the significant portion of the country near a CVS.

Again: these are starting points, not final answers. The plan that's best for someone on Eliquis + Jardiance + a Tier 1 blood pressure med in Phoenix, Arizona in 2026 is not the same plan as what's best for someone on only generics in rural Wisconsin. Run your personal numbers.

faq.question: What is the average Part D premium in 2026?
faq.answer: The average standalone Part D premium is $34.50/month in 2026, down from $38.31 in 2025. But average means little — premiums range from $0 to $100+ depending on the plan and your location. The premium alone should never drive your plan choice. Total estimated annual drug cost is the number that matters.

faq.question: What is the maximum Part D deductible in 2026?
faq.answer: No Part D plan can charge more than $615 as a deductible in 2026. Many plans charge less, and some charge $0. Importantly, most plans waive the deductible for Tier 1 and Tier 2 drugs, so the deductible often only applies to brand-name and specialty drugs.

faq.question: Are Eliquis and Jardiance covered by all Part D plans in 2026?
faq.answer: Yes. Both are among the 10 IRA-negotiated drugs that every Part D plan and MA-PD plan is required to cover starting January 1, 2026. The negotiated price for Eliquis is $231 for a 30-day supply (down from $521 in 2023). Tier placement and any prior authorization requirements still vary by plan.

faq.question: How many Part D plans are available in 2026?
faq.answer: There are 360 standalone PDPs available nationally in 2026, down from 464 in 2025 — the third consecutive year of plan contraction. The number available to you specifically depends on your zip code.

faq.question: Can I switch Part D plans?
faq.answer: Yes. During Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 – December 7), you can switch Part D plans with new coverage starting January 1. If you're enrolled in a Medicare Advantage plan, you can also switch during Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment (January 1 – March 31), though that change may be more limited.

faq.question: What's the best Part D plan for someone only taking generics?
faq.answer: For generic-only users, a $0 or very low premium plan with Tier 1 drugs at $0-3/fill at your preferred pharmacy is almost always the winner. The Humana Walmart Value Rx Plan has been consistently competitive for this use case, though you should verify current year terms and availability in your area using Medicare.gov/plan-compare.

faq.question: Do Part D plan star ratings matter?
faq.answer: Yes, and they're often overlooked. CMS rates plans 1-5 stars based on quality measures including drug pricing accuracy, member complaints, medication adherence support, and customer service. Target 4+ stars. Plans rated below 3 stars have documented quality problems and those problems affect real members.

h1: Medicare Part D $2,100 Out-of-Pocket Cap Explained
metaDescription: How the IRA's $2,100 Medicare Part D out-of-pocket cap works in 2026. Phase-by-phase cost breakdown, what counts toward the cap, the monthly payment option, and real examples with Eliquis and Jardiance.
summary: I don't say that lightly. Before the Inflation Reduction Act, there was no hard cap on what a Medicare beneficiary could spend out of pocket on drugs in a year. None. The old catastrophic phase kicked in at a certain threshold but you still paid 5% — and on a $50,000 cancer drug regimen, 5% is $2,50

sections.heading: This Is the Biggest Medicare Drug Change in 20 Years
sections.content:
I don't say that lightly. Before the Inflation Reduction Act, there was no hard cap on what a Medicare beneficiary could spend out of pocket on drugs in a year. None. The old catastrophic phase kicked in at a certain threshold but you still paid 5% — and on a $50,000 cancer drug regimen, 5% is $2,500 per month.

The IRA changed that. Effective 2025, there's a hard stop. You spend $2,000 OOP, you're done paying for drugs that year. In 2026, that number is $2,100 — adjusted slightly upward by CMS based on average drug expenditure growth. Still a hard cap. Still a completely different world from what existed before.

If you're on expensive drugs — Eliquis, Jardiance, Entresto, any specialty biologic, any cancer medication — this cap may be the most important number in your financial life. Not your Part B premium. Not your Medicare Advantage premium. This $2,100.

Let me walk through exactly how it works.

sections.heading: The Two-Phase Structure (The Donut Hole Is Dead)
sections.content:
Old Part D had three phases: deductible, initial coverage, coverage gap (the infamous donut hole), then catastrophic. That was the framework from 2006 to 2024. Genuinely confusing. People fell into the donut hole without understanding it, suddenly paying 25% of brand-name drug costs themselves.

In 2026, that's gone. CMS restructured Part D into two phases:

Phase 1: Initial Coverage. You pay your deductible (if your plan has one), then you pay your plan's normal cost-sharing — copays or coinsurance — on covered drugs. This continues until your out-of-pocket spending hits $2,100.

Phase 2: Catastrophic Coverage. After you've spent $2,100 out of pocket, you pay $0 for covered drugs for the rest of the calendar year.

That's it. Two phases. No donut hole. No 5% catastrophic cost-sharing. Just a clean threshold where your spending stops.

The donut hole was partially closed by the ACA starting in 2010 and then fully eliminated by the IRA for 2025 forward. The 2026 cap is $2,100 (up $100 from the $2,000 that debuted in 2025), adjusted by CMS annually.

The simplicity is genuinely new. It took 18 years to get here.

sections.heading: What Counts Toward the $2,100 Cap
sections.content:
This is where people get confused and it matters a lot.

What COUNTS toward your $2,100:
- Your deductible payments (what you pay before coverage kicks in)
- Your copays and coinsurance for covered Part D drugs
- Payments made by Extra Help (the Low Income Subsidy)
- Payments made by the AIDS Drug Assistance Program (ADAP)
- Manufacturer discounts on covered brand-name drugs (this is the big one — more on this below)

What does NOT count:
- Your monthly plan premium
- The cost of drugs not covered by your formulary
- Any out-of-pocket costs for drugs bought outside the US
- Payments made by other insurance (like a retiree plan or TRICARE)

The manufacturer discount piece is important and often surprises people. When you're in the initial coverage phase and you take a brand-name drug, the brand manufacturer contributes a discount to the cost. A portion of that manufacturer discount counts toward your $2,100 OOP accumulation even though you didn't pay it yourself. This accelerates your path to the cap.

For 2026, the manufacturer discount rate on brand-name drugs is 10% for beneficiaries before the cap. The insurer covers 65%, you pay 25%. But — and this is the part that matters — that 10% manufacturer discount does count toward your out-of-pocket accumulation. So effectively you're accruing toward the $2,100 cap faster than your actual cash outlay.

Practical upshot: heavy brand-name drug users will hit the cap earlier in the year than a strict reading of their cash payments might suggest.

sections.heading: Phase-by-Phase Math: What You Actually Pay
sections.content:
Let me walk through a real scenario. We'll use a beneficiary on Eliquis (apixaban) — 30-day supply at the 2026 IRA negotiated price of $231.

Assume they're on a plan with: $415 deductible (applies to Tier 3 drugs), Eliquis placed on Tier 3 at 25% coinsurance after deductible, no other significant prescriptions.

January — Deductible phase:
Eliquis costs $231/fill. Plan applies deductible. Beneficiary pays $231 (counts toward $415 deductible AND toward $2,100 OOP cap).

February — Still in deductible:
Pays another $231 fill. Now $462 paid — deductible hit at $415 partway through the month. Rest of February fill after deductible hit: at 25% coinsurance, $231 x 25% = $57.75/fill.
Simplified: February net outlay roughly $185 (finishing deductible + first post-deductible fill). Cumulative OOP: approximately $415.

March through June — Initial coverage at 25% coinsurance:
Monthly: $57.75 per fill. Accruing OOP credit: $57.75 cash + portion of manufacturer discount (10% of $231 = $23.10). Effective OOP accrual: ~$80/month.

With ~$415 already in the bank from deductible, and ~$80/month accruing through ICV phase: remaining cap gap is $2,100 - $415 = $1,685. At $80 effective monthly accrual, that's roughly 21 months of additional accumulation — meaning a single-drug Eliquis user on this scenario might not hit the cap this year.

Now change the scenario: add Jardiance ($231/fill negotiated price), Entresto ($450/fill), and a specialty biologic at $800/fill. The math shifts dramatically. With four expensive drugs, hitting $2,100 by March or April is plausible.

The cap is most powerful for people on multiple high-cost drugs simultaneously. If you're on one expensive medication and otherwise healthy, you'll benefit from the negotiated pricing but may not hit the cap in a given year. If you're managing multiple chronic conditions with expensive drugs, the cap is genuinely life-changing.

sections.heading: The Monthly Payment Option — Huge for Fixed-Income Retirees
sections.content:
Starting in 2025 (still available in 2026), CMS introduced the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan — sometimes called the M3P. Here's what it does and why it matters.

The problem it solves: under the old structure and even now, most of your high drug costs hit in the first few months of the year — before you've hit your OOP cap. A retiree on Social Security might spend $800 in January on drugs (deductible + first fills), $600 in February, then $300 in March, then almost nothing after hitting the cap. That January-February crunch is brutal on a fixed income.

M3P lets you smooth that spending. Instead of paying large drug costs when they hit at the pharmacy, you opt in and your plan spreads your estimated annual OOP drug costs across 12 equal monthly payments. You pay via an invoice from your plan, not at the pharmacy counter.

This doesn't reduce what you pay. Total annual cost is the same — it's cash flow management, not a discount. But for someone on Social Security getting $1,800/month, the difference between a $800 pharmacy bill in January and a $175/month invoice is enormous in practice.

How to opt in: contact your Part D plan directly. CMS required all Part D plans to offer this option starting in 2025. You can enroll at any time during the plan year, and your remaining drug costs get spread over the remaining months. If you enroll in March, you divide remaining OOP estimates over 10 months instead of 12.

Who should consider this: anyone who takes expensive brand-name or specialty drugs, particularly if they expect to hit the OOP cap. If you only take cheap generics and never get anywhere near $2,100 in drug costs, M3P doesn't do much for you.

sections.heading: Real Eliquis Example: Before and After the IRA
sections.content:
This comparison illustrates why 2026 is genuinely different for high-cost drug users.

Same patient. On Eliquis for AFib. No other expensive medications.

2023 (before IRA full implementation):
- Eliquis list price: ~$521/30-day supply
- Before hitting the coverage gap: paid 25% = ~$130/fill
- Entered donut hole mid-year, paid 25% of full cost (slightly different math due to gap discount structure)
- Catastrophic phase kicked in but still paid 5% of each fill
- Estimated annual OOP for Eliquis alone: $2,000-2,800+ depending on when they hit each phase

2026 (post-IRA):
- Eliquis IRA negotiated price: $231/30-day supply
- 25% coinsurance in initial coverage phase: $57.75/fill
- After $2,100 OOP cap: $0/fill for rest of year
- Estimated annual OOP for Eliquis alone (if only taking Eliquis): roughly $800-1,200 depending on plan deductible and other OOP accumulations
- If on other expensive drugs that push OOP to $2,100 faster: even less

The savings are real. Not fake real. People who have been rationing Eliquis — and some have been, even on Medicare, because the old cost structure was brutal — are in meaningfully better shape in 2026.

Jardiance users see similar math. The IRA negotiated price for Jardiance is roughly comparable. For someone managing both AFib (Eliquis) and Type 2 diabetes (Jardiance), hitting the $2,100 cap before summer is genuinely achievable. After that: $0 for covered drugs through December 31.

sections.heading: What the Cap Doesn't Fix
sections.content:
I'd be doing you a disservice if I didn't also say this.

The $2,100 cap only applies to drugs covered by your Part D plan's formulary. If your drug isn't on the formulary, its costs don't count toward the cap — and you're paying full price out of pocket with no protection.

Drugs on higher tiers still cost significant money before you hit the cap. A specialty Tier 5 drug at 25-33% coinsurance, hitting $2,100 cap means you've already spent $2,100. That's not nothing. For someone on Social Security, that's still a major financial event — the cap just means it stops at $2,100 instead of running to $5,000, $8,000, or more.

The cap resets January 1 every year. You don't carry over prior-year accumulation. So if you hit the cap in October, you start from zero in January. January is always an expensive month again.

Premiums don't count. You're paying $34-100+/month in Part D premium all year regardless of whether you hit the cap. That's a real cost that doesn't factor into the $2,100 calculation.

If you're in Extra Help / Low Income Subsidy, the mechanics are different — you have much lower cost-sharing regardless, and the cap still provides a ceiling, but your actual spend will almost certainly never reach $2,100. The cap primarily matters for middle-income beneficiaries without Extra Help who take expensive medications.

sections.heading: Planning Around the Cap
sections.content:
This section is for people who want to be strategic about it, not just reactive.

If you know you'll hit the cap: consider filling 90-day supplies early in the year when possible. More drug early = faster accumulation toward the cap = more months of $0 cost-sharing at the back end of the year. Some people do a 90-day fill in January specifically to accelerate through the initial coverage phase.

Consider the monthly payment plan if cash flow is the issue. See the M3P section above.

Make sure your expensive drugs are actually on your plan's formulary before January 1. If you need a formulary exception or prior auth, start that process in November or December — not mid-January when you're standing at the pharmacy counter.

If you switch plans during AEP (October 15 – December 7), your OOP accumulation resets with the new plan. So if you hit the cap by September and are considering switching for next year — you're making that decision knowing you'll restart at $0 in January under any plan.

Track your OOP accumulation. Your plan is required to send you an Explanation of Benefits showing cumulative costs. Actually read it. Knowing where you are relative to the $2,100 threshold helps you plan fill timing, especially toward year-end.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket cap in 2026?
faq.answer: The hard cap on out-of-pocket drug spending under Part D is $2,100 in 2026. Once you've spent $2,100 on covered drugs, you pay $0 for the rest of the calendar year. This is up from the $2,000 cap that debuted in 2025.

faq.question: Does the Part D premium count toward the $2,100 cap?
faq.answer: No. Your monthly plan premium does not count toward the $2,100 out-of-pocket cap. Only your actual drug cost-sharing — deductibles, copays, and coinsurance for covered drugs — accumulates toward the cap.

faq.question: What was the old donut hole and is it still around?
faq.answer: The coverage gap (donut hole) was a phase of Part D where beneficiaries paid a higher percentage of drug costs after initial coverage ran out. The IRA eliminated it entirely starting in 2025. In 2026, Part D has only two phases: initial coverage (where you pay your plan's normal cost-sharing) and catastrophic (where you pay $0 after hitting the $2,100 OOP cap).

faq.question: How does the manufacturer discount affect OOP accumulation?
faq.answer: When you take a brand-name drug in the initial coverage phase, the brand manufacturer contributes a 10% discount to the cost. That manufacturer discount portion counts toward your $2,100 OOP accumulation even though you didn't pay it yourself. This means you reach the cap faster than your actual cash outlay alone would suggest.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan and how do I enroll?
faq.answer: The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (M3P) lets you spread your annual out-of-pocket drug costs across 12 equal monthly payments instead of paying large amounts at the pharmacy. It doesn't reduce your total cost — it just smooths the cash flow. All Part D plans are required to offer this option. Contact your plan to enroll at any time during the plan year.

faq.question: Does the $2,100 cap apply to drugs not on my formulary?
faq.answer: No. Only costs for drugs covered by your plan's formulary count toward the OOP cap. If you're paying for a drug not covered by your plan, those costs have no ceiling and don't accumulate toward the $2,100 threshold.

faq.question: What happened to Eliquis pricing in 2026?
faq.answer: Eliquis was among the 10 drugs with CMS-negotiated prices under the Inflation Reduction Act, effective January 1, 2026. The negotiated price for a 30-day supply is $231, down from approximately $521 in 2023 — a 56% reduction. All Part D plans are required to cover Eliquis at this negotiated price.

h1: Medicare Extra Help (LIS): Income Limits & How to Apply
metaDescription: Full 2026 eligibility for Medicare Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy). Income limits, asset limits, what costs it covers, how to apply through SSA, and who gets auto-enrolled through Medicaid and SSI.
summary: This is the one that keeps me up at night, honestly. Extra Help — the federal program that nearly eliminates your drug costs if you have limited income and assets — is one of the most underutilized benefits in Medicare. Somewhere around a third of eligible people aren't enrolled. Not because they do

sections.heading: Most People Who Qualify Don't Know They Qualify
sections.content:
This is the one that keeps me up at night, honestly. Extra Help — the federal program that nearly eliminates your drug costs if you have limited income and assets — is one of the most underutilized benefits in Medicare. Somewhere around a third of eligible people aren't enrolled. Not because they don't need it. Because they don't know it exists, or they assume their income is too high, or they started the application and gave up.

If you have Medicare Part D and your income is under roughly $24,000/year as a single person (or $48,000 for a couple), you should at minimum go through the eligibility checklist in this article before concluding you don't qualify. The income and asset rules have more flexibility than the headline numbers suggest, and the benefit — when you do qualify — is enormous.

In 2026, Extra Help beneficiaries pay $0-12.65 per prescription fill instead of normal Part D cost-sharing. No deductible. No gap. No hitting the $2,100 cap — because they'll almost certainly never get anywhere near it.

Let me lay out exactly who qualifies and how to get it.

sections.heading: The 2026 Income Limits
sections.content:
Extra Help is available to Medicare beneficiaries with income at or below 150% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). For 2026, that translates to:

Single individual: $22,590/year ($1,882.50/month)
Married couple: $30,660/year ($2,555/month)

Note: Some sources cite slightly different figures depending on whether they're using 2026 FPL figures vs. estimates. The Social Security Administration updates these numbers annually. The $16,590 single / $33,100 couple figures frequently cited are the resource (asset) limits, not income limits — a common source of confusion. I'll cover both clearly.

Important nuance: These income limits are not as hard-edged as they look. SSA doesn't count all income. Things that are excluded from the calculation include:
- One $20 general income exclusion
- Earned income exclusion ($65/month plus half of remaining earnings for people who still work)
- Some forms of assistance payments
- The value of food stamps or SNAP benefits
- Housing assistance payments

So if someone is working part-time on top of Social Security, their countable income for Extra Help purposes is substantially lower than their gross income. This is why checking with SSA directly — or using Medicare.gov/extra-help — is worth doing even if your first instinct is that you're over the limit.

sections.heading: The 2026 Asset (Resource) Limits
sections.content:
Even if you pass the income test, you still need to pass the asset test. For 2026, the resource limits are:

Single individual: $16,590 in countable resources
Married couple: $33,100 in countable resources

These limits include an automatic $1,500 per person ($3,000 for a couple) already built in for burial expenses — meaning if you have up to $1,500 set aside for burial, that portion isn't subtracted separately from your asset limit.

What counts as a resource (assets that count):
- Checking and savings accounts
- Stocks, bonds, mutual funds
- IRAs and 401(k)s (yes, retirement accounts count)
- Cash on hand
- Real estate other than your primary home

What does NOT count:
- Your primary home (regardless of value)
- One vehicle (regardless of value)
- Personal property like furniture, jewelry
- Life insurance with cash surrender value up to $1,500/person
- Burial plots and burial funds set aside (up to $1,500/person)

The home exclusion is significant. Someone who owns a $400,000 house but has minimal savings can still qualify for Extra Help. The program was designed this way intentionally — CMS recognized that home equity shouldn't disqualify retired homeowners from drug coverage assistance.

A nuance that trips people up: the resource limit is measured as of the application date, not as an annual average. If you had $20,000 in savings but spent down to $15,000 before applying — and that spending was on legitimate living expenses — you may qualify. SSA isn't looking for spend-down games, but legitimate fluctuation in assets is normal and expected.

sections.heading: Full LIS vs. Partial LIS — The Two Benefit Levels
sections.content:
Extra Help isn't binary. There are actually two benefit levels, and the distinction matters.

Full Extra Help (Full LIS): Available to people with income at or below 135% FPL and resources within limits. Also automatically granted to people on full Medicaid, SSI, or a Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, QI).

With Full Extra Help in 2026:
- No Part D deductible
- Copays for generic drugs: $1.60-4.90 per fill depending on income level
- Copays for brand-name drugs: $4.90-9.85 per fill
- No premiums for benchmark Part D plans (more on this below)
- Essentially unlimited drug coverage with minimal OOP

Partial Extra Help: Available to people with income between 135%-150% FPL with resources within limits.

With Partial Extra Help in 2026:
- Reduced deductible (not $0, but lower)
- Higher copays than Full LIS but still far below normal cost-sharing
- Some premium assistance depending on income

For both levels: once you qualify, there is no OOP cap to worry about because cost-sharing is so low you'll almost certainly never reach $2,100 anyway. The cap and LIS are both safety nets, but they serve different populations. The cap helps middle-income people with expensive drugs. LIS/Extra Help helps lower-income people stay healthy in the first place.

The specific 2026 copay tiers:
Income ≤100% FPL: $1.60/generic, $4.90/brand
Income 100-135% FPL (Full LIS): $4.90/generic, $9.85/brand  
Income 135-150% FPL (Partial LIS): $12.65/brand, $5.10/generic (approximate)
QMB beneficiaries: $4.90 maximum per fill

sections.heading: Auto-Enrollment: Who Gets It Without Applying
sections.content:
Here's the part that surprises most people: a significant subset of eligible beneficiaries are automatically enrolled in Extra Help without doing anything.

You are automatically enrolled in Extra Help if you:
- Are enrolled in full Medicaid (dual eligible)
- Receive Supplemental Security Income (SSI)
- Are enrolled in a Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, or QI)

CMS identifies these individuals through data sharing with SSA and state Medicaid agencies. If you fall into one of these categories, you'll receive a letter from CMS confirming your Extra Help enrollment — you don't apply, you don't do anything. It just happens.

The letter matters though. It's called a Notice of Deemed Eligibility, and it confirms your LIS status. If you lose it, call 1-800-MEDICARE and they can confirm your status in the system.

For auto-enrollees on a benchmark plan: CMS will auto-assign you to a qualifying low-cost Part D plan if you don't choose one yourself. You can always switch to a different plan — and you should check whether the auto-assigned plan has your drugs on formulary — but the assignment ensures you're not stuck without coverage.

Dual-eligible beneficiaries (on both Medicare and Medicaid) are a particularly important subset. They get full Extra Help automatically, but they also need to make sure their Part D plan has their medications covered. The automatic assignment doesn't guarantee a perfect formulary match. Checking annually during AEP is worth it even for auto-enrollees.

sections.heading: How to Apply If You're Not Auto-Enrolled
sections.content:
If you're not automatically enrolled, you need to apply. It's not complicated, but there are three paths and knowing the differences matters.

Path 1: Online at SSA.gov/extrahelp. This is the fastest option. The application takes about 15-20 minutes if you have your financial information ready. SSA processes online applications relatively quickly — often within a few weeks.

Path 2: Call Social Security at 1-800-772-1213. You can apply over the phone. If you or a family member prefers this over online forms, it's a fully supported option. TTY users: 1-800-325-0778.

Path 3: Visit your local Social Security office. Bring documentation: Medicare card, Social Security number, recent bank statements, list of assets. In-person applications work well for people who have complex financial situations or questions they want answered while applying.

One important clarification: Extra Help is administered by the Social Security Administration, not by Medicare. You apply through SSA, not through Medicare.gov. A lot of people get confused on this and submit their application to the wrong place.

What you'll need:
- Your Medicare number
- Information about income (Social Security award letter, pension info, any work income)
- Bank account balances
- Other asset values (stocks, bonds, etc.)
- Information about any burial fund or prepaid funeral

SSA will mail you a decision. If approved, they'll also tell you the effective date and your benefit level. If denied, you have the right to appeal — and I'll cover that process below.

sections.heading: What Extra Help Actually Covers (And What It Doesn't)
sections.content:
People sometimes think Extra Help is a separate insurance program. It's not. It's a subsidy that sits on top of your existing Part D plan and reduces what you owe at the pharmacy counter.

With Extra Help, your Part D plan still determines which drugs are covered (the formulary). Extra Help doesn't change that. What it changes is how much you pay for the drugs that are covered.

It covers:
- Most of your Part D premium (for benchmark/low-premium plans, the entire premium is covered)
- Your Part D deductible (fully for Full LIS beneficiaries)
- Your drug copays, down to the low levels described above

It does not cover:
- Drugs not on your plan's formulary (same as for non-LIS beneficiaries)
- Part A and Part B costs — Extra Help is strictly for Part D drug coverage
- Any costs associated with hospital stays, doctor visits, or medical services
- Over-the-counter medications

About the premium benefit: Extra Help pays your plan's premium up to the benchmark premium amount for your state. The benchmark is recalculated annually. In most states, there are Part D plans with premiums at or below the benchmark, meaning Extra Help covers them entirely — $0 premium to you. If you choose a plan with a premium above the benchmark, you pay the difference yourself.

This is why Extra Help recipients should choose plans at or near the benchmark whenever their formulary needs are met. Paying excess premium reduces the value of Extra Help without adding drug coverage benefit.

sections.heading: If You Were Denied: The Appeal and the State Programs
sections.content:
SSA denial isn't the end. Not even close.

First: appeal the SSA decision if you disagree. You have 60 days from the denial notice to request reconsideration. If your income or assets are genuinely at or near the limits, denials sometimes happen because of data discrepancies or misreported information — and reconsiderations frequently reverse initial denials.

Second: apply for the Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs). MSPs are state programs that help pay Medicare costs for people with limited income. There are four levels — QMB, SLMB, QI, and QDWI. Qualifying for any MSP automatically qualifies you for Full Extra Help. And MSP income limits are sometimes slightly different from SSA's Extra Help limits, meaning some people who don't qualify for Extra Help directly might qualify via MSP. Apply through your state Medicaid office.

Third: check state pharmaceutical assistance programs (SPAPs). Many states have their own programs that help Medicare beneficiaries with drug costs — some even for people above federal Extra Help income limits. These programs vary wildly by state and are worth a separate search for your state. NCOA's BenefitsCheckUp tool (benefitscheckup.org) catalogs state-level programs.

Fourth: manufacturer patient assistance programs. For specific expensive drugs, the manufacturer may offer direct assistance — particularly for people who are over Extra Help income limits but still struggling with costs. NeedyMeds.org and RxAssist.org are good starting points.

The system has more safety nets than people realize. The mistake is giving up at 'no.'

faq.question: What is the income limit for Medicare Extra Help in 2026?
faq.answer: To qualify for Extra Help, your income must be at or below 150% of the Federal Poverty Level — approximately $22,590/year for a single person and $30,660/year for a married couple in 2026. SSA excludes certain types of income from the calculation, including a $20 general exclusion and earned income exclusions, so your countable income may be lower than your total income.

faq.question: What is the asset limit for Medicare Extra Help in 2026?
faq.answer: Resource limits for 2026 are $16,590 for a single individual and $33,100 for a married couple. Your primary home and one vehicle are not counted regardless of value. Retirement accounts (IRAs, 401(k)s) do count. The limits include a built-in $1,500 per person allowance for burial expenses.

faq.question: Who is automatically enrolled in Extra Help without applying?
faq.answer: People enrolled in full Medicaid, receiving SSI (Supplemental Security Income), or enrolled in a Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, or QI) are automatically enrolled in Full Extra Help. CMS mails a confirmation notice. Everyone else must apply through the Social Security Administration.

faq.question: How much do I pay for prescriptions with Extra Help in 2026?
faq.answer: It depends on your income level. At or below 100% FPL: $1.60/generic, $4.90/brand. Between 100-135% FPL: $4.90/generic, $9.85/brand. Between 135-150% FPL (partial LIS): approximately $5.10/generic, $12.65/brand. QMB beneficiaries: $4.90 maximum per fill. There is no deductible for Full LIS beneficiaries.

faq.question: Where do I apply for Extra Help?
faq.answer: Through the Social Security Administration — not through Medicare. Online at SSA.gov/extrahelp, by calling 1-800-772-1213, or at a local Social Security office. Do not submit Extra Help applications to Medicare.gov; SSA administers this program.

faq.question: Does my house count against the Extra Help asset limit?
faq.answer: No. Your primary residence is excluded from the resource count regardless of its value. This means a homeowner with significant home equity but limited liquid assets can still qualify for Extra Help.

faq.question: What if I don't qualify for Extra Help but still can't afford my drugs?
faq.answer: Check state Medicare Savings Programs (through your state Medicaid office) — qualifying for any MSP also grants Full Extra Help. Check state pharmaceutical assistance programs via BenefitsCheckUp.org. Check manufacturer patient assistance programs through NeedyMeds.org. And remember: if you were denied Extra Help, you have 60 days to appeal.

h1: Medicare Part D Formulary: Understanding Drug Tiers
metaDescription: How Medicare Part D drug tiers work in 2026. What Tiers 1 through 5 mean for your costs. How to check whether your drug is covered. What to do when your drug isn't on the formulary or has restrictions.
summary: I've worked with Medicare beneficiaries who spend hours comparing plan premiums down to the dollar — and don't even know what tier their most expensive medication is on. That's backwards. The formulary and the tier structure determine the majority of your actual drug costs. The premium is almost sec

sections.heading: The Formulary Is the Most Important Document Most Medicare Beneficiaries Never Read
sections.content:
I've worked with Medicare beneficiaries who spend hours comparing plan premiums down to the dollar — and don't even know what tier their most expensive medication is on. That's backwards. The formulary and the tier structure determine the majority of your actual drug costs. The premium is almost secondary.

A formulary is just a list of drugs that a Part D plan covers, along with the tier — and therefore the cost-sharing — assigned to each drug. Every Part D plan has one. They're required by CMS to publish it and update it at least quarterly.

The thing people don't always realize: formularies are not uniform across plans. The same drug can be on Tier 2 in one plan and Tier 4 in another. That difference can mean hundreds or thousands of dollars per year. And plans can change formularies during the plan year — though they're required to give notice for most changes.

This article is going to make you fluent in how tiers work, what the restrictions mean, and what you can do when the system doesn't cooperate.

sections.heading: The Five-Tier Structure — What Each Level Means
sections.content:
Most Part D plans use a five-tier structure, though some use four tiers and a few use six. Here's the standard breakdown:

Tier 1 — Preferred Generics
Lowest cost. These are generic drugs — chemical equivalents to brand-name drugs — that the plan has designated as preferred. The term 'preferred' means the plan has negotiated better pricing with the manufacturer or distributor, passing savings to you. Typical cost-sharing: $0-5 copay per fill. Some plans charge $0 for Tier 1 at preferred pharmacies.

Tier 2 — Non-Preferred Generics (or Generic)
Generic drugs that didn't land in Tier 1's preferred category. Still generics, still cheap — just slightly more than Tier 1. Typical cost-sharing: $5-15 copay per fill.

Tier 3 — Preferred Brand-Name Drugs
Brand-name drugs the plan considers preferred — usually because they're clinically advantageous relative to alternatives, the plan has a better negotiated rate, or both. This tier is important because several expensive medications (including some IRA-negotiated drugs in favorable plan structures) can land here. Typical cost-sharing: $30-50 copay per fill, or 20-25% coinsurance.

Tier 4 — Non-Preferred Brand-Name Drugs
Brand-name drugs on formulary but not preferred. Higher cost-sharing. A drug landing on Tier 4 instead of Tier 3 can mean $40-80 more per fill depending on the drug's price. Typical cost-sharing: $75-100 copay or 30-45% coinsurance.

Tier 5 — Specialty Drugs
High-cost drugs — biologics, cancer medications, some MS drugs, certain HIV treatments — that require special handling or have extremely high list prices. This tier almost always uses percentage coinsurance rather than flat copays, typically 25-33% of the negotiated price. On a $10,000/month specialty drug at 25% coinsurance, that's $2,500/month — which is why hitting the $2,100 annual OOP cap is so critical for specialty drug users.

Some plans add a Tier 6 for select care or preventive drugs (often at $0 cost). The IRA also requires certain insulin types to be available at $35/month or less — some plans put insulin on a special Tier or handle it via the mandate structure separately.

sections.heading: How to Find Your Drug's Tier
sections.content:
You have three good options here and you should use at least two of them.

Option 1: Medicare.gov Plan Finder. When you enter your drugs at Medicare.gov/plan-compare, the tool shows you not just whether a drug is covered but which tier it's on and what you'd pay per fill under each available plan. This is the most comprehensive tool and the one I'd start with during Annual Enrollment Period.

Option 2: The plan's own formulary search. Every Part D plan maintains a searchable drug list on their website (it may be labeled 'formulary,' 'drug list,' or 'covered drugs'). You can search by drug name, and the result shows the tier and any utilization management requirements (prior auth, step therapy, quantity limits). The plan's formulary search is sometimes more current than the Plan Finder — especially mid-year when quarterly updates have been applied.

Option 3: Call the plan directly. Sometimes the online tools lag or are unclear about a specific form or dosage. A 10-minute call to the plan's pharmacy line can clarify exactly what tier your specific medication, dose, and form factor lands on and what the cost-sharing is.

Two things worth checking beyond just the tier:

Preferred vs. non-preferred pharmacy cost-sharing. Many plans have different cost-sharing tiers for preferred vs. standard vs. mail-order pharmacies. A Tier 2 drug at a preferred pharmacy might be $10/fill. The same drug at a non-preferred pharmacy might be $25/fill. The same drug through mail order for a 90-day supply might be $20. These differences compound over 12 months.

Whether the drug requires prior authorization, step therapy, or quantity limits. I cover those in detail in the next section — but if you're checking a drug's tier and see any of these flags, plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Prior Authorization: What It Is and How to Navigate It
sections.content:
Prior authorization means your prescriber has to get approval from the plan before the plan will cover the drug. The plan wants documentation that the drug is medically necessary for your specific situation.

Why plans require it: partly cost management (making sure expensive drugs are used appropriately), partly clinical gatekeeping (some drugs have narrow approved indications and plans want confirmation you meet them).

How it works in practice: Your doctor's office submits a PA request — usually a form plus supporting clinical documentation — to the plan. The plan has 72 hours to respond for a standard request, or 24 hours for an expedited request if there's urgent medical need.

When PA gets denied (and it does), you have options:
1. Have your doctor submit additional clinical documentation supporting the necessity. Many initial denials are overturned on reconsideration when more complete records are submitted.
2. Request a formulary exception (covered below).
3. File a formal appeal — which has multiple levels including the plan level, the Independent Review Entity (IRE), and ultimately courts if needed.

The critical timing issue: prior auth problems almost always show up in January. You switched plans, or the plan changed its PA requirements, and now you're standing at the pharmacy on January 2nd and the system says 'prior auth required.' This is why it's essential to check for PA requirements BEFORE January — during AEP when you're evaluating plans. If your plan of choice requires PA on your primary medication, start the PA process in December so coverage is in place at the start of the year.

Expedited PA is available and you have a right to request it if your condition is urgent. 'Urgent' is intentionally defined somewhat broadly — if waiting 72 hours would seriously jeopardize your health, expedited review (24-hour decision) applies.

sections.heading: Step Therapy: The 'Try This First' Requirement
sections.content:
Step therapy is a type of prior authorization with a specific mechanism: before the plan will cover Drug B, you have to try Drug A (a cheaper alternative) and demonstrate it didn't work or caused adverse effects.

The theory is clinically sound — lots of conditions respond well to cheaper first-line drugs, and it's reasonable policy to try those before jumping to expensive options. The problem is when the 'stepping' requirement is applied to situations where:
1. You already tried the step therapy drug years ago under a different plan and it failed
2. The cheaper drug is contraindicated for you based on a different medical condition
3. Your doctor has clinical reasons to start with the higher-tier drug

In all three cases, you can request a step therapy exception by providing documentation to the plan. Your doctor writes a letter explaining why the required step therapy drug is clinically inappropriate for you specifically. With adequate documentation, plans are required to consider this.

Step therapy exceptions have a specific legal framework under CMS rules. Plans cannot refuse to consider them. And if the plan denies the exception request, you can appeal.

One thing to know: step therapy records from other plans generally don't transfer automatically. If you tried and failed Drug A under your previous plan, your new plan doesn't know that. Your doctor's office needs to provide that history when requesting an exception. It's worth a preemptive call to the doctor's billing/PA coordinator in December to flag any upcoming step therapy issues.

sections.heading: Quantity Limits and Why They Exist
sections.content:
Quantity limits restrict how much of a drug the plan will cover in a given time period. Common examples: only 30 tablets per 30-day period, or no more than 2 refills in 30 days for a drug with misuse potential.

The reasons for quantity limits vary:

Safety: Some drugs have dosing limits where exceeding them is clinically dangerous. A quantity limit enforces the safe maximum dose.

Clinical appropriateness: Limits ensure the drug is being used as prescribed and not stockpiled or shared.

Cost management: Limiting quantity on expensive drugs controls plan spend.

When quantity limits are a problem: Usually when a patient legitimately needs more than the standard limit. For example, someone with severe pain who takes opioid medications at higher doses than average, or a patient with high pill burden needing more frequent fills due to dosing requirements.

Getting around legitimate quantity limits: Your doctor can request a quantity limit exception with clinical justification. The same exception framework that applies to PA and step therapy applies here. Document why the standard quantity limit is insufficient for your specific treatment plan.

Don't confuse a quantity limit with a plan simply not covering more than 30 days at retail. Many plans cover 90-day supplies — at lower per-unit cost-sharing — through mail order. If your issue is pharmacy convenience vs. cost, mail order often resolves both.

sections.heading: What to Do When Your Drug Isn't on the Formulary
sections.content:
Finding out your drug isn't covered is a real scenario that happens more than it should. Here's the playbook.

First, double-check. Search the plan's formulary for alternative brand names, generic names, and different forms of the drug. Sometimes the drug IS on the formulary but under a different name than you searched. Call the plan to confirm.

If it's genuinely not covered, you have four options:

1. Request a Formulary Exception
This is a formal request for the plan to cover a drug not on its formulary. Your doctor provides clinical justification — why the covered alternatives are medically inappropriate for you and why this specific drug is necessary. The plan has 72 hours to respond (24 hours for expedited). If denied, you can appeal.

Formulary exceptions are not always granted, but they're worth pursuing if the clinical justification is solid. The more specific and documented the doctor's reasoning, the better the outcome.

2. Appeal the Denial
If your formulary exception is denied, you enter the formal Medicare appeals process:
- Level 1: Redetermination by the plan
- Level 2: Reconsideration by the Independent Review Entity (IRE)
- Level 3: Administrative Law Judge hearing (available if drug value ≥$200 in 2026)
- Level 4: Medicare Appeals Council
- Level 5: Federal District Court (drug value ≥$1,960 in 2026)

Most legitimate denials get resolved by Level 2. The IRE is external and independent from the plan, and it overturns plan denials at a meaningful rate.

3. Switch Plans at the Next Opportunity
If this is a regular medication and the exception/appeal fails, seriously consider whether this plan is right for you. During AEP (Oct 15 – Dec 7), switch to a plan that covers your drug on formulary. The cost-saving from a lower premium on a plan that doesn't cover your critical medication is almost never worth it.

4. Use Manufacturer or Pharmacy Programs as a Bridge
If you're waiting on an appeal or exception decision and need the drug now, ask your doctor about manufacturer samples, call the manufacturer's patient assistance line, or check GoodRx for cash pricing. These are bridge solutions, not permanent fixes — but they keep you on your medication while the system catches up.

sections.heading: Formulary Changes During the Year
sections.content:
This surprises people. Part D plans can change their formularies during the year — not just at AEP renewal. CMS allows mid-year changes subject to rules:

Plans can add new drugs at any time.

For removing a drug or moving it to a higher cost-sharing tier: plans must give 60 days advance notice EXCEPT for safety reasons (FDA safety alerts or new clinical evidence of harm allow faster removal).

For adding new utilization management requirements (prior auth, step therapy) to a currently-covered drug: 60 days advance notice required.

If you receive a formulary change notice and the change affects a drug you take: you have the right to request an exception to keep your current coverage terms while you appeal or find an alternative. This is specifically protected under CMS rules.

Protection for people stabilized on a drug: CMS has a rule that if you're currently stabilized on a drug and the plan removes it from formulary mid-year, the plan must cover it through the end of the calendar year. This prevents the most disruptive mid-year coverage gaps.

The practical takeaway: pay attention to your mail and email from your Part D plan. The 60-day notices are real notices of real changes. If you get one and your drug is affected, don't ignore it.

faq.question: What is a Medicare Part D formulary?
faq.answer: A formulary is the official list of prescription drugs covered by a Part D plan, organized into cost-sharing tiers. Plans must publish their formulary and update it at least quarterly. The tier your drug is on directly determines your copay or coinsurance amount.

faq.question: What are the five tiers in Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Standard tiers: Tier 1 (preferred generics, lowest cost), Tier 2 (non-preferred generics), Tier 3 (preferred brand-name drugs), Tier 4 (non-preferred brand-name drugs), Tier 5 (specialty drugs, highest cost-sharing, often 25-33% coinsurance). Some plans have four or six tiers — always check your specific plan's structure.

faq.question: How do I check what tier my drug is on?
faq.answer: Three options: Medicare.gov/plan-compare (enter your drugs to see tier placement across all available plans), your plan's formulary search tool on their website, or call your plan's pharmacy help line directly. Check both the tier and any utilization management requirements like prior authorization.

faq.question: What is prior authorization in Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Prior authorization requires your prescriber to get plan approval before coverage kicks in. The plan has 72 hours for standard decisions, 24 hours for expedited requests. If your drug requires PA, start the process before January — ideally in December if you're staying on the plan, or before enrolling if you're switching.

faq.question: What is step therapy and can I avoid it?
faq.answer: Step therapy requires you to try a less expensive drug first before the plan covers your prescribed drug. You can request a step therapy exception if the required drug is contraindicated, you already tried it and it failed, or your doctor has clinical reasons to skip it. Provide detailed medical documentation with the exception request.

faq.question: What happens if my drug isn't on the formulary?
faq.answer: Request a formulary exception — your doctor submits clinical justification for why covered alternatives are inappropriate. If denied, appeal through the formal Medicare appeals process (up to five levels). If you need the drug now while waiting, ask your doctor about samples or check manufacturer assistance programs as a bridge.

faq.question: Can a Part D plan remove my drug from formulary mid-year?
faq.answer: Yes, but with limitations. Plans must give 60 days advance notice before removing a drug or moving it to a higher tier (except for safety-related FDA actions). If you're currently stabilized on a drug that's removed, CMS rules protect your coverage through the end of the calendar year.

h1: Medicare Part D Late Enrollment Penalty
metaDescription: How the Medicare Part D late enrollment penalty is calculated, real dollar examples at 12, 24, and 60 months late, what creditable coverage exempts you, and how to appeal if you think the penalty was applied in error.
summary: Every year, Medicare counselors have some version of this conversation: someone turned 65, was healthy, didn't take any prescriptions, figured they'd enroll in Part D when they needed it. Seemed reasonable at the time. Now they're 70, they need six medications, and they're about to discover that the

sections.heading: The Penalty Nobody Thinks Will Apply to Them
sections.content:
Every year, Medicare counselors have some version of this conversation: someone turned 65, was healthy, didn't take any prescriptions, figured they'd enroll in Part D when they needed it. Seemed reasonable at the time. Now they're 70, they need six medications, and they're about to discover that the Medicare late enrollment penalty has been quietly accruing for 60 months.

The penalty is permanent. It stays with you for as long as you have Part D coverage. And it compounds annually because it's calculated as a percentage of the national base beneficiary premium, which goes up every year.

I've watched people absorb $50-80/month in penalty on top of their plan premium for the rest of their lives over this. For someone who lives 20 years into Medicare, that's $12,000-19,200 in extra premium payments from a completely avoidable mistake.

So let me be very direct about how this works.

sections.heading: The Core Calculation — 1% Per Month, Permanent
sections.content:
The Part D late enrollment penalty formula is:

1% x (number of uncovered months) x (national base beneficiary premium)

For 2026, the national base beneficiary premium is $38.99.

The 'uncovered months' is the number of full calendar months you went without Part D coverage OR qualifying creditable coverage after your initial enrollment period ended. The count starts from the end of your Initial Enrollment Period — not your 65th birthday specifically, though they're closely related.

The math: if you have 24 uncovered months, your penalty is 24% of $38.99 = $9.36/month, rounded to the nearest $0.10 = $9.40/month.

That $9.40 gets added to your Part D plan premium every month for life. Not for 24 months. For life.

And here's the compounding problem: the national base beneficiary premium changes every year. The penalty percentage (24% in this example) stays fixed — but the dollar amount changes as the base premium changes. So if the base premium goes up next year, your 24% penalty in dollar terms goes up too.

This is why the penalty can quietly grow from $9.40/month to $10+ to $12+ over a decade without you necessarily noticing, because the plan premium is also changing and it's hard to separate what's penalty and what's regular premium.

sections.heading: What Counts as 'Creditable Coverage' — The Key to Avoiding the Penalty
sections.content:
The penalty clock only runs for months when you have neither Part D coverage nor creditable coverage. Creditable coverage is any drug coverage that's expected to pay, on average, at least as much as standard Medicare Part D coverage.

What qualifies as creditable coverage:

Employer or union group health insurance: If you're still working at 65 (or covered by a working spouse's insurance), most employer drug coverage is creditable. Your employer is required to tell you annually whether their coverage is creditable. Look for the Annual Notice of Creditable Coverage in the fall — it should arrive around October. Keep it. If you later need to prove creditable coverage to contest a penalty, this is your documentation.

VA (Department of Veterans Affairs) drug coverage: VA drug benefits count as creditable coverage for Part D purposes. Veterans who have VA coverage and don't enroll in Part D are protected from the penalty as long as they maintain their VA benefits.

TRICARE: Active-duty military coverage. TRICARE for Life (TFL) in retirement also qualifies.

FEHP (Federal Employees Health Benefits Program): Federal government employee/retiree coverage qualifies.

Not all retiree drug coverage qualifies — specifically check that your retiree plan's drug coverage is documented as creditable. Some bare-bones retiree plans are not creditable, and people who assumed they were covered have discovered painful penalties years later.

What does NOT count:

Discount drug cards (GoodRx and similar): These are not insurance and do not count as creditable coverage. People who used GoodRx for years instead of enrolling in Part D sometimes have this hard conversation.

Medicare Supplement (Medigap) alone: Medigap plans don't cover outpatient drugs. A Medigap plan without Part D is not creditable drug coverage.

Most short-term health plans: These typically don't have qualifying drug benefits.

The 63-day rule: You can have a gap between creditable coverage and Part D enrollment of up to 63 days without triggering the penalty. This allows for normal transition times between plans. But 64 days or more — even once — starts the penalty clock.

sections.heading: Real Dollar Examples: 12, 24, and 60 Months Late
sections.content:
Let's put real numbers on this using the 2026 base premium of $38.99.

Scenario 1: 12 Months Late
Someone retired at 65, thought their spouse's employer coverage was creditable but didn't check, and it turned out it wasn't. They enrolled in Part D 12 months after their IEP ended.

Penalty: 12% x $38.99 = $4.68, rounded to $4.70/month
Annual penalty cost: ~$56.40

Not devastating. But it never goes away. Over a 20-year retirement, that 12-month oversight costs over $1,000 in penalty — not counting future premium base increases.

Scenario 2: 24 Months Late
Someone who retired at 65, had no drug coverage, was healthy, didn't see the point. Two years later, a chronic condition diagnosis finally prompted enrollment.

Penalty: 24% x $38.99 = $9.36, rounded to $9.40/month
Annual penalty cost: ~$112.80

Over 20 years (with some base premium growth): potentially $2,500-3,000 total extra paid in penalties.

Scenario 3: 60 Months Late (5 Years)
Someone who worked freelance, had no drug coverage, didn't realize the rule applied to them. Five years after becoming Medicare-eligible, finally enrolled.

Penalty: 60% x $38.99 = $23.39, rounded to $23.40/month
Annual penalty cost: ~$280.80

At $23.40/month penalty, a person on a $25/month Part D plan is now paying $48.40/month. Over a 20-year retirement, accounting for base premium changes: potentially $7,000-10,000 in total penalty payments. For a benefit they chose to forgo for five years while healthy.

Scenario 4: The Frank L. Example from CMS
Frank became Medicare-eligible in May 2022, had no coverage, enrolled in December 2025 (43-month gap).

Penalty: 43% x $38.99 = $16.77, rounded to $16.80/month

That $16.80 penalty follows Frank forever, adjusted annually as the base premium changes.

sections.heading: When Does the Penalty Clock Start?
sections.content:
This is worth understanding precisely because the timing is non-obvious.

Your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) is a 7-month window: 3 months before your 65th birthday month, your birthday month itself, and 3 months after. If you turn 65 in July, your IEP runs April through October.

After the IEP closes, the penalty clock can start. Specifically: the clock starts if you go 63+ consecutive days without Part D or creditable coverage, at any point after your IEP.

Complication: if you enroll in Part D during your IEP but then voluntarily drop coverage without replacing it with creditable coverage, the clock starts from when you dropped coverage — not from when your IEP ended.

Another complication: if you had a Special Enrollment Period (SEP) — for example, you were covered by employer insurance while working and enrolling during an SEP when you retired — and you missed that SEP window, the clock starts from when the SEP window closed.

For most people the simplest rule is: enroll in Part D during your IEP unless you have documented, creditable alternative drug coverage. Don't wait. Don't assume.

If you're turning 65 and still working with qualifying employer coverage: get the Annual Notice of Creditable Coverage from your employer, file it somewhere you can find it, and only enroll in Part D when you lose that employer coverage (which triggers an SEP with no penalty gap).

sections.heading: How to Avoid the Penalty — The Full Prevention Checklist
sections.content:
Prevention is much better than dealing with the penalty after the fact. Here's the complete checklist.

At 65 (if no other qualifying coverage): Enroll in Part D during your IEP. Even if you take no prescriptions and expect to pay near-zero in drug costs, a low-premium plan ($0-10/month) is almost always worth it as penalty insurance. You're essentially paying a small amount now to avoid a permanent surcharge later.

If you're still working at 65 with employer drug coverage: Get written documentation (the Annual Notice) that your employer coverage is creditable. Keep it. You'll potentially need it years later when you do enroll.

If you have VA coverage: VA drug benefits are creditable. You don't need Part D as long as you maintain VA enrollment and access. But if you ever lose VA coverage, enroll in Part D within 63 days — the SEP applies.

If you're on TRICARE or FEHBP: Both are creditable. Same principle — maintain documentation and enroll in Part D if you ever lose this coverage.

If you retire before 65: This isn't a Part D issue yet — Part D eligibility is tied to Medicare eligibility, not retirement age. But be aware of this coming at 65 and plan accordingly.

If you join a Medicare Advantage plan: If your MA plan includes drug coverage (called MA-PD), you're enrolled in Part D equivalent coverage and the late enrollment penalty doesn't apply. But if you join an MA plan WITHOUT drug coverage and don't have other creditable coverage, the clock runs.

Document everything. The penalty disputes that succeed are overwhelmingly the ones where the beneficiary has documentation of prior creditable coverage. Disputes without documentation almost never succeed.

sections.heading: How to Appeal If You Think the Penalty Is Wrong
sections.content:
You can appeal a Part D late enrollment penalty. The process is specific and the outcome depends heavily on documentation.

Step 1: Request a reconsideration from Medicare.
When you receive notice of your penalty (typically at enrollment), you'll have information about how to dispute it. Request a reconsideration in writing from Medicare's contractor (CMS designates the Independent Review Entity for this). You must provide documentation of any creditable coverage periods.

Step 2: Gather your documentation.
This is where appeals succeed or fail. You need:
- Annual Notices of Creditable Coverage from employer plans
- Letters from VA, TRICARE, FEHBP confirming coverage dates
- Termination notices from prior coverage showing exact end dates
- Any written communications from a previous counselor or broker telling you coverage was creditable (if applicable)

Step 3: File within the appeal timeframe.
You'll have a specific window to appeal — typically noted in the penalty notice. Don't wait.

Common grounds for successful appeals:
- You had creditable coverage but it wasn't counted correctly
- The penalty calculation included months when you actually had coverage
- You had a Special Enrollment Period that should have applied
- You received incorrect guidance from official Medicare sources that led to the gap

The last one — incorrect official guidance — is a real basis for appeal. If a Medicare counselor, broker, or SSA representative told you your coverage was creditable when it wasn't, document that interaction to the best of your ability.

Reality check: if you genuinely had no coverage and no creditable alternative for the period in question, the penalty calculation is almost certainly correct and won't be overturned. The appeal process is for errors, not for relitigating valid penalties. The time to prevent the penalty is before you have a gap — not after.

sections.heading: The Penalty and Extra Help (LIS)
sections.content:
One important interaction: if you qualify for Extra Help (the Low Income Subsidy), the late enrollment penalty is waived. You won't pay it.

This applies both to new Extra Help recipients and, in some interpretations, to people who gain Extra Help after having been assessed a penalty. The subsidy effectively absorbs the penalty cost as part of its broader premium and cost-sharing assistance.

If you're in a situation where you have a significant late enrollment penalty AND you might qualify for Extra Help (see the income and asset limits in the Extra Help article), applying for Extra Help can provide double benefit: it reduces your drug cost-sharing dramatically AND eliminates the penalty surcharge.

This isn't widely known and it's one of the more important intersections in the Part D system. A counselor who only focuses on the penalty without asking about the person's financial situation is leaving a potentially significant benefit on the table.

faq.question: How is the Medicare Part D late enrollment penalty calculated?
faq.answer: The penalty is 1% of the national base beneficiary premium ($38.99 in 2026) multiplied by the number of full months you went without Part D or qualifying creditable coverage. The result is rounded to the nearest $0.10 and added to your monthly premium permanently. Example: 24 months uncovered = 24% x $38.99 = $9.40/month added to your premium forever.

faq.question: Is the late enrollment penalty permanent?
faq.answer: Yes. The penalty stays with you as long as you have Medicare drug coverage, even if you switch plans. The percentage stays fixed but the dollar amount can fluctuate slightly year to year because it's tied to the national base beneficiary premium, which CMS adjusts annually.

faq.question: What is creditable coverage for Part D?
faq.answer: Creditable coverage is drug insurance expected to pay at least as much as standard Medicare Part D. It includes most employer/union group plans, VA drug benefits, TRICARE, and FEHBP. GoodRx and discount cards are NOT creditable coverage. Your employer must send you an Annual Notice of Creditable Coverage each fall — keep it.

faq.question: What is the 63-day rule?
faq.answer: You can have a gap of up to 63 consecutive days between creditable coverage and Part D enrollment without triggering the penalty. On day 64 of a gap, the penalty clock starts. This 63-day grace period allows for normal plan transition times.

faq.question: Does the penalty apply if I have VA drug coverage?
faq.answer: No. VA drug benefits count as creditable coverage for Part D purposes. Veterans can delay or forgo Part D enrollment without penalty as long as they maintain VA coverage. If VA coverage ends, enroll in Part D within 63 days to avoid triggering the penalty.

faq.question: Can I appeal the late enrollment penalty?
faq.answer: Yes. Request reconsideration from Medicare's designated Independent Review Entity. Appeals succeed when you have documentation of creditable coverage that wasn't credited — Annual Notices, VA/TRICARE confirmation letters, termination notices. Appeals rarely succeed without documentation. If you genuinely had a coverage gap, the penalty will almost certainly stand.

faq.question: Does the penalty go away if I qualify for Extra Help?
faq.answer: Yes. If you qualify for Medicare Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy), the late enrollment penalty is waived. This is one of the most important reasons to check Extra Help eligibility if you have a substantial penalty — Extra Help both eliminates the penalty and dramatically reduces your drug cost-sharing.

h1: Medicare Open Enrollment Period 2026: Dates & What to Do
metaDescription: Medicare's Annual Enrollment Period runs October 15 through December 7, 2026. Here's exactly what you can change, what you should review, and the mistakes that cost people real money every single year.
summary: October 15 through December 7. Every year. Has been for a long time. And yet every January, the Medicare Rights Center and SHIP counselors across the country get flooded with calls from people who missed the window, didn't review their plan, got hit with a premium increase they didn't see coming, or

sections.heading: The Dates Are the Same Every Year — and People Still Miss Them
sections.content:
October 15 through December 7. Every year. Has been for a long time. And yet every January, the Medicare Rights Center and SHIP counselors across the country get flooded with calls from people who missed the window, didn't review their plan, got hit with a premium increase they didn't see coming, or—worse—found out their doctor is no longer in-network on January 1.

The Annual Enrollment Period, which Medicare officially calls the AEP (and some people call Open Enrollment, though that term technically means something slightly different), is the one time each year when most Medicare beneficiaries can make changes to their coverage. If you have Original Medicare with a standalone Part D drug plan, you can swap that drug plan for a different one. If you have a Medicare Advantage plan, you can switch to a different Advantage plan or go back to Original Medicare. Any changes you make take effect January 1 of the following year.

For 2026 coverage: AEP ran October 15 – December 7, 2025. If you're reading this in early 2026 and missed it, don't panic—there's still the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period running January 1 through March 31, which lets you make one plan change if you're currently in an Advantage plan. But that's a narrower window with fewer options. The next full AEP for 2027 coverage opens October 15, 2026.

Mark the calendar now. Seriously. Set a recurring reminder for October 14 every year that just says "Medicare — review plans tomorrow."

sections.heading: What You Can Actually Change During AEP
sections.content:
Let's be specific here because this is where people get confused. There are different enrollment periods for different situations, and AEP doesn't cover everything.

During AEP (Oct 15 – Dec 7) you can:

Switch from Original Medicare (Parts A and B) to a Medicare Advantage plan (Part C). If you've been on Original Medicare and want the all-in-one managed care option, this is your main window.

Switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to a different Medicare Advantage plan. Same plan type, different carrier or different plan within the same carrier. Maybe your current plan dropped your specialist, maybe another plan has better drug coverage, maybe the premiums changed.

Switch from Medicare Advantage back to Original Medicare. This is a big one. People sometimes join Advantage for the low premium, discover the network is too restrictive, and want out. AEP lets you do this. But read the section on Medigap below — the timing matters a lot.

Join a Part D drug plan if you don't have one. If you have Original Medicare and no drug coverage at all, AEP lets you add a standalone Part D plan. Be aware: if you've been without creditable drug coverage for more than 63 consecutive days since you first became eligible, you'll likely owe a late enrollment penalty on top of your premium. That penalty is permanent.

Switch from one Part D plan to a different Part D plan. Drug plans change their formularies — the list of covered drugs — every year. A drug that cost you $15/month in 2025 might cost $60/month in 2026 on the same plan. Check every year.

Drop a Part D plan entirely without penalty, but only if you're joining an Advantage plan that includes drug coverage (MAPD plan).

What you CANNOT do during AEP: buy a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plan with guaranteed issue rights. Medigap has its own separate enrollment rules. You can apply for Medigap during AEP, but insurers in most states can deny you or charge higher premiums based on health history unless you have guaranteed issue rights from a different trigger. More on that below.

sections.heading: The 2026 Changes You Need to Know Before You Review Plans
sections.content:
Not reviewing your plan every year is a genuine mistake. Plans change. Here's what's materially different for 2026 that should affect how you shop.

The biggest change for 2026 is the Part D out-of-pocket cap. Starting January 1, 2026, once your total spending on covered drugs hits $2,100 for the year — including your deductible and cost-sharing — you owe zero copays or coinsurance for the rest of the year. This is called the catastrophic phase, and it effectively eliminates the coverage gap ("donut hole") that used to cost some people thousands of dollars.

This cap also changes how you should compare plans. In 2025, some people paid extra for plans with low copays on expensive specialty drugs because the gap could devastate them. In 2026, the cap protects you. A plan with a higher cost-sharing structure but a lower premium might actually be the smarter buy now, depending on your specific medications.

The standard Part B premium is $202.90/month in 2026. The Part B deductible is $257. These aren't Advantage-specific — they apply to anyone on Original Medicare (and most Advantage plans pass the Part B premium through to you regardless).

Part D base beneficiary premium: $38.99/month. This is the benchmark number used to calculate late enrollment penalties — not necessarily what you'll pay, but what CMS uses as the floor.

Many Medicare Advantage plans have been scaling back supplemental benefits in 2026 — things like dental, vision, hearing, and gym memberships that got generous during the expansion years. If those extras were a reason you chose your current plan, verify they're still there and still cover what you need.

sections.heading: Step-by-Step Action Plan for AEP
sections.content:
This is the actual process. Not theory — what to physically do.

Step 1: Pull out your current plan documents. You need your Evidence of Coverage (EOC) and Summary of Benefits. Your plan mailed these to you in September/October. If you threw them out, log into your plan's member portal or call the number on your card. Medicare Advantage plans are also required to send you an Annual Notice of Change (ANOC) explaining what's different next year.

Step 2: List your doctors and specialists. Write down every provider you saw in the past 12 months and anyone you expect to see next year. Then verify—actually verify, don't assume—that each one is in-network for any plan you're considering. Call the doctor's billing office if you're not sure. Provider directories on plan websites are notoriously outdated.

Step 3: List your prescriptions. Every drug, every dosage, every frequency. Go to medicare.gov's Plan Finder tool (plan.medicare.gov) and enter your drugs. This tool is legitimately useful — it'll calculate your estimated total annual drug costs across multiple plans including premiums, deductibles, and copays. Run this comparison every single year even if nothing in your life has changed, because the plans themselves change.

Step 4: Look at what changed on your current plan. If your plan sent an ANOC saying your primary care copay goes from $5 to $25, or that your Part D deductible is going from $0 to $545 (the 2026 maximum), or that your oncologist dropped out of network — those are real dollar consequences. Don't assume "same plan" means same cost.

Step 5: Compare 2-3 alternatives. Use the Plan Finder or call your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for free help. SHIP counselors are not selling anything and have no commission incentive. They're paid by the state to give you unbiased guidance. Use them.

Step 6: Make your decision before December 1. Don't wait until December 7 to enroll. Systems crash, agents get overwhelmed, and if you hit a snag, you have no time to fix it. Give yourself a week of buffer.

Step 7: Confirm the enrollment went through. Call the plan directly or check online. Confirmations get lost. You want proof.

sections.heading: The Medigap Problem Nobody Warns You About
sections.content:
Here's a scenario that plays out constantly and it drives SHIP counselors crazy.

Someone is on Medicare Advantage for a few years. During AEP they decide they want to go back to Original Medicare — maybe they've had cancer treatment and the Advantage network is too restrictive, maybe they're traveling more and want nationwide coverage, maybe they just want predictable costs. So they switch to Original Medicare during AEP. Effective January 1.

Then they try to buy a Medigap plan to cover the 20% Original Medicare doesn't pay. And they get denied. Or quoted a premium three times what they'd expected. Or told they can't get Plan G at all because of a pre-existing condition.

Why? Because unlike Medicare Advantage, Medigap insurers in most states can underwrite. They can look at your health history and turn you down. The guaranteed issue window — the period where they must accept you regardless of health — is six months starting from when you first enroll in Part B at 65. Miss that window and you're subject to medical underwriting in most states.

Some states have protections.
