California, Oregon, Nevada, New York, and a handful of others have either birthday rules (you get an annual window to switch Medigap plans without underwriting) or year-round guaranteed issue. But in most of the country, once your initial Medigap enrollment window closes, you're on your own healthwise.

If you're thinking about switching from Advantage back to Original Medicare, check your state's Medigap rules before you make the switch. In some states you'll be fine. In others, if your health has changed, you may be stuck with Original Medicare and full cost exposure.

sections.heading: The Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period: Your Second Chance
sections.content:
If you missed AEP or made a change and immediately regretted it, there's a secondary window.

January 1 through March 31 is the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (MA OEP). During this period, anyone currently enrolled in a Medicare Advantage plan can make one switch: either to a different Medicare Advantage plan, or back to Original Medicare (with or without a Part D plan).

You can only make one change. So if you switch in January, you're done until next AEP. And changes take effect the first of the month after you enroll — January switch means February 1 start, February switch means March 1, March switch means April 1.

This window does NOT let you:
- Join Medicare Advantage if you're currently on Original Medicare
- Enroll in a standalone Part D plan if you're not switching from MA
- Make a second change after your first one

The MA OEP is a genuine safety valve, especially for people who joined a new plan in October/November, got hit with a scary January bill, and realized the plan doesn't work for them. Use it.

sections.heading: Six Mistakes That Cost People Real Money Every AEP
sections.content:
These aren't edge cases. These are the things SHIP counselors see constantly.

Mistake 1: Assuming your plan is fine because it was fine last year. Plans change formularies, networks, and premiums every year. A plan that was perfect in 2025 might be wrong for you in 2026. Review every year without exception.

Mistake 2: Not entering your actual drugs in the Plan Finder. People compare plans by premium alone, which is completely meaningless for drug coverage. A plan with a $0 premium and a $545 deductible might cost you $1,800 more per year than a plan with a $30 premium and $0 deductible, depending on your specific medications. Use the tool. Enter your drugs.

Mistake 3: Assuming your doctor takes the new plan. Provider directories are often 12-18 months out of date. Your doctor's office may have left a network months ago and the directory still shows them as in-network. Call the office directly. Ask billing. Confirm before you enroll.

Mistake 4: Switching back to Original Medicare without researching Medigap first. As described above, this can trap you in a situation where you can't get supplemental coverage at any price. Check your state's rules first.

Mistake 5: Enrolling in the wrong plan because of TV ads. The plans advertising heavily in October and November are not necessarily the best plans. They have marketing budgets. Check star ratings on medicare.gov (5 stars is best), check formulary coverage for your specific drugs, check the network for your specific doctors. The plan with the loudest commercial might be great or it might be terrible. Do the work.

Mistake 6: Missing the December 7 deadline entirely because "I'll do it tomorrow." AEP ends at midnight on December 7. After that, barring a qualifying life event, you're locked in until next October. Don't procrastinate this one.

sections.heading: When You Don't Need to Do Anything
sections.content:
Not every person on Medicare needs to make a change every AEP. If you're on Original Medicare with a Medigap policy you're happy with, your Medigap coverage rolls over automatically — no action needed. You might still want to review your standalone Part D plan because drug plans do change, but if you're healthy and your plan's formulary hasn't changed, you may be fine.

If you're on Medicare Advantage and your plan renewed without major changes to your network or drug coverage, and your doctors and prescriptions are still covered at comparable costs, staying put is a legitimate option.

The key word there is "if." You need to actually verify those things rather than assume them. Pull the ANOC. Run the Plan Finder. Make the call. Then decide whether to stay or switch.

faq.question: When exactly does Medicare AEP 2026 run?
faq.answer: The AEP for 2026 coverage ran October 15 through December 7, 2025. Any changes made during that period took effect January 1, 2026. The next AEP — for 2027 coverage — runs October 15 through December 7, 2026.

faq.question: Can I switch from Medicare Advantage to Original Medicare during AEP?
faq.answer: Yes. You can switch from Medicare Advantage to Original Medicare during AEP (October 15 – December 7). Your change takes effect January 1. Before doing this, research whether you can get a Medigap plan in your state — in most states, Medigap insurers can deny you coverage based on health history unless you have guaranteed issue rights.

faq.question: What is the Part D out-of-pocket cap for 2026?
faq.answer: Starting January 1, 2026, once you've spent $2,100 out of pocket on covered prescription drugs, your cost-sharing drops to $0 for the rest of the year. This eliminates the old coverage gap (donut hole) and caps your annual drug costs.

faq.question: What's the difference between AEP and the MA Open Enrollment Period?
faq.answer: AEP (October 15 – December 7) is for everyone on Medicare and lets you make any plan type change. The MA OEP (January 1 – March 31) is only for people currently enrolled in Medicare Advantage, and lets you make one change: switch to a different MA plan or go back to Original Medicare. If you're on Original Medicare, the MA OEP doesn't apply to you.

faq.question: Do I have to do anything during AEP if I'm happy with my current plan?
faq.answer: You're not required to make a change, but you should review your plan annually. Plans change their premiums, drug formularies, and provider networks each year. Check your Annual Notice of Change letter, run your prescriptions through the Medicare Plan Finder, and verify your doctors are still in-network. Then decide if staying put makes sense.

faq.question: Can I add Medigap coverage during AEP?
faq.answer: You can apply for Medigap during AEP, but in most states you're subject to medical underwriting outside of your guaranteed issue window. The guaranteed issue window is generally the 6 months starting when you first enroll in Part B at 65. Outside that window, insurers in most states can deny you or charge higher premiums based on health history.

faq.question: Where can I get free, unbiased help comparing plans?
faq.answer: Contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP). These are state-funded counselors with zero financial interest in which plan you choose — no commissions, no sales pressure. Find your local SHIP through medicare.gov or call 1-800-MEDICARE. Also useful: the Medicare Plan Finder at plan.medicare.gov lets you compare plans by entering your specific drugs and seeing estimated annual costs.

h1: Medicare at 65: What to Do 3 Months Before Your Birthday
metaDescription: Turning 65 triggers a 7-month enrollment window you can't afford to blow. Here's the complete timeline — from the SSA application to Part D decisions to the Medigap window that closes faster than most people realize.
summary: There is no grace period for figuring this out. Medicare has a specific enrollment window tied to your 65th birthday called the Initial Enrollment Period, and it runs for exactly 7 months. Three months before the month you turn 65. The month you turn 65. And three months after.

sections.heading: The Clock Starts 3 Months Before You Turn 65
sections.content:
There is no grace period for figuring this out. Medicare has a specific enrollment window tied to your 65th birthday called the Initial Enrollment Period, and it runs for exactly 7 months. Three months before the month you turn 65. The month you turn 65. And three months after.

Miss it — or miss parts of it — and you're looking at permanent premium penalties on Part B and Part D that follow you for life. Not a temporary surcharge. Permanent. For as long as you have Medicare.

The other thing most people don't realize: when you enroll within that 7-month window affects when your coverage actually starts. Enroll in the 3 months before your birthday month, and your coverage starts the first day of your birthday month. Enroll during your birthday month or in the 3 months after it, and your coverage starts the first of the following month — meaning you could have a gap.

Note on birthday timing: if your birthday falls on the first of the month, Medicare treats your birthday month as the prior month. So if you're born June 1, your birthday month for Medicare purposes is May. That's a CMS quirk that trips people up.

Bottom line: start the process 3 months out. Not 3 weeks. Not 3 days. Three months.

sections.heading: Month Minus 3: Start Here
sections.content:
Three months before the month you turn 65 is when your enrollment window opens. This is also the sweet spot — enrolling now gets you coverage starting exactly on your 65th birthday with no gap.

First task: figure out whether you're automatically enrolled or whether you need to apply. If you're already receiving Social Security retirement or disability benefits (SSDI), you're automatically enrolled in Medicare Part A and Part B when you turn 65. You'll receive your red, white, and blue Medicare card in the mail about 3 months before your birthday. Don't lose it.

If you're NOT receiving Social Security benefits — because you're still working, because you delayed Social Security to maximize your benefit, or for any other reason — you are NOT automatically enrolled. You have to apply.

Apply online at ssa.gov/medicare. Takes about 10 minutes. You don't need to visit a Social Security office, though you can call 1-800-772-1213 if you prefer. Have your Social Security number, birthdate, and employment history handy.

If you're still working and have employer coverage, stop here and read the section on working past 65 before you enroll in Part B. You may be able to delay without penalty — but the rules are strict and the employer size matters.

sections.heading: Month Minus 3 Through Birthday Month: Part A
sections.content:
Most people qualify for premium-free Part A if they or their spouse paid Medicare taxes while working for at least 40 quarters (10 years). Check your Social Security statement at ssa.gov/myaccount to confirm your work credits.

If you have 40+ quarters: Part A is free. Enroll without hesitation. There's essentially no reason to delay free hospital insurance.

If you have 30-39 quarters: You'll pay a premium for Part A — $278/month in 2026. Still worth having in most cases, but the cost calculation is different.

If you have fewer than 30 quarters: The full Part A premium is $505/month in 2026. At that level, you need to seriously weigh whether Part A alone makes sense versus other coverage options.

For the vast majority of people — anyone who worked in the US for 10 years — Part A is free and the decision is easy: enroll. The penalty for late Part A enrollment is 10% of whatever premium you'd pay, for twice the number of years you delayed. If your premium is $0, the penalty is also $0. But once you're past your Initial Enrollment Period without creditable coverage, you're stuck paying penalties when you do eventually enroll.

sections.heading: Part B: The Premium, The Decision, The Deadline
sections.content:
Part B is where it gets real.

Part B covers doctor visits, outpatient care, preventive services, durable medical equipment, and most of the things that aren't a hospital stay. The standard premium is $202.90/month in 2026. If your income is above certain thresholds (roughly $106,000 for single filers), you pay more through IRMAA surcharges.

If you're not working or your employer has fewer than 20 employees: enroll in Part B during your Initial Enrollment Period. Full stop. Don't delay. Delaying without a qualifying excuse triggers the late enrollment penalty — 10% permanently added to your premium for every full 12-month period you went without coverage. Delay 3 years, pay 30% more forever.

If your employer has 20 or more employees and you're actively working: you may be able to delay Part B without penalty. The employer plan is considered the primary payer, and Medicare allows you to defer Part B as long as you remain covered by active employer coverage. When you eventually leave that job, you get an 8-month Special Enrollment Period to sign up for Part B without penalty.

Key word: "active" employment. COBRA does not count. Retiree coverage does not count. Only coverage tied to current active employment at a qualifying employer. This distinction is how people accidentally get hit with penalties they didn't expect.

If you're receiving Social Security benefits already, you're automatically enrolled in Part B and your premium is deducted from your Social Security check. You have to actively opt out if you don't want it (and you should only do that if you have qualifying employer coverage).

sections.heading: Month of Your Birthday: Part D — The Drug Coverage Deadline
sections.content:
You need prescription drug coverage. Not eventually. From the start.

Part D is standalone prescription drug coverage or it's bundled into a Medicare Advantage plan (MAPD). If you go with Original Medicare, you need a separate Part D plan. If you go with Advantage, most Advantage plans include drug coverage.

The Part D late enrollment penalty clock starts ticking when your Initial Enrollment Period ends if you don't have creditable drug coverage. Creditable coverage means coverage that's at least as good as Medicare's standard drug benefit — most employer drug plans qualify, VA benefits qualify, TRICARE qualifies. Your insurer is required to tell you every year whether your current coverage is creditable.

The penalty: 1% of the national base beneficiary premium ($38.99 in 2026) for every month you were without creditable coverage. That's $0.39/month per uncovered month, rounded to the nearest dime. Doesn't sound like much, but it adds up over years and it compounds because the base premium can rise. And it never goes away.

Two years without Part D drug coverage = 24% penalty = $9.36/month added to your premium in 2026. For life. For every year you keep Part D going forward.

If you genuinely don't take any medications: you still might want Part D. Drug costs are unpredictable. A single prescription for a serious diagnosis can cost hundreds per month. The penalty for going without is permanent; a cheap $10/month Part D plan is your insurance against an ugly surprise.

sections.heading: The Medigap Window: Use It or Lose It
sections.content:
This is the single most important Medicare enrollment fact most people don't know going into their 65th year.

Your Medigap Open Enrollment Period — the 6-month window where you have guaranteed issue rights to buy any Medicare Supplement policy in your state, regardless of health — starts the first month you have Part B and you're 65 or older. Six months. That's it.

During those six months, no insurer can deny you Medigap coverage. They cannot ask about pre-existing conditions. They cannot charge you more because you have diabetes or a heart condition or cancer history. They must sell you any plan they offer. This is the only time this is true in most states.

After those six months close, in most states you're subject to full medical underwriting. Insurers can turn you down flat. They can charge you significantly more. They can exclude pre-existing conditions. If you've had a serious health event — cancer, cardiac surgery, MS, COPD — getting Medigap coverage outside your initial window may be difficult or impossible at any price.

What this means practically: if you want Medigap, buy it now. Plan G is the most comprehensive option available to new Medicare enrollees (Plan F was discontinued for people turning 65 after 2020). Plan G covers everything Original Medicare doesn't pay except the $257 Part B deductible.

Don't wait to see if you need it. The whole point of buying insurance is that you don't know yet whether you'll need it. Once you have a serious diagnosis, the option may be gone.

Exceptions: A handful of states (New York, Connecticut, Massachusetts, Maine) have year-round guaranteed issue for Medigap. Several other states including California, Oregon, Nevada, Idaho, and others have birthday rules that provide an annual window to switch plans. If you're in those states, you have more flexibility — but guaranteed issue at initial enrollment is still the cleanest path.

sections.heading: Month Plus 3: Last Call and Common Gaps
sections.content:
The last three months of your Initial Enrollment Period are a safety net, not a strategy. If you enroll in month +1, +2, or +3, your coverage doesn't start until the first of the following month. So a gap of 1-3 months after you turn 65 where you're paying out of pocket.

That gap can be bridged with COBRA from a prior job or through spouse's employer coverage if you have it. But it's a gap, and healthcare in your 60s is expensive.

Common scenarios that cause last-minute scrambles:

Birthday falls in December and people are distracted by the holidays. Coverage gaps starting January 1 are costly. Set reminders for September.

People assume their spouse's employer plan covers them the same as it did at 60. It might. Or the employer might have rules that change when you hit Medicare eligibility. Ask HR directly — in writing, so you have proof.

Rental of durable medical equipment (wheelchairs, CPAP machines, oxygen) gets tricky during coverage gaps. If you depend on DME, do not allow any gap in Part B coverage.

Retirement timing doesn't match Medicare timing. You retire in April, you turn 65 in July, and you try to figure out COBRA for 3 months. That works — but make sure you enroll in Part B before the end of your Initial Enrollment Period, not just before COBRA runs out. They're different clocks.

sections.heading: Still Working? Read This Before You Do Anything
sections.content:
If you're turning 65 and still working with employer coverage, your path is different. Employer size is the key variable — specifically whether your employer has 20 or more employees.

20+ employees: Your employer plan is primary. Medicare is secondary. You can delay Part B enrollment without penalty as long as you remain on active employer coverage. When you eventually leave (retirement, layoff, voluntary departure), you get an 8-month SEP to sign up for Part B without penalty. You should generally still enroll in Part A at 65 if it's free, but you can delay Part B.

Fewer than 20 employees: The rules flip. Medicare becomes primary, employer plan secondary. You must enroll in Medicare at 65 or your employer plan essentially doesn't work properly. Your employer plan coordinates as secondary, so it covers what Medicare doesn't. If you don't enroll in Medicare on time in this scenario, you risk penalty and coverage problems simultaneously.

HSA issue: If you're contributing to a Health Savings Account through your employer HDHP, enrolling in ANY part of Medicare — including Part A — makes you immediately ineligible to contribute to the HSA. Even Part A, which is usually free. This is a significant financial consideration. If you have years of high-income, maxing your HSA, the math on delaying Medicare (and thus delaying HSA ineligibility) might be meaningful. For 2026, the HSA contribution limit is $4,400 for self-only coverage, $8,750 for family. Stop contributing 6 months before you plan to enroll in Medicare, because Medicare Part A can backdate coverage up to 6 months.

Social Security linkage: If you want to delay Medicare and keep contributing to your HSA, you cannot collect Social Security retirement benefits either. Social Security automatically enrolls you in Medicare when you start receiving benefits. You can't get one without the other.

faq.question: When does my Medicare enrollment window open?
faq.answer: Your Initial Enrollment Period opens 3 months before the month you turn 65. It runs for 7 months total — 3 months before your birthday month, your birthday month, and 3 months after. Enrolling in the first 3 months gets you coverage starting on your actual birthday. Enrolling during or after your birthday month delays your start date.

faq.question: Do I have to sign up for Medicare at 65 if I have insurance through work?
faq.answer: It depends on your employer's size. If you work for a company with 20 or more employees, you can delay Medicare without penalty while covered by active employer insurance. If your company has fewer than 20 employees, Medicare becomes primary at 65 and you need to enroll to avoid penalties and coverage gaps. Retiring and taking COBRA does NOT qualify you to delay — only active employment does.

faq.question: What happens if I miss my Initial Enrollment Period?
faq.answer: For Part B, you pay a permanent 10% penalty on your premium for every full 12-month period you went without coverage. The 2026 standard Part B premium is $202.90 — a 2-year delay means you pay $40.58 more per month for life. For Part D, the penalty is 1% of $38.99 per uncovered month, also permanent. You also can't enroll in either until the General Enrollment Period (January 1 – March 31), with coverage not starting until July 1.

faq.question: When is the best time to buy Medigap?
faq.answer: The moment you enroll in Part B at 65, or as close to that as possible. Your 6-month guaranteed issue window is the only time in most states when insurers must sell you any Medigap policy without medical underwriting. After that window closes, they can turn you down based on health history. Don't wait to see if you need it.

faq.question: Do I need Part D if I don't take any prescriptions?
faq.answer: You're not legally required to have it, but the late enrollment penalty clock starts as soon as your Initial Enrollment Period ends if you go without creditable drug coverage. The penalty is permanent. A basic Part D plan might cost $10-15/month. If you ever do need medications — and healthcare needs tend to increase with age — the protection is worth more than the premium.

faq.question: Can I contribute to my HSA after turning 65?
faq.answer: No, not once you enroll in any part of Medicare. Enrolling in Part A (even free Part A) ends your eligibility to contribute to an HSA. If keeping HSA contributions is a priority, you need to delay ALL Medicare enrollment and also delay Social Security retirement benefits (which automatically triggers Medicare). Stop HSA contributions 6 months before you plan to enroll, because Part A can retroactively cover up to 6 months.

faq.question: What's the automatic enrollment rule?
faq.answer: If you're receiving Social Security retirement or disability benefits before 65, you're automatically enrolled in Parts A and B when you turn 65. You'll get your Medicare card in the mail 3 months before your birthday. If you're NOT receiving Social Security benefits yet (because you delayed or are still working), you must apply for Medicare yourself at ssa.gov or by calling 1-800-MEDICARE.

h1: Medicare Late Enrollment Penalties: Part A, B, and D
metaDescription: Missing your Medicare enrollment window costs real money — permanently. Here's the exact math on Part A, Part B, and Part D penalties, how they compound over time, what triggers them, and how to appeal if you think you got one in error.
summary: When the Medicare enrollment guides say "permanent penalty," they mean for the rest of your life on Medicare. Not for a year. Not until you hit a cap. For every month you pay your Medicare premium, that penalty percentage is baked in.

sections.heading: These Penalties Are Permanent. That's Not a Figure of Speech.
sections.content:
When the Medicare enrollment guides say "permanent penalty," they mean for the rest of your life on Medicare. Not for a year. Not until you hit a cap. For every month you pay your Medicare premium, that penalty percentage is baked in.

The IRS has a statute of limitations. Medicare penalties don't.

This is not a scare tactic — it's the actual mechanics of the system. Congress designed these penalties specifically to prevent people from waiting until they get sick to enroll in Medicare, which would destroy the risk pool. So they made the deterrent severe and permanent.

Understanding how each penalty works, how to calculate it, and what exceptions exist is genuinely important financial planning. A two-year Part B delay without qualifying coverage costs someone paying the standard 2026 premium an extra $40.58 per month. Over a 20-year retirement, that's $9,739.20. And that assumes the Part B premium never increases, which it always does — the penalty percentage is fixed but it applies to whatever the current premium is.

sections.heading: Part A Penalty: Who It Affects and How It Works
sections.content:
Most people don't pay a premium for Part A. If you or your spouse worked and paid Medicare taxes for 40 quarters (10 years), Part A is free. Roughly 99% of Medicare enrollees qualify for premium-free Part A. If your premium is $0, your penalty is also $0, no matter when you enroll.

For the minority who must pay a Part A premium — immigrants who didn't accumulate enough work credits, some federal workers under older civil service retirement systems, certain state and local government workers — the late enrollment penalty matters.

2026 Part A premiums:
- 30-39 quarters of covered employment: $278/month
- Fewer than 30 quarters: $505/month

The penalty: if you should have enrolled in Part A (because you didn't have premium-free Part A from work credits and you weren't covered by qualifying employer coverage) and you didn't, your premium goes up 10%. And you pay that elevated premium for twice the number of years you delayed enrollment.

Example: You have 32 quarters of work credits. Your premium would be $278/month. You delay Part A for 3 years without qualifying coverage. Your penalty is 10% — $27.80/month more. You pay that for 6 years (twice the 3-year delay), after which the penalty drops. This is the one Medicare penalty that isn't truly permanent — it does eventually end after double the delay period. But during those penalty years, you're paying more.

Practical note: For people with 40+ work quarters, Part A enrollment is basically a non-decision — it's free, there's no penalty for delaying when it's free, but there's also no reason to delay. Just enroll.

sections.heading: Part B Penalty: The Big One That Gets People
sections.content:
This is the one that destroys retirement budgets. The Part B late enrollment penalty is the most commonly triggered Medicare penalty, it's genuinely permanent, and it applies to one of the largest ongoing Medicare expenses you'll have.

The standard Part B premium in 2026 is $202.90/month. The penalty is 10% added for every full 12-month period you were without Part B coverage and didn't have a qualifying reason to delay.

Full 12-month periods matter here. If you delayed 25 months, that's 2 full periods (2 x 10% = 20%). The remaining month doesn't count. Partial years don't accumulate. You need to complete full years for each 10% increment.

Let's run the real numbers for 2026:

1 full year delayed: 10% penalty. $202.90 x 1.10 = $223.19/month. Extra cost vs no penalty: $20.29/month, $243.48/year.

2 full years delayed: 20% penalty. $202.90 x 1.20 = $243.48/month. Extra cost: $40.58/month, $486.96/year.

3 full years delayed: 30% penalty. $202.90 x 1.30 = $263.77/month. Extra cost: $60.87/month, $730.44/year.

5 full years delayed: 50% penalty. $202.90 x 1.50 = $304.35/month. Extra cost: $101.45/month, $1,217.40/year.

7 full years delayed: 70% penalty. $202.90 x 1.70 = $344.93/month. Extra cost: $141.43/month, $1,697.16/year.

And remember: the Part B premium increases every year. A 30% penalty on a $202.90 premium in 2026 is $60.87/month. But when the Part B premium is $250/month — which is likely within a decade at historical rate increases — that same 30% penalty is $75/month. The penalty percentage is locked in, but the dollar amount climbs with the premium.

Over a 20-year retirement, a 30% penalty at today's premium (ignoring future increases) is $14,608.80 in excess premiums. The math is brutal.

sections.heading: Part B: What Qualifies as a Valid Delay
sections.content:
You avoid the Part B penalty if — and only if — you had what Medicare calls "creditable coverage" through current employment at the time you were eligible for Part B.

Valid reason to delay Part B without penalty: You are actively employed at a company with 20 or more employees, and you are covered by that employer's group health plan. When you leave that employment, you have 8 months to enroll in Part B with no penalty.

Not valid: COBRA. I'll say that again louder because this gets people every year — COBRA IS NOT CREDITABLE COVERAGE FOR PART B DELAY PURPOSES. COBRA is continuation coverage, not employer-sponsored active coverage. If you retired, took COBRA, and planned to enroll in Part B when COBRA ended, you already missed your window. Your 8-month SEP started when you left active employment — not when COBRA ended.

Not valid: Retiree health coverage from a former employer. This is coverage tied to retirement, not active employment. It does not protect you from the Part B penalty.

Not valid: Individual market plans (marketplace/ACA). These are not employer-based active coverage.

Not valid: Spouse's coverage at a small employer (fewer than 20 employees). This doesn't qualify.

Valid but complicated: Spouse's active employer coverage at a company with 20+ employees can qualify you for delay if you are covered under their plan. When your spouse retires or loses that coverage, you have an 8-month SEP to enroll.

This is genuinely confusing and the stakes are permanently high. If you have any uncertainty about whether your coverage qualifies, call your local SHIP or call Medicare at 1-800-MEDICARE before your Initial Enrollment Period closes. Get confirmation in writing if you can.

sections.heading: Part D Penalty: How the Math Actually Works
sections.content:
The Part D late enrollment penalty is calculated differently from Part B and it catches people off guard because the monthly dollar amount seems small at first.

The formula: 1% times the national base beneficiary premium times the number of full, uncovered months since your Part D Initial Enrollment Period ended.

The 2026 national base beneficiary premium is $38.99/month.

So: 1% of $38.99 = $0.3899, rounded to the nearest $0.10 = $0.39/month per uncovered month.

Examples:

12 uncovered months: 12 x $0.39 = $4.68/month penalty. That's 12% of $38.99.

24 uncovered months: 24 x $0.39 = $9.36/month penalty. Rounded, $9.40.

36 uncovered months: 36 x $0.39 = $14.04/month. Rounded, $14.00.

60 uncovered months (5 years): 60 x $0.39 = $23.40/month.

The penalty is added to whatever your actual Part D plan premium is. So if your plan costs $30/month and you have a $14 penalty, you pay $44/month.

Here's the catch that makes the long-term math worse: the base beneficiary premium is recalculated every year and can increase. Your penalty percentage is locked (24%, 36%, whatever it is based on your uncovered months), but the dollar amount recalculates against the new base each year. When the base premium goes up, your penalty goes up with it — even though you've had drug coverage for years.

Example: You delayed 2 years (24 months) then enrolled. Your penalty is 24%. In 2026, that's 24% of $38.99 = $9.36/month. In a future year when the base premium is $50/month, your 24% penalty becomes $12/month. The underlying penalty percentage never changes but the cost does.

The penalty can only be triggered if you go 63 or more consecutive days without creditable prescription drug coverage. Within 63 days, you're fine. At 63 days without coverage, the clock has started.

sections.heading: The COBRA Trap — and Why It Kills People's Part D Too
sections.content:
COBRA comes up enough in Medicare penalty conversations that it deserves its own section.

When you leave a job with health and drug coverage and take COBRA, you're maintaining the prior employer's plan on your own dime. For Part D purposes, COBRA drug coverage IS creditable — your former employer's plan was likely creditable and COBRA continues that plan. So taking COBRA doesn't automatically trigger a Part D penalty.

But COBRA for Part B is a completely different story. As discussed above, COBRA is not active employer coverage. It doesn't protect you from the Part B late enrollment penalty. If your Initial Enrollment Period (or 8-month SEP from leaving employment) passes while you're on COBRA, you're past your window.

People get trapped when: they leave work at 64 and a half, take COBRA, turn 65 while on COBRA, assume COBRA counts as their "coverage" for Medicare delay purposes, and don't enroll in Part B until COBRA ends. By then, they're past their window and they owe a penalty.

The actual safe strategy when leaving work near 65: enroll in Medicare (at least Part A if it's free, and seriously consider Part B) when you first become eligible, use COBRA as a secondary payer, and let Medicare primary cover you. Or skip COBRA entirely and buy a standalone Medigap plan. COBRA is expensive — often $500-$1,500+/month in premiums — and Medicare + Medigap is usually cheaper at 65 anyway.

Another COBRA trap: people who think COBRA extends their enrollment window. It doesn't. The 8-month Part B SEP starts when you leave active employment, period. Not when COBRA ends.

sections.heading: How Penalties Compound and Why the Real Cost Is Higher Than You Think
sections.content:
Individual penalty numbers don't tell the full story. The compounding effect over a 20+ year retirement is where the real damage shows up.

Consider someone who delayed Part B for 3 years and Part D for 3 years (both legitimately forgivable mistakes — maybe they were on a small-employer plan and didn't realize COBRA didn't count).

Part B penalty: 30% permanently. At 2026 premiums: $60.87 extra/month.

Part D penalty: 36 uncovered months x $0.39 = $14.04/month penalty (in 2026 base premium terms).

Combined monthly penalty: roughly $75/month.

Over 15 years (not unusual for someone enrolling at 68): 180 months x $75 = $13,500. And that's holding premiums flat, which won't happen. As Part B premiums increase over the years, that 30% penalty multiplies each increase. As the Part D base premium increases, that 36% penalty multiplies that.

Realistic 20-year projection assuming modest premium growth: the total excess cost from a 3-year delay (with no qualifying reason) could easily exceed $25,000-$30,000.

People sometimes think the penalties are bureaucratic annoyances. They're not. They're significant long-term financial consequences.

sections.heading: Appealing a Late Enrollment Penalty
sections.content:
You can appeal. It's not easy, but it's worth trying if you think the penalty was applied in error.

The most common valid basis for appeal: you did have creditable coverage, but CMS applied the penalty anyway because they didn't have documentation of that coverage.

How to appeal: Request a review by contacting Medicare at 1-800-MEDICARE. You can also contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for free help navigating the process. For Part D penalties, you can request reconsideration through your Part D plan.

Documentation you'll need: Proof of creditable coverage for the period in question. This means letters from your employer's HR department confirming you were on the group health plan and that the drug coverage was creditable. Insurers are required to send creditable coverage notices — dig those out if you have them. Old pay stubs or enrollment confirmation letters can help establish employment dates.

The Equitable Relief option: If you were given incorrect information by Medicare, an insurance company, or a Social Security office about when and whether to enroll, you can request equitable relief. This requires demonstrating that you relied on official incorrect advice and that the advice caused your delayed enrollment. It's harder to win but it does happen.

For Part B specifically: if you have documentation that you were covered by a qualifying employer plan and simply weren't informed about the 8-month SEP timeline, that's worth pursuing. The Medicare Rights Center (medicarerights.org) has published guidance on this exact issue and can help.

Timeline: Don't wait. If you receive a penalty notice, request a review within 60 days. The process moves slowly after that but the 60-day window to formally request review matters.

sections.heading: How to Avoid Penalties Completely
sections.content:
The avoidance strategy is simple. The execution requires paying attention.

For Part A: If you have 40+ work quarters, just enroll when you turn 65. It's free, there's no downside, and it starts the coverage you'll eventually need. If you have fewer than 40 quarters but more than 30, enroll during your IEP anyway — the premium is lower and the late penalty on an already-reduced premium is relatively modest.

For Part B: Enroll during your Initial Enrollment Period unless you have active employer coverage at a 20+ employee company. If you are delaying, make sure your coverage truly qualifies. Talk to HR. Get it in writing. Know your 8-month SEP window.

For Part D: Enroll in drug coverage as soon as you enroll in Medicare. Even if you don't take medications, a basic low-premium Part D plan is cheap insurance against the permanent penalty. The 2026 base premium is $38.99 — actual plan premiums start around $10-15/month. Protect yourself.

If you have drug coverage through an employer, VA, TRICARE, or other source: make sure you get written confirmation that coverage is creditable every year. Insurers are required to send this. Keep those letters.

If you're between jobs: a gap of up to 63 days won't trigger a Part D penalty. More than 63 consecutive days without creditable coverage starts the penalty clock. Budget your transitions accordingly.

The single most reliable thing you can do: start researching Medicare 6 months before you turn 65 and talk to your local SHIP counselor. Free, unbiased, and they've seen every scenario.

faq.question: What is the Part B late enrollment penalty in 2026?
faq.answer: The Part B penalty is 10% of the standard premium for every full 12-month period you were without coverage. The 2026 standard premium is $202.90/month. One year delayed: $20.29/month extra. Two years: $40.58/month extra. Three years: $60.87/month extra. These amounts are permanent — they stay with you as long as you have Part B, and they scale up as the base premium increases over time.

faq.question: Does COBRA count as creditable coverage to avoid the Part B penalty?
faq.answer: No. COBRA is not active employer coverage. For Part B delay purposes, only active employment at a 20+ employee company provides penalty protection. Your 8-month Special Enrollment Period to enroll in Part B without penalty starts when you leave active employment — not when COBRA ends. Many people make this mistake and end up with permanent Part B penalties.

faq.question: What is the Part D late enrollment penalty for 2026?
faq.answer: The penalty is 1% of the national base beneficiary premium ($38.99 in 2026) for every full month you were without creditable drug coverage. That's roughly $0.39/month per uncovered month. After 24 uncovered months, your penalty is $9.36/month — added to your plan premium forever, and it can increase as the base premium increases year over year.

faq.question: Is the Part A late enrollment penalty permanent?
faq.answer: Partially. The Part A penalty is 10% of whatever premium you'd owe, and you pay it for twice the number of years you delayed. Unlike the Part B and Part D penalties, it does eventually end after that doubled period. However, most Americans don't pay a Part A premium at all (40+ work quarters = free Part A), so for most people the Part A penalty is irrelevant.

faq.question: Can I appeal a Medicare late enrollment penalty?
faq.answer: Yes. Contact Medicare at 1-800-MEDICARE or your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for help. You'll need documentation of creditable coverage for the disputed period — letters from HR, creditable coverage notices, enrollment confirmations. If you were given incorrect information by an official Medicare source, you can request equitable relief. File within 60 days of receiving the penalty notice.

faq.question: What counts as creditable drug coverage for Part D purposes?
faq.answer: Coverage that's at least as good as Medicare's standard drug benefit. Most employer drug plans qualify. VA drug coverage qualifies. TRICARE qualifies. COBRA drug coverage qualifies (it continues the prior employer plan). Individual ACA marketplace plans may or may not qualify — your insurer must send you an annual creditable coverage notice. Keep those letters — you'll need them if your coverage is ever questioned.

faq.question: How long does the Part D penalty clock run before triggering?
faq.answer: You need to go 63 or more consecutive days without creditable drug coverage for the penalty to start. Within 63 days, no penalty accumulates. The 63-day window is important for people transitioning between coverage — it gives you a short gap to work with when switching jobs or plans.

h1: Medicare Special Enrollment Periods: All Qualifying Events
metaDescription: Life doesn't stop at Medicare's enrollment windows. Special Enrollment Periods let you enroll or change plans outside the standard timelines when qualifying life events happen. Here's every trigger, every timeline, and what you can and can't do with each.
summary: Medicare has set enrollment windows for a reason — the Annual Enrollment Period, the Initial Enrollment Period, the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period. But life doesn't coordinate with calendar quarters. Jobs end unexpectedly. People move. Plans get discontinued. Spouses die.

sections.heading: What a Special Enrollment Period Actually Is
sections.content:
Medicare has set enrollment windows for a reason — the Annual Enrollment Period, the Initial Enrollment Period, the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period. But life doesn't coordinate with calendar quarters. Jobs end unexpectedly. People move. Plans get discontinued. Spouses die.

Special Enrollment Periods are the system's acknowledgment that rigid windows don't work for real life. An SEP is a time-limited opportunity to enroll in or change Medicare coverage outside of standard enrollment periods, triggered by a qualifying life event. Miss the SEP window that your event triggered, and you're back to waiting — possibly with a penalty.

There are two major categories of SEPs:

Part B SEPs: These relate to enrolling in Original Medicare Part B outside your Initial Enrollment Period, usually triggered by leaving employer or union coverage.

Medicare Advantage and Part D SEPs: These relate to changing, dropping, or enrolling in Medicare Advantage plans or standalone Part D drug plans outside the Annual Enrollment Period.

Some events trigger both. Some only trigger one. The window lengths differ. And the rules are specific enough that the difference between 2 months and 8 months can mean the difference between enrolling on time or owing a penalty.

This article covers every qualifying SEP trigger I know of, organized by event type.

sections.heading: SEP #1: Losing Employer or Union Coverage
sections.content:
This is the most common SEP and the one with the most financial consequences if you miss it.

The trigger: You lose coverage from an employer or union group health plan based on your own or a spouse's current employment. This includes: you retire, you get laid off, your employer eliminates the health plan, your employer drops to fewer than 20 employees and changes the coverage structure, your spouse retires or loses their employment-based coverage that was covering you.

The window: 8 months from the date you lose the employer/union coverage OR from the date employment ends, whichever comes first. This is an 8-month window — longer than most other SEPs.

What you can do: Enroll in Part B without penalty. You can also enroll in a Medicare Advantage plan or standalone Part D plan.

Critical clarification: This is an 8-month SEP to enroll in Part B. But for Medicare Advantage or Part D, the window is only 2 months from the date you lose other coverage. Two different windows triggered by the same event.

The COBRA mistake again: The 8-month Part B SEP starts when employment ends, not when COBRA ends. If you leave work in March, start COBRA in March, and COBRA runs through September — your Part B SEP ends in November (8 months from March), not months after COBRA ends. Many people realize this too late.

For Part B enrollment, you generally don't want to use all 8 months. Enroll as quickly as possible after losing employer coverage to minimize any gap. You have 8 months to do it without penalty — that doesn't mean you should wait 8 months.

sections.heading: SEP #2: Moving Out of Your Plan's Service Area
sections.content:
Medicare Advantage plans and Part D plans are geographically bounded. When you move, the plan you're on may no longer serve your new area.

The trigger: You permanently move to a new address that's outside your current plan's service area. Note: "service area" is specific to your plan's network, not just state lines. Moving across town can sometimes trigger this if the plan has a narrow service area.

The window: 2 months before AND 2 months after the month you moved. So you have up to a 5-month window centered on your move date.

What you can do: Join a new Medicare Advantage plan or Part D plan that serves your new area. If no Advantage plan is available in your new area, you can switch to Original Medicare.

What you cannot do: This SEP doesn't let you change Medigap plans with guaranteed issue in most states. Medigap has separate guaranteed issue triggers.

Documentation: Plans may ask for proof of your move — a new driver's license, utility bill, or lease agreement. Have these ready.

Note on temporary moves: This SEP doesn't apply to temporary moves. Snowbirds who spend winters in Florida and summers in Michigan typically can't trigger this SEP twice a year. However, some plans do have nationwide or multi-state networks. If you live in multiple locations seasonally, look for plans with broader service areas.

sections.heading: SEP #3: Plan Termination or Contract Non-Renewal
sections.content:
Occasionally, Medicare Advantage plans or Part D plans exit specific markets or lose their Medicare contract entirely.

The trigger: Your current Medicare Advantage plan or Part D plan leaves the Medicare program, stops serving your area, or loses its Medicare contract — involuntarily (CMS terminates the contract) or voluntarily (plan decides to exit).

The window: 2 months after you receive notice of the termination or the date coverage ends, whichever is later.

What you can do: Join any available Medicare Advantage or Part D plan in your area. If no Advantage plan is available, you can return to Original Medicare.

Guaranteed issue for Medigap: This is one of the situations that triggers guaranteed issue rights for Medigap in most states. If your Medicare Advantage plan's contract is terminated (not if you voluntarily drop it), you have a 63-day guaranteed issue window to buy a Medigap policy. This is one of the only guaranteed issue triggers outside of your initial enrollment period in states without additional protections.

Plan downgrades: If your plan significantly reduces its service area (like dropping a county you live in) or significantly reduces its provider network, this also triggers an SEP. The plan is required to notify you in writing.

sections.heading: SEP #4: Gaining or Losing Medicaid, Extra Help, or Medicare Savings Programs
sections.content:
Dual-eligible beneficiaries (people who qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid) and those who receive Low Income Subsidy (Extra Help) for Part D have ongoing SEP rights because their coverage situation can change throughout the year.

The trigger: You gain eligibility for, or lose eligibility for, Medicaid, Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy for Part D), or a Medicare Savings Program (MSP). Also triggered by changes in your level of Extra Help — moving from full extra help to partial, or losing it entirely.

The window: Monthly for Medicaid/dual-eligible beneficiaries — you can make changes once per month, every month, as long as you're dually eligible.

For gaining or losing Extra Help: 1 month before and up to 3 months after the change in your Extra Help status.

What you can do: Switch Medicare Advantage or Part D plans. Dual-eligible beneficiaries generally have the most flexibility here — they can change plans monthly rather than being locked in until AEP.

This SEP is particularly important for people experiencing income changes — a new job, a change in household size, a change in assets. If you lose Extra Help because your income increased slightly, you may need to switch to a plan you can afford without the subsidy. The SEP gives you time to do that.

sections.heading: SEP #5: Moving Into, Residing In, or Leaving a Skilled Nursing Facility or Other Institutional Setting
sections.content:
People who enter or leave institutional settings have ongoing Medicare plan-change rights because their coverage needs and available providers often change significantly.

The trigger: You move into a skilled nursing facility, long-term care hospital, or other qualifying institutional setting, OR you leave one.

The window: Once per month while residing in the institution, and up to 2 months after leaving.

What you can do: Switch Medicare Advantage plans or Part D plans. Many people enter a facility covered by an Advantage plan, discover their specific facility or care team isn't in-network, and need to switch to Original Medicare to access the care they need.

This is one of the SEPs where having someone to help you exercise your rights really matters. People in institutions may not be in a position to manage enrollment paperwork themselves. Family members and social workers play a critical role here. Know that this SEP exists — it can mean the difference between coverage and large out-of-pocket costs.

sections.heading: SEP #6: The 5-Star Special Enrollment Period
sections.content:
This is a lesser-known SEP that allows you to switch into a 5-star rated plan — once per year.

The trigger: A Medicare Advantage plan or Part D plan in your area receives a 5-star rating from CMS. You can switch into it outside of AEP.

The window: December 8 through November 30. Runs for almost the entire year following the AEP where ratings are assigned.

What you can do: Switch from your current plan (any star rating) to a 5-star plan. Coverage starts the first of the following month after enrollment.

Limitations: You can only use this SEP once per calendar year. The plan must be in your service area. There are very few 5-star plans — in 2026, 5-star plans are available in 19 states and Puerto Rico, and they represent only about 3% of all Medicare Advantage plans. Don't count on this option being available in your area.

If you do have a 5-star plan available: this SEP is a genuine option if you're stuck in a poor-performing plan and AEP is months away. But check network and drug formulary carefully before switching — a 5-star plan that doesn't cover your doctors or drugs is still the wrong plan, regardless of its rating.

sections.heading: SEP #7: Losing Medicaid Eligibility — the 'Unwinding' SEP
sections.content:
During and after the COVID-19 public health emergency, millions of people maintained Medicaid coverage through continuous enrollment protections. When those protections ended and states began redetermining eligibility (Medicaid "unwinding"), many people lost Medicaid for the first time and became newly eligible for Medicare as their primary coverage.

The trigger: You lose Medicaid coverage and become eligible for Medicare.

The window: 3 months following the loss of Medicaid coverage.

What you can do: Enroll in Medicare (Parts A, B, Advantage, or Part D) without the standard enrollment window restrictions and without penalty.

This SEP has been used heavily in 2024-2026 as Medicaid unwinding continues. If you or someone you know lost Medicaid coverage and is over 65 or has a qualifying disability, this SEP may apply. Don't wait — 3 months is a short window.

sections.heading: SEP #8: Released from Incarceration
sections.content:
People who have been incarcerated and are released after reaching Medicare eligibility age or after a period during which they would have been eligible have an SEP to enroll.

The trigger: Release from incarceration.

The window: 12 months from the date of release.

What you can do: Enroll in Medicare Parts A and B (with no penalty for the period during incarceration), and enroll in a Medicare Advantage or Part D plan.

How it works with Part B penalties: While incarcerated, people are not eligible to enroll in Medicare, so the standard late enrollment penalty clock does not run during incarceration. This means the penalty period is based on time you could have enrolled but didn't — which excludes incarceration time.

sections.heading: SEP #9: PACE and Other Special Situations
sections.content:
Several other situations can trigger SEPs. These are less common but worth knowing about.

Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE): People who enroll in or leave a PACE program have an SEP to change their Medicare coverage.

Plan significantly changes benefits mid-year: If your plan makes a mid-year change that significantly affects you — changes to formulary outside the standard annual update, benefit reductions — you may have an SEP. These situations are less clearly defined, but CMS does have provisions for material plan changes.

Recently returning from foreign travel or living abroad: People who are Medicare-eligible but were living outside the US have an SEP upon returning to the US.

Inadequate notice from plan: If you weren't properly notified of a change to your plan, CMS can grant an SEP. This requires filing a complaint and having it upheld, but it is possible.

Equity and Exceptional Circumstances SEP: CMS has discretionary authority to grant SEPs for situations not covered by the standard list — called Exceptional Circumstances SEPs. These are rare and require CMS approval, but they exist for genuine edge cases where no standard SEP applies and denying one would cause serious harm. Examples: natural disasters that prevented enrollment, documented misinformation from an authorized Medicare source, or complex coverage situations that no standard rule addresses.

sections.heading: How to Actually Use a Special Enrollment Period
sections.content:
Knowing an SEP exists is only half the battle. You have to actually execute the enrollment within the window.

For Part B SEPs: Contact the Social Security Administration. You can do this online at ssa.gov, by phone at 1-800-772-1213, or in person at your local Social Security office. You'll need documentation of the qualifying event — a letter from your employer confirming coverage end date, for example.

For Medicare Advantage and Part D SEPs: Contact the plan directly, use the Medicare Plan Finder at plan.medicare.gov, or call 1-800-MEDICARE. You'll typically need to self-attest to the qualifying event and may need to provide supporting documentation.

Timing documentation: Keep records of qualifying events. The date your employer coverage ended. The date your plan termination notice was received. The date you moved. These dates are often disputed and documentation makes the difference.

If you're denied: Request a formal review. You have the right to appeal SEP denials. Your SHIP counselor can help you navigate this. The Medicare Rights Center at medicarerights.org also provides free assistance with enrollment appeals.

Don't test the edges of windows: If your SEP window is 2 months and you're at 6 weeks, don't wait until day 60. Systems have processing times, documentation can get lost, and mistakes get made. Submit as early as you can after the qualifying event.

faq.question: How long is a Medicare Special Enrollment Period?
faq.answer: It depends on the trigger. The most common — losing employer coverage — gives you 8 months to enroll in Part B without penalty, but only 2 months for Medicare Advantage or Part D. Moving out of your plan's service area gives you a 5-month window. Most other SEPs give you 2 months. Dual-eligible and Medicaid-related SEPs may be monthly. Always check the specific window for your triggering event.

faq.question: Does COBRA trigger a Special Enrollment Period for Part B?
faq.answer: No. COBRA itself is not a qualifying event for an SEP. The qualifying event is losing active employer coverage, which starts your 8-month Part B SEP. The SEP starts when employment ends, not when COBRA ends. This is one of the most common Medicare enrollment mistakes — people assume they have more time because their COBRA hasn't expired yet.

faq.question: Can I get a Medigap plan through a Special Enrollment Period?
faq.answer: In most states, Medigap has limited guaranteed issue triggers outside of your initial 6-month enrollment window. One of the exceptions: if your Medicare Advantage plan's contract is terminated (not voluntarily dropped), you have a 63-day guaranteed issue window. Some state-specific rules also apply — states with birthday rules or year-round guaranteed issue have more options. Check your specific state's rules.

faq.question: What if there's no standard SEP for my situation?
faq.answer: CMS has an Exceptional Circumstances SEP that applies to situations not covered by standard SEP categories. These are approved on a case-by-case basis and typically require demonstrating that you were given incorrect official information, experienced a natural disaster, or had another extraordinary circumstance that prevented timely enrollment. Contact 1-800-MEDICARE or your local SHIP to explore this option.

faq.question: I moved to a new state. Do I automatically get an SEP?
faq.answer: If you moved out of your Medicare Advantage or Part D plan's service area, yes — you have a 2-month window before and 2 months after your move to change plans. If you have Original Medicare with Medigap, moving states doesn't typically give you a Medigap SEP (Medigap policies generally work nationwide), but check your current plan's terms. And if you're on a Medicaid-based dual-eligible plan, moving states may trigger a separate SEP.

faq.question: What's the 5-star SEP and should I use it?
faq.answer: The 5-star SEP lets you switch into a plan with a 5-star CMS rating at any time between December 8 and November 30, once per year. It's real but limited — only about 3% of Medicare Advantage plans have 5-star ratings, and they're only available in some areas. If a 5-star plan happens to serve your area and cover your doctors and drugs, it's a legitimate option to switch outside AEP. Just verify network and formulary before jumping.

h1: Medicare if You're Still Working at 65
metaDescription: Working past 65 with employer coverage? The rules are counterintuitive, the stakes are high, and employer size changes everything. Here's exactly what to do with Medicare, Part B, your HSA, and COBRA based on your specific situation.
summary: People getting Medicare advice at 65 while still working often get frustrated because the guidance is contradictory. One source says delay Part B, another says enroll now. Both can be right depending on one variable: how many employees your company has.

sections.heading: The First Question Is About Employer Size
sections.content:
People getting Medicare advice at 65 while still working often get frustrated because the guidance is contradictory. One source says delay Part B, another says enroll now. Both can be right depending on one variable: how many employees your company has.

Twenty. That's the number.

If your employer has 20 or more employees: your group health plan is the primary payer. Medicare is secondary. You can delay Part B — and in many cases, you should — without penalty. Medicare will be a backup, not the main event.

If your employer has fewer than 20 employees: the rules flip completely. Medicare becomes primary. Your employer plan is secondary. You must enroll in Medicare on time or your employer coverage effectively becomes a shell — it'll pay after Medicare, but Medicare isn't there. Your employer plan may refuse to pay what Medicare would have covered. You end up with unexpected bills and a permanent penalty.

Everything else in this article flows from that employer size question. Get that answer first, then figure out the rest.

sections.heading: 20+ Employees: The Part B Delay Strategy
sections.content:
If your employer has 20 or more employees and you're actively enrolled in the group health plan, you have the right to delay Medicare Part B without any penalty. This is an intentional design in the law — Congress didn't want to force people off working spouses' plans or active employer coverage before they were ready.

Here's what that looks like in practice:

Part A: Usually enroll at 65 even if you're delaying Part B. Part A is free if you have 40+ work quarters, and it serves as secondary coverage alongside your employer plan. There's one major exception to this, which is the HSA situation — more on that below.

Part B: You can delay enrollment as long as you remain actively employed and covered by your employer's group plan. No penalty accumulates during this period. When you eventually leave that employment, you have an 8-month Special Enrollment Period to sign up for Part B without penalty.

Part D: If your employer's drug coverage is creditable (it almost certainly is — your insurer is required to send you an annual notice confirming this), you can delay Part D enrollment without penalty. Keep those creditable coverage notices.

The financial case for delaying Part B: The standard 2026 premium is $202.90/month. If you're paying $0 for employer coverage (or very little) because your employer subsidizes it heavily, that's $202.90/month you keep in your pocket for each month you delay Part B. Over a 3-year delay, that's $7,304.40 saved — assuming your employer coverage remains in place and the employer subsidy holds.

The catch: this only works while you're actively employed. The moment employment ends for any reason — retirement, layoff, voluntary resignation, disability — your 8-month SEP window starts. Mark that date carefully.

sections.heading: Under 20 Employees: You Need Medicare Now
sections.content:
Small employers — those with fewer than 20 employees — are not required to make their group health plan primary over Medicare. The law says for companies under 20, Medicare is primary. This means:

You must enroll in both Part A and Part B when you turn 65, during your Initial Enrollment Period. Not doing so creates two problems simultaneously: your employer plan won't pay what Medicare would have covered (leaving you exposed to significant costs), and you'll owe a late enrollment penalty when you do eventually enroll in Part B.

Some small employer plans are coordinated to work alongside Medicare as a secondary payer. In those cases, having both is actually better coverage than either alone — Medicare pays first, employer plan pays most of the remainder, and your out-of-pocket approaches zero. But Medicare has to be there for this to work.

If you're not sure about your employer's size or how your plan coordinates with Medicare, ask HR directly. Ask specifically: "Does our plan require Medicare to be primary when an employee turns 65?" Get the answer in writing. If they say yes, enroll in Medicare during your Initial Enrollment Period.

Note: employer size is based on total employees in the prior year, not current headcount. A company that had 25 employees last year but now has 18 might still be considered a "large employer" for Medicare coordination purposes. When in doubt, ask your benefits administrator to clarify how your plan treats Medicare.

sections.heading: The HSA Problem Nobody Mentions Until It's Too Late
sections.content:
If your employer coverage runs through a High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) and you've been contributing to a Health Savings Account — this section might be the most important thing you read before your 65th birthday.

Here's the rule: you cannot contribute to an HSA in any month that you are enrolled in Medicare. Any part of Medicare. Including Part A. Even free Part A. The moment you enroll in Medicare Part A, your monthly HSA contribution limit drops to $0.

The 2026 HSA contribution limits: $4,400 for self-only HDHP coverage, $8,750 for family coverage. For people 55 and older, there's an additional $1,000 catch-up contribution allowed. That's meaningful money to give up.

This creates a real decision point for high earners who are still employed at 65 and have been maximizing HSA contributions. The math of continuing to contribute to an HSA — and deferring Medicare — needs to be weighed against:

1. The cost of your employer coverage vs. Medicare + Medigap cost once you retire
2. Your projected retirement date and how long you'd maintain HSA contribution eligibility
3. The potential penalty exposure if you miscalculate

The retroactive coverage trap: Medicare Part A can retroactively cover up to 6 months before you enroll — meaning if you sign up at 66, your Part A coverage might start at 65 and a half. If you were contributing to your HSA during those 6 months, those contributions become excess contributions subject to a 6% excise tax.

The safe move: stop HSA contributions 6 months before you plan to enroll in any part of Medicare. Six full months of no contributions as a buffer against the retroactive coverage issue.

Social Security linkage: If you want to keep contributing to your HSA, you cannot start collecting Social Security retirement benefits. Social Security enrollment automatically triggers Medicare Part A enrollment. You can't get SS without getting Medicare. So if you're 65, still working, still HSA-eligible, and want to keep the contributions flowing — don't file for Social Security. This is sometimes counterintuitive for people who want the Social Security income while still working. You have to pick one.

sections.heading: When You Finally Retire: The 8-Month SEP Window
sections.content:
You've done everything right. You're 65 or older, you've been on employer coverage at a 20+ employee company, you've been delaying Medicare Part B legitimately. Now you're retiring.

Your 8-month Special Enrollment Period starts the day your active employer coverage ends — or the day you leave employment, whichever comes first. These are often the same day, but sometimes employers continue coverage through the end of the month or quarter. The SEP starts from whichever date comes first.

Eight months sounds like plenty of time. Don't use all of it.

The Part B application to Medicare takes time to process. Your Medigap application takes time. Your Part D plan enrollment takes time. If you want no gap in coverage starting on your retirement date, file 30-60 days before your employment ends.

Step-by-step for retiring after 65:

Step 1: Contact Social Security 1-3 months before your retirement date to enroll in Part B. Apply online at ssa.gov/medicare or call 1-800-772-1213. If you haven't enrolled in Part A yet either (because you were deferring HSA contributions), enroll in that too.

Step 2: If you want Medigap coverage with original Medicare: apply now. Guaranteed issue rights typically apply when you lose employer coverage — this is one of the specific SEP triggers for guaranteed issue Medigap in most states. You have 63 days from the date your employer coverage ends. This window is different from (and shorter than) your Part B SEP window.

Step 3: Enroll in Part D if you're going with Original Medicare, or choose a Medicare Advantage plan that includes drug coverage if that's your preference.

Step 4: Verify. Call your new plan to confirm enrollment. Check that your Medicare card arrived or that your Medicare status is updated in the SSA system.

The Medigap timing issue bears repeating: you have a 63-day guaranteed issue window for Medigap when you lose employer coverage. If you wait all 8 months of your Part B SEP before figuring out Medigap, you may be outside the 63-day window and subject to underwriting in most states. Solve Part B and Medigap together, simultaneously.

sections.heading: COBRA: What It Does and Doesn't Do for You
sections.content:
COBRA comes up constantly in this conversation and the rules around it are genuinely confusing.

What COBRA is: continuation coverage that lets you keep your former employer's group health plan for up to 18 months (36 months in some circumstances) after you leave employment. You pay the full premium — what you paid plus what your employer was subsidizing — which is typically $500-$1,500/month for an individual, sometimes more.

What COBRA does NOT do:

It does NOT extend your Medicare enrollment window. Your 8-month Part B SEP starts when employment ends, period. Not when COBRA ends.

It does NOT count as active employer coverage for Part B delay purposes. If you're 65 and covered only by COBRA, you need to be enrolled in Part B or you're past your window and accruing penalties.

It does NOT trigger a new SEP when it ends. When COBRA expires, you don't get a fresh Medicare enrollment window. If you delayed Medicare to stay on COBRA and COBRA ends 18 months later, you've potentially been without Medicare for 18+ months after your SEP closed.

What COBRA DOES do:

For Part D purposes only: COBRA drug coverage is creditable if the underlying employer plan was creditable. This means being on COBRA drug coverage doesn't trigger a Part D late enrollment penalty.

For bridging coverage: If you retire right before 65, COBRA can bridge the gap between job-based coverage and Medicare eligibility. This is a legitimate use case. You're on COBRA for a few months, then you turn 65, then you enroll in Medicare during your Initial Enrollment Period. No penalty, no gap. Just expensive bridge coverage.

The math often works against COBRA as a post-65 strategy: Medicare Part B ($202.90/month) plus Medigap Plan G (typically $100-$200/month depending on age and state) plus a basic Part D plan ($10-$30/month) totals roughly $300-$450/month at 65. COBRA for comparable coverage might cost $800-$1,500+/month. For most people at 65, Medicare is just cheaper than COBRA — even before considering the penalty risk.

sections.heading: What If Your Spouse Is Still Working?
sections.content:
Your situation gets complicated fast if you're not working but your spouse is, and you're covered under their employer plan.

If your spouse's employer has 20+ employees: You may be able to delay your own Medicare Part B enrollment without penalty, covered as a dependent under your spouse's plan. The same logic applies — the employer plan is primary, Medicare can wait, and when your spouse eventually retires or loses coverage, you have an 8-month SEP to enroll in Part B.

If your spouse's employer has fewer than 20 employees: Medicare becomes primary for you at 65 regardless. You need to enroll in Part A and Part B when you turn 65.

The HSA complication: If you're covered under your spouse's employer HDHP and your spouse is contributing to an HSA — your enrollment in Medicare doesn't directly affect your spouse's HSA eligibility, because the HSA is in your spouse's name. Your spouse can keep contributing to the HSA. But you personally cannot make HSA contributions once you're on Medicare.

If the HSA is yours (you're the employee-spouse's dependent and the HSA is in your name): different situation. Enroll in Medicare and your contribution eligibility ends.

Retirement timing: couples often stagger their retirement for exactly these reasons. One spouse retires, goes on Medicare. The other stays employed, keeps the employer coverage for both (if allowed) while also being the bridge to continued employer coverage options. If you're in this situation, map out the 8-month SEPs carefully so neither spouse misses their window when the second retirement happens.

sections.heading: The Comparison Most People Don't Do: Employer Coverage vs. Medicare
sections.content:
Before deciding whether to delay Medicare, you should actually run the numbers on what each option costs.

Your employer coverage might be great. Heavily subsidized, low deductible, broad network. In that case, delaying Medicare and keeping employer coverage is probably the right call.

But a lot of employer plans aren't great by 65. High deductibles. High out-of-pocket maximums. Network restrictions. And many companies shift more of the premium burden to employees as they age or as the company's overall healthcare costs rise.

Medicare + Medigap comparison (2026 rough numbers for an active 65-year-old):
- Part B premium: $202.90/month
- Medigap Plan G: approximately $100-$180/month depending on state and gender
- Part D basic plan: $10-$30/month
- Total: approximately $313-$413/month with essentially comprehensive coverage and nationwide access to any doctor who accepts Medicare

If your employer plan premiums plus out-of-pocket exposure exceed that — and you have flexibility in your employment situation — switching to Medicare at 65 might actually be the cheaper, more comprehensive option.

The transition point: for most people with heavily subsidized employer coverage (where they pay little to nothing for the premium), delaying Medicare while employed makes sense. For people with expensive employer coverage where they pay several hundred dollars a month and face high deductibles, running the comparison is worth the time.

Your local SHIP counselor can help run this comparison with your specific numbers. That service is free and the counselors know what questions to ask.

sections.heading: Disability Before 65: Different Rules
sections.content:
Everything above assumes you're hitting 65 as a working adult. But some people working at 65 became eligible for Medicare before 65 through disability — specifically, through 24 months of receiving Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI).

If you became entitled to Medicare through disability before 65, you've already been through an Initial Enrollment Period and are presumably already on Medicare Parts A and B. Turning 65 doesn't restart your enrollment periods.

However, if you were on SSDI, became Medicare-eligible, but delayed Part B because you had employer coverage — the rules above about the 8-month SEP still apply when you eventually lose that employer coverage. Disability Medicare recipients have the same SEP rights as age-eligible recipients when it comes to employer coverage transitions.

Coordination for disabled workers with employer coverage: the same employer size rule applies. 20+ employees means employer primary, Medicare secondary, you can delay Part B. Fewer than 20 employees means Medicare primary, enroll when you become eligible.

For people who became eligible for Medicare through disability and are still working at 65: contact your local SHIP counselor. The overlap of disability Medicare and age-based Medicare, combined with active employment, creates a genuinely complex situation that benefits from individual review.

faq.question: Can I delay Medicare if I'm still working at 65?
faq.answer: Yes, but only if your employer has 20 or more employees and you're covered by the active group health plan. In that case, you can delay Part B without penalty. When you eventually leave employment, you have an 8-month Special Enrollment Period to sign up for Part B without penalty. If your employer has fewer than 20 employees, you must enroll in Medicare on time — your employer plan won't work properly as primary payer.

faq.question: Does taking COBRA after retirement extend my Medicare enrollment window?
faq.answer: No. COBRA has no effect on your Medicare enrollment window. Your 8-month Part B Special Enrollment Period starts when you leave active employment, not when COBRA ends. If you rely on COBRA and let your SEP expire, you'll face a permanent Part B late enrollment penalty and be limited to the General Enrollment Period (January 1 – March 31) with a July 1 coverage start.

faq.question: Can I keep contributing to my HSA after enrolling in Medicare?
faq.answer: No. Enrolling in any part of Medicare — including free Part A — immediately ends your eligibility to make HSA contributions. Stop contributing 6 months before you plan to enroll in Medicare to avoid problems with Part A's 6-month retroactive coverage. If you want to keep contributing to your HSA, you must delay ALL Medicare enrollment and cannot start collecting Social Security retirement benefits.

faq.question: What's the Part B premium I'd pay in 2026?
faq.answer: The standard Part B premium for 2026 is $202.90/month. If your income is above approximately $106,000 as a single filer or $212,000 as a married couple filing jointly, you pay more through income-related adjustment (IRMAA). The additional amounts range from $73.90/month to $443.90/month depending on your income level, based on your 2024 tax return.

faq.question: When should I enroll in Medicare if I'm retiring after 65?
faq.answer: Start the Medicare enrollment process 1-3 months before your retirement date to ensure seamless coverage. Apply for Part B through Social Security (ssa.gov or 1-800-772-1213). If you want Medigap, apply simultaneously — you have a 63-day guaranteed issue window from the date your employer coverage ends, which is shorter than your 8-month Part B SEP. Don't wait until your employer coverage actually ends to start the paperwork.

faq.question: What if my spouse's employer covers me — can I still delay Medicare?
faq.answer: If you're covered as a dependent under your spouse's employer plan and that employer has 20+ employees, you can delay your own Medicare Part B without penalty. The same employer size rule applies to coverage through a spouse. When your spouse retires or loses coverage, you have an 8-month SEP to enroll in Part B without penalty.

faq.question: Is Medicare cheaper than employer coverage at 65?
faq.answer: It depends on your employer's subsidy structure. For people with heavily subsidized employer coverage (paying little out of pocket), staying on employer coverage may be cheaper. For people paying several hundred dollars monthly for employer coverage with high deductibles, Medicare Part B ($202.90/month) plus Medigap Plan G ($100-$180/month) plus basic Part D ($10-$30/month) — roughly $313-$413/month total with comprehensive coverage — may actually be cheaper. Run your specific numbers.

h1: Best Homeowners Insurance Companies 2026
