VSP's EasyOptions plan lets you customize your benefits — add more frame allowance, add more contacts allowance, or add lens enhancements. It's a flexible structure that works well for people with specific coverage priorities.

The network is VSP's biggest edge. At 40,000+ providers including Visionworks, a subset of LensCrafters, Costco Optical, and thousands of private practices, you have genuine flexibility. Compare that to EyeMed's retail-chain-heavy network and the difference matters if you prefer your local eye doctor.

Who VSP is for: anyone who has a preferred independent optometrist, wants broad provider choice, or values the private practice relationship over retail convenience.

Pros: Largest network, best for independent optometrists. Flexible plan options. Strong LASIK discount program.
Cons: Retail chain coverage is more limited than EyeMed. Some plans are available through employers only, not direct individual purchase in all states.

sections.heading: EyeMed — Best for Retail Convenience and Cheap Entry Plans
sections.content:
EyeMed's network is built around retail chains — LensCrafters is their flagship partner, and the network includes Target Optical, Sears Optical, JCPenney Optical, and Pearle Vision. If you prefer shopping for glasses at a retail optical center or want the convenience of a mall location, EyeMed's network structure works well.

On pricing, EyeMed has the most aggressive entry point: their most basic plan (the Bright plan) starts around $5-6/month — seriously, that low — though it offers limited benefits. More practical is their Bright plan at $17.50/month, which provides:
— Eye exam with $10 copay in-network
— $130 frame or contact lens allowance
— 40% off additional pairs of glasses
— 15% off LASIK at participating providers

The higher-tier Bold plan (~$26/month) bumps the frame allowance to $200 and adds 20% off LASIK.

EyeMed's weakness is that independent optometrist coverage is thinner. If your eye doctor isn't affiliated with a retail chain and isn't in the EyeMed network, you're either paying out-of-network rates or switching providers. For urban and suburban markets this is usually fine — there's always a LensCrafters nearby. For smaller markets or rural areas, verify network coverage before buying.

A notable EyeMed advantage: out-of-network benefits. EyeMed pays set amounts for out-of-network services — $35-45 toward an out-of-network exam, $70-105 toward frames — which is better than some competitors who pay zero out-of-network. Not amazing, but a buffer.

Who EyeMed is for: people who shop at retail optical centers, want the lowest possible premium, or value the convenience of LensCrafters specifically.

Pros: Cheapest entry-level pricing. LensCrafters integration. Out-of-network partial benefits. Good for retail shoppers.
Cons: Thinner independent practice network than VSP. Retail-chain focus isn't for everyone.

sections.heading: Davis Vision — Best for Families and Frame Warranty
sections.content:
Davis Vision is smaller than VSP and EyeMed in terms of market share and name recognition, but they offer a product that's genuinely different in a few ways worth knowing.

Davis Vision's plans are structured around three tiers — Essential, Classic, and Premier — starting at around $12/month for individuals and scaling up for more comprehensive coverage. The Essential plan is preventive-focused. Classic and Premier add more substantial frames and contacts allowances ($150-200+), with Premier offering $200+ toward frames or contacts per benefit period.

Two things Davis Vision does differently.

First: the unconditional frame and lens warranty. If your frames or lenses break within the first year after purchase, Davis will replace them at no cost. For families with kids — and anyone who's sat on their glasses — this is a real feature. Accidents happen. Most carriers let you buy extended warranties; Davis includes it.

Second: the exams and provider network includes both retail and private practice optometrists. The coverage area isn't as large as VSP's 40,000+ but it's solid in the markets where Davis Vision operates. Check the local network before committing.

Davis Vision also allows family plan structures that bundle multiple people more cost-effectively than buying individual policies separately — particularly advantageous for families of four or more.

Who Davis Vision is for: families with kids (the frame warranty alone is worth it), anyone who breaks glasses regularly, and people looking for solid mid-market coverage at competitive pricing.

Pros: Unconditional frame/lens warranty. Family plan pricing. Good exam coverage. Designer frame access on higher tiers.
Cons: Smaller total network than VSP or EyeMed. Less name recognition may mean fewer plan options through employers.

sections.heading: The Worth-It Math: Do You Actually Need Vision Insurance?
sections.content:
Let's run this honestly because vision insurance is one of those products where the math actually matters and the answer is different for different people.

The break-even scenario. A standard vision insurance plan costs around $15-20/month, or $180-240/year. What do you get? An eye exam (retail value ~$150) and a contacts or glasses allowance ($130-200).

If you wear glasses: you get $150 in exam coverage plus $150-200 toward frames. That's $300-350 in benefits for $180-240 in premiums. You're ahead, but only slightly — and only if you actually buy new glasses every year. If you buy new glasses every two years (very common), you're now getting $150 exam + $75 amortized frames benefit = $225 per year in value while paying $200. Barely worth it, and that's before accounting for the fact that most people buying glasses spend above the allowance and pay the difference.

If you wear contacts: you get the exam plus $150-200 contact lens allowance. Annual soft contact costs run $200-600+ depending on type (dailies vs monthlies, brand, prescription complexity). With a $200 allowance, you're covering maybe half of moderate contact costs. So: $150 exam + $200 contacts allowance = $350 value vs $200 premium = genuinely worth it for contact wearers, especially daily lens users.

If you're considering LASIK: the insurance doesn't pay for it — it discounts it 15-20%. On $4,500 LASIK, that's $675-900 off. Keep the plan for one year, use the LASIK discount, then cancel — you've gotten your eye exam, used your glasses or contacts allowance, and saved $675+ on the procedure. Then you probably don't need vision insurance for several years post-LASIK. Net: buying vision insurance for the LASIK discount is often excellent one-year ROI.

Bottom line: vision insurance makes most sense for contact wearers, families, and people pursuing LASIK. For glasses wearers who buy frames infrequently, you might be better off going uninsured and shopping at Costco Optical, Warby Parker, or Zenni — where quality frames with lenses run $50-200 without insurance.

sections.heading: Vision Insurance Costs in 2026
sections.content:
Here's the real pricing landscape for individual and family vision insurance plans.

Individual plans:
— Budget tier (EyeMed basic, Humana preventive-focused): $5-15/month
— Standard PPO (VSP, EyeMed mid-tier, Davis Vision Essential): $12-20/month
— Comprehensive (VSP EasyOptions, Davis Vision Premier): $20-30/month

Family plans:
— Two adults: $25-45/month depending on carrier
— Family of four: $45-70/month depending on carrier

Through employer benefits, premiums are often subsidized 50-100%, making it almost always worth enrolling if available. The benefit packages through employer group vision are typically richer than individual plans at the same price point due to group negotiating leverage.

Premiums have held relatively steady compared to health or homeowners insurance — vision is a low-severity, high-frequency coverage that's fairly predictable for insurers to price. The bigger factor in cost is your benefit tier selection and whether you bundle with dental.

Bundling dental + vision: most major carriers (Guardian, Cigna, Humana, MetLife) offer combined dental + vision packages. The discount for bundling is typically $3-8/month off the combined premium. Not massive savings, but the administrative simplicity of one carrier, one ID card, one bill is worth something. If you need both coverages, compare the bundle price against buying separately.

sections.heading: Contacts vs Glasses vs LASIK: The Real Financial Math
sections.content:
Let's actually do the lifetime math because this affects how you think about vision insurance forever.

Contacts (monthly lenses, mid-range brand): roughly $250-400/year in lenses plus $150/year in solution. $400-550/year ongoing, every year. Over 20 years: $8,000-11,000. Vision insurance with a $200 contacts allowance saves you maybe $180-200/year in net benefit after premium — saving roughly $3,600-4,000 over 20 years.

Glasses only (new frames every 2 years, mid-range): $300-600 per pair at a retail optical, $150-300 at Warby Parker or Costco. Annual cost averaged: $150-300. Vision insurance with $200 allowance and exam coverage: saves roughly $100-200/year, or $2,000-4,000 over 20 years. The math works but it's tighter.

LASIK (one-time): $3,000-6,000 for both eyes, procedure done. After LASIK, most people go 5-15 years before needing reading glasses. Using vision insurance for one year to get the LASIK discount ($600-1,200 off), then canceling, then spending $150/year on a reading glasses pair when needed — this is often the cheapest long-term path. The total lifetime eye care cost for a LASIK recipient can be 40-60% lower than a lifetime contacts wearer.

The question LASIK candidates usually ask: is LASIK worth it financially? At $4,000-5,000 total, if you're currently spending $500/year on contacts, you break even in 8-10 years. For a 30-year-old, yes — that's worth it. For a 55-year-old who'll need progressive readers anyway in a few years, the math is less obvious.

sections.heading: What Vision Insurance Won't Cover
sections.content:
A few things that trip people up.

Medical eye conditions are not covered by vision insurance. Glaucoma treatment, diabetic retinopathy management, macular degeneration — these are medical diagnoses treated by ophthalmologists and covered under your health insurance, not your vision plan. Vision insurance covers routine exams for prescriptions, not medical eye care.

Premium lens add-ons cost extra out of pocket almost universally. Anti-reflective coating ($30-80), blue-light blocking ($20-50), photochromic/Transitions lenses ($100-150+) — these are rarely covered by vision insurance even in-network. Budget for them separately.

Frame brands above the allowance. The $150-200 allowance covers a lot of frames at retail optical centers, but designer frames (Warby Parker's premium tier, Oakley, Ray-Ban, most designer brands) start at $200-300 or higher. You'll pay the difference.

Contact lens fitting fees. Your vision plan covers an exam and a contact lens allowance, but the annual contact lens fitting fee (charged separately from the exam at many providers) may not be covered or may have a separate copay. Ask before your appointment.

faq.question: How much does vision insurance cost per month in 2026?
faq.answer: Individual vision insurance runs $5-30/month depending on plan tier and carrier. Budget plans start around $5-15/month. Standard plans with solid exam and glasses/contacts allowances run $12-20/month. Family plans range from $25-70/month. EyeMed has the cheapest entry point; VSP and Davis Vision offer the best mid-tier value.

faq.question: VSP vs EyeMed — which is better?
faq.answer: VSP has the larger network (40,000+ providers) with strong independent optometrist coverage — better if you have a preferred eye doctor at a private practice. EyeMed has aggressive pricing (some plans under $10/month) and strong retail chain coverage through LensCrafters — better if you prefer retail optical convenience or need the cheapest premium.

faq.question: Does vision insurance cover LASIK?
faq.answer: No — but most major plans offer LASIK discounts of 15-20% at participating providers. On a $4,000-5,000 procedure, that's $600-1,000 in savings. It's a discount program, not coverage. Using vision insurance for one year, capturing the LASIK discount, then canceling is often excellent short-term financial strategy.

faq.question: Is vision insurance worth it?
faq.answer: For contact lens wearers and families, yes — the math generally works. For glasses wearers who buy new frames infrequently, you might break even or come out slightly ahead. If you're considering LASIK, buy a plan for the discount and cancel after. If you have excellent vision and only need an exam every two years, the math often doesn't work — just pay out of pocket.

faq.question: Does vision insurance cover prescription sunglasses?
faq.answer: Partially. Your frames/lenses allowance can typically be applied to prescription sunglasses — they count as your annual glasses benefit. Tinted lenses or photochromic (Transitions) upgrades cost extra out of pocket. Non-prescription sunglasses are not covered.

faq.question: What does Davis Vision's frame warranty cover?
faq.answer: Davis Vision offers an unconditional warranty replacing frames or lenses that break within the first year of purchase at no cost to the member. This is a genuine differentiator — most carriers let you purchase extended warranties separately, while Davis includes it. Particularly valuable for families with children.

faq.question: Can I bundle vision insurance with dental insurance?
faq.answer: Yes, and most major carriers encourage it. Guardian, Cigna, Humana, and MetLife all offer combined dental + vision packages. The premium discount for bundling is typically $3-8/month. The bigger benefit is administrative simplicity — one carrier, one premium, one set of ID cards.

faq.question: Are eye exams covered by health insurance?
faq.answer: Routine eye exams for vision correction (prescription updates) are typically covered by vision insurance, not health insurance. However, if an eye exam reveals a medical condition (glaucoma, cataracts, diabetic eye disease), follow-up care is usually covered under your health insurance since it's medical treatment rather than routine vision care.

title: Medicare Part B Premium 2026: $202.90 Explained
metaDescription: The standard Medicare Part B premium hit $202.90 in 2026 — a $17.90 jump from 2025. Here's exactly why it went up, how IRMAA can push your bill to $689.90/month, the two-year lookback rule, and every real strategy to keep costs down.
h1: Medicare Part B Premium 2026: $202.90 Explained
summary: Your Medicare card shows up. You enroll in Part B. Then you find out Social Security is pulling $202.90 out of your check every month before you ever step into a doctor's office. That's the 2026 standard premium,

sections.heading: The Number Everyone Sees: $202.90
sections.content:
Your Medicare card shows up. You enroll in Part B. Then you find out Social Security is pulling $202.90 out of your check every month before you ever step into a doctor's office.

That's the 2026 standard premium, confirmed by CMS in November 2025. Up from $185.00 in 2025. A $17.90 increase — roughly 9.7% — which, ngl, is a gut punch when you're on a fixed income.

But here's the thing most people don't realize: $202.90 is the floor, not the ceiling. About 7 to 8 percent of Medicare beneficiaries pay significantly more because of something called IRMAA. And a smaller number — the ones who qualify for Medicare Savings Programs — pay nothing at all. The actual range in 2026 runs from $0 to $689.90 per month, same exact benefit, wildly different price tag. Where you land depends almost entirely on your income from two years ago.

So before you accept whatever number shows up in your Medicare letter, it's worth understanding every variable that went into it — because some of them are negotiable.

sections.heading: Why Did It Go Up? The Real Explanation
sections.content:
CMS doesn't set the Part B premium arbitrarily. The standard premium has to cover 25% of the projected cost of Part B services — the other 75% comes from general federal revenues. So when Medicare spending goes up, your premium goes up with it.

For 2026, the main drivers were projected price increases across Part B services — physician visits, outpatient hospital care, durable medical equipment, labs — combined with assumed utilization increases. Translation: more people using more services at higher prices.

There's also a specific storyline behind the 2026 number. CMS noted that without changes to the 2026 Physician Fee Schedule Final Rule addressing skin substitute products, the premium increase would have been roughly $11 higher per month. Skin substitutes had become a massive, fast-growing expense line — the final rule cut projected spending on them by about 90%. That one regulatory decision effectively saved beneficiaries $11/month. That kind of specificity is worth knowing — it shows how directly CMS decisions translate into your premium.

The Part B deductible also increased, from $257 in 2025 to $283 in 2026. You pay that once per year before Medicare starts splitting costs with you on most Part B services.

And for Part A — the hospital coverage most people get free — the inpatient hospital deductible rose to $1,676 per benefit period in 2026, up from $1,632. That's per benefit period, not per year, which is a nuance that bites people who have multiple hospitalizations.

sections.heading: The Two-Year Lookback: Why 2024 Income Controls 2026 Premiums
sections.content:
This is the mechanism that surprises people most, and it's genuinely important to internalize.

Social Security determines your 2026 Medicare premiums using your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income — MAGI — because that's the most recent tax return IRS has processed and shared with SSA by the time they're setting 2026 rates. The sequence works like this: you file your 2024 taxes in early 2025, IRS processes and transmits the data to SSA mid-2025, SSA uses that data to set your 2026 premium notice, which lands in your mailbox around November 2025.

For the standard premium of $202.90, the 2024 income threshold is under $106,000 if you filed single, or under $212,000 if you filed jointly. The vast majority of beneficiaries — around 93% — fall under those thresholds.

But the two-year lookback creates a specific trap for people who retired in 2024 or 2025 from high-income careers. You could be living on Social Security and a small IRA distribution in 2026, genuinely income-constrained, while paying IRMAA surcharges based on your last full working year's salary. The premium bill reflects who you were financially two years ago, not who you are now.

That mismatch is exactly what the life-changing event appeal process exists to fix — but you have to proactively file it. SSA doesn't know you retired unless you tell them.

sections.heading: IRMAA Brackets: When $202.90 Becomes $689.90
sections.content:
IRMAA stands for Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount. It's a surcharge added on top of the standard $202.90 premium, and it triggers at specific income thresholds based on 2024 MAGI.

Here are the 2026 Part B brackets:

For individual filers (or married filing separately):
- $106,000 or less: $202.90/month (standard, no surcharge)
- $106,001 – $133,000: $284.10/month (surcharge: $81.20)
- $133,001 – $167,000: $394.60/month (surcharge: $191.70)
- $167,001 – $200,000: $505.10/month (surcharge: $302.20)
- $200,001 – $500,000: $601.70/month (surcharge: $398.80)
- Above $500,000: $689.90/month (surcharge: $487.00)

For married filing jointly:
- $212,000 or less: $202.90/month
- $212,001 – $266,000: $284.10/month
- $266,001 – $334,000: $394.60/month
- $334,001 – $400,000: $505.10/month
- $400,001 – $750,000: $601.70/month
- Above $750,000: $689.90/month

This is a cliff structure — $1 of income above a threshold triggers the full surcharge for the entire bracket. That's not a typo. One dollar over $133,000 as a single filer means your premium jumps from $284.10 to $394.60. The difference between IRMAA bracket 1 and bracket 2 is $110.50 per month, and it can hinge on whether you recognized a small capital gain or did a Roth conversion in 2024.

Part D IRMAA surcharges layer on top of this. They run from $14.50/month at the first bracket to $91.00/month at the highest, based on the same income thresholds. So at the top IRMAA bracket, a married couple could each be paying $689.90 for Part B plus their Part D plan premium plus a $91.00/month surcharge on Part D — for two people, that's over $1,500/month in Medicare premiums alone, before any cost-sharing.

sections.heading: What MAGI Includes That Surprises People
sections.content:
MAGI for IRMAA purposes is your Adjusted Gross Income plus tax-exempt interest income. That last part catches people off guard.

Municipal bond interest is tax-exempt at the federal level — most people think that means invisible to IRMAA. It's not. That muni bond income gets added back in when SSA calculates whether you hit an IRMAA bracket.

Here's what's included in MAGI for Medicare purposes:
- Wages, salary, self-employment income
- Taxable Social Security benefits
- Required minimum distributions from traditional IRAs and 401(k)s
- Capital gains (short and long term)
- Dividends (qualified and ordinary)
- Roth conversion income — this one is massive and discussed more below
- Rental income
- Tax-exempt interest (municipal bonds)

What's not included:
- Roth IRA distributions (if qualified)
- Health Savings Account distributions for qualified medical expenses
- Reverse mortgage proceeds
- Life insurance proceeds

The practical implication is that retirees with significant traditional IRA balances face a compounding problem: every required minimum distribution inflates MAGI, which potentially pushes them into an IRMAA bracket, which raises their Part B and Part D premiums. This is one of the strongest arguments for doing Roth conversions before age 73 when RMDs begin — but the conversion income itself also counts toward MAGI in the conversion year, so timing matters enormously.

sections.heading: Roth Conversions and the IRMAA Trap
sections.content:
This deserves its own section because it's one of the most common expensive mistakes people make.

Roth conversions are a powerful strategy — you pay tax now on traditional IRA funds, move them into a Roth, and all future growth and qualified distributions are tax-free. The problem is that conversion income hits your MAGI in the conversion year, and that MAGI determines your Medicare premiums two years later.

So if you do a large Roth conversion in 2026, you're setting up your 2028 Medicare premiums. Convert $100,000 as a single filer with $80,000 in other income — suddenly your 2028 MAGI is $180,000, which puts you solidly in IRMAA bracket 3 ($394.60/month for Part B). Two years of elevated premiums could easily cost $4,000-$5,000 extra depending on your Part D situation. That has to factor into your conversion math.

The sweet spot most advisors target is a 'conversion ladder' — doing smaller conversions each year to stay under the next IRMAA threshold. For a single filer aiming to stay under the $133,000 threshold in 2026, that means keeping total MAGI below that number including the conversion amount.

The caveat is that nobody should avoid Roth conversions entirely because of IRMAA. Paying $2,000-$3,000 extra per year in Medicare premiums might still be worth it if it eliminates $200,000 in future RMDs that would otherwise be taxed and IRMAA-counted annually. The math is individual. But you have to actually run the math — you can't just convert aggressively without modeling the downstream IRMAA hit.

sections.heading: Life-Changing Event Appeals: How to Fight Back
sections.content:
Here's the thing about IRMAA: SSA has a formal process specifically designed for situations where your current income is dramatically lower than what they used to calculate your premium.

Qualifying life-changing events include:
- Retirement (work stoppage or reduction)
- Marriage
- Divorce or annulment
- Death of a spouse
- Loss or reduction of pension income
- Loss of income-producing property (not from your own actions)
- Employer settlement payment

If any of these apply, you file Form SSA-44 — downloadable from SSA.gov. On the form, you check the qualifying event, enter the date it occurred, and provide your actual or estimated MAGI for a more recent year that SSA should use instead.

Process in practice: don't file until you've received your IRMAA determination letter from SSA. That letter is your trigger. Once you have it, complete SSA-44, gather documentation (retirement letter or final pay stub, marriage/death/divorce certificate as applicable), and take it to your local SSA office or mail it in. In-person generally moves faster.

If approved, SSA adjusts your premium going forward and refunds any overpayment you've already made — typically via direct deposit or check. Most straightforward appeals resolve within 30-60 days.

If your income dropped but not due to a qualifying life-changing event — say, a market downturn reduced your investment income — you can't use SSA-44 but you can request a new IRMAA determination directly through SSA if you believe the income data they used was incorrect.

sections.heading: Strategies to Actually Reduce What You Pay
sections.content:
The premium is what it is — but for a meaningful slice of beneficiaries, there are legitimate ways to lower the bill.

Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs) are the highest-impact option if your income qualifies. The QMB program pays both your premium and your cost-sharing. SLMB pays the Part B premium directly. These are state-administered programs you apply for through your state Medicaid office. Millions of people who qualify aren't enrolled because they never knew to apply. Income limits are higher than most people expect — $1,350/month for a single person to qualify for QMB in 2026.

IRMAA bracket management, if you're close to a threshold, is worth real effort. For single filers near $106,000 or $133,000, strategies include: timing capital gains realizations, bunching deductions to reduce AGI, maximizing HSA contributions if you're not yet on Medicare, and structuring Roth conversions to stay under the cliff. A $500 payment to a financial planner to model this can save $5,000 over two years.

Delayed enrollment — if you're still working and covered by employer insurance at 65 — means you don't pay Part B premiums at all during that period. Make sure your employer coverage is creditable to avoid late enrollment penalties later.

Qualified Charitable Distributions (QCDs) if you're 70.5 or older let you send up to $105,000 per year from an IRA directly to charity without the distribution hitting your AGI. That can meaningfully reduce MAGI and prevent IRMAA bumps caused by RMDs.

And if you're on Medicare Advantage rather than original Medicare, some plans offer Part B premium reduction benefits — they rebate a portion of the $202.90 back to you. These plans exist specifically in markets where CMS star ratings are high enough to generate excess rebate dollars. Worth checking what's available in your zip code during open enrollment.

faq.question: Why is the 2026 Medicare Part B premium $202.90 specifically?
faq.answer: CMS sets it to cover 25% of projected Part B spending. The other 75% comes from federal revenues. The 2026 number reflects projected cost increases across Part B services — physician visits, outpatient care, labs, equipment — plus assumed utilization growth. Without a regulatory change cutting skin substitute spending by 90%, the premium would have been roughly $11 higher at around $214.

faq.question: Does everyone pay $202.90 for Part B in 2026?
faq.answer: No. About 7-8% of beneficiaries with higher incomes pay more due to IRMAA surcharges — up to $689.90/month. And beneficiaries who qualify for Medicare Savings Programs like QMB can have their premium paid entirely by Medicaid, effectively $0 out of pocket.

faq.question: How does the two-year lookback work exactly?
faq.answer: Your 2026 Part B premium is based on your 2024 MAGI — the most recent tax return IRS has processed and shared with SSA when they're setting 2026 rates. If your income was high in 2024 but dropped significantly in 2025 due to retirement or another life-changing event, you can appeal using Form SSA-44 to get SSA to use your more current, lower income.

faq.question: What income triggers IRMAA in 2026?
faq.answer: Single filers with 2024 MAGI above $106,000, and married filing jointly above $212,000. Below those thresholds, you pay the standard $202.90. IRMAA surcharges apply to both Part B and Part D, and they stack — so high-income beneficiaries pay more for both.

faq.question: Can I appeal my IRMAA surcharge?
faq.answer: Yes. If your income dropped due to a qualifying life-changing event (retirement, marriage, divorce, death of spouse, loss of pension, loss of income-producing property), file Form SSA-44. You'll need documentation of the event and your current or estimated income. SSA can use more recent income data to recalculate your premium. Any overpayment gets refunded.

faq.question: Does Roth conversion income affect Medicare premiums?
faq.answer: Yes — Roth conversion income counts as MAGI in the year of conversion, which sets your Medicare premiums two years later. A large conversion in 2026 impacts 2028 premiums. Most planners recommend a conversion ladder approach — smaller annual conversions sized to stay under IRMAA thresholds — rather than large one-time conversions.

faq.question: Are municipal bond earnings counted toward IRMAA?
faq.answer: Yes. MAGI for Medicare IRMAA purposes adds tax-exempt interest (including municipal bond interest) back to AGI. A lot of retirees miss this because muni bonds are federally tax-exempt — but they're not IRMAA-exempt. That income gets counted when SSA determines your bracket.

faq.question: Is there a penalty for late Part B enrollment?
faq.answer: Yes — 10% per 12-month period you were eligible but didn't enroll, and it's permanent. The exception is if you had creditable employer coverage. If you're covered under active employment (yours or a spouse's) through a group plan when you turn 65, you can delay Part B without penalty and enroll when that coverage ends.

title: Medicare IRMAA: Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount Explained
metaDescription: IRMAA is the Medicare surcharge that can push your Part B and Part D premiums from $202.90 to $689.90 per month. Full 2026 bracket tables, MAGI calculation breakdown, Roth conversion impact, how to appeal with Form SSA-44, and planning strategies that actually work.
h1: Medicare IRMAA: Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount Explained
summary: You enroll in Medicare. You expect to pay $202.90 a month for Part B. Then a letter arrives from Social Security telling you your premium is actually $394.60. Or $505.10. Or higher. That's IRMAA — the Income-Related

sections.heading: What IRMAA Is and Why Most People Learn About It the Hard Way
sections.content:
You enroll in Medicare. You expect to pay $202.90 a month for Part B. Then a letter arrives from Social Security telling you your premium is actually $394.60. Or $505.10. Or higher.

That's IRMAA — the Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount. It's a tiered surcharge on top of the standard Medicare Part B and Part D premiums that applies to beneficiaries above certain income thresholds. Congress created it as part of the Medicare Modernization Act in 2003, on the principle that higher-income beneficiaries should bear a larger share of program costs.

The mechanics are straightforward but the details bite. SSA looks at your Modified Adjusted Gross Income from two years prior — your 2024 return sets 2026 rates. If that income exceeds the first threshold ($106,000 for single filers, $212,000 for joint filers in 2026), you pay a surcharge. The surcharge is not a percentage — it's a fixed dollar amount per bracket, applied monthly, and it's identical whether your income is $1 over the threshold or $30,000 over it.

That cliff structure is what makes IRMAA dangerous to ignore in planning. The gap between just under a threshold and just over it can be worth $1,200-$4,800 per year in premiums. For both spouses on Medicare, double it. Over a 20-year retirement? We're talking about real money that can be managed — or not — depending on whether someone ever explained this to you.

sections.heading: The 2026 IRMAA Bracket Tables: Part B
sections.content:
Your 2026 Part B premium is determined by your 2024 MAGI and filing status. Here are the exact brackets:

Single filers / Married filing separately:

| 2024 MAGI | Monthly Part B Premium | IRMAA Surcharge |
|---|---|---|
| $106,000 or less | $202.90 | None |
| $106,001 – $133,000 | $284.10 | $81.20 |
| $133,001 – $167,000 | $394.60 | $191.70 |
| $167,001 – $200,000 | $505.10 | $302.20 |
| $200,001 – $500,000 | $601.70 | $398.80 |
| Above $500,000 | $689.90 | $487.00 |

Married filing jointly:

| 2024 MAGI | Monthly Part B Premium | IRMAA Surcharge |
|---|---|---|
| $212,000 or less | $202.90 | None |
| $212,001 – $266,000 | $284.10 | $81.20 |
| $266,001 – $334,000 | $394.60 | $191.70 |
| $334,001 – $400,000 | $505.10 | $302.20 |
| $400,001 – $750,000 | $601.70 | $398.80 |
| Above $750,000 | $689.90 | $487.00 |

Note on married filing separately: this category uses the single-filer thresholds but is compressed — most married couples will pay much more filing separately than jointly if one spouse has high income. SSA uses your actual filing status, not the more favorable one.

For 2026, the income brackets increased approximately 3% from 2025 to account for inflation. The surcharge amounts increased about 9%, reflecting the overall increase in Part B program costs. The top two brackets (above $200K single / above $400K joint, and above $500K / above $750K) are currently not indexed for inflation — those thresholds stay frozen until 2028.

sections.heading: The 2026 IRMAA Bracket Tables: Part D
sections.content:
Part D IRMAA surcharges use the same income thresholds as Part B — same brackets, same two-year lookback, same filing status rules. But Part D IRMAA is a flat monthly surcharge added on top of whatever your actual Part D plan premium is.

Single / Married filing separately:

| 2024 MAGI | Monthly Part D Surcharge |
|---|---|
| $106,000 or less | $0 |
| $106,001 – $133,000 | $14.50 |
| $133,001 – $167,000 | $37.30 |
| $167,001 – $200,000 | $60.20 |
| $200,001 – $500,000 | $83.00 |
| Above $500,000 | $91.00 |

Married filing jointly:

| 2024 MAGI | Monthly Part D Surcharge |
|---|---|
| $212,000 or less | $0 |
| $212,001 – $266,000 | $14.50 |
| $266,001 – $334,000 | $37.30 |
| $334,001 – $400,000 | $60.20 |
| $400,001 – $750,000 | $83.00 |
| Above $750,000 | $91.00 |

Unlike Part B, where SSA deducts the premium directly from your Social Security check, Part D IRMAA is billed by SSA separately — you pay it directly to SSA, not to your Part D plan. Your drug plan still charges its normal monthly premium. The IRMAA is on top of that, billed separately.

At the highest IRMAA bracket, a single beneficiary in 2026 pays $689.90 (Part B) + their Part D plan premium (average ~$46.50) + $91.00 (Part D IRMAA) = roughly $827.40/month in Medicare premiums. That's nearly $10,000/year, before any claims.

sections.heading: How MAGI Is Calculated for IRMAA Purposes
sections.content:
MAGI for Medicare IRMAA is not the same as MAGI for ACA subsidies or other purposes — though the components overlap significantly. For Medicare, it's your AGI from your federal tax return plus tax-exempt interest income.

AGI (line 11 of Form 1040) already includes:
- Wages, salaries, tips
- Self-employment income
- Taxable Social Security benefits (up to 85% of SS can be taxable depending on your income)
- Pension and annuity income
- Traditional IRA and 401(k) distributions (not Roth)
- Required minimum distributions
- Capital gains (short and long term)
- Dividends (qualified and ordinary)
- Roth conversion income (included in the year converted)
- Rental income, royalties, partnership income
- Alimony received (for divorces finalized before 2019)

Then SSA adds:
- Tax-exempt interest — this includes municipal bond interest, which does NOT appear on your 1040 income but DOES flow through Schedule B and gets reported on line 2a. It's added back to MAGI for IRMAA.

What is NOT included:
- Roth IRA distributions (if qualified — account open 5+ years, age 59.5+)
- HSA distributions used for qualified medical expenses
- Reverse mortgage proceeds
- Life insurance death benefits
- Gifts and inheritances
- Return of basis from non-deductible IRA contributions (pro-rata rules apply)

The practical implication: a retired couple with significant muni bond holdings, some traditional IRA distributions, and Social Security could easily hit an IRMAA bracket despite feeling 'not that wealthy.' If you're near a threshold, running your actual numbers against the brackets should be an annual exercise.

sections.heading: Roth Conversions and IRMAA: The Math Nobody Runs
sections.content:
Roth conversions are generally good. The ability to move money from a taxable vehicle (traditional IRA) to a tax-free vehicle (Roth) is one of the highest-value planning moves available in early retirement before RMDs hit. But the IRMAA dimension adds a two-year lag variable that most people underweight.

Conversion income in year X raises MAGI in year X. SSA uses that MAGI to set Medicare premiums in year X+2. So if you do an aggressive Roth conversion in 2026 that pushes 2026 MAGI above an IRMAA threshold, your 2028 Medicare premiums will reflect it.

The numbers on a specific example: a single beneficiary with $75,000 in baseline income (SS + RMD) converting $60,000 in 2026 has $135,000 MAGI. That's above the $133,001 threshold — triggering IRMAA bracket 2 in 2028 at $394.60/month instead of $284.10. The difference is $110.50/month, $1,326/year, times 2 years of elevated premiums = $2,652 in additional Medicare costs from that one conversion.

Is the conversion still worth it? Probably yes, if the Roth grows tax-free for 15-20 years and eliminates future RMDs. But that $2,652 has to go into the model. If the conversion was $58,000 instead of $60,000 — staying under $133,000 MAGI — you save $2,652 over the next two years at the cost of $2,000 less converted. That math usually favors staying under the threshold.

The optimal approach is an annual bracket optimization: determine your projected MAGI from non-conversion sources, then convert exactly enough to fill up to the next IRMAA threshold without crossing it. Not $1 more. Repeat annually.

sections.heading: The SSA-44 Appeal Process, Step by Step
sections.content:
IRMAA isn't always permanent. If your income dropped because of a qualifying life-changing event, SSA can recalculate your premium using more current income data. The vehicle is Form SSA-44.

Qualifying life-changing events under SSA's rules:
1. Marriage
2. Divorce or annulment
3. Death of a spouse
4. Work stoppage (retirement)
5. Work reduction (reduced hours/income)
6. Loss of income-producing property (involuntary — flood, fire, etc.)
7. Loss or reduction of pension income
8. Receipt of employer settlement payment

Note what's NOT on that list: investment losses, voluntary early withdrawal from investments, simply choosing to take less money, or any income drop not tied to one of those specific events. If your portfolio took a 30% hit in 2024 and your income dropped as a result, that doesn't qualify. SSA's qualifying events are specific and documented.

If you qualify, here's the process:

Step 1: Wait for your IRMAA determination letter. Don't preemptively file — SSA needs to have made a determination for there to be something to appeal.

Step 2: Download and complete Form SSA-44 from SSA.gov. The December 2025 edition is current. Check the qualifying event, enter the date, and provide your actual or estimated MAGI for the year you want SSA to use.

Step 3: Gather documentation. Retirement/work reduction: final pay stub, termination/retirement letter, or W-2 from the reduced-income year. Marriage: marriage certificate. Death of spouse: death certificate. Loss of pension: pension change notice or letter from plan administrator. Loss of property: insurance claim, foreclosure notice, disaster declaration.

Step 4: Submit to SSA. Your local Social Security office is fastest — walk in with the form and originals of all documents. Mailing is acceptable but adds 2-4 weeks. SSA doesn't accept digital submissions for SSA-44 currently.

Step 5: Watch for the decision letter, typically within 30-60 days. If approved, future premiums reflect the lower calculation and any overpayments are refunded via direct deposit or check.

sections.heading: Planning Strategies That Actually Move the Needle
sections.content:
IRMAA is a real cost but it's also one of the more plannable Medicare expenses — you have a two-year runway and a known cliff structure. Here's what actually works:

Annual MAGI modeling. Every year, before December 31, estimate your MAGI for that tax year. Compare to the IRMAA thresholds that will apply two years out (2026 income → 2028 premiums). If you're within striking distance of a threshold, you may have moves left — tax-loss harvesting to offset capital gains, accelerating or delaying income recognition, adjusting Roth conversion amounts.

Qualified Charitable Distributions for RMDs. If you're 70.5 or older, you can send up to $105,000 per year directly from an IRA to a qualifying charity. The distribution counts toward your RMD but doesn't hit AGI at all. For someone whose RMDs are nudging them into an IRMAA bracket, QCDs are one of the cleanest ways to reduce MAGI — especially if you're already donating to charity anyway.

HSA strategy before Medicare enrollment. Once you're on Medicare, you can't contribute to an HSA — but you can use an existing HSA balance tax-free for qualified medical expenses (including Medicare premiums). In the years before Medicare, maxing HSA contributions reduces current-year AGI and builds a tax-free reserve for healthcare costs.

Bunching deductions. If you're close to the standard deduction threshold, bunching charitable contributions or other deductible expenses into alternate years can reduce MAGI in high-income years.

Timing large income events. If you're selling a business, a rental property, or a large investment position, timing matters. In some cases it's worth spanning a transaction across two calendar years to keep each year's MAGI under an IRMAA threshold.

For people who are deep into IRMAA brackets — $400K+ annual income — the math on these strategies changes. At that income level, you're paying IRMAA regardless; the focus shifts to Roth conversion ladder optimization to reduce future RMDs and thus future MAGI in later years when you have more income flexibility.

sections.heading: Common IRMAA Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
sections.content:
A few patterns come up again and again in IRMAA situations:

Not knowing your MAGI until it's too late. Most people don't think about MAGI during the year — they see it for the first time when they file taxes in April. By then the year is closed, the income is locked, and the IRMAA determination two years out is set. Getting a mid-year estimate from your tax preparer or running your own projection in October gives you a window to make moves before December 31.

Ignoring muni bonds in the calculation. Tax-exempt interest is very visible on your brokerage statement as 'exempt from federal tax' but it absolutely counts toward IRMAA MAGI. Someone holding $500,000 in muni bonds at a 4% yield has $20,000 of IRMAA-counted income they might not be mentally tracking.

Forgetting that both spouses' income counts on a joint return. IRMAA thresholds for joint filers ($212,000 base) are twice the single threshold ($106,000), which is actually favorable — but both spouses' income flows into the same MAGI number. A couple where both are physicians or both have large RMDs can hit IRMAA brackets even though each individual income would be below the single-filer threshold.

Not filing SSA-44 after retirement. This is probably the most common and costly mistake. Someone retires at 66, works at a high salary through mid-year, files their taxes showing high income, then gets hit with IRMAA for the next year based on that final working-year income. They're now living on significantly lower income but paying IRMAA as if the high income continues. The SSA-44 fix is right there — most people just don't know it exists.

faq.question: What is IRMAA and who has to pay it?
faq.answer: IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) is a surcharge added to standard Medicare Part B and Part D premiums for higher-income beneficiaries. In 2026, it applies to single filers with 2024 MAGI above $106,000 and joint filers above $212,000. About 7-8% of Medicare beneficiaries pay IRMAA.

faq.question: What income does SSA use to calculate 2026 IRMAA?
faq.answer: Your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income — that's your 2024 AGI plus tax-exempt interest income. SSA gets this from IRS after you file your 2024 tax return.

faq.question: What are the 2026 IRMAA income thresholds?
faq.answer: For single filers: $106,000 (bracket 1), $133,000 (bracket 2), $167,000 (bracket 3), $200,000 (bracket 4), $500,000 (bracket 5). For joint filers: double those first four thresholds, with the top bracket at $750,000. The brackets are cliffs — one dollar over triggers the full surcharge for that tier.

faq.question: Does Roth IRA conversion income count toward IRMAA?
faq.answer: Yes. Roth conversion income is included in your AGI in the year converted, which rolls into MAGI. That elevated MAGI then sets your Medicare premiums two years later. Roth conversion amounts should be sized to avoid crossing IRMAA thresholds where possible.

faq.question: How do I appeal my IRMAA determination?
faq.answer: File Form SSA-44 after receiving your IRMAA determination letter. The form is available at SSA.gov. You must have experienced a qualifying life-changing event — retirement, work reduction, marriage, divorce, death of spouse, loss of pension, or loss of income-producing property. Bring documentation of the event and your current/estimated income to your local SSA office.

faq.question: Can IRMAA apply even if I'm not collecting Social Security?
faq.answer: Yes. If you haven't started collecting Social Security, SSA will bill you directly for the IRMAA surcharge rather than deducting it from a benefit payment. Same surcharge, just a different collection method.

faq.question: Do Medicare Advantage plans avoid IRMAA?
faq.answer: No. IRMAA is tied to Part B enrollment, and Medicare Advantage beneficiaries still pay the Part B premium (and IRMAA surcharge if applicable) in addition to any plan premium. There's no way to avoid IRMAA by switching to MA — you have to address the income side.

faq.question: What if I get a large one-time payment that pushes me into IRMAA?
faq.answer: If it's from a qualifying life-changing event (like an employer settlement payment), you can potentially appeal. If it's a one-time capital gain, stock sale, or other non-qualifying income, you'll pay the IRMAA surcharge for the two years it affects. Planning the timing of large income events before they happen is the only real mitigation.

title: Medicare Savings Programs 2026: QMB, SLMB, QI and QDWI Explained
metaDescription: Four Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate or dramatically reduce what you pay for Medicare in 2026. Full income and asset limits for QMB, SLMB, QI, and QDWI — plus how to apply, what each covers, and how enrollment automatically triggers Extra Help for drug costs.
h1: Medicare Savings Programs 2026: QMB, SLMB, QI and QDWI Explained
summary: Medicare costs are brutal at the low end of the income spectrum. A $202.90 monthly Part B premium is one thing when you have a pension and investments. It's a different thing entirely when you're living on $1,200/month

sections.heading: The Programs Nobody Tells You About
sections.content:
Medicare costs are brutal at the low end of the income spectrum. A $202.90 monthly Part B premium is one thing when you have a pension and investments. It's a different thing entirely when you're living on $1,200/month in Social Security.

Medicare Savings Programs exist specifically for this situation. They're state-administered programs, funded jointly by states and the federal government, that pay some or all of your Medicare costs. The four programs — QMB, SLMB, QI, and QDWI — form a stack organized by income level, each covering a different slice of Medicare expenses.

Here's the thing that should frustrate you: somewhere between 30 and 50 percent of people who qualify for these programs aren't enrolled in them. Some don't know the programs exist. Some think they won't qualify. Some applied years ago, got rejected for one reason, and never went back. The income limits are meaningfully higher than people expect — in 2026, a single person earning up to $1,350/month can qualify for QMB, which covers not just the premium but all Medicare deductibles and cost-sharing. That's real money.

If you or a family member is on Medicare with limited income, reading this section carefully could be worth thousands of dollars per year. And the application process, while bureaucratic, is not complicated.

sections.heading: QMB: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary — The Most Comprehensive Program
sections.content:
QMB is the most valuable of the four programs and covers the widest range of Medicare costs. If you qualify for QMB, Medicaid pays:

- Your Medicare Part A premium (if you'd otherwise have to pay it — most people get Part A free, but some higher-income beneficiaries who didn't work long enough pay up to $518/month in 2026)
- Your Medicare Part B premium ($202.90/month)
- Medicare Part A deductible ($1,676 per benefit period in 2026)
- Medicare Part B deductible ($283/year)
- Medicare Part A and Part B coinsurance and copayments

That last point is significant. Under QMB, providers are prohibited from billing you for Medicare cost-sharing. If you're hospitalized, your QMB status means you owe nothing out of pocket for services Medicare covers — no deductible, no 20% coinsurance. Providers who bill QMB enrollees for Medicare cost-sharing are violating federal law.

2026 income limits for QMB (48 contiguous states and DC):
- Individual: $1,350/month gross income
- Couple: $1,824/month gross income

Note: SSA applies a $20 general income disregard when calculating countable income, so the effective limits are slightly higher. Alaska and Hawaii have higher limits.

2026 asset/resource limits for QMB:
- Individual: $9,950
- Couple: $14,910

Important: as of January 2026, about a dozen states have eliminated asset limits for MSPs entirely — including Alabama, Arizona, Connecticut, Delaware, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Mississippi, New Mexico, New York, Oregon, Vermont, and DC. If you're in one of those states, assets don't disqualify you.

Countable assets include: bank accounts, stocks, bonds, mutual funds, second homes, most retirement accounts. Excluded: your primary home, one car, personal belongings, life insurance with cash value under $1,500, and burial funds.

sections.heading: SLMB: Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary
sections.content:
SLMB covers one thing and one thing only: your Medicare Part B premium. But that one thing is $202.90/month — $2,434.80/year — paid directly by the state to Medicare on your behalf. Your Part A premium (if you'd otherwise owe it), deductibles, and cost-sharing are not covered under SLMB.

SLMB is designed for people with income above the QMB limit but still modest — roughly 100% to 120% of the federal poverty level.

2026 income limits for SLMB:
- Individual: $1,616/month
- Couple: $2,184/month

2026 asset/resource limits:
- Same as QMB: $9,950 individual, $14,910 couple (unless your state has eliminated asset limits)

If your income is between $1,350 and $1,616/month as a single person, SLMB is likely your MSP. It won't cover your deductibles or copays, but eliminating the $202.90 Part B premium can free up meaningful cash each month.

SLMB enrollment also automatically qualifies you for Extra Help (also called Low Income Subsidy or LIS) on prescription drugs — the same Extra Help that comes with QMB. That benefit is discussed in detail below, but the quick version: Extra Help eliminates the Part D deductible and caps drug copays at a few dollars per prescription.

sections.heading: QI: Qualifying Individual
sections.content:
The Qualifying Individual program is the third tier — it covers all or most of your Part B premium for people with income slightly above the SLMB range.

2026 income limits for QI:
- Individual: up to approximately $1,816/month
- Couple: up to approximately $2,455/month

QI covers the Medicare Part B premium ($202.90/month) but doesn't cover deductibles or cost-sharing — same premium coverage as SLMB but extended to a higher income range.

There are two practical differences between QI and SLMB worth knowing:

First, QI is funded differently. It's a federal block grant program rather than an entitlement. States have limited funding for QI each year, and enrollment is first-come, first-served. If you qualify for QI but apply late in the year, you might be waitlisted. Apply early.

Second, QI applications do not carry over automatically from year to year. Even if you were enrolled in QI last year, you must reapply every year. SLMB and QMB also require annual redeterminations, but QI's non-entitlement status makes this especially important to stay on top of.

Like SLMB, QI enrollment triggers automatic Extra Help enrollment for Part D drug costs.

sections.heading: QDWI: Qualified Disabled and Working Individuals
sections.content:
The fourth program — QDWI — targets a narrow but important population: people under 65 who have a disability, are working, have lost premium-free Part A coverage, and have low income.

When someone with a disability returns to work, they can lose premium-free Medicare Part A after a transition period. Without QDWI, they'd have to pay up to $518/month for Part A. QDWI covers that Part A premium.

2026 income limits for QDWI:
- Individual: up to about $4,946/month (roughly 200% of FPL)
- Couple: up to about $6,659/month

Asset limits are higher than the other MSPs — $4,000 for individuals and $6,000 for couples — but the higher income limits reflect that working people with disabilities often earn more than retirees on fixed income.

QDWI does NOT trigger automatic Extra Help enrollment, unlike QMB, SLMB, and QI. If you're in QDWI and need help with drug costs, you'd apply for Extra Help separately through SSA.

The QDWI population is smaller than the other MSPs but the financial impact is significant — $518/month in Part A premiums for someone with a disability who's trying to maintain employment is a genuine barrier to work. QDWI removes it.

sections.heading: Extra Help / LIS: The Automatic Enrollment Nobody Expects
sections.content:
This might be the most underappreciated benefit in the entire Medicare system.

Enrollment in QMB, SLMB, or QI automatically qualifies you for Extra Help, the federal Low Income Subsidy (LIS) for Medicare Part D prescription drug costs. You don't apply separately. SSA automatically enrolls you when your state MSP enrollment is reported.

What Extra Help does in 2026:
- Eliminates the Part D deductible (normally up to $590 in 2026)
- Caps copayments at $4.90 for generic drugs and $12.15 for brand-name drugs
- Eliminates the Part D coverage gap ('donut hole') — though this is less relevant after the IRA eliminated the gap in 2025
- Ensures you qualify for a benchmark plan with no premium

For someone on multiple medications, this benefit is enormous. A person taking three branded medications who would otherwise hit the deductible and face standard cost-sharing could easily save $200-$400/month on drug costs with Extra Help.

You can also apply for Extra Help independently of MSP enrollment — directly through SSA — if your income and assets qualify but you don't meet MSP income limits. Extra Help has its own income threshold (roughly 150% of FPL) and asset limits ($16,590 individual, $33,100 married for the full subsidy in 2026).

If you're on Extra Help, SSA will auto-enroll you in a benchmark Part D plan during open enrollment if you don't choose one yourself. You can change plans during the annual enrollment period to find one that best covers your specific medications.

sections.heading: How to Apply: The Actual Process
sections.content:
MSP applications go to your state Medicaid agency — not to Medicare, not to SSA. This trips people up constantly. Medicare.gov will tell you to contact your state, but doesn't always make it obvious what that means in practice.

For most states, there are three application channels:

Option 1: Apply online through your state Medicaid portal. Most states now have online applications. A quick search for '[your state] Medicare Savings Program application' should get you there. This is often the fastest path.

Option 2: Apply by phone with your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP). SHIP is a federally funded counseling program in every state — free counseling from trained specialists who will help you complete the application. Find your local SHIP through Medicare.gov/supplemental-insurance or by calling 1-800-Medicare.

Option 3: Apply in person at your local Medicaid or social services office. Bring: Medicare card, Social Security card, proof of income (Social Security award letter, pension statement, recent bank statements), proof of resources (bank statements, investment account statements), and photo ID.

What to expect: processing typically takes 30-45 days. If approved, your state will notify Medicare directly and begin paying your premiums. For Part B specifically, your premium deduction from Social Security will stop — you'll see the $202.90 (or appropriate amount) return to your Social Security payment. States can also provide retroactive coverage for the three months prior to your application date in some cases.

If you're denied: request a fair hearing. Common denial reasons include income calculation errors (they may not have applied the $20 income disregard), asset miscounting (your home is excluded), or missing documentation. Many initial denials on MSP applications are reversed on appeal.

sections.heading: State Variations That Actually Matter
sections.content:
MSPs are federally defined in their basic structure, but states have significant flexibility to be more generous — and many are.

Some states have expanded MSP income limits above the federal minimums. New York, for example, has historically had higher income limits. California has its own expansion. If you're near a threshold, it's worth checking your specific state's limits rather than assuming the federal floor applies.

The asset limit situation is genuinely significant. Twelve states and DC currently have NO asset test for MSP eligibility — they look only at income. If you're in one of these states and have significant savings or a second vehicle but low income, you may qualify for MSP even though you'd be disqualified under the federal asset limits. Those states include Alabama, Arizona, Connecticut, Delaware, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Mississippi, New Mexico, New York, Oregon, Vermont, and DC.

Some states automatically enroll QMB-eligible beneficiaries — they identify people who would qualify based on existing Medicaid data and enroll them without a separate application. Others require you to affirmatively apply. This disparity in automatic enrollment is one reason millions of eligible people are unenrolled.

For dual-eligible beneficiaries — people on both Medicare and Medicaid — much of this is handled automatically, but it's worth verifying that your QMB status is being applied correctly to billing. Complaints about improper billing of QMB enrollees are not uncommon; knowing your rights matters.

faq.question: What is the income limit for Medicare Savings Programs in 2026?
faq.answer: It depends on the program. QMB: $1,350/month individual, $1,824/month couple. SLMB: $1,616/month individual, $2,184/month couple. QI: approximately $1,816/month individual, $2,455/month couple. All figures include a $20 income disregard and are for the 48 contiguous states — Alaska and Hawaii are higher.

faq.question: What does QMB actually cover?
faq.answer: QMB covers your Part B premium ($202.90/month), Part A premium if applicable, the Part B deductible ($283), the Part A inpatient deductible ($1,676/benefit period), and all Part A and B coinsurance and copayments. Providers cannot legally bill you for Medicare cost-sharing if you're in QMB.

faq.question: Does SLMB cover anything besides the Part B premium?
faq.answer: No — SLMB pays the Part B premium only. It doesn't cover the deductible, coinsurance, or Part A costs. But SLMB enrollment automatically qualifies you for Extra Help on Part D drug costs, which can be extremely valuable.

faq.question: What assets are counted for Medicare Savings Program eligibility?
faq.answer: Bank accounts, stocks, bonds, mutual funds, additional real estate, and most retirement accounts. Your primary home, one car, personal property, and small life insurance policies are excluded. In 2026, about 12 states and DC have eliminated asset limits entirely — in those states, only income matters.

faq.question: Does enrolling in a Medicare Savings Program automatically enroll me in Extra Help?
faq.answer: Yes, for QMB, SLMB, and QI enrollment. You automatically qualify for the full Extra Help (LIS) benefit on Part D drug costs without a separate application. QDWI does not trigger automatic Extra Help enrollment.

faq.question: Where do I apply for Medicare Savings Programs?
faq.answer: Through your state Medicaid agency — not through Medicare or SSA. Search for your state's Medicaid office or contact your local SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program) for free help with the application. The Medicare.gov website has a 'Get Help Paying Costs' section that links to state contacts.

faq.question: Can I apply for Medicare Savings Programs if I'm on Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: Yes. MSP eligibility is based on Medicare enrollment and income/assets, not on whether you're in Original Medicare or Medicare Advantage. If you're on MA and qualify for QMB, your state pays your Part B premium and you'd still owe nothing for Medicare-covered services under a QMB-accepting plan.

faq.question: What if I'm denied for a Medicare Savings Program?
faq.answer: Request a fair hearing. Denial reasons often include income calculation errors (the $20 income disregard not applied), incorrect asset counting, or missing documentation. Bring complete documentation to the hearing — many initial denials are overturned on appeal. SHIP counselors can help you through this process at no charge.

title: Best Health Insurance Companies 2026: Ranked and Reviewed
metaDescription: Kaiser, UHC, BCBS, Cigna, Aetna, Molina, Ambetter, Oscar — ranked for 2026 by NCQA ratings, network size, marketplace availability, and customer experience. What each carrier does well, where they fall short, and who they're actually right for.
h1: Best Health Insurance Companies 2026: Ranked and Reviewed
summary: Best is a loaded word in health insurance. Best for a 28-year-old in Austin shopping on HealthCare.gov is a completely different answer than best for a 62-year-old retired federal employee in suburban Maryland. Best for

sections.heading: How We're Actually Measuring 'Best'
sections.content:
Best is a loaded word in health insurance. Best for a 28-year-old in Austin shopping on HealthCare.gov is a completely different answer than best for a 62-year-old retired federal employee in suburban Maryland. Best for someone managing a chronic condition looks nothing like best for someone who just needs catastrophic coverage.

So here's the methodology being used for 2026:

NCQA Health Plan Ratings are the most credible third-party quality measure in the industry — they score commercial, Medicare Advantage, and Medicaid plans on HEDIS clinical quality measures, CAHPS member satisfaction surveys, and accreditation status. Nearly 1,000 plans were rated for the 2025 cycle (which informs 2026 carrier assessments). A score above 4.0 is strong; 4.5 or higher puts you in the top tier nationally.

Marketplace presence matters for anyone shopping on HealthCare.gov or a state exchange — some insurers are only available in select markets. Network breadth matters because a plan with low premiums but a narrow network that excludes your doctors isn't actually cheap.

Price competitiveness and plan variety matter because they determine real-world affordability for the range of buyers.

And financial stability — AM Best or Moody's ratings — matters because you're entering a contract with this company that should last a year, and you want them to be there when you need them.

With that framing, here are the eight carriers worth knowing in 2026.

sections.heading: Kaiser Permanente: The Gold Standard
sections.content:
Kaiser has held the #1 spot in most credible health plan rankings for years running. Their NCQA score sits at 4.59, and both Northern and Southern California plans earned 5-star ratings. For the ninth consecutive NCQA ratings cycle, Kaiser has more 5-star and 4.5-star plans combined than any other healthcare organization.

Why Kaiser consistently tops the rankings comes down to its integrated model. Kaiser isn't just an insurer — it's an integrated health system. Kaiser doctors are Kaiser employees. Kaiser hospitals are Kaiser hospitals. The insurer and the care delivery system are the same entity, which eliminates the information asymmetry and adversarial dynamics that create friction in most insurance relationships. Your doctor's incentives are aligned with your health outcomes in a way that's structurally impossible with an insurer contracting with an independent provider network.

The result shows in outcomes data: preventive care rates, chronic disease management, member satisfaction — Kaiser consistently outperforms on all of them.

The catch is availability. Kaiser operates in California, Colorado, Georgia, Hawaii, Maryland/Virginia/DC, Oregon/Washington, and a few other markets. If you're not in one of those regions, Kaiser isn't an option. And even in Kaiser markets, if you want to see providers outside the Kaiser system, you're largely out of luck — it's a closed network by design.

For people in Kaiser markets who are comfortable with the integrated model: this is the standard to beat. For everyone else: the next options.

sections.heading: Blue Cross Blue Shield: The Everywhere Option
sections.content:
BCBS isn't a single company — it's a federation of 33 independent regional carriers operating under a common brand and a shared national network access agreement. That distinction matters when assessing quality, because the Horizon BCBS of New Jersey that topped the J.D. Power rankings for member satisfaction in 2026 is operationally separate from BCBS of Mississippi.

What the BCBS brand consistently delivers is breadth. The BlueCard network gives BCBS members access to providers in all 50 states — useful for anyone who travels frequently, works remotely across state lines, or wants the ability to get in-network care anywhere. For employer-sponsored coverage, BCBS plans remain among the most common offerings because HR departments value the administrative simplicity of a carrier that works everywhere.

On the ACA marketplace, BCBS affiliates are present in most states with competitive tier options from Bronze through Platinum. PPO plans are more consistently available through BCBS than most competitors, which matters for people who value specialist access without referrals.

NCQA scores vary significantly by BCBS affiliate — some regional plans score 4.0+, others are below average. If you're evaluating a BCBS plan, look up the specific regional carrier's NCQA score rather than assuming the national brand quality applies. The brand guarantees access more reliably than it guarantees quality.

Best for: people who want maximum provider choice, PPO access, or who frequently need care in multiple states.

sections.heading: UnitedHealthcare: Scale and Technology
sections.content:
UHC is the largest health insurer in the United States by revenue and membership. Scale has real advantages — UHC's negotiated rates with providers, its pharmacy benefit management, and its claims processing infrastructure are industry-leading in some respects. They're also investing heavily in digital health technology, member apps, and AI-driven care management.

In 2026, UHC faces some significant headwinds in terms of public perception — the company has been under scrutiny for claims denial practices and algorithm-based utilization management. Whether that affects your actual experience depends heavily on your plan type, your state, and whether you're dealing with routine or complex claims.

For employer-sponsored coverage, UHC remains a top-three choice for large employers. Their network — especially for specialists and major academic medical centers — is extensive. If you're in a major metro area, you probably won't have network access issues.

On the individual marketplace, UHC's participation varies by state — they pulled back from many ACA markets in 2016-2017 and have been gradually re-entering. Check your specific market for availability.

Medicare Advantage: UHC is one of the top MA carriers by enrollment, with a wide range of plan options. NCQA scores for UHC MA plans vary by market.

Best for: large employer group plans where UHC is offered, people who need broad specialist network access in major metros.

sections.heading: Kaiser, Cigna, Aetna: The Clinical and Commercial Middle Tier
sections.content:
Cigna and Aetna (now owned by CVS Health) are large national carriers that perform consistently but rarely top the rankings in any single dimension.

Cigna's strength is its behavioral health network and its international coverage — for employers with globally mobile workforces, Cigna is frequently the go-to. On the individual market, Cigna participates in a moderate number of states with competitive pricing. Their NCQA scores for commercial plans hover around 3.5-4.0 in most markets. One specific advantage: Cigna's customer service ratings tend to be above industry average, with faster claims processing and lower complaint rates in several markets.

Aetna, post-CVS merger, has leaned into its pharmacy vertical — CVS locations function as care hubs, and Aetna members get some specific advantages around pharmacy access and MinuteClinic services. That integration is genuinely useful for people managing chronic conditions who do a lot of their healthcare through pharmacy-based services. NCQA scores are competitive in most markets. Individual market presence is solid in most states where Aetna competes. Financial stability is strong — backed by CVS's balance sheet.

For both Cigna and Aetna: quality is acceptable to good, networks are competitive in major markets, and you're unlikely to have a dramatically bad experience. They're solid choices when Kaiser isn't available and BCBS is more expensive in your market.

sections.heading: Molina Healthcare: The Medicaid-to-Marketplace Specialist
sections.content:
Molina operates in about 15 states, with a particular focus on Medicaid managed care and ACA marketplace plans for low-to-moderate income populations. If you qualify for Medicaid or have income between 100-250% of FPL shopping on HealthCare.gov, Molina is frequently one of the most competitive premium options available.

The model is built around serving the cost-sensitive buyer. Molina plans consistently offer lower premiums than national competitors in the markets where they operate. They've built care coordination systems specifically for populations with higher rates of chronic disease and less healthcare utilization experience.

The trade-off is network. Molina networks are narrower than national PPO carriers — the premium savings come partly from contracting with a more limited set of providers at lower rates. In urban markets, this usually isn't a practical issue. In more rural areas or for anyone who wants access to specific specialists or academic medical centers, Molina's network may not deliver.

For 2026: Molina's marketplace presence is expanding. They're available in California, Florida, Michigan, Missouri, Nevada, New Mexico, New York, Ohio, South Carolina, Texas, Utah, Virginia, Washington, and Wisconsin. Premium competitiveness in these markets is consistently strong, especially for people with subsidy-eligible income.

Best for: subsidy-eligible buyers prioritizing lower premiums, people who primarily use primary care and urgent care rather than specialist-heavy care.

sections.heading: Ambetter and Oscar: The New-Generation Marketplace Carriers
sections.content:
Ambetter (operated by Centene, one of the largest Medicaid managed care companies) competes in 29 states on the ACA marketplace. Like Molina, their model is premium-competitive with narrower networks — they've built a large marketplace book of business largely by being consistently among the lowest-premium options in their markets.

Ambetter's specific advantages include wellness programs with financial incentives (MyHealthPays rewards can credit up to a few hundred dollars per year for completing preventive care and health engagement activities) and competitive Bronze and Silver tier pricing. They're a significant presence in states like Texas, Florida, and the Southeast generally.

The customer service experience with Ambetter gets mixed reviews — claims processing complaints and prior authorization friction are higher than national average in some markets. For routine primary and preventive care, the experience is generally fine. For complex claims, some beneficiaries report significant friction.

Oscar Health takes a different approach. Launched in 2012 as a tech-first insurer, Oscar built from the ground up around a digital member experience — app-based care management, free virtual primary care visits, physician-matching, and concierge team navigation. In 2026, Oscar operates in about 20 states.

Oscar's premiums run cheaper than average in most markets. The virtual primary care model — unlimited free video visits with a primary care physician — is a genuine differentiator for people comfortable with telehealth. The technology is legitimately good compared to legacy carriers. In more rural areas where in-person network access is thinner, the telehealth-first model is especially valuable.

Best for Ambetter: cost-conscious buyers in markets where Ambetter is available, particularly in the South and Southeast. Best for Oscar: tech-comfortable buyers who use telehealth heavily, younger enrollees, people in rural areas.

sections.heading: How to Actually Choose for Your Situation
sections.content:
Rankings and reviews matter, but the real question is which carrier is best for you specifically. A few factors to actually work through:

Check your doctors first. Before you get excited about premiums, verify that your primary care physician and any specialists you see regularly are in-network for the plans you're considering. Most carriers have provider search tools on their websites. This step alone eliminates a lot of options.

Understand HMO vs. PPO vs. EPO structure. An HMO requires a primary care physician and referrals for specialists, with no out-of-network coverage except emergencies. A PPO gives you freedom to see any provider but costs more. An EPO (used by several marketplace carriers) combines some PPO flexibility with the narrower network of an HMO — no referrals required, but out-of-network care isn't covered. Know what you're buying.

For marketplace plans, run your actual subsidy-adjusted premiums. The carrier with the lowest unsubsidized premium isn't necessarily the best value after tax credits. A Silver plan from a more expensive carrier might actually cost you less after subsidies than a cheap Bronze plan, and give you better actuarial value.

If you have prescriptions, check the formulary. Every plan has a drug formulary — the list of covered drugs and their tier (and thus cost). Before enrolling, verify your specific medications are on the formulary at a tier you can afford.

For anyone with a chronic condition or anticipating significant medical care in the year: run the numbers on annual out-of-pocket maximums, not just premiums. A plan with a $200 higher monthly premium but $3,000 lower out-of-pocket maximum looks very different if you're going to hit that maximum.

faq.question: Which health insurance company is rated #1 for 2026?
faq.answer: Kaiser Permanente consistently holds the top position in NCQA Health Plan Ratings, with an overall score of 4.59 and five-star ratings on multiple commercial and Medicare Advantage plans. Kaiser has held more 5-star and 4.5-star plans than any other organization for nine consecutive NCQA rating cycles. The caveat is geographic availability — Kaiser only operates in certain states.

faq.question: What does NCQA rating mean for health insurance?
faq.answer: NCQA (National Committee for Quality Assurance) rates health plans on clinical quality measures (HEDIS), member satisfaction surveys (CAHPS), and accreditation status. Ratings run from 0-5 stars. A score above 4.0 indicates strong quality; 4.5+ puts a plan in the top ~6.5% nationally. It's one of the most credible third-party quality indicators in the industry.

faq.question: Is Oscar Health a real insurance company or just an app?
faq.answer: Oscar is a licensed health insurer operating in about 20 states, not just an app. They're a fully risk-bearing carrier. The technology-first model — digital care management, free virtual primary care, concierge navigation — is the differentiator, but they're paying claims and taking on insurance risk like any traditional carrier. AM Best rates Oscar's financial strength as acceptable.

faq.question: Is Molina or Ambetter good insurance?
faq.answer: Both are legitimate, regulated insurers that work well for cost-conscious buyers with moderate healthcare utilization. Their premium competitiveness is genuine. The trade-off is narrower provider networks than national PPO carriers. For routine primary and preventive care, both work fine. For complex specialist access or care at major academic medical centers, check the specific network in your area before enrolling.

faq.question: What's the difference between an HMO and PPO for health insurance?
faq.answer: An HMO requires you to choose a primary care physician who coordinates your care and provides referrals for specialists. You generally can't see out-of-network providers except in emergencies. A PPO lets you see any provider in or out of network without referrals, with higher cost-sharing for out-of-network care. PPOs offer more flexibility at higher premiums; HMOs are more restrictive but generally less expensive.

faq.question: Can I switch health insurance companies if I'm unhappy mid-year?
