If your health has changed significantly since your original Medigap enrollment, that switch may not be available to you.

sections.heading: Who Should Consider Mutual of Omaha vs. Who Should Shop Harder
sections.content:
The honest take on who Mutual of Omaha Medigap makes sense for:

Couples where both spouses are enrolling simultaneously and qualify for the household discount — the combined savings narrows the price gap with competitors and the combined peace-of-mind of one trusted carrier is real.

Beneficiaries who prioritize clean, fast claims payment and low complaint rates over premium optimization — Mutual of Omaha's operational quality is among the best in the Medigap market.

People who are already familiar with Mutual of Omaha from life insurance or other products and have an established relationship — agent knowledge and policy consolidation have value.

Beneficiaries in states where Mutual of Omaha's rate increase history has been moderate — not all states have seen the aggressive increases Texas experienced. Check your state's history.

Who should absolutely comparison shop before defaulting to Mutual of Omaha:

Single enrollees without a household discount — you're paying the full premium differential without the offset.

Beneficiaries in states with recent large Mutual of Omaha rate increases — the current premium is less important than the trajectory.

Healthy 65-year-olds who are very price-sensitive and understand they can potentially switch carriers later (during a Special Enrollment Period) if a cheaper competitor's rate increases become favorable by comparison.

Bottom line: Mutual of Omaha is a legitimate, financially strong, low-complaint Medigap carrier. It is not the cheapest option in most markets, and the premium gap is real enough to matter over a 10-20 year Medigap relationship. Run the numbers with the household discount applied, check the state-specific rate increase history, and make an informed comparison — don't just sign up because the name is familiar.

faq.question: Is Mutual of Omaha's Medigap Plan G worth the higher premium?
faq.answer: It depends on your situation. Their Plan G premiums run roughly 42% above the cheapest alternative on average, but the household discount and their low complaint rates are genuine differentiators. For couples enrolling together or beneficiaries prioritizing operational quality over pure premium savings, the premium gap can be justified. For price-sensitive single enrollees, comparison shopping is essential.

faq.question: What is Mutual of Omaha's financial strength rating?
faq.answer: Mutual of Omaha holds an A (Superior) rating from A.M. Best — a strong rating that indicates solid long-term financial health. This is meaningful for a Medigap policy you may hold for 20+ years. However, several major competitors (UnitedHealthcare, BCBS affiliates) hold comparable or slightly higher ratings.

faq.question: How does the Mutual of Omaha household discount work?
faq.answer: Up to 12% off each policy when two household members both hold Mutual of Omaha Medigap coverage. In most states both members must have Mutual of Omaha policies to qualify. For couples enrolling together, this discount meaningfully narrows the price gap versus cheaper alternatives.

faq.question: What are Mutual of Omaha's recent rate increases?
faq.answer: Rate increases have been above-average in several states. Notably, Texas saw a +21.6% filing in June 2025. The national average Medigap rate increase ran about 8% from 2024 to 2025. Rate increase history by state is publicly available through state insurance department filings — checking this data before enrolling is important for long-term cost planning.

faq.question: What's the difference between Plan G and Plan N?
faq.answer: Plan G covers everything except the annual Part B deductible ($257 in 2026). Plan N is similar but doesn't cover Part B excess charges and requires $20 copays for office visits and $50 copays for ER visits. Plan N premiums are lower. For most beneficiaries whose doctors accept Medicare assignment (the majority), Plan N's coverage gaps rarely cost them anything in practice.

faq.question: Can I be denied Medigap coverage from Mutual of Omaha?
faq.answer: Not during your Medigap Open Enrollment Period (the six months starting when you're 65 and enrolled in Medicare Part B). Outside that window, Mutual of Omaha will underwrite your application. Depending on your health history, you could face a waiting period for pre-existing conditions (up to six months) or denial of coverage.

faq.question: Is Plan N a good alternative to Plan G?
faq.answer: For many beneficiaries, yes. If you're in a market where your primary care physicians and specialists accept Medicare assignment (as most do), the Part B excess charge gap in Plan N rarely costs you anything. You save $20-40/month versus Plan G, and the practical coverage difference is minimal for typical usage patterns.

title: Devoted Health Medicare Review 2026
metaDescription: Devoted Health isn't a household name yet, but it's building something genuinely different in Medicare Advantage — a concierge model at scale with above-average star ratings, $0-premium plans, and an aggressive expansion into 29 states for 2026. Here's who it makes sense for and where it still falls short.
h1: Devoted Health Medicare Review 2026
summary: Devoted Health was founded in 2017 by Todd Park and Ed Park — both veterans of healthcare data and technology — with a premise that sounds obvious when you say it out loud: Medicare Advantage members are mostly older

sections.heading: Devoted Health in 2026: The Medicare Advantage Upstart That Kept Growing
sections.content:
Devoted Health was founded in 2017 by Todd Park and Ed Park — both veterans of healthcare data and technology — with a premise that sounds obvious when you say it out loud: Medicare Advantage members are mostly older adults who need more support navigating a complicated healthcare system, not less. So instead of building call centers optimized to minimize handle time, build a concierge-level service model that actually helps people.

That premise has played out well enough that Devoted is now operating in 29 states for 2026, up from 20 states the year prior. That's a nine-state expansion in a single year, which is aggressive for a Medicare Advantage carrier. It's also the reason their average star ratings have gotten complicated — nine new states mean nine new contracts without enough enrollment history for CMS to assign star ratings, which can drag down the overall picture for new members.

The headline 2026 numbers: Devoted Health Medicare Advantage plans average 4.19 stars out of 5 from CMS — above the industry average of roughly 4.0. About 70% of Devoted's enrolled members are in plans rated 4 stars or above. More than 92% of their non-SNP plans have a $0 monthly consolidated premium in 2026.

Those numbers are genuinely good for a carrier of Devoted's age and size. The limitations — and there are real ones — mostly come down to geography and network.

sections.heading: The Concierge Model: What 'Devoted Guide' Actually Means
sections.content:
Every Devoted Health member gets access to what they call Devoted Guides — US-based customer service agents whose job is not just answering questions but proactively helping members navigate their care. The distinction sounds like marketing but there's something real in the implementation.

Traditional Medicare Advantage customer service is designed around call deflection: keep calls short, resolve tier-one questions quickly, escalate only when unavoidable. Devoted's model is built around the opposite premise. Guides are supposed to have time with members, understand their situation, help them find in-network providers, schedule appointments, understand benefit explanations, and follow up.

The practical impact: Devoted consistently scores above-average on member experience metrics. CMS star ratings weight member experience heavily, and it's not a coincidence that Devoted's ratings trend above the industry average for a company this young.

Where this matters most: Medicare beneficiaries transitioning from Original Medicare to Medicare Advantage for the first time. The shift from the near-unlimited provider access of Original Medicare to the network constraints of a Medicare Advantage HMO is confusing and genuinely consequential. Having a Guide who can explain what's covered, which providers are in-network, and what the prior authorization process looks like for specialist visits — that reduces bad outcomes.

Where it matters less: members who are healthy, rarely use their benefits beyond an annual wellness visit, and primarily value the $0 premium. For that profile, the Guide model is nice to have but not the primary purchase driver.

sections.heading: 2026 Plan Benefits: What You Actually Get
sections.content:
Devoted Health Medicare Advantage plans in 2026 cover Medicare Parts A and B (hospital and medical) plus in most plans Part D prescription drug coverage. Beyond the Medicare basics, here are the supplemental benefits that distinguish Devoted plans in markets where they operate:

Dental: routine dental is included — cleanings, exams, x-rays, and in many plans partial coverage for more extensive dental work. The extent of dental coverage varies by specific plan and state. In some markets Devoted plans cover up to $1,500-2,000/year in dental benefits, which is meaningful given that Original Medicare has zero dental coverage.

Vision: routine eye exams covered, eyewear allowances typically $100-200/year depending on plan. Standard for the Medicare Advantage space but real value versus Original Medicare.

Hearing: routine hearing exams covered, hearing aid allowances in most plans. Again, Original Medicare covers neither.

Fitness Benefits: SilverSneakers or equivalent gym membership access included in most Devoted plans.

Over-the-Counter Allowances: a quarterly OTC allowance ($25-100/quarter depending on plan) for eligible health products available at participating retailers or by mail order. This covers vitamins, first aid supplies, dental hygiene products, and similar items. Small dollar amount but useful.

Food and Home Essentials: some Devoted plans (particularly D-SNP plans for dual-eligible members) include food allowances or home health supply benefits. Not universal across all Devoted plans.

Primary Care Copays: $0 copay for in-network PCP visits in many 2026 plans. Specialist copays vary by plan but tend to run $30-50 for standard specialist visits.

Out-of-Pocket Maximums: in-network OOP maximums for 2026 Devoted plans typically run $4,000-6,700 depending on the specific plan, which is within the Medicare Advantage range. Always compare this number when evaluating plans — a $0-premium plan with a $7,550 OOP max (the federal ceiling for in-network Medicare Advantage) is a different risk proposition than one with a $4,000 cap.

sections.heading: 5-Star Contracts and CMS Ratings: The Full Picture
sections.content:
CMS assigns star ratings to Medicare Advantage plans annually — ratings from 1 to 5 stars based on quality measures, member experience surveys, and administrative metrics. 5-star status is rare and prestigious: in 2026, only 18 plans across all MA carriers in the country achieved 5-star ratings, up from 7 the prior year.

Devoted Health averages 4.19 stars across its rated plans for 2026. That's above the industry average and notable for a carrier that's still relatively young in the market. The 70% enrollment in 4-star-plus plans is a meaningful benchmark — most major national carriers don't hit that threshold.

However, there's an important nuance for 2026 specifically: nine of Devoted's states are new for this contract year. New plan contracts don't have enough member history to receive star ratings from CMS. This means new members in Devoted's nine expansion states are enrolling in plans without a star rating — not a bad rating, but no rating. CMS allows members to disenroll from unrated plans mid-year and select a 5-star plan if one is available in their county, which is worth knowing.

The star rating also matters for a practical reason that doesn't get discussed enough: 5-star plans get a permanent open enrollment period — members can switch into a 5-star plan at any time of year, not just during Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7). If Devoted plans in your market achieve 5-star status, that flexibility has real value.

Devoted's trajectory on star ratings has been upward since their launch. Whether the nine new-state expansions dilute their average ratings in the 2027 cycle remains to be seen.

sections.heading: Premium Structure: The $0 Premium Reality
sections.content:
More than 92% of Devoted's non-SNP Medicare Advantage plans carry a $0 monthly consolidated premium in 2026. That's the headline number and it's real — you pay nothing to Devoted beyond what you're already paying Medicare for Part B coverage ($185/month in 2026 for most beneficiaries).

But $0 premium doesn't mean $0 healthcare costs, and this distinction is important enough that it's worth spending a minute on.

You still pay:
- Your Medicare Part B premium ($185/month in 2026 for standard-income beneficiaries)
- Copays when you use services (typically $0 for PCP, $30-50 for specialists, $250-350 per inpatient hospital day)
- Cost-sharing for prescription drugs (even with Part D included, drug tiers create variable cost-sharing)
- Costs for out-of-network care if you use it (in an HMO, out-of-network except emergencies may not be covered at all)

The $0 premium Devoted plan versus a $0 premium competitor plan comparison needs to be done at the total cost level: compare premiums (both $0), drug formulary (which covers your medications, at what tier), network (does your doctor participate), and out-of-pocket maximum (what's your worst-case annual exposure).

Devoted's $0-premium plans are genuine value. They're not a trick. But the $0 premium being identical across many MA carriers means you're comparing on every other dimension — and network access is where Devoted's geographic limitation creates the most risk for members who travel or have specialists outside the service area.

sections.heading: Market Expansion 2026: What Nine New States Means
sections.content:
Devoted Health added nine states for the 2026 plan year, expanding from 20 to 29 states. The expansion states as of 2026 include markets in the South, Midwest, and Southwest — regions where Medicare Advantage penetration is high and competition among carriers is intense.

Why this matters for potential members:

If you're in an expansion state, Devoted's plans in your county are new. There's no star rating yet because CMS needs enrollment and claims history to assign ratings. There's also less accumulated network development — the provider partnerships, prior authorization processes, and operational infrastructure are newer and potentially less refined than in Devoted's established markets.

That's not necessarily a dealbreaker. Devoted builds on a technology platform that's designed for efficient expansion — they're not standing up paper processes in each new state. But there's a meaningful difference between Devoted's operations in Florida, where they've been operating for several years, versus a market where they launched for the first time in January 2026.

If you're in an established Devoted market (Florida, Texas, Ohio, Indiana, Arizona, and their original footprint): the product is mature, the ratings are real, and the networks are developed. The concierge model has had time to demonstrate results.

If you're in a new 2026 market: ask specific questions. Is your primary care physician in-network? Is your preferred specialist in-network? What's the prior authorization process for your ongoing medications or specialty care? Don't assume network or operational quality based on Devoted's performance in established markets.

sections.heading: Geographic Limitations: The Real Constraint
sections.content:
Twenty-nine states sounds like broad national coverage. But Medicare Advantage plans don't cover states — they cover specific counties within states. Devoted's presence within each state is concentrated in certain metropolitan areas and their surrounding counties, not statewide.

This creates a few categories of people for whom Devoted may not work regardless of how much they like the model:

Rural residents: Medicare Advantage in general has thinner networks in rural areas, and Devoted as a relatively young carrier has prioritized building networks in metro markets. If you're in a rural county, check carefully whether your physicians are in-network before enrolling.

Snowbirds: if you split your time between states — Florida winters, Northeast summers is the classic pattern — an HMO Medicare Advantage plan creates a real problem. You're covered for emergencies anywhere, but for routine care you need to be in your plan's service area. Devoted plans are HMO-structured, which means your PCP, specialist referrals, and elective care need to stay in the network. If you're spending five months in a state where Devoted doesn't operate or where your plan isn't available, you're on emergency-only coverage for half the year.

For snowbirds specifically, either a PPO-structured Medicare Advantage plan or a Medigap with Original Medicare is almost always the better structural solution. The $0 premium of a Devoted HMO is not worth the coverage gap if you're traveling or relocating seasonally.

Frequent travelers or those with specialists in other states: same issue. The HMO structure means care coordination stays within the plan's network. If your oncologist, cardiologist, or other specialist is at a major academic medical center that's not in Devoted's network, that's a non-starter.

This isn't a Devoted-specific problem — it's an HMO Medicare Advantage problem. Devoted just hasn't built a PPO product yet, which limits their addressable market.

sections.heading: Who Devoted Health Is For — and Who Should Look Elsewhere
sections.content:
Devoted's ideal member: someone who lives full-time in a Devoted service area (established market preferred), has a primary care doctor who's in-network, doesn't travel extensively for ongoing specialist care, wants the best customer service in Medicare Advantage, and cares about the supplemental benefits (dental, vision, hearing, fitness, OTC) that Original Medicare doesn't provide. If you want someone to actually help you navigate healthcare rather than just process transactions — Devoted is trying to be that.

The $0 premium combined with genuinely above-average star ratings and a service model that's demonstrably different makes Devoted one of the more interesting Medicare Advantage options in the markets where it's established.

Who should look elsewhere:

Snowbirds and frequent travelers who need care access beyond a single service area. Go PPO or Medigap.

Beneficiaries with complex specialist relationships at academic medical centers or out-of-network facilities. Verify network participation before enrolling — the HMO structure is unforgiving if your key providers aren't in it.

Beneficiaries in Devoted's nine new 2026 expansion states who haven't verified their providers are in-network. The brand's quality in established markets doesn't guarantee the same in markets that launched in January 2026.

Beneficiaries who prioritize unrestricted provider access above everything else. Original Medicare plus a Plan G or Plan N Medigap is a different product than Medicare Advantage entirely — broader access, no network, no referrals, but a significant monthly premium versus $0. The right choice depends on your priorities.

Devoted is building something genuinely good. The concierge model, the tech-forward operations, the above-average ratings — these aren't marketing fabrications. The limitations are real too, and geographic. If you're in the right market with the right provider situation, Devoted deserves a serious look in the 2026 Annual Enrollment Period.

faq.question: What is Devoted Health's CMS star rating for 2026?
faq.answer: Devoted Health averages 4.19 stars out of 5 from CMS for 2026 — above the industry average. About 70% of Devoted's enrolled members are in plans rated 4 stars or above. Nine new states added for 2026 don't yet have star ratings since CMS needs enrollment history to assign them.

faq.question: Does Devoted Health really have $0 premiums?
faq.answer: Yes — more than 92% of Devoted's non-SNP plans have a $0 monthly consolidated premium in 2026. You still pay your Medicare Part B premium ($185/month for most beneficiaries in 2026) and cost-sharing when you use services. The $0 refers to what you pay Devoted on top of Medicare.

faq.question: What is a Devoted Guide?
faq.answer: Devoted Guides are US-based customer service agents whose role is to help members navigate healthcare — finding in-network providers, understanding benefits, scheduling appointments, and following up on care coordination. It's the core of Devoted's concierge model and a meaningful differentiator from call-center-focused competitors.

faq.question: Is Devoted Health available in my state?
faq.answer: Devoted operates in 29 states as of 2026, up from 20 the prior year. However, availability is county-specific within each state — they focus on metropolitan areas. Check Devoted's site or Medicare.gov's plan finder with your zip code to confirm availability and which providers are in-network.

faq.question: Can snowbirds use Devoted Health?
faq.answer: Not ideally. Devoted plans are HMO-structured, which means routine care must stay within the plan's service area. Emergency care is covered anywhere, but if you're spending significant time in a state where Devoted doesn't operate or where your plan isn't available, you'll be on emergency-only coverage for that period. Snowbirds generally need a PPO Medicare Advantage plan or Original Medicare with Medigap.

faq.question: What supplemental benefits do Devoted Health plans include?
faq.answer: Most 2026 plans include dental (exams, cleanings, and varying levels of restorative coverage), vision (routine exams plus eyewear allowance), hearing (exams and hearing aid allowances), fitness benefits (SilverSneakers or equivalent), and an over-the-counter quarterly allowance. Some plans, particularly D-SNP plans for dual-eligible members, include food and home health allowances.

faq.question: How does Devoted compare to other Medicare Advantage carriers?
faq.answer: Devoted's above-average star ratings and concierge service model distinguish it from larger carriers like Humana and UnitedHealthcare. Its major limitation is geographic — 29 states in specific counties versus national coverage. For beneficiaries in Devoted's established markets, it's a genuinely competitive option. In new 2026 expansion markets, more due diligence on network and operational maturity is warranted.

title: Medicare for People with Disabilities: The Complete Guide
metaDescription: If you're under 65 and on SSDI, Medicare works differently than you think. Here's how the 24-month waiting period actually works, who skips it entirely, and what to do about coverage in the meantime.
h1: Medicare for People with Disabilities: The Complete Guide
summary: Here's the thing that trips people up constantly. When Social Security approves your disability claim, you don't get Medicare on day one. You wait. Twenty-four months from the date your SSDI benefits start — not from

sections.heading: The 24-Month Waiting Period Nobody Explains Clearly
sections.content:
Here's the thing that trips people up constantly. When Social Security approves your disability claim, you don't get Medicare on day one. You wait. Twenty-four months from the date your SSDI benefits start — not from when you applied, not from when you were approved, but from when that first benefit check hits.

And that five-month waiting period before SSDI payments begin? That counts separately. So realistically you're looking at 29 months from onset of disability before Medicare kicks in for most people. That's a brutal gap. The Social Security Administration calls your Medicare eligibility start the 25th month of receiving SSDI — because month one starts when benefits start, not the wait before them.

Let's be concrete. Say your disability onset is January 2024. SSA imposes a 5-month waiting period, so benefits start June 2024. Medicare then kicks in after 24 months of receiving benefits — June 2026. That's two and a half years with no Medicare from a program you paid into your entire working life. During that window you're either on your employer's plan, your spouse's plan, ACA marketplace coverage, or you're uninsured. None of those options are free.

What the 24-month clock actually measures: it runs from the first month you're entitled to SSDI cash payments. Doesn't matter if those payments are small. Doesn't matter if they're withheld because of earned income. The clock ticks regardless.

Automatic enrollment is the default once that 24-month mark hits. CMS and SSA coordinate automatically — you should receive your red, white, and blue Medicare card about three months before your coverage actually starts. If you don't, call Social Security at 1-800-772-1213 because you don't want to miss that window.

Part A is premium-free for most people who qualify through SSDI because they (or a spouse) worked enough quarters. Part B costs $202.90 per month in 2026. That gets deducted directly from your SSDI payment — you don't see a separate bill, it just reduces what you receive each month. If your SSDI benefit is small, this can sting.

The hold-harmless rule provides some protection for SSDI recipients: your Social Security benefit can't decrease from one year to the next solely because Part B premiums went up, as long as your COLA increase is less than the premium increase. But that's a partial cushion, not a full one.

sections.heading: ALS: The Waiting Period That Doesn't Exist
sections.content:
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis — ALS, Lou Gehrig's disease — gets completely different treatment from every other disability. Congress eliminated the 24-month waiting period for ALS in 2001, and they also eliminated the 5-month waiting period for SSDI payments themselves.

So if you're diagnosed with ALS and qualify for SSDI, Medicare coverage begins the same month your SSDI benefits start. Day one. Not month 25. Not even month two.

This matters enormously because ALS progresses fast. A 29-month wait would be devastating and in many cases would cover most of a patient's remaining lifespan. The immediate coverage reflects that clinical reality.

For ALS patients, Part A and Part B both begin simultaneously with SSDI entitlement. You should be automatically enrolled — your neurologist's office and the hospital billing departments will expect this coordination. If there's any confusion about your Medicare start date, the answer is: it began when your SSDI did, with no waiting period applied.

One additional thing worth knowing: ALS patients remain eligible for Medicare for their entire life even if they somehow recover enough to work again, though this is extraordinarily rare with ALS. The permanent Medicare entitlement is written into the statute specifically for this diagnosis.

sections.heading: ESRD: Medicare at Any Age, With a Catch
sections.content:
End-Stage Renal Disease gives you Medicare at any age — you don't need to be 65, you don't need to be on SSDI, you don't need any other qualification. But the timing is more complicated than the ALS situation.

For most ESRD patients starting dialysis, Medicare eligibility begins on the first day of the fourth month of regular dialysis. That means months one through three of dialysis, you're typically still on whatever coverage you had before. Month four is when Medicare begins. So there's still a waiting period, just shorter than the 24-month SSDI gap — three months instead of two years.

There are ways to shorten or eliminate that three-month dialysis wait:

Home dialysis training: If you're training to do dialysis at home, Medicare can start the month training begins — no three-month wait.

Expedited dialysis training: Some ESRD patients who meet certain criteria can get Medicare starting their first month of regular dialysis if they complete home training within a specific timeframe.

Kidney transplant: If you receive a kidney transplant without ever going on regular dialysis, Medicare eligibility begins the month you're admitted to the hospital for the transplant.

After a successful transplant, Medicare coverage continues for 36 months after the month of the transplant. This catches a lot of people off guard. If your transplant succeeds and you've been off dialysis for three years, your Medicare based on ESRD ends. You need to either qualify through age (65+) or through SSDI for continuing coverage, or get back onto private insurance. Transplant coordinators at major centers should walk you through this transition planning — ask explicitly.

For ESRD, the enrollment itself isn't fully automatic the way it is for SSDI recipients. You or your dialysis center will typically need to file a CMS-2728 form (the ESRD Medical Evidence Report) with your Medicare contractor. Your dialysis facility's billing staff does this constantly and can guide you, but don't assume it's happening — confirm.

sections.heading: Keeping Employer Coverage During the Waiting Period
sections.content:
This is where people make expensive mistakes. During that 24-month SSDI waiting period, you have real options — and some real traps.

COBRA continuation coverage: If you lose a job because of your disability, you can continue your former employer's group health plan via COBRA for up to 18 months ordinarily. But here's the disability extension most people don't know about: if you're determined disabled by SSA at any point during the first 60 days of COBRA, you can extend COBRA to 29 months total. That's almost exactly long enough to bridge the gap to Medicare. The premium during the disability extension period can go up to 150% of the full cost (vs. 102% for standard COBRA), which is expensive. But 29 months of COBRA timed right can cover that entire waiting period.

Employer group coverage coordination: If you or a spouse is still employed and you're covered under an active employer plan, that employer plan is primary over Medicare once Medicare kicks in — unless the employer has fewer than 100 employees (the cutoff for primary payer determination). Employers with 100+ employees: their group plan pays first, Medicare pays second. Employers with fewer than 100 employees: Medicare pays first.

This coordination rule matters when you're deciding whether to elect Part B immediately or delay it. If you have quality employer coverage and won't actually use Medicare as primary, you might consider delaying Part B enrollment — but only if you're on an active employer plan through current employment. COBRA does NOT count as employer-sponsored coverage for purposes of delaying Part B without penalty. If you're on COBRA and turn 65, you need Part B or you'll face the late enrollment penalty.

Marketplace coverage: For the disability waiting period, ACA marketplace plans are another bridge option. You qualify for a Special Enrollment Period when you lose employer coverage. Premium tax credits are available based on income. The coverage won't be as comprehensive as what Medicare eventually provides, but it keeps you from going bare.

Medicaid is also worth checking during the waiting period. If your income is low enough — which is common on SSDI — you may qualify for Medicaid in your state, which could coordinate with or supplement your coverage during the 24-month gap. Some states have specific Medicaid programs for people with disabilities.

sections.heading: Part B During the Disability Waiting Period: Should You Enroll?
sections.content:
This question comes up constantly and the answer is: it depends on what other coverage you have.

You're not eligible for Part B before your Medicare coverage actually starts — you can't enroll early during the waiting period itself. Part B enrollment starts automatically at month 25 of SSDI, or earlier for ALS and ESRD. But the question people are really asking is: once Medicare does kick in, should I take Part B immediately?

For most SSDI recipients newly entering Medicare, the answer is yes, take Part B. Here's why: if you decline Part B when you're first eligible and try to enroll later, you'll pay a permanent 10% premium penalty for every full 12-month period you were eligible but not enrolled. On a $202.90 base premium, a two-year delay means a $40.58 monthly surcharge for life. That adds up to over $486 per year, forever.

The exception: if you're covered under an active employer plan through current employment (yours or a spouse's), you can delay Part B without penalty and sign up during a Special Enrollment Period when that coverage ends. Document this employer coverage carefully. When you eventually enroll in Part B, you'll need to show evidence of the continuous employer coverage to avoid the penalty.

Part D drug coverage follows similar rules. If you have creditable drug coverage through an employer plan, you can delay Part D without penalty. If you go more than 63 days without creditable drug coverage, you'll start accumulating a Part D late enrollment penalty (1% of the national base beneficiary premium per month uncovered, permanent). In 2026, the national base beneficiary premium is approximately $36.78, so small monthly increases can compound over years.

For SSDI recipients on low incomes, Extra Help (the Low Income Subsidy) program can reduce or eliminate Part D premiums and cost-sharing. Apply through SSA at any Social Security office or online. Many SSDI recipients qualify automatically if they're also on Medicaid.

sections.heading: Medigap Under 65: The State-by-State Lottery
sections.content:
Here's the harsh reality: federal law doesn't require insurers to sell Medigap to anyone under 65. Your guaranteed-issue right to buy Medigap — the right that exists for 65-year-olds during their initial enrollment window — simply doesn't exist at the federal level for people under 65 with disabilities.

Some states have stepped in. But the patchwork is genuinely complicated and changes state to state.

States that require guaranteed-issue Medigap for under-65 Medicare beneficiaries include Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, New York, and others — but the specific plans available and the premium rules vary significantly even within this group.

Massachusetts, Minnesota, and Wisconsin have their own standardized Medigap systems different from the federal A-through-N plan structure, so comparisons get even messier.

States that require at least some under-65 options but with restrictions include California, Colorado, Florida, Illinois, Louisiana, Missouri, New Jersey, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Texas, and Wisconsin. The restrictions vary — some states cap premiums at a multiple of the age-65 rate, some allow insurers to underwrite for pre-existing conditions, some only require a subset of plan types.

Nevada added new protections effective in 2026: premiums for Plans A, B, and D are capped at the age-65 rate for under-65 beneficiaries, and other plans are capped at 200% of the age-65 rate. There was a one-time enrollment window from October 2025 through April 2026 for existing under-65 Medicare beneficiaries in Nevada to enroll without underwriting.

States with no under-65 Medigap requirements: if you're in one of these — and most states fall in this bucket — insurers can refuse to sell you Medigap entirely, or charge you whatever they want. In practice, many do sell under-65 policies but underwrite heavily and charge two to three times the 65-year-old rate.

The big event to watch for: when you turn 65, you get a guaranteed six-month open enrollment period for Medigap regardless of health status. This is the moment to buy. Even if you've been on Medicare since you were 40, that 65th birthday open enrollment window is your clean shot at any Medigap plan at standard rates. Mark it on a calendar three months ahead and start shopping.

For under-65 SSDI recipients in states without protections, Medicare Advantage often becomes the de facto supplemental option. MA plans are guaranteed-issue year-round for people newly eligible for Medicare, regardless of age or health status. The tradeoff is network restrictions and prior authorization requirements that traditional Medicare with Medigap avoids.

sections.heading: What to Do When Medicare Starts: A Practical Checklist
sections.content:
Three months before your Medicare start date, CMS should mail your Medicare card. If it doesn't arrive, call Social Security. Don't wait until the month coverage starts.

Verify your Part A and Part B effective dates. The card shows both. For SSDI recipients, both should start the same month — month 25 of your benefit receipt. If something looks off, call Medicare at 1-800-633-4227.

Decide on Part D before your Medicare start date. You have a 63-day enrollment window. If you miss it, you'll accumulate the late enrollment penalty. Compare plans on Medicare.gov Plan Finder using your actual medication list — the cost differences between plans for the same drug can be hundreds of dollars per year.

If you want Medicare Advantage instead of Original Medicare, your Initial Coverage Election Period for MA begins three months before your Medicare Part A and B effective date and ends three months after. You can switch back to Original Medicare during Annual Enrollment (October 15 - December 7) each year.

Contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) for free one-on-one counseling. These are trained volunteers and counselors who know your state's specific rules. They're not selling you anything. Find your local SHIP at shiphelp.org — every state has one, they're federally funded, and for people with disabilities navigating early Medicare, this is genuinely the most useful resource available.

faq.question: When exactly does Medicare start if I'm approved for SSDI?
faq.answer: Medicare starts on the first day of the 25th month you receive SSDI benefits. Because SSDI itself has a five-month waiting period after disability onset, most people wait about 29 months from the start of their disability before Medicare begins. Your Medicare card should arrive three months before your coverage start date.

faq.question: Does ALS really skip the Medicare waiting period entirely?
faq.answer: Yes. People with ALS get Medicare starting the same month their SSDI benefits begin — no 24-month wait, no five-month SSDI wait either. Both waiting periods are waived by statute for ALS. This has been the law since 2001.

faq.question: I'm on dialysis. When does my Medicare start?
faq.answer: For most people starting regular in-center dialysis, Medicare begins on the first day of the fourth month of dialysis — meaning the first three months you're on dialysis, Medicare hasn't started yet. If you're training for home dialysis, Medicare can start the month training begins, with no three-month wait. A successful kidney transplant starts Medicare the month of hospital admission.

faq.question: Can I buy Medigap if I'm under 65 and on disability Medicare?
faq.answer: Federal law doesn't guarantee it, so it depends entirely on your state. About 26 states have some protections for under-65 Medicare beneficiaries, ranging from full guaranteed-issue rights to requiring at least one plan option. The remaining states allow insurers to deny you or charge very high premiums. When you turn 65, you get a guaranteed six-month Medigap open enrollment window regardless of health.

faq.question: Should I take Part B when Medicare starts if I still have employer coverage?
faq.answer: If you're covered under an active employer group health plan through current employment — yours or a spouse's — you can delay Part B without penalty. But COBRA is NOT active employer coverage for this purpose. If you're on COBRA and Medicare starts, take Part B or you'll accumulate a permanent 10% premium penalty per year of delay.

faq.question: What does Medicare cost per month for SSDI recipients in 2026?
faq.answer: Part A is typically premium-free if you worked enough quarters. Part B is $202.90 per month in 2026, automatically deducted from your SSDI payment. If your income is above $109,000 (single) or $218,000 (joint) based on 2024 tax returns, IRMAA surcharges apply and Part B costs more — up to $689.90 per month.

faq.question: What is Extra Help and do I qualify as an SSDI recipient?
faq.answer: Extra Help (the Low Income Subsidy) reduces or eliminates Part D drug plan premiums and cost-sharing. If you're on both Medicare and Medicaid, you automatically qualify. Otherwise, income and asset limits apply — in 2026, roughly $22,590 for singles or $30,660 for couples in income, with asset limits around $17,220 single/$34,360 couple. Apply through Social Security at ssa.gov.

title: Medicare and TRICARE: How They Work Together
metaDescription: Retired military with both Medicare and TRICARE? Understanding how they coordinate — who pays first, what pharmacy costs, and what happens overseas — can save you thousands and a lot of confusion at the billing desk.
h1: Medicare and TRICARE: How They Work Together
summary: TRICARE for Life isn't a separate insurance plan you apply for. It's automatic coverage that kicks in when you have both Medicare Part A and Part B and you're TRICARE-eligible. There's no premium beyond what you pay for

sections.heading: TRICARE for Life: What It Actually Is
sections.content:
TRICARE for Life isn't a separate insurance plan you apply for. It's automatic coverage that kicks in when you have both Medicare Part A and Part B and you're TRICARE-eligible. There's no premium beyond what you pay for Part B. No enrollment form to fill out. You just need both parts of Medicare active and your TRICARE eligibility intact.

Who qualifies: retired uniformed service members who served at least 20 years, their dependents, and surviving spouses. Medal of Honor recipients get TRICARE at any age regardless of other factors.

The mechanic is straightforward: Medicare acts as the primary payer. TRICARE for Life acts as the secondary payer, picking up most of what Medicare leaves behind — deductibles, copayments, coinsurance. In the ideal scenario where both cover a service, your out-of-pocket cost is zero. Nothing. That's a genuinely remarkable benefit if you understand how to use it.

But here's what gets people: TRICARE for Life requires you to have Medicare Part B enrolled and actively paying premiums. This isn't optional. It's not waivable. It doesn't matter if you're overseas where Medicare is useless. It doesn't matter if you think Medicare Part B provides no value to your situation. You need Part B active to keep TRICARE for Life. Drop Part B, you lose TFL. Miss Part B premiums long enough to lose coverage, you lose TFL. The Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS) coordinates with CMS on this — they can see your Part B status.

In 2026, Part B runs $202.90 per month at standard rates. Think of this as your TFL premium — because it is. The tradeoff is typically very favorable given what TFL provides in return.

WIS (Wisconsin Physicians Service) is the TFL contractor that processes claims for TRICARE for Life. When you get care, providers bill Medicare first. Medicare pays its share and automatically forwards the claim to WPS for TFL processing. In most cases you don't do anything — no manual claim filing for covered services at participating providers.

sections.heading: How the Payment Waterfall Actually Works
sections.content:
Four scenarios, and knowing which one applies to your situation matters.

Scenario 1 — Both Medicare and TRICARE cover the service: Medicare pays first. TRICARE for Life pays the Medicare deductible, copayment, and coinsurance. Your cost: zero. This is the sweet spot and it covers the majority of standard medical services — doctor visits, hospital stays, surgery, labs, imaging, durable medical equipment — when provided by Medicare-participating providers.

Scenario 2 — Medicare covers it but TRICARE doesn't: Medicare pays its standard amount. TRICARE doesn't add anything. You're responsible for Medicare cost-sharing — the Part B 20% coinsurance after the annual deductible ($283 in 2026), or the Part A inpatient deductible ($1,676 in 2026 for days 1-60). This scenario is actually rare for standard medical care because TFL covers most of what Medicare covers.

Scenario 3 — TRICARE covers it but Medicare doesn't: TRICARE becomes the primary payer. You pay TRICARE cost-shares — annual deductible ($162 individual/$328 family in 2026 for TFL), and the standard TRICARE coinsurance rates. This covers services like some preventive care that Medicare doesn't include, or services received at military treatment facilities.

Scenario 4 — Neither covers it: you pay everything out of pocket. Examples include non-covered cosmetic procedures, experimental treatments, or some alternative medicine services.

The TFL catastrophic cap is your safety net: $3,000 per calendar year. Once you hit $3,000 in cost-shares for TRICARE-covered services, TFL covers 100% of covered services for the rest of the calendar year. For people with serious illnesses requiring high utilization, this cap prevents financial catastrophe.

Military Treatment Facilities (MTFs): If you get care at an MTF and you're TFL-eligible, the MTF bills Medicare as primary. If Medicare doesn't cover the service at an MTF, you typically pay nothing out of pocket — TRICARE covers it without cost-shares at an MTF. This makes MTF care extremely attractive when accessible.

sections.heading: The Part B Enrollment Trap: Don't Miss This
sections.content:
Retired military who didn't take Part B at 65 — maybe because they had employer coverage or just didn't know TFL required it — face a compounding problem.

Missing Part B enrollment when first eligible creates the standard late enrollment penalty: 10% per year of delay, permanent, added to your base premium for as long as you have Part B. But it also means you've had a gap in TRICARE for Life coverage during that same period. TFL won't retroactively cover services rendered when Part B wasn't active.

The General Enrollment Period for Part B runs January 1 through March 31 each year, with coverage beginning July 1. That's a four-month delay between when you enroll and when you're actually covered. During that gap, you're without TFL unless you have other coverage.

If you're still on active duty or covered under a spouse's employer plan when you first become eligible for Medicare, you can delay Part B without penalty. You'll have a Special Enrollment Period for Part B when that coverage ends. Document your continuous coverage carefully.

For surviving spouses: if a sponsor dies before enrolling in Part B, the surviving spouse needs to enroll in Medicare at their own age-65 eligibility to maintain TRICARE for Life. TRICARE eligibility based on the sponsor's service continues for the surviving spouse, but the Part B requirement still applies to the surviving spouse specifically — based on their own Medicare eligibility, not the sponsor's.

Sometimes we see people who retired from the military early — say, mid-40s — who are two decades away from Medicare eligibility. They're on TRICARE Prime or TRICARE Select for those intervening years. When they hit 65 and enroll in Medicare, they move to TRICARE for Life automatically. The transition is usually seamless but worth verifying with your TRICARE regional contractor.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Benefits: Where TFL Gets Really Good
sections.content:
The pharmacy benefit is one of TFL's strongest features and it's one that many beneficiaries underutilize.

Three tiers of pharmacy access exist for TFL beneficiaries:

Military pharmacy (MTF): Free. Zero cost for any covered medication filled at a military treatment facility pharmacy. This is the best deal in American healthcare — literally no copay for formulary drugs. Access depends on your proximity to a base and that base's pharmacy capacity.

TRICARE Pharmacy Home Delivery (Express Scripts): Low cost. The formulary tiers starting January 2026 are roughly $0-13 for generics (depending on tier), with brand-name drugs costing more. Home delivery typically provides a 90-day supply per fill. This is the tier most beneficiaries not near a base should be using for maintenance medications.

Retail network pharmacy (Express Scripts network): Slightly higher than home delivery but convenient. Copays changed January 1, 2026, with most beneficiaries paying $1-9 more per prescription than in prior years at retail.

Critical interaction with Medicare Part D: TRICARE pharmacy coverage counts as creditable coverage for Part D purposes. This means you won't accumulate Part D late enrollment penalties while covered by TRICARE. You generally don't need to enroll in a separate Part D plan if TRICARE covers your drugs — but verify your specific medications are on the TRICARE formulary if you have complex drug needs.

If Medicare Part D covers a drug that TRICARE doesn't, the coordination gets complicated. In general, TRICARE for Life doesn't coordinate with Part D — you can't use both for the same prescription. Most TFL beneficiaries stick with TRICARE pharmacy benefits and skip standalone Part D enrollment. If you enroll in a Medicare Advantage plan (which includes drug coverage), TRICARE no longer acts as secondary payer for the same services. Choosing Medicare Advantage effectively suspends TFL — you'd be using MA instead of TFL coordination. Most TRICARE-eligible beneficiaries are better served by Original Medicare plus TFL than by Medicare Advantage.

sections.heading: When to Use Each Plan — The Practical Decision Tree
sections.content:
The honest answer is: most of the time, you don't decide. The claims process decides for you. Medicare-participating providers bill Medicare, Medicare pays, Medicare forwards to WPS, TFL pays the remainder. You get an explanation of benefits. Done.

But knowing when to be proactive helps:

Use a Medicare-participating provider whenever possible. Non-participating providers can charge more than Medicare's approved amount, and TFL won't cover the excess charges above the Medicare fee schedule. Balanced billing by non-participating providers is legal in most states and you can end up owing the difference.

Military Treatment Facilities: go here for routine and preventive care when accessible. Zero cost-sharing, formulary drugs free, and care quality at major MTFs is excellent. The tradeoff is appointment availability — MTFs give priority to active duty.

For emergencies: go to the nearest emergency room. Don't worry about which plan covers it in an emergency. Get care. Claims sort themselves out — Medicare goes primary at any ER.

Non-covered services: some preventive screenings that Medicare doesn't cover (certain dental work, vision exams for glasses, hearing aids) may or may not be covered by TRICARE. Check the TRICARE formulary and benefit handbook before assuming coverage. TFL doesn't cover services that neither Medicare nor TRICARE covers.

For mental health care: both Medicare and TRICARE cover mental health services from participating providers. The coordination works the same way — Medicare primary, TFL secondary. This means essentially no cost-sharing for mental health visits with in-network Medicare providers.

sections.heading: Overseas Coverage: Where TRICARE Fills Medicare's Gap
sections.content:
Medicare has a well-known limitation: it doesn't cover care outside the United States except in very narrow circumstances (emergency care in Canada or Mexico if it's closer than a US hospital, certain Canadian border situations). For active travelers or expats, this is a real gap.

TRICARE fills it — but not identically to domestic coverage.

For TFL beneficiaries living or traveling overseas, TRICARE becomes the primary payer since Medicare can't pay. TRICARE Overseas Program (TOP) handles claims from overseas. You'll typically pay upfront and submit claims for reimbursement rather than the automatic billing that works domestically.

The Part B requirement doesn't go away just because you're overseas. This is the part that frustrates many retirees: you must continue paying Part B premiums even when living abroad where Medicare literally cannot pay for your healthcare. The rationale from the DOD's perspective is that the Part B requirement is a condition of maintaining TRICARE eligibility, not just TFL coordination. It's written into law. Complaining to your congressman has been tried — the requirement remains.

Prescription drugs overseas: TRICARE Home Delivery isn't available overseas unless you have an APO/FPO address or are at a US Embassy or State Department location with that kind of address. For other overseas locations, you fill prescriptions at host-nation pharmacies, pay full cost upfront, and submit claims for reimbursement through TOP. Military pharmacies on overseas bases remain free for formulary drugs if you have access.

Overseas military treatment facilities: if you're near a base overseas — Germany, Japan, South Korea, elsewhere — you can use the MTF just like stateside. Zero cost for covered services.

Claims submission overseas: download and use the TOP claims form (DD Form 2642 or the online equivalent at tricare-overseas.com). Keep receipts for everything. Reimbursement can take several weeks. The rates reimbursed are based on TRICARE allowed amounts, not what foreign providers charge — sometimes there's a gap.

Bottom line on overseas: TFL is meaningfully better than Medicare-only for overseas retirees, but it's not as seamless as domestic care. Build an emergency fund, keep good records, and know the TOP claims process before you need it.

sections.heading: Common TFL Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
sections.content:
Mistake 1: Enrolling in Medicare Advantage. When you enroll in MA, you step outside Original Medicare. TFL no longer coordinates as secondary payer for the services your MA plan covers. You'd be using your MA plan instead of Medicare + TFL. For most TRICARE-eligible retirees, this is a worse deal — you trade a free TFL wrap for an MA plan with networks, prior auths, and limited coverage outside network. Don't do it without very careful analysis.

Mistake 2: Letting Part B lapse. If you fall behind on Part B premiums and CMS terminates Part B, you lose TFL. Getting Part B reinstated requires going through the General Enrollment Period (January-March, coverage starting July 1) with a late enrollment penalty. Set up automatic Part B premium deduction from your Social Security benefit.

Mistake 3: Expecting automatic claim forwarding when seeing non-Medicare providers. If your provider doesn't accept Medicare assignment, automatic claims forwarding doesn't always work smoothly. Verify providers accept Medicare before scheduling non-emergency care.

Mistake 4: Not verifying TRICARE eligibility after major life changes. Divorce, a sponsor's death, a child aging out of dependency — all affect TRICARE eligibility. Verify through the DEERS system (Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System) after any major life change. An eligibility gap in TRICARE you don't know about can mean unexpected claims denials.

Mistake 5: Thinking TFL and Medicare Advantage can both be active. They can't, for practical purposes. See mistake 1.

faq.question: Do I need to enroll in TRICARE for Life separately?
faq.answer: No. If you're TRICARE-eligible and have both Medicare Part A and Part B, TRICARE for Life is automatic. There's no enrollment form or application. Your Part B enrollment triggers TFL coverage.

faq.question: What happens if I don't enroll in Medicare Part B as a military retiree?
faq.answer: You lose TRICARE for Life coverage. The Part B requirement is non-negotiable — you must have active Part B to keep TFL. You'd still have TRICARE eligibility but would need to enroll in TRICARE Prime or TRICARE Select instead, which have premiums and different cost-sharing structures.

faq.question: Can I use my TRICARE pharmacy benefit overseas?
faq.answer: Partially. Military treatment facility pharmacies overseas are free as always. TRICARE Home Delivery through Express Scripts is only available if you have an APO/FPO address. For other overseas locations, you pay upfront at local pharmacies and submit claims for reimbursement through TRICARE Overseas Program.

faq.question: Should TRICARE for Life beneficiaries enroll in Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Usually not. TRICARE pharmacy coverage counts as creditable coverage for Part D, so you won't accumulate late enrollment penalties by skipping Part D. Most TFL beneficiaries skip Part D and use TRICARE pharmacy benefits. The exception is if you have medications not covered by TRICARE but covered by Part D plans — in that narrow case, a Part D plan might help.

faq.question: What is the TRICARE for Life catastrophic cap in 2026?
faq.answer: $3,000 per calendar year for TRICARE-covered services. Once you hit that out-of-pocket maximum in TRICARE cost-shares, covered services are free for the remainder of the year.

faq.question: Does Medicare pay anything overseas?
faq.answer: Almost nothing. Medicare covers emergency care in Canada or Mexico in very limited geographic circumstances. For all other overseas care, TRICARE becomes your primary payer. You still must maintain Part B while overseas to keep TRICARE eligibility.

faq.question: Can I have both Medicare Advantage and TRICARE for Life?
faq.answer: You can technically be enrolled in both, but they don't coordinate the way Original Medicare and TFL do. Enrolling in MA effectively suspends your TFL wrap coverage for the same services. Most military retirees are better served by Original Medicare plus TFL than by switching to MA.

title: UnitedHealthcare vs Humana Medicare: 2026 Head-to-Head
metaDescription: UnitedHealthcare and Humana are the two biggest Medicare Advantage carriers in the country. Their plans differ in ways that matter a lot depending on where you live, what drugs you take, and what kind of network you can tolerate. Here's what the actual 2026 data shows.
h1: UnitedHealthcare vs Humana Medicare: 2026 Head-to-Head
summary: UnitedHealthcare and Humana collectively cover close to a third of all Medicare Advantage enrollees in the country. That scale matters — it means more negotiating leverage with providers, more data to improve care

sections.heading: The Headline Numbers: Where They Stand in 2026
sections.content:
UnitedHealthcare and Humana collectively cover close to a third of all Medicare Advantage enrollees in the country. That scale matters — it means more negotiating leverage with providers, more data to improve care management, and broader network reach. But size doesn't mean interchangeable.

UnitedHealthcare's membership numbers are larger — the company is the biggest MA carrier in the country, serving roughly 9 million Medicare Advantage members in 2026. Humana comes in second at approximately 5.8 million MA members. Both are national carriers with plans in most states, but their geographic strength differs dramatically by market.

On star ratings — the CMS quality scoring system that runs from 1 to 5 stars and determines bonus payments to plans — UnitedHealthcare made notable gains for 2026. About 78% of UnitedHealthcare Medicare Advantage members are now in plans rated 4 stars or higher. That's a strong improvement from prior years. Approximately 40% are in 4.5-star plans specifically.

Humana had a rougher year. Their average star rating across the MA portfolio came in at approximately 3.61 stars for 2026 — relatively stable year-over-year but below UHC's portfolio average. About 14% of Humana's members are in 4.5-star or higher plans, up from 3% in 2025. Still, only roughly 20% of Humana members are in 4-star-and-above plans across the full book of business.

This star rating gap has real consequences. Plans rated 4 stars and above receive CMS quality bonus payments, which they use to fund richer benefits — lower premiums, better coverage, $0 copays. If most of your members are in sub-4-star plans, you can't offer as much. This is exactly why Humana has been trimming its geographic footprint for 2026 — pulling back from counties where it can't maintain quality scores.

Both insurers trimmed their plan counts and service areas for 2026. The industry-wide pullback from Medicare Advantage reflects CMS reimbursement rate pressures and higher-than-expected member utilization post-COVID. Neither UHC nor Humana was immune.

sections.heading: Plan Availability: Where You Can Actually Enroll
sections.content:
UnitedHealthcare's plans will be available to approximately 94% of Medicare-eligible beneficiaries in 2026. That's near-universal coverage. In major markets, you'll typically find 20-50 plan options depending on your county — Texas, Florida, and California have the highest plan counts, with Texas offering 50 or more UHC plan options.

Humana operates in 48 states but has been more aggressive in pulling back from counties with thin membership or poor performance. Their 2026 footprint is meaningfully smaller than 2024. For beneficiaries in rural markets or smaller metros, Humana availability is less reliable than UHC's.

The plan type breakdown matters for network flexibility:

Both carriers offer HMO (requires referrals, in-network care only) and PPO (no referrals, out-of-network coverage at higher cost) options. UnitedHealthcare's PPO footprint is particularly strong in markets where provider networks are broad. Humana historically had strength in HMO markets in the South and Southeast.

For people who travel frequently or split time between two states (snowbirds, for instance), PPO plans from either carrier work better than HMO. But UHC's network breadth gives it an advantage for beneficiaries moving between high-cost metros.

Special Needs Plans (SNPs): both carriers offer Dual-Eligible SNPs (D-SNPs) for people on both Medicare and Medicaid, and Chronic Condition SNPs (C-SNPs) for specific conditions. If you're dual-eligible, compare D-SNPs separately from standard MA plans — the benefit structures are different and often richer.

The practical tip: availability is always zip-code specific. UHC's 94% national coverage number means nothing if your county is in the 6%. Use Medicare.gov's Plan Finder to enter your zip code and see what's actually available.

sections.heading: Cost Comparison: Premiums, Deductibles, and MOOP
sections.content:
This is where the comparison gets specific and honestly depends on which plan within each carrier's portfolio you're looking at. But the averages tell a story.

UnitedHealthcare's HMO plans in 2026 average $0 monthly premium across the portfolio, with an average maximum out-of-pocket (MOOP) of approximately $3,233. That's about $1,674 below the national MA average MOOP. For PPO plans, UHC averages around $37 per month premium with an approximately $6,571 MOOP.

Humana's plan structure for 2026 has shifted toward simplification. Their Giveback plans (which credit a portion of your Part B premium back) have a MOOP around $2,450 in some markets. However, some Humana PPO plans carry MOOP limits as high as $9,250 — significantly above the UHC PPO average. The spread within Humana's portfolio is wider than within UHC's.

Humana's drug benefit is notable: for Part D coverage within MA plans, approximately 83% of Humana PDPs are seeing premium decreases for 2026. The drug out-of-pocket cap (set by CMS at $2,100 for 2026) applies across both carriers' MA-PD plans.

Deductibles vary by plan. Zero-deductible plans exist in both portfolios — they're popular but sometimes come with tighter networks or higher copays per service. Don't fixate exclusively on the premium and deductible; look at the copay structure for services you actually use. An annual physical or specialist visit costs will hit your pocket more often than the deductible.

The honest guidance: if UHC is available in your area and you want the lower average MOOP, that HMO average is genuinely strong. If you need PPO flexibility and can tolerate higher MOOP, compare specific PPO plans from both. Humana's $0-premium Giveback plans are attractive in some markets where they're available and where the network fits your doctors.

sections.heading: Dental, Vision, and Hearing: Supplemental Benefit Comparison
sections.content:
This is an area where MA plans compete fiercely and both carriers do reasonably well — but the details matter and vary wildly by plan and location.

Humana's 2026 commitment is notable: 100% of Humana non-SNP Medicare Advantage plans include dental, vision, and hearing coverage. All of them. No hunting through plan details to find which Humana plan skips dental. Additionally, Humana offers $0 copays for covered preventive dental services across the board. That includes cleanings, exams, and X-rays at in-network dentists.

UnitedHealthcare also includes dental, vision, and hearing in most plans, though not universally across all plan types in all markets. The benefit generosity varies more across UHC's portfolio — some plans have comprehensive coverage, others have lower dollar limits. UHC's Hearing benefit often includes hearing aid allowances through UHC Hearing (formerly Hi HealthInnovations), which can cover a significant portion of aid costs.

For vision: both carriers typically cover routine eye exams and provide an allowance toward frames or contacts. The allowance amounts differ — compare specific plan documents for the actual dollar figures in your area.

Over-the-counter (OTC) allowances: both carriers include OTC benefits on many plans — a quarterly or annual allowance to purchase eligible health and wellness products. UHC's OTC benefit is available through a dedicated card and catalog. Humana also includes OTC allowances on a majority of plans. The dollar amounts range from roughly $25 to $150+ per quarter depending on the specific plan.

Fitness benefits: both carriers include gym membership benefits (UHC through Renew Active, Humana through SilverSneakers or similar). These are standard at this point across major carriers.

Where things get less clean: comprehensive restorative dental (crowns, implants, root canals) varies enormously by plan. Some UHC and Humana plans include significant restorative coverage; many do not or have low annual maximums. If dental is a priority, drill into the dental section of the Summary of Benefits for the specific plan you're considering — not just whether dental is included, but what the annual maximum is and whether major services are covered.

sections.heading: Drug Coverage: Formularies, Tiers, and the 2026 Cap
sections.content:
Both carriers comply with the CMS $2,100 annual out-of-pocket drug cap for 2026, which is a new requirement phasing in — this is a major win for people on expensive medications and it applies across all MA-PD plans from both carriers.

Formulary differences are where things get granular. Neither carrier publishes a single national formulary — formularies vary by specific plan and region. What matters is whether your specific drugs are covered and at which tier.

Tier structure is relatively standard across the industry: Tier 1 (preferred generic, lowest cost), Tier 2 (generic), Tier 3 (preferred brand), Tier 4 (non-preferred brand), Tier 5 (specialty). Cost-sharing increases at each tier.

UnitedHealthcare uses the OptumRx pharmacy network, which is extensive nationally. Preferred pharmacy networks within OptumRx can offer lower cost-sharing for filling prescriptions at preferred locations. CVS, Walgreens, and Walmart pharmacies are typically included in UHC preferred networks in most markets.

Humana uses its own pharmacy network, which has historically included broad retail coverage. Humana offers mail-order pharmacy for 90-day supplies at lower per-dose costs than retail for maintenance medications.

Prior authorization requirements for specialty drugs exist at both carriers. This is unavoidable with any major MA carrier. If you're on a specialty medication or anticipate needing biologics, check the specific plan's prior authorization and step therapy requirements before enrolling — denials and appeals are time-consuming.

For low-income beneficiaries eligible for Extra Help, both carriers have plans that accept the LIS benchmark and may have significantly reduced drug cost-sharing. This can make either carrier's plan essentially free for drug costs.

The bottom line on drugs: don't compare carriers — compare specific plans. Enter your exact drug list in the Medicare.gov Plan Finder and compare estimated annual drug costs for the specific plans available in your zip code. The cheapest plan on premium can be the most expensive when drug costs are added. The Plan Finder shows a total estimated cost number that combines premium, deductible, and drug costs — that's the number to compare.

sections.heading: Network Size and Provider Access
sections.content:
This is the factor that matters most in day-to-day healthcare and the one hardest to generalize across two massive, regionally variable carriers.

UnitedHealthcare's network is arguably the broadest of any MA carrier nationally. The UHC provider directory in most major metro areas is extensive. In rural areas, the story is more variable — call the carrier or check the online directory for your specific doctors before enrolling.

Humana's network strength historically concentrated in the South and Southeast — Kentucky (its corporate home), Florida, Texas, Georgia — where it has deep relationships with regional health systems. In the Northeast and Mountain West, Humana networks tend to be thinner.

For both carriers, the critical question is whether your specific doctors — primary care, specialists, hospitals — are in-network. Directories are notoriously imperfect. The correct process: identify the plan you're considering, find the online provider directory or call the plan, and verify your specific doctors by NPI number or name. Then call the doctor's office and confirm they accept that specific plan. Provider directories are updated periodically but aren't always real-time.

PPO vs HMO for network flexibility: with either carrier's PPO plan, you can see out-of-network providers at higher cost, which gives you a safety net. With HMO plans, out-of-network care (except emergencies) may not be covered at all. If you have established relationships with specific specialists outside any potential in-network options, factor this into the PPO vs HMO decision heavily.

sections.heading: 2026 Changes and the Bigger Picture
sections.content:
Both carriers pulled back in 2026 — fewer plans, smaller geographic footprints, higher cost-sharing in some markets — reflecting the broader MA industry pressure from CMS reimbursement rate adjustments and elevated member utilization.

UnitedHealthcare's strategic move for 2026 involved leaning into its higher star ratings to maintain benefit richness where it has quality bonus payments. Its 78% of members in 4-star-and-above plans gives it more room to offer competitive benefits than carriers with lower-rated portfolios.

Humana's challenges are more fundamental. The star rating decline hitting most of its membership means less bonus revenue, which forces either benefit reductions or market exits. Humana explicitly positioned its 2026 plans around simplicity and stability — fewer plan options, clearer benefit structures — rather than trying to compete with benefit richness. That's a reasonable strategic response to the environment but it means less variety in what Humana offers.

The competitive dynamic going into 2027 enrollment will depend heavily on whether Humana can recover its star ratings. CMS star ratings used for 2026 payments were calculated from 2024 performance data. If Humana's 2024 quality performance was better, those stars will show up in future years. But the trend since COVID has been difficult for most large MA carriers.

For beneficiaries, the practical advice: don't be loyal to a carrier across years. Check Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7) every year. Both UHC and Humana change their plan designs, networks, and formularies annually. A plan that was optimal in 2025 might not be in 2026. Run the Plan Finder comparison with your current drug list each October.

faq.question: Which carrier has better star ratings for 2026 — UnitedHealthcare or Humana?
faq.answer: UnitedHealthcare. About 78% of UHC Medicare Advantage members are in plans rated 4 stars or higher, with 40% in 4.5-star plans. Humana's average across its portfolio is approximately 3.61 stars, with only about 20% of members in 4-star-and-above plans. This gap matters because higher-rated plans receive CMS bonus payments that fund better benefits.

faq.question: Does Humana really include dental on all plans?
faq.answer: Humana's 2026 commitment is that 100% of non-SNP Medicare Advantage plans include dental, vision, and hearing coverage, with $0 copays for preventive dental services. That's across the board, not on select plans.

faq.question: What is the maximum out-of-pocket for UnitedHealthcare HMO plans in 2026?
faq.answer: UnitedHealthcare HMO plans average approximately $3,233 MOOP for 2026, which is about $1,674 below the national MA average. Individual plans vary — some will be lower, some higher. PPO plans average around $6,571 MOOP.

faq.question: Are UnitedHealthcare and Humana available nationwide?
faq.answer: UHC plans are available to approximately 94% of Medicare-eligible beneficiaries nationally. Humana operates in 48 states but trimmed its county-level footprint significantly for 2026. Availability is always county-specific — enter your zip code in Medicare.gov Plan Finder to see what's actually available where you live.

faq.question: Can I switch from Humana to UnitedHealthcare mid-year?
faq.answer: Generally no. Changes to Medicare Advantage plans are mostly locked to the Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7) for coverage starting January 1. The Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 - March 31) allows one switch from MA to MA or MA to Original Medicare. Certain qualifying life events create Special Enrollment Periods.

faq.question: What is the 2026 drug out-of-pocket cap for Medicare Advantage plans?
faq.answer: $2,100 per year for covered Part D drugs under Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans, as set by CMS. This is a new hard cap phasing in under the Inflation Reduction Act provisions, and it applies to both UHC and Humana MA-PD plans.

title: Aetna vs Cigna Medicare: 2026 Full Comparison
metaDescription: Aetna is now a CVS Health company with MinuteClinic integration and 43-state reach. Cigna just sold its Medicare business to become HealthSpring under HCSC. In 2026, these are two different companies with genuinely different strengths. Here's the actual comparison.
h1: Aetna vs Cigna Medicare: 2026 Full Comparison
summary: Before comparing specific plan benefits, you need to understand what these companies actually are in 2026 because both underwent major structural changes. Aetna is owned by CVS Health — the merger completed in 2018.

sections.heading: The Big Structural Difference: CVS vs HealthSpring
sections.content:
Before comparing specific plan benefits, you need to understand what these companies actually are in 2026 because both underwent major structural changes.

Aetna is owned by CVS Health — the merger completed in 2018. What that means for Medicare members in 2026 is real integration: MinuteClinic access, CVS Pharmacy preferred network pricing, OTC allowances redeemable at CVS locations, and the CVS Health at Home program for members with chronic conditions. This isn't just corporate parentage — it's an integrated care model that uses the CVS retail footprint as actual clinical infrastructure.

Cigna, as most people knew it, sold its Medicare Advantage business. In March 2025, Cigna completed the sale of its Medicare assets to Health Care Service Corporation (HCSC), the parent of BlueCross BlueShield plans in Illinois, Montana, New Mexico, Oklahoma, and Texas. The Medicare plans rebranded as HealthSpring for 2026. If you had a Cigna Medicare plan, it's now a HealthSpring plan. Same people, largely same infrastructure, different brand.

This matters for the comparison because Aetna is a national carrier with 43-state reach and growing integration with a national pharmacy/retail footprint. HealthSpring (formerly Cigna) is a more regionally concentrated carrier operating in 29 states, with particular depth in the South and certain Midwest markets. They're not the same kind of competitor anymore.

For beneficiaries: if you're in a state where HealthSpring (formerly Cigna) is strong — Tennessee, Texas, Alabama, Georgia, Florida — the comparison is live and meaningful. If you're in the Northeast or Pacific Coast, HealthSpring may not even be available and the comparison is moot. Check your zip code first.

sections.heading: Star Ratings: Aetna's Edge
sections.content:
Star ratings matter because they proxy for care quality, member satisfaction, and plan stability — and because high-rated plans get bonus revenue that funds better benefits.

Aetna performed well in 2026 star ratings. Over 81% of Aetna Medicare Advantage members are in plans rated 4 stars or higher, with over 63% in 4.5-star plans. Aetna's average CMS star rating across the portfolio is approximately 3.88 stars. For HMO plans specifically, Aetna averages 4.14 stars. For PPO plans, 4.05 stars — both meaningfully above national plan-type averages.

HealthSpring (formerly Cigna) had a more difficult 2026 rating cycle. HMO plans average approximately 3.77 stars; PPO plans average 3.01 stars — notably below the 3.63 national PPO average. The overall HealthSpring portfolio averages roughly 3.66 stars. Not terrible, but clearly trailing Aetna across every plan type.

The practical implication: Aetna's higher star ratings translate to more CMS bonus revenue, which flows into richer benefits for members. Plans hovering around 3 stars don't get those bonuses and have to either accept thinner margins or reduce benefits. HealthSpring's sub-4-star PPO performance is particularly concerning for beneficiaries who prioritize PPO flexibility — those plans aren't getting quality bonuses at current ratings.

Stars are scored on a mix of measures: preventive care rates (flu shots, cancer screenings, diabetes monitoring), chronic disease management, member complaints, call center performance, and plan administration. Aetna's improvement has been driven largely by better chronic disease management scores and lower member complaint rates — outcomes that correlate with actual care quality, not just administrative metrics.

One caveat: ratings are plan-level, not carrier-level. Aetna might have a 4.5-star plan in your county and a 3.5-star plan in the neighboring county. Always look at the star rating of the specific plan you're considering, not the carrier average.

sections.heading: Geographic Coverage: 43 States vs 29 States
sections.content:
