title: WellCare Medicare Review 2026
metaDescription: WellCare is a Centene subsidiary built for low-income and dual-eligible Medicare beneficiaries. $0 premiums on 72% of MA plans, massive footprint across 32 states, and aggressive expansion in 2026. Star ratings are below average. Here's the full picture.
h1: WellCare Medicare Review 2026
summary: WellCare isn't trying to be the premium Medicare option. They're not competing with UnitedHealthcare Optum on network quality or with Humana on member experienc

sections.heading: What WellCare Actually Is
sections.content:
WellCare isn't trying to be the premium Medicare option. They're not competing with UnitedHealthcare Optum on network quality or with Humana on member experience scores. WellCare is built for affordability and access — specifically for people on Medicaid, people who are dual-eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid, and beneficiaries in lower-income brackets who need $0 premiums and low-cost prescriptions.

The company is a subsidiary of Centene Corporation, the largest Medicaid managed care organization in the country. That heritage matters. Centene built its business managing government health programs for populations that other insurers underprice or ignore. WellCare carries that DNA into Medicare.

Centene is a $130+ billion revenue company. WellCare has real capital behind it.

For 2026, WellCare will cover Medicare Advantage beneficiaries in 32 states and offers Prescription Drug Plans in all 50 states plus DC. That's a meaningful footprint — and it's growing, with 51 new counties added across California, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Nevada, Texas, and North Carolina for the new plan year.

If you're dual-eligible or on a tight budget, WellCare belongs in your comparison. If you're a high-income Medicare beneficiary prioritizing network quality and star ratings, you'll probably be happier elsewhere.

sections.heading: Star Ratings — The Honest Assessment
sections.content:
WellCare's average Medicare Advantage star rating for 2026 is 3.39 stars out of 5, weighted by enrollment. The overall industry average is higher than that. To put it plainly: WellCare is a below-average rated carrier.

Star ratings matter for a few reasons beyond just quality signaling. Plans with 5 stars can be switched into at any time during the year (not just during open enrollment). Plans rated 3 stars or below for three consecutive years face contract termination from CMS. WellCare is not at risk of the 3-year threshold currently, but the 3.39 average is something to track.

On the Part D (prescription drug plan) side, WellCare actually outperforms many competitors in 2026. Their drug plan star ratings are stronger than their Medicare Advantage ratings. If you're primarily concerned with prescription drug coverage and cost, the star rating picture is better than the MA numbers suggest.

Low star ratings don't necessarily mean you'll have a bad experience. They're aggregate measures that include metrics like chronic disease management, member complaints, and administrative accuracy. For straightforward Medicare coverage with no complex chronic conditions, a 3.5-star plan can serve you just fine. The star rating matters most when comparing plans in the same geography with similar premiums.

sections.heading: $0 Premiums — The Main Draw
sections.content:
About 72% of WellCare's Medicare Advantage plans that aren't special needs plans carry a $0 monthly premium for 2026. That's the headline and it's accurate.

Among plans that do charge a premium, the average is $32.90 per month — well below most major competitors.

Remember: even on a $0 premium MA plan, you still pay your Medicare Part B premium. In 2026, Part B is $202.90 per month. That's federally mandated and applies regardless of which Medicare Advantage plan you choose. The "$0 premium" means WellCare isn't charging an additional monthly fee on top of Part B — not that Medicare is free.

WellCare's Prescription Drug Plans are similarly affordable. Part D plan premiums range from $0 to $45.70/month, with an average of $8.16/month. That's one of the lowest average PDP premiums in the market. For beneficiaries who don't need extensive prescription coverage and want to keep drug plan costs minimal, WellCare PDP options are worth serious consideration.

The low-premium model works because WellCare designs these plans around low-cost networks and leans on Centene's managed care infrastructure — the same model that keeps Medicaid costs manageable. The tradeoff is network breadth and sometimes provider quality.

sections.heading: Dual-Eligible Plans — Where WellCare Specializes
sections.content:
This is WellCare's genuine competitive advantage. Dual Special Needs Plans (D-SNPs) — for people eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid — are where Centene/WellCare's managed care expertise translates directly into better plan design.

For 2026, WellCare is launching an enhanced integrated Duals model in eight states: Michigan, Arizona, Delaware, Illinois, Iowa, Ohio, South Carolina, and Texas. This model integrates both Medicare and Medicaid benefits into a single plan, eliminating the administrative nightmare that dual-eligible beneficiaries typically face managing two separate programs.

In five of those eight states (Illinois, Michigan, Ohio, South Carolina, Texas), WellCare was already managing Medicaid-Medicare Plan (MMP) contracts, and members in those programs are transitioning seamlessly to the new integrated D-SNPs on January 1, 2026.

For a dual-eligible beneficiary, this matters enormously. Managing Medicare and Medicaid separately means two sets of rules, two prior authorization processes, two networks potentially, and two sets of cost-sharing to track. An integrated D-SNP with one carrier and one care coordinator reduces that complexity to one call, one card, one plan.

WellCare's D-SNPs also include supplemental benefits specifically designed for Medicaid-eligible populations: transportation assistance, meal delivery, expanded OTC allowances, and dental/vision coverage that goes beyond what traditional Medicare provides.

sections.heading: Supplemental Benefits — The WellCare Spendables Card
sections.content:
WellCare's supplemental benefits package varies by plan and geography, but the signature feature is the Wellcare Spendables card — a prepaid benefits card accepted at more than 66,000 nationwide retailers.

The Spendables card comes preloaded with funds that can be used for approved health-related purchases across WellCare's 32-state footprint. Think over-the-counter medications, health supplies, select grocery items (for eligible plans), and personal care products. The card is a meaningful financial benefit for lower-income beneficiaries where even modest OTC costs create real budget pressure.

Additional supplemental benefits available on select plans include:

Dental coverage — routine cleanings, exams, and x-rays included on most plans; some plans include allowances for dentures and more complex dental work.

Vision coverage — routine eye exams and allowances toward glasses or contacts.

Hearing benefits — coverage toward hearing aid costs, which traditional Medicare doesn't cover at all.

Transportation assistance — rides to medical appointments, a frequently overlooked but practically critical benefit for elderly beneficiaries who no longer drive.

Meal delivery — available on some plans following a qualifying hospital stay or for members with specific chronic conditions.

Special Supplemental Benefits for the Chronically Ill (SSBCI) — WellCare offers expanded benefits for members with qualifying conditions including cancer, cardiovascular disorders, diabetes, chronic lung disorders, and chronic/disabling mental health conditions. These can include non-medical benefits like home modifications, pest control, and air filtration that go well beyond what any other insurance type covers.

The catch is that SSBCI benefits require meeting the qualifying criteria. Not every WellCare member gets them.

sections.heading: Prescription Drug Coverage — Actually a Strength
sections.content:
WellCare's Part D coverage is, frankly, one of their better products. They offer two main PDP options in 2026: Value Script and Classic, both available across all 50 states and DC.

The Value Script plan is designed for beneficiaries who take mostly generic medications and want the lowest possible monthly premium. It's a bare-bones plan that works well if your prescriptions are mostly Tier 1 and Tier 2 generics.

The Classic plan offers broader formulary coverage for beneficiaries with more complex medication needs.

Both plans participate in the new Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (sometimes called the "smoothing" provision) introduced under the Inflation Reduction Act, which allows beneficiaries to spread their drug cost liability across the year in monthly installments rather than paying a large sum at the pharmacy counter early in the year when deductibles are accumulating.

The 2026 cap on out-of-pocket prescription drug costs is $2,000 — a significant new benefit that applies across all Medicare Part D plans regardless of carrier. This is worth understanding because it fundamentally changes the risk calculation for beneficiaries taking expensive medications.

sections.heading: Geographic Availability — Where WellCare Works
sections.content:
WellCare offers Medicare Advantage in 32 states for 2026. That's substantial coverage. But the depth of plan options within those states varies considerably.

States with the most robust WellCare MA presence tend to be Centene's Medicaid strongholds: Texas, Florida, Georgia, Ohio, Illinois, and several Southeast states. In those markets, WellCare offers multiple plan types — HMO, PPO, HMO-POS — with stronger networks.

In states where WellCare just expanded (51 new counties in 2026 across California, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Nevada, Texas, North Carolina), plan availability and network depth may be thinner initially. Expansion often means the plan is new to that market and hasn't yet built the provider relationships that more established plans have.

Prescription Drug Plans are truly national — all 50 states and DC. If WellCare doesn't offer Medicare Advantage in your area but you want their PDP pricing, that's an option.

And in the eight states participating in the new integrated Duals model, WellCare's D-SNPs are particularly competitive and worth prioritizing for dual-eligible beneficiaries.

sections.heading: Limitations and Who Should Look Elsewhere
sections.content:
Star ratings below the industry average are the biggest flag. For the 2026 plan year, 3.39 weighted average is meaningful. If you're comparing WellCare to a competitor plan with 4+ stars at the same or similar premium in your county, the higher-rated plan is generally the better choice unless WellCare has a substantially superior drug formulary or network for your specific providers.

Network restrictions can be meaningful. WellCare's $0-premium HMO plans require using in-network providers for all covered services except emergencies. If your preferred specialist or hospital is out of network, a $0-premium plan can end up costing more than a PPO with a modest premium that covers your providers.

Always check provider network before enrolling. This is the most important step for any Medicare Advantage decision and the one most people skip.

For beneficiaries in urban markets with significant Medicare Advantage competition — New York City, Los Angeles, Chicago, Houston — there are often multiple $0-premium options from different carriers with higher star ratings. WellCare's low-cost positioning is less distinctive in competitive urban markets.

WellCare is not designed for premium-tier service expectations. Their model is high-volume, managed-cost, government-program administration. If you have complex medical needs and want concierge-level care coordination, carriers like Humana Gold Plus or UnitedHealthcare with higher star ratings in your area will likely serve you better.

sections.heading: Bottom Line
sections.content:
WellCare is the right answer for a specific type of Medicare beneficiary. Dual-eligible individuals who need integrated Medicare-Medicaid management, low-income beneficiaries for whom the Spendables card and $0 premium represent real budget relief, and people in WellCare's Medicaid-stronghold states where their networks and care management infrastructure are most developed.

For everyone else — particularly people with complex medical needs, strong provider preferences, or the ability to pay a modest monthly premium for higher-rated plans — WellCare should be compared against 4-star options in their geography before deciding.

The star rating picture is honest and it matters. But millions of beneficiaries are well-served by WellCare's affordable coverage every year, and for the dual-eligible population specifically, their 2026 integrated D-SNP expansion is genuinely good news.

faq.question: What is WellCare's relationship to Centene?
faq.answer: WellCare is a subsidiary of Centene Corporation, the largest Medicaid managed care organization in the US. Centene acquired WellCare in 2020 and uses the brand specifically for Medicare products, leveraging Centene's government health program expertise.

faq.question: How much do WellCare Medicare Advantage plans cost in 2026?
faq.answer: About 72% of WellCare's non-SNP Medicare Advantage plans have $0 monthly premiums. Plans with premiums average $32.90/month. Remember: you still pay Medicare Part B ($202.90/month in 2026) regardless of which plan you choose.

faq.question: What are WellCare's star ratings for 2026?
faq.answer: WellCare's weighted average Medicare Advantage star rating is 3.39 out of 5 — below the industry average. Their Part D prescription drug plan ratings are stronger. Low star ratings don't necessarily mean poor individual experience, but higher-rated plans should be compared when available at similar cost.

faq.question: What is a WellCare dual-eligible plan?
faq.answer: WellCare's Dual Special Needs Plans (D-SNPs) are designed for people eligible for both Medicare and Medicaid. For 2026, WellCare is launching integrated D-SNPs in eight states that administer both Medicare and Medicaid benefits through one plan, eliminating the complexity of managing two separate programs.

faq.question: What is the WellCare Spendables card?
faq.answer: A prepaid benefits card accepted at 66,000+ nationwide retailers, preloaded with funds for approved health-related purchases including OTC medications, health supplies, and select grocery items (on qualifying plans). Availability and card amount vary by plan and location.

faq.question: In how many states is WellCare Medicare Advantage available?
faq.answer: 32 states for the 2026 plan year, with 51 new counties added across California, Illinois, Iowa, Kansas, Michigan, Nevada, Texas, and North Carolina. WellCare Part D plans are available in all 50 states and DC.

faq.question: Does WellCare cover dental and vision?
faq.answer: Most WellCare Medicare Advantage plans include routine dental and vision benefits. Coverage depth varies by plan — some include allowances for dentures and more extensive dental work, while basic plans cover cleanings, exams, and x-rays.

faq.question: What is the $2,000 prescription drug cap for 2026?
faq.answer: Starting 2026, Medicare Part D plans including WellCare's are capped at $2,000 in out-of-pocket drug costs annually. This applies across all Medicare Part D coverage regardless of carrier — it's a federal benefit change under the Inflation Reduction Act.

title: Anthem Medicare Review 2026
metaDescription: Anthem is a Blue Cross Blue Shield affiliate with strong standard benefits, SilverSneakers fitness, solid dental and vision coverage, and plans in 13 states. Star ratings run below the industry average but the coverage breadth is real. Here's the honest breakdown.
h1: Anthem Medicare Review 2026
summary: Anthem is one of the largest Blue Cross Blue Shield licensees in the country — a massive private insurer operating BCBS plans in 14 states for commercial covera

sections.heading: What Anthem Medicare Actually Is
sections.content:
Anthem is one of the largest Blue Cross Blue Shield licensees in the country — a massive private insurer operating BCBS plans in 14 states for commercial coverage and offering Medicare Advantage in 13 states under various Anthem and BCBS brand names.

The Medicare Advantage side of Anthem is sizable but geographically constrained. They're not a national Medicare carrier. If you live in California, Colorado, Connecticut, Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, Maine, Missouri, Nevada, New Hampshire, New York, Ohio, Virginia, or Wisconsin, Anthem Medicare plans may be available to you. If you're elsewhere, Anthem isn't an option — but another BCBS affiliate might be. The BCBS system is a federation, not a single company, and different states have different BCBS licensees.

For the beneficiaries Anthem does serve, the value proposition is reasonably strong: established BCBS network relationships, SilverSneakers fitness benefits, decent dental and vision add-ons, and a brand with 45+ million commercial members behind it. Their Medicare Advantage enrollment is around 1 million — significant but not in the same tier as UnitedHealthcare (8+ million MA members) or Humana (5+ million).

The star rating picture is below average. The out-of-pocket costs run high on average. But the coverage features have genuine substance. Understanding which side of that tradeoff matters more for your situation is the key to deciding whether Anthem belongs in your comparison.

sections.heading: Star Ratings — Below Average But Not Terrible
sections.content:
Anthem's weighted average star rating for 2026 is 3.64 out of 5, compared to the overall industry average of 3.98 for all Medicare Advantage plans. That's a meaningful gap — about a third of a star below the field.

Here's the nuance: 45% of Anthem's enrolled members are in plans rated 4 stars or better. So while the enrollment-weighted average is below 3.98, a meaningful portion of Anthem's plans do hit or exceed the 4-star threshold. The 3.64 average is dragged down by lower-rated plans in certain markets.

What this means practically: if you're considering Anthem in your county, look up the specific plan's star rating, not just the company average. A 4-star Anthem plan in Virginia is a different product from a 3-star Anthem plan in Ohio. Medicare.gov's plan finder tool shows individual plan ratings by county.

CMS calculates star ratings using 40+ measures covering prevention, chronic disease management, member experience, access to care, and plan administration. A below-average composite score reflects real issues somewhere in the organization — higher complaint rates, lower chronic disease management scores, or gaps in administrative accuracy. For most straightforward Medicare users, these metrics matter less than network access and drug formulary. For people with significant chronic disease management needs, the chronic disease measures matter more.

sections.heading: SilverSneakers — Underrated and Actually Valuable
sections.content:
Anthem includes SilverSneakers fitness membership on most Medicare Advantage plans at no additional cost. This is worth more than it sounds.

SilverSneakers is a fitness program specifically designed for adults 65+. Membership includes access to thousands of participating fitness locations across the country — gyms, YMCAs, fitness centers — at no additional cost. Live online fitness classes are included. On-demand video workouts are included. In-person instructor-led classes at participating locations are included.

The network of participating locations exceeds 15,000 nationwide. For someone who exercises regularly, a typical gym membership runs $30-$80/month — $360-$960 annually. Getting that as a bundled benefit with your Medicare plan has real dollar value.

The fitness benefit also extends to social connection, which has documented health impacts for the 65+ population. SilverSneakers classes and locations create community and accountability in ways that solo home exercise doesn't.

Not every Anthem plan includes SilverSneakers — verify before enrolling — but the majority of Anthem Medicare Advantage plans do. It's one of the few supplemental benefits where the value is clear, quantifiable, and actually used by a large portion of members.

sections.heading: Dental and Vision — Solid Coverage
sections.content:
Anthem's dental and vision coverage is a genuine strength of the product.

On the dental side, 2026 Anthem Medicare Advantage plans include benefits worth hundreds of dollars annually according to Anthem's own benefit materials. This covers routine dental cleanings, exams, and x-rays at minimum. Select plans include coverage for dentures, crowns, extractions, and more extensive restorative work. The exact scope varies by specific plan and location.

Traditional Medicare (Parts A and B) covers essentially no routine dental care. Dental costs in retirement are a major budget concern for many seniors — the American Dental Association reports that 1 in 3 adults over 65 haven't been to a dentist in at least a year, largely due to cost. Having dental coverage built into your Medicare plan eliminates one barrier to needed care.

On the vision side, Anthem plans include routine eye exams and allowances toward eyeglasses or contact lenses. Prescription eyewear costs have been significant — a basic pair of glasses with progressive lenses from a retail optician runs $200-$400+. Even a modest vision allowance of $150-$200 covers a significant portion of that cost.

Hearing benefits are included on some 2026 Anthem plans, with coverage up to $3,000 toward hearing aids. Traditional Medicare covers hearing exams under limited circumstances and covers zero hearing aids. At $3,000-$7,000 per pair for quality hearing aids, this benefit has major financial impact for members who need it.

The dental, vision, and hearing benefits are consistently the most used supplemental benefits across Medicare Advantage plans — not just at Anthem but across the market. These aren't gimmick benefits. They cover real and recurring needs.

sections.heading: Premium and Out-of-Pocket Costs
sections.content:
About 32% of Anthem Medicare Advantage plans carry $0 premiums for 2026. That's meaningful but lower than WellCare's 72% — Anthem isn't leading on affordability the way WellCare is.

For Anthem plans with premiums, the average monthly cost is $47.14 for Medicare Advantage plans with a prescription drug component (MAPD plans). That's above the market average and positions Anthem in the mid-to-upper tier for premium cost among MA carriers.

Remember: all of this is on top of the Medicare Part B premium of $202.90/month that applies universally in 2026.

The out-of-pocket maximum picture is where things get genuinely concerning. Anthem has the highest maximum out-of-pocket costs, on average, among large Medicare Advantage companies. The 2026 federal limit on in-network MOOP is $9,250 (down from $9,350 in 2025). Some Anthem plans set their MOOP close to that federal maximum.

This matters because the MOOP is your worst-case annual exposure for in-network covered services. If you have a major medical event in a plan year, you're potentially on the hook for up to $9,250 before coverage takes over fully. That's a real number and higher than most beneficiaries can absorb without financial stress.

If you're comparison shopping Anthem against alternatives, pay close attention to the specific MOOP for each plan in your county. A plan with a $25/month premium and $9,200 MOOP might be worse value than a plan with a $47/month premium and $5,500 MOOP, depending on your health situation and risk tolerance.

sections.heading: Network Strength — The BCBS Advantage
sections.content:
The Blue Cross Blue Shield affiliation gives Anthem's Medicare plans access to broad provider networks. In markets where Anthem has been operating for years — Indiana, Virginia, Ohio — the physician and hospital network depth is significant.

For HMO plans, the network defines your covered care universe. Staying in-network is mandatory except for emergencies. Anthem's HMO networks in established markets are generally large enough to include most major hospitals and the majority of primary care physicians and specialists.

For PPO plans, in-network care is covered at full benefit levels and out-of-network care is covered at reduced (but not zero) rates. The PPO flexibility is valuable for people who travel frequently or have providers in multiple regions.

Blue Card coverage — the BCBS national portability program — applies to some Anthem plans for out-of-state emergency care. This can matter for snowbirds and frequent travelers who split time between states.

The network strength varies considerably by state. In Anthem's core markets (California as Anthem Blue Cross, Indiana as Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield, Virginia as Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield), the networks are mature and extensive. In states where Anthem operates but has smaller enrollment, network depth may be more limited.

As with all Medicare Advantage decisions: verify that your specific doctors and hospitals participate in the plan's network before enrolling. This is the single most important due diligence step and the most commonly skipped.

sections.heading: Geographic Coverage — The 13-State Reality
sections.content:
Anthem Medicare Advantage is available in 13 states: California, Colorado, Connecticut, Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, Maine, Missouri, Nevada, New Hampshire, New York, Ohio, Virginia, and Wisconsin. That's it. If you live anywhere else, Anthem isn't an option for MA.

This is a meaningful limitation for people who live in the 37 other states or who split time between a covered and non-covered state. Moving retirement plans can affect which carriers are available to you.

Within those 13 states, plan availability is county-specific. Metro areas within Anthem states generally have full plan availability. Rural counties may have fewer options or no Anthem MA plans at all.

For beneficiaries in Anthem's core states — particularly Indiana, Virginia, Ohio, and Georgia where Anthem has deep commercial insurance roots — the Medicare product inherits strong provider relationships and administrative infrastructure built over decades.

For beneficiaries outside those 13 states looking for BCBS-affiliated Medicare options, other BCBS licensees operate independently in different states. Highmark in Pennsylvania, BCBS of Michigan, BCBS of North Carolina — each is a different company under the BCBS umbrella with their own Medicare Advantage products and ratings.

sections.heading: How Anthem Compares to Alternatives
sections.content:
In most Anthem markets, you're comparing against UnitedHealthcare, Humana, Aetna, and possibly regional carriers. Here's a straightforward comparison frame:

Versus UnitedHealthcare: UHC typically has higher star ratings and larger networks, but also higher premiums and sometimes tighter formularies. UHC is generally the safer choice for people who want established, well-rated coverage and are willing to pay more.

Versus Humana: Humana competes heavily on dental and vision benefits and has been strong in rural areas. Humana's star ratings run higher than Anthem's in most markets. If Humana is available and competitive, compare closely.

Versus WellCare: WellCare wins on $0 premium availability (72% vs Anthem's 32%) and on dual-eligible specialization. Anthem wins on star ratings (3.64 vs 3.39) and on supplemental benefit quality. If you're dual-eligible, WellCare probably wins. If you're not dual-eligible, the comparison is closer.

Versus Aetna: Aetna has strong star ratings in most markets and competitive dental benefits. Aetna is typically a serious alternative to Anthem in markets where both operate.

The most honest advice: use Medicare.gov's plan finder tool, filter to your county, sort by star rating, and compare the top 3-4 options including Anthem on specific plan details — monthly premium, MOOP, drug formulary, dental/vision coverage, and SilverSneakers availability. The decision at the county level often looks different from the national brand comparison.

sections.heading: Bottom Line
sections.content:
Anthem is a legitimate Medicare Advantage option, not a great one and not a terrible one. Their 3.64 star average is real and reflects areas where the product underperforms. Their MOOP costs running toward the high end of the market is a real financial risk.

But SilverSneakers is a genuinely valuable benefit. The dental and vision coverage is real and used. The hearing aid benefit up to $3,000 is significant. The BCBS network relationships in Anthem's established markets are strong.

If you're in one of the 13 Anthem states and comparing options, include Anthem in your shortlist — especially if SilverSneakers and dental/vision are priorities. But don't stop there. Pull the specific plan details, check the MOOP on the plans you're considering, verify your providers are in-network, and compare against local alternatives with higher star ratings.

For dual-eligible beneficiaries, WellCare's integrated D-SNP programs are likely better suited. For people who want the lowest possible premiums, WellCare also wins. Anthem's lane is mid-tier pricing with solid supplemental benefits for beneficiaries in their 13-state footprint who want an established, BCBS-affiliated carrier.

faq.question: In which states is Anthem Medicare Advantage available?
faq.answer: Anthem Medicare Advantage is available in 13 states: California, Colorado, Connecticut, Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, Maine, Missouri, Nevada, New Hampshire, New York, Ohio, Virginia, and Wisconsin. Within those states, specific plan availability varies by county.

faq.question: What is Anthem's Medicare star rating for 2026?
faq.answer: Anthem's weighted average star rating is 3.64 out of 5 — below the industry average of 3.98. However, 45% of enrolled Anthem members are in plans rated 4 stars or better. Star ratings vary significantly by specific plan and county — check individual plan ratings on Medicare.gov.

faq.question: Does Anthem Medicare include SilverSneakers?
faq.answer: Most Anthem Medicare Advantage plans include SilverSneakers fitness membership at no additional cost. SilverSneakers provides access to 15,000+ participating gyms and fitness centers, live online classes, and on-demand video workouts. Not every Anthem plan includes it — confirm before enrolling.

faq.question: What dental and vision coverage does Anthem Medicare offer?
faq.answer: Anthem Medicare Advantage plans include routine dental (cleanings, exams, x-rays), routine vision (exams and allowances toward glasses/contacts), and hearing benefits up to $3,000 on select plans. Traditional Medicare covers none of these — the MA supplemental benefits represent significant added value.

faq.question: What are Anthem's Medicare Advantage premium costs?
faq.answer: About 32% of Anthem MA plans have $0 monthly premiums. Plans with premiums average $47.14/month. All beneficiaries still pay Medicare Part B ($202.90/month in 2026) regardless of their MA plan choice.

faq.question: What is the out-of-pocket maximum on Anthem Medicare plans?
faq.answer: Anthem has among the highest average out-of-pocket maximums of large Medicare Advantage carriers. The 2026 federal MOOP limit is $9,250 for in-network services, and some Anthem plans set their MOOP near that maximum. Compare specific plan MOOP against alternatives before enrolling.

faq.question: Is Anthem part of Blue Cross Blue Shield?
faq.answer: Yes — Anthem is one of the largest BCBS licensees in the US, operating Blue Cross Blue Shield plans in 14 states for commercial coverage. Their Medicare Advantage products operate under Anthem and BCBS brand names depending on the state.

faq.question: How does Anthem compare to WellCare for Medicare?
faq.answer: Anthem has higher star ratings (3.64 vs 3.39) and stronger supplemental benefits (SilverSneakers, deeper dental/vision). WellCare wins on $0 premium availability (72% vs 32%) and dual-eligible specialization. For dual-eligible beneficiaries, WellCare's integrated D-SNPs are generally the better choice.

title: HMO vs PPO vs EPO vs POS: Plan Types Explained
metaDescription: HMO, PPO, EPO, POS — four plan types that look almost identical on paper until you actually need care. Here's how each one works, what it costs you, and which one fits your life.
h1: HMO vs PPO vs EPO vs POS: Plan Types Explained
summary: Most people pick a health plan based on the monthly premium. That's it. They look at the number, grab the cheapest one, and move on. Then they get a referral de

sections.heading: Why This Matters More Than You Think
sections.content:
Most people pick a health plan based on the monthly premium. That's it. They look at the number, grab the cheapest one, and move on. Then they get a referral denial six months later or a $4,000 out-of-network bill from a surgeon they didn't know was outside their network, and suddenly they're furious at a system they never actually understood.

The plan type — HMO, PPO, EPO, or POS — isn't a minor detail. It's the architecture. It controls where you can go, how you get there, and how much you'll pay when something actually goes wrong. A $40/month premium difference can easily flip into a $3,000 difference in real costs depending on how you use care.

So yeah. This is worth spending 20 minutes on.

sections.heading: HMO — Health Maintenance Organization
sections.content:
The HMO is the most structured of the four. You pick a primary care physician — your PCP — and that person is your gatekeeper. Want to see a cardiologist? You're not calling one directly. You call your PCP, they evaluate you, they write a referral, and then you see the cardiologist. That's the model.

This sounds like a pain, and sometimes it is. But here's why HMOs exist: coordination. The idea is that a PCP who knows your full history makes better decisions than a specialist who's seeing you cold. That's actually true in a lot of cases, especially for chronic conditions.

Networks are tighter on HMOs. You stay in-network or you pay full price — sometimes literally nothing gets covered outside the network except genuine emergencies. That ER visit in another state while you're on vacation? Covered. The dermatologist your friend recommended who doesn't take your plan? Not covered. At all.

The tradeoff is cost. HMOs are almost always the cheapest premium option. Deductibles tend to be lower. Copays are predictable — you know you're paying $20 at the PCP, $40 at a specialist. If you're healthy, use a regular doctor, and stay in your home metro area, an HMO is honestly a great deal.

Real example: a 34-year-old in Chicago on a Blue Cross HMO might pay $310/month with a $1,500 deductible. The equivalent PPO? $460/month with a $2,500 deductible. That's $150/month and $1,000 in deductible difference. If you never leave the network, you just saved $2,800/year.

Where HMOs break down: if you have a complex condition that requires multiple specialists who don't coordinate well within the network, or you travel frequently and regularly need care outside your home area, or you have an existing relationship with an out-of-network doctor you trust. In those cases the structure becomes a cage.

sections.heading: PPO — Preferred Provider Organization
sections.content:
The PPO is what most people mean when they say they want 'good insurance.' More flexibility, no referral requirements, access to both in-network and out-of-network providers — just at different cost levels.

With a PPO you can walk into a specialist's office and book directly. No PCP approval needed. Want a second opinion from a doctor across town who's out of network? You can do that — you'll pay more, but it's your call. That autonomy is real and it matters, especially if you have ongoing health issues or strong opinions about who you see.

The in-network vs out-of-network math is where people get surprised. In-network on a PPO typically means 80/20 coinsurance after your deductible — you pay 20%, the plan pays 80%. Out-of-network? Could be 60/40 or even 50/50, with a separate (and higher) out-of-network deductible you have to hit first. People see 'covered out of network' and assume it's similar to in-network. It's not.

PPO premiums are the highest of the four types. On the ACA marketplace in 2026 — after the expiration of enhanced subsidies — that gap is meaningful. For a 45-year-old making $70,000 in a mid-size market, a silver PPO can run $550-650/month before subsidies. Same person on a silver HMO might be $380-420.

The case for PPO: you or a family member has a specialist you've been seeing for years who's out of network. You split time between cities. You have a complex condition that benefits from direct specialist access. You're simply not going to call your PCP for a referral every time and want the freedom to manage your own care. All valid. Just go in knowing you're paying for that freedom.

One thing that's genuinely changed in 2026 — PPO networks have been quietly narrowing in some states. Insurers have been 'tiering' networks, where some providers are preferred-in-network (cheaper) and others are standard-in-network (more expensive) even within the PPO. Always verify where your actual doctors land before assuming they're in the cheaper tier.

sections.heading: EPO — Exclusive Provider Organization
sections.content:
The EPO is the weird middle child. It combines some of the strictness of an HMO with some of the pricing of a PPO, but in a way that confuses a lot of people.

Here's the key: EPO networks are in-network only. No out-of-network coverage (except emergencies), same as an HMO. But — and this is the difference — most EPOs don't require referrals. You can go directly to a specialist within the network without asking your PCP first.

So you get HMO-like costs, PPO-like access within the network, and no out-of-network safety net whatsoever. That last part trips people up. They assume 'I can see any specialist' means they can see any specialist in the country. Nope. Any specialist in the network. Outside that? Full price, baby.

EPOs tend to be priced between HMOs and PPOs. They've become more common on ACA marketplaces over the last few years because insurers like the structure — they get premium revenue without the liability of out-of-network claims.

Who should consider an EPO: you want specialist access without referral bureaucracy, you're confident your doctors are in-network, you never really use out-of-network care anyway, and you want to save a couple hundred bucks a month over a PPO. That's a reasonable profile.

Who should avoid an EPO: anyone with a specialist relationship they're not sure about, frequent travelers who might need care in other states, or people who might want a second opinion outside the network. A $200/month EPO premium discount means nothing if you end up with a $15,000 out-of-network bill from a procedure done during a trip.

sections.heading: POS — Point of Service
sections.content:
The POS plan is genuinely the most confusing of the four, which is probably why it's also the least common. Think of it as an HMO that lets you go out of network if you're willing to pay for it — but keeps the referral requirement from the HMO side.

Here's how it works in practice: you pick a PCP, you get referrals for specialists, you use in-network providers and pay HMO-like costs. But if you want to see an out-of-network provider, you can — similar to a PPO. You'll pay more, you'll likely need to meet an out-of-network deductible, and the reimbursement rates are messier, but the door isn't locked.

The PCP referral requirement is the sticking point. Unlike an EPO where you can just call a specialist, a POS makes you go through your primary care doctor first — even for out-of-network care in most cases. Some people genuinely don't mind this. They like having a care coordinator. Others find it maddening.

POS premiums are typically in the middle range, often similar to EPOs and usually cheaper than PPOs. They were more popular before EPOs proliferated, and in some markets they're getting harder to find.

Honest take: if your choice is between a POS and an EPO at similar prices, most people are better off with the EPO — you get the same network and cost structure without the referral hassle. The only time POS wins is if you genuinely want out-of-network coverage as a safety valve AND you're comfortable with the referral process AND the POS premium is meaningfully lower than the PPO.

sections.heading: The Real Cost Comparison
sections.content:
Premium is what you pay every month whether you use care or not. But total cost of care is the number that actually matters.

Let's run a simple scenario. 38-year-old, one urgent care visit, one specialist visit, one $2,000 procedure. Mid-Atlantic market.

HMO: $350/month premium, $1,500 deductible, $20/$40 copays after deductible. Total annual cost if all in-network: around $5,200 including premiums.

PPO: $490/month premium, $2,500 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance. Total annual cost in-network: around $6,800.

EPO: $410/month premium, $2,000 deductible, same coinsurance structure as PPO but in-network only. Total in-network: around $6,000.

POS: $430/month premium, similar to EPO with out-of-network option adding maybe $500 in out-of-network costs: around $6,700.

For a healthy person who stays in network, the HMO wins by a mile. For someone who has one out-of-network specialist visit that would have cost $2,000 in full, the math flips quickly.

The calculation you actually need to do: estimate your real care usage, identify your must-see providers, check which networks they're in, then price out the total scenarios. The premium alone is bait. Don't take it.

sections.heading: Network Verification — The Step Everyone Skips
sections.content:
This deserves its own section because it's where people consistently get burned.

Every insurer has an online 'find a doctor' tool. These tools are wrong surprisingly often — doctors leave networks, update their participation, change locations — and the insurer databases lag by months. So 'in-network' on the website doesn't always mean in-network when you show up.

Call the doctor's office directly. Ask: 'Do you take [plan name] from [insurer]? And do you take [specific plan type — HMO vs PPO]?' This is the only reliable verification. Spending five minutes on this call can save you four figures.

For EPOs and HMOs in particular, also verify whether the hospital your doctor uses is in-network. A surgeon can be in-network but operate at an out-of-network facility — you'll pay in-network rates for the surgeon and facility rates that blow your deductible into another ZIP code.

And if you're on an HMO or POS: confirm the specialist you want accepts referrals from your specific PCP. In theory all in-network providers work together. In practice some specialists have informal relationships or capacity limits that affect this.

sections.heading: Which Plan Type Should You Actually Pick
sections.content:
Here's the honest breakdown, not the hedged version.

Pick an HMO if: you're generally healthy, you have a good primary care doctor (or don't care much), you mostly live in one city, and you want the lowest premiums. This is probably the right call for more people than choose it.

Pick a PPO if: you have ongoing specialist care, you split time between cities or travel a lot for work, you have out-of-network providers you genuinely need access to, or you just hate referrals and are willing to pay for the autonomy. The premium is real but so is the flexibility.

Pick an EPO if: you want direct specialist access without referrals, you're confident in your network, and you want to save money compared to a PPO. It's a good call as long as you've actually verified your providers are in the network.

Pick a POS if: you have a specific out-of-network provider you need occasional access to, you're okay with the referral requirement, and the POS premium is meaningfully cheaper than the PPO. Otherwise, probably not worth the structure complexity.

The one-size answer doesn't exist. A 28-year-old freelancer who rarely sees a doctor and an 52-year-old with Type 2 diabetes, a cardiologist, and an endocrinologist have completely different answers here. Think through your actual usage, not your aspirational usage.

faq.question: Do I need a referral with an EPO plan?
faq.answer: Most EPO plans don't require referrals — that's one of the things that distinguishes them from HMOs. You can generally book directly with in-network specialists. That said, always check your specific plan documents because a small number of EPOs have built referral requirements in for certain specialists like behavioral health or physical therapy.

faq.question: What happens if I use an out-of-network provider on an HMO?
faq.answer: Outside of genuine emergencies, you'll pay 100% of the cost yourself. HMOs do not cover out-of-network care as a general rule. The only exception federal law requires is for emergency stabilization — if you're in a true medical emergency, you're covered regardless of network. But a non-emergency visit to an out-of-network doctor? That's on you entirely.

faq.question: Can I switch from an HMO to a PPO mid-year?
faq.answer: No, not without a qualifying life event — losing a job, getting married, having a child, moving to a new coverage area. Outside of open enrollment (usually November 1 through January 15 for ACA marketplace plans), your plan type is locked for the year. If you picked the wrong one, you're mostly stuck until the next open enrollment period.

faq.question: Is a PPO always better than an HMO?
faq.answer: No, and honestly the 'PPO is premium insurance' perception is mostly marketing. For people who stay in network, use a regular primary care doctor, and don't have complex specialist needs, an HMO will often cost thousands less per year for identical or better care. The PPO premium difference is worth it in specific situations, not universally.

faq.question: What does 'in-network' actually mean and why does it matter so much?
faq.answer: When a provider is in-network, they've negotiated a contracted rate with your insurer. That negotiated rate is typically 40-70% lower than the provider's full 'chargemaster' rate. So in-network, a $5,000 procedure might be billed at $1,800 after the contracted discount, and your cost-sharing applies to that $1,800. Out-of-network, you may face the full $5,000 with minimal insurance coverage — or balance billing on top of it. Network status is genuinely one of the highest-leverage factors in what you actually pay.

faq.question: Can my PCP change on an HMO without my knowing?
faq.answer: Technically, if your assigned PCP leaves the network mid-year, the insurer is supposed to notify you and help you transition to a new in-network PCP. In practice, this notification can be slow and the transition clunky. Always verify your PCP is still in-network at the start of each plan year, especially if you're on an HMO.

title: Health Insurance for the Self-Employed: Every Option Explained
metaDescription: No employer benefits, no HR department to explain your options. Here's a complete guide to health insurance for freelancers, contractors, and small business owners — including the tax deduction most people underuse.
h1: Health Insurance for the Self-Employed: Every Option Explained
summary: When you leave a job — or never had one — you lose the thing most people take for granted: an employer eating 70-80% of your health insurance premium and handling all the paperwork.

sections.heading: The Self-Employed Coverage Problem
sections.content:
When you leave a job — or never had one — you lose the thing most people take for granted: an employer eating 70-80% of your health insurance premium and handling all the paperwork. That group plan your old employer offered? The company was likely paying $600-900/month toward your coverage. You just saw the $150 payroll deduction.

Now it's all on you. And in 2026, the ACA's enhanced premium tax credits that temporarily softened this blow for marketplace enrollees have expired. Premiums on the marketplace for a 40-year-old are running $450-700/month before subsidies in most markets. Family plans are regularly north of $1,500/month.

This is hard. But there are more options than most self-employed people realize, and the tax strategy layer can meaningfully change the actual cost. Let's go through everything.

sections.heading: ACA Marketplace — The Default Starting Point
sections.content:
The ACA marketplace (healthcare.gov, or your state exchange) is where most self-employed people land, and for good reason. It's the most regulated option, it covers pre-existing conditions, and subsidies based on income can make it affordable depending on where you land on the income spectrum.

How subsidies work in 2026: Premium Tax Credits (PTCs) are based on your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) relative to the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). The ARP-era enhanced subsidies expired at the end of 2025, so the old income cliff is back. If your MAGI exceeds 400% of the FPL — roughly $60,240 for a single person in 2026 — you lose subsidy eligibility entirely. One dollar over the line costs you the whole credit. This matters a lot if you're a freelancer with variable income.

The benchmark plan used to calculate subsidies is the second-lowest-cost silver plan in your area (the SLCSP). Your subsidy is calculated to bring that plan to no more than a certain percentage of your income. But here's the thing — you don't have to buy that silver plan. You can take the subsidy amount and apply it to any metal tier. Buying a bronze plan with a silver subsidy can mean near-zero premiums if your income is low enough.

For 2026, bronze and catastrophic plans on the marketplace are all classified as HDHPs — High Deductible Health Plans. That means they're HSA-eligible by default, which adds a major tax savings layer (more on this later).

Income volatility is the hardest part. If you estimate your income at the start of the year and it ends up 30% higher, you may owe back a substantial portion of the tax credits you already collected. If it ends up lower, you get a refund. For freelancers this creates real financial planning tension — you're essentially betting on your own earnings 12 months out.

Practical move: if your income fluctuates, either estimate conservatively and accept a potentially higher monthly cost, or plan to recapture credits at tax time. Do not overestimate income to get lower credits and then end up owing a windfall. The reconciliation math goes both ways but the surprise refund is nicer than the surprise bill.

sections.heading: Getting on a Spouse's or Domestic Partner's Plan
sections.content:
This is the most underrated option and the first one to check.

If your spouse has employer-sponsored coverage and their employer allows adding dependents or spouses, getting on that plan is almost always cheaper than buying your own marketplace coverage. Even if the employer only covers the employee (not dependents), the group rates the employer negotiated are typically better than anything you'll find on the individual market.

The cost varies wildly. Some employers offer free family coverage — you'd be foolish not to use it. Others charge the employee 100% of the family premium, which can run $800-1,400/month in payroll deductions. At that point you'd want to compare against marketplace options with any subsidies applied.

Worth knowing: if your spouse's employer offers coverage that meets ACA minimum value and affordability standards, you're not eligible for premium tax credits on the marketplace even if the family premium would be a significant budget burden. This is the 'family glitch' — partially fixed in 2023 but still complex depending on your situation. If the employee-only coverage is 'affordable' (under 9.02% of household income in 2026) but the family addition is expensive, there may be a hybrid approach worth modeling with a benefits advisor.

sections.heading: COBRA as a Bridge
sections.content:
If you just left a job, you have up to 60 days to elect COBRA and maintain your exact same employer plan. This is worth considering as a temporary bridge — not a long-term strategy.

COBRA costs the full premium plus a 2% administrative fee. Whatever your employer was paying toward your coverage gets added to your share. A plan that cost you $200/month via payroll deductions might cost $700-850/month on COBRA. That hurts.

But the value proposition is continuity. If you're mid-treatment, have a deductible you've already partially met, or just had a baby and don't want to change anything mid-year, COBRA preserves your exact coverage and provider relationships without interruption.

The 60-day election window is the key thing. You have 60 days from your coverage loss date (or from when you receive the COBRA election notice, whichever is later) to decide. If you elect and then decide it's too expensive, you can drop it. But you can't retroactively elect it after the 60-day window closes — so don't let it expire if you think you might need it.

If you stay on COBRA and then lose it at 18 months, that's a qualifying event that opens a marketplace special enrollment period. The transition is smooth if you plan it.

sections.heading: SHOP Marketplace — For Small Business Owners With Employees
sections.content:
If you have at least one W-2 employee (yourself doesn't count if you're a sole proprietor), you may be eligible for SHOP — the Small Business Health Options Program.

SHOP lets small employers with 1-50 full-time equivalent employees offer ACA-compliant health insurance and potentially qualify for the Small Business Health Care Tax Credit. The credit can cover up to 50% of premiums paid for employees (25% for nonprofits) if you have fewer than 25 full-time equivalent employees with average wages under $56,000/year.

The honest assessment: SHOP is more administratively complex than marketplace coverage and isn't available in all states. For a solo operator or very small shop, the math often doesn't work out significantly better than the individual marketplace. But if you're building a team and want to offer benefits to attract employees, it's the right framework to start with.

Alternatively, many small employers now offer QSEHRA — Qualified Small Employer HRA. Instead of choosing and administering a group plan, you give employees a monthly allowance ($6,350/individual or $12,800/family in 2026) to buy their own marketplace coverage and get reimbursed tax-free. Much simpler to run, still gives your team a benefit, and the amounts are deductible to the business.

sections.heading: Health Sharing Ministries — What They Are and Aren't
sections.content:
Health sharing ministries get mentioned in every self-employed health insurance discussion, so they deserve honest treatment.

These are not insurance. They are cost-sharing arrangements among members who agree to help pay each other's medical bills according to shared religious or ethical values. They're exempt from ACA regulations, which means they can and do exclude pre-existing conditions, don't cover mental health or substance abuse treatment (in most cases), and have caps on benefits that wouldn't exist in a regulated plan.

They're significantly cheaper — a healthy 40-year-old might pay $300-400/month for a sharing ministry plan versus $500-650 for a bronze ACA plan. And for routine catastrophic coverage for a healthy person, they sometimes work fine.

But here's what people find out too late: the 'sharing' is voluntary. Other members aren't legally required to share your bills. If the ministry has cash flow problems or your claim doesn't meet their guidelines — guidelines that are more subjective than an insurance contract — you can be left holding a $50,000 hospital bill with no legal recourse.

They're also not HSA-eligible. Premiums are not deductible as self-employed health insurance premiums. And counting on them as your primary coverage carries real financial risk that most marketing materials don't emphasize.

If you're young, healthy, have a strong emergency fund, understand the limitations completely, and want to save money — go in with eyes open. But don't mistake them for insurance.

sections.heading: The Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction
sections.content:
This is the biggest tax lever available to self-employed people and it's underused — partly because it doesn't show up in the standard deduction calculation and partly because the interaction with ACA subsidies is confusing.

Here's the deal: if you're self-employed (sole proprietor, LLC taxed as sole prop, partnership, S-corp shareholder with > 2% ownership), you can deduct 100% of health insurance premiums paid for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents — directly from gross income. This is an above-the-line deduction, meaning it reduces your AGI even if you take the standard deduction. It doesn't require itemizing.

For someone paying $600/month in premiums ($7,200/year) in the 22% tax bracket, this deduction saves about $1,584 in federal income tax. Plus it reduces self-employment tax exposure in some cases.

The complexity: this deduction reduces your MAGI, which is the income used to calculate ACA premium tax credits. But your ACA premium tax credit is also based on your health insurance costs... and the calculation is circular. The IRS has a worksheet (Form 7206) that iterates to a solution, but it's not intuitive. If you're getting marketplace subsidies AND taking the self-employed deduction, run the numbers carefully or pay your accountant the hour it takes to do it right.

One important limit: the deduction can't exceed your net self-employment income for the year. If you had a bad year and your business lost money, you can't deduct more health insurance premiums than you earned from self-employment.

sections.heading: Pairing HSA with a High-Deductible Plan
sections.content:
If you're buying a high-deductible health plan (HDHP) — deductible of at least $1,700 for self-only coverage or $3,400 for family coverage in 2026 — you're eligible to open and contribute to a Health Savings Account.

The 2026 contribution limits: $4,400 for self-only coverage, $8,750 for family coverage. If you're 55 or older, add $1,000 more as a catch-up contribution.

HSA contributions are deductible above-the-line, grow tax-free if invested, and are withdrawn tax-free for qualified medical expenses. It's the only triple-tax-advantaged account in the U.S. tax code.

For self-employed people, this is a particularly powerful strategy: lower-premium HDHP (saving $150-200/month versus a low-deductible plan), self-employed premium deduction on that premium, AND HSA contributions reducing AGI further. The combined tax savings can effectively reduce your net health insurance cost by 30-40% compared to face value.

The execution: set up an HSA at Fidelity (no fees, 2.19% on cash), contribute the maximum, and invest it in index funds if you don't need it immediately. Let it compound. You can withdraw for qualified medical expenses at any time, now or 40 years from now. There's no use-it-or-lose-it rule like an FSA.

sections.heading: Building Your 2026 Strategy
sections.content:
If you're self-employed in 2026 and trying to figure this out, here's the decision sequence that actually makes sense.

First: is your spouse on employer coverage that allows you to join at reasonable cost? Check this first. If yes, that's usually your best deal.

Second: what's your projected MAGI? If it's under 400% FPL (~$60,240 single, ~$124,800 family of four in 2026), run the marketplace subsidy calculator. The enhanced subsidies are gone but standard subsidies still apply at lower income levels and can be significant.

Third: if your MAGI puts you over the subsidy cliff or close to it, look hard at bronze HDHP plans paired with an HSA. The tax deductions on premiums plus HSA contributions can meaningfully lower your effective cost.

Fourth: if you have employees, evaluate QSEHRA. It's simpler than running a group plan and still gives your team a benefit.

Fifth: COBRA as a short-term bridge if you just left employment and have in-progress care or a partially-met deductible — but don't let it become a permanent strategy at $800/month.

faq.question: Can I deduct health insurance if my business lost money this year?
faq.answer: The self-employed health insurance deduction is limited to your net self-employment income. If your business had a net loss, you can't take the deduction. However, you might still be able to deduct those premiums as an itemized medical expense on Schedule A (subject to the 7.5% AGI floor), which is a weaker benefit but not nothing.

faq.question: Do I need to buy health insurance if I'm self-employed?
faq.answer: There's no longer a federal individual mandate penalty. You're not legally required to have insurance. But one major medical event without coverage can wipe out years of savings — a three-day hospital stay routinely runs $30,000-80,000 at full rates. The legal question and the financial risk question have very different answers.

faq.question: What is MAGI and why does it matter for marketplace subsidies?
faq.answer: MAGI is Modified Adjusted Gross Income — essentially your AGI with certain items added back. It's the income figure the ACA uses to calculate premium tax credits. Your self-employed health insurance deduction reduces your AGI, which reduces your MAGI, which can improve your subsidy eligibility. That's why the interaction between these two is important to calculate correctly.

faq.question: Can I get an HSA if I buy my insurance through the marketplace?
faq.answer: Yes, but only if the marketplace plan qualifies as an HDHP. In 2026, all bronze and catastrophic plans on the ACA marketplace are classified as HDHPs, so they're HSA-eligible by default. Silver, gold, and platinum plans generally are not HDHP-qualified and don't allow HSA contributions.

faq.question: What's the income limit for ACA subsidies in 2026?
faq.answer: With the expiration of enhanced subsidies at the end of 2025, the traditional 400% FPL cliff is back for 2026. For a single person, that's approximately $60,240/year. For a family of four, approximately $124,800. Earn one dollar over that threshold and you lose premium tax credit eligibility entirely — the 'subsidy cliff' is real again and can cost you thousands.

faq.question: Is a health sharing ministry tax deductible like insurance premiums?
faq.answer: No. Health sharing ministry payments are not deductible as self-employed health insurance premiums because sharing ministries are not insurance plans. They also don't qualify for HSA pairing. Some members have tried deducting them as charitable contributions, but that's on shaky ground and has been challenged by the IRS.

title: COBRA Insurance: How Long, How Much, and What to Do Instead
metaDescription: COBRA lets you keep your employer health plan after losing coverage — but at full price plus 2%. Here's how long it lasts, what it actually costs, and whether the marketplace might be a better and cheaper option.
h1: COBRA Insurance: How Long, How Much, and What to Do Instead
summary: COBRA stands for Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act — a 1985 federal law that requires most employers to let departing employees continue their grou

sections.heading: What COBRA Actually Is
sections.content:
COBRA stands for Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act — a 1985 federal law that requires most employers to let departing employees continue their group health plan coverage for a period of time after their coverage would otherwise end.

Before COBRA, losing your job meant losing your health insurance immediately. If you were mid-treatment, tough. Your kids were on your plan? Tough. COBRA changed that. It created a continuation right — you can stay on the exact same plan you had, with the exact same network and benefits, for a defined period.

The catch: you pay for all of it. Every dollar your employer was contributing toward your premium, plus your own share, plus a 2% administrative fee. That full cost is typically two to four times what you were paying through payroll deductions.

It's not a subsidy. It's not charity. It's just the option to buy into your old group plan at group rates — which are still often better than what's available on the individual market, though the ACA has narrowed that gap considerably.

sections.heading: Who Qualifies and What the Qualifying Events Are
sections.content:
COBRA applies to employers with 20 or more employees. If you worked somewhere with 19 people, federal COBRA doesn't apply — though many states have 'mini-COBRA' laws that extend similar rights to smaller employers, sometimes down to 2 employees.

The qualifying event is what triggers your COBRA rights. Not every separation from employment qualifies and the event matters enormously because it determines how long coverage lasts.

For employees, qualifying events are: voluntary or involuntary job loss (except for gross misconduct) and reduction in hours that causes loss of coverage. These give you 18 months of continuation coverage.

For spouses and dependent children, the list is longer: the qualifying events above, plus the covered employee becoming eligible for Medicare, divorce or legal separation from the covered employee, and death of the covered employee. These give the spouse and dependents 36 months.

Dependent children have one additional qualifying event: aging out of dependent status under the plan (usually turning 26 under ACA rules). This also triggers 36 months of COBRA eligibility for the former dependent.

The gross misconduct exception is worth noting. If you're fired for gross misconduct — and the standard is high, not just 'fired for cause' — the employer can deny you COBRA. This is relatively rare and contested, but it exists.

sections.heading: How Long COBRA Lasts
sections.content:
The standard duration is 18 months for employees who lose their jobs or have hours reduced. This is the most common scenario.

The 36-month scenarios apply to spouses and dependent children in specific situations: death of the covered employee, divorce or legal separation, the employee becoming entitled to Medicare, or a dependent child aging off the plan. In these cases, the family members can continue coverage for 36 months from the original qualifying event.

There's also a layering provision that extends the 18-month period. If a second qualifying event occurs during the 18-month COBRA period — say the employee dies or gets divorced while the family is on COBRA from a job loss — the spouse and dependents can extend their coverage to the full 36-month maximum.

Disability can also extend coverage. If a COBRA beneficiary is determined to be disabled by Social Security within the first 60 days of COBRA continuation, coverage can be extended from 18 to 29 months for all qualified beneficiaries in the family. A small surcharge (up to 150% of the premium instead of 102%) applies during months 19-29.

There's no provision for extending beyond 36 months at any point. When COBRA runs out, it runs out — which is a significant planning consideration if you're using COBRA as a bridge and need to have your next coverage option ready before the clock expires.

sections.heading: What COBRA Actually Costs in 2026
sections.content:
This is where people get shocked.

The formula is simple: the full group premium (employee + employer share) plus up to 2%. But most employees have no idea what the full premium is because they only see their payroll deduction.

National averages in 2026: the total premium for employer-sponsored single coverage averages around $8,400/year ($700/month). For family coverage it's around $24,000/year ($2,000/month). Employees typically paid 18-25% of that — say $150/month for single, $500/month for family. COBRA flips that completely.

So: that plan that cost you $150/month on payroll just became $714/month on COBRA (100% of $700 + 2%). That $500/month family plan? Now $2,040/month. These aren't edge cases — they're the math.

Regional variation is significant. Vermont averages around $1,275/month for a single plan on COBRA. Idaho averages around $307/month. Where you live matters.

There's also the deductible consideration that cuts both ways. If you're electing COBRA mid-year and you've already met $800 of your $2,000 deductible, your remaining exposure on that plan is only $1,200. Switching to a new plan mid-year resets your deductible to zero. That can make COBRA's high premium worth it for the rest of the calendar year — you're effectively prepaying against a deductible you'd have to meet again anyway on a new plan.

sections.heading: The Election Window and Retroactive Coverage
sections.content:
The COBRA election window is 60 days. You have 60 days from either the date coverage ends or the date you receive the COBRA election notice — whichever is later — to decide.

Here's the piece that saves people: COBRA coverage is retroactive to the day your employer coverage ended. If you elect COBRA on day 59, your coverage is continuous from day 1. This means you can wait and see if you need care before committing. If you stay healthy for two months and then elect on day 59, you've saved two months of premium. If something happens on day 45, you can elect on day 46 and submit that claim retroactively.

The administration of this is clunky — you'll likely need to pay the back-premiums for the months you didn't pay — but the retroactive protection is real. Don't elect COBRA automatically on day one unless you have immediate ongoing care. Evaluate your situation, price your marketplace alternatives during those 60 days, and decide with full information.

One timing trap: the 60-day window can close before you've even received the notice if your former employer is slow in sending it. Employers are required to send the COBRA notice within 14 days of being notified of the qualifying event, but delays happen. Track the date your coverage actually ends and count from there.

sections.heading: The Marketplace Alternative — Often Cheaper With Subsidies
sections.content:
This is the comparison most people don't run because they assume their employer plan was good and COBRA must therefore be the best option. That assumption is often wrong in 2026.

If your income qualifies for ACA premium tax credits — under 400% of the Federal Poverty Level, roughly $60,240 for a single person in 2026 — a marketplace plan with subsidies can be significantly cheaper than COBRA. Sometimes dramatically so.

A 38-year-old in Texas who just lost a $55,000/year job: COBRA on their old employer plan might run $680/month. On the marketplace with an estimated $55,000 income (or lower if they expect to earn less while job hunting), they might qualify for a silver plan at $200-350/month after subsidies. Same broad coverage category, similar benefit structure, potentially $300-400/month cheaper.

Losing employer coverage is a qualifying life event that opens a 60-day Special Enrollment Period on the marketplace. You don't have to wait for open enrollment. If you lose job-based coverage, go to healthcare.gov and price it out before making the COBRA decision.

The comparison isn't always in the marketplace's favor. If you have ongoing care with in-network providers on your current plan, if you've partially met your deductible, if your family has complex needs that fit the current plan well — COBRA continuity has real value that pure premium comparison doesn't capture. But do the comparison. Don't assume.

sections.heading: State COBRA and Mini-COBRA Laws
sections.content:
Federal COBRA only applies to employers with 20 or more employees. But most states have their own continuation coverage laws — often called 'mini-COBRA' — that cover smaller employers.

These state laws vary significantly. Some are quite generous: California's Cal-COBRA covers groups of 2-19 employees. New York extends COBRA-equivalent rights to employers with just 2 employees. Many states use 20 employees as the threshold like the federal law.

The duration also varies. Some state mini-COBRA laws offer 12 months of continuation instead of 18. A few offer the same 18 months. The premium calculation is generally similar — full premium plus an administrative fee.

If you worked for a small employer and are trying to figure out your rights, contact your state's insurance commissioner or department of insurance. The rules differ enough that what applies in Florida isn't what applies in Massachusetts, and the difference can be thousands of dollars in coverage decisions.

Also worth knowing: church plans and federal government plans are exempt from COBRA. Federal employees have equivalent protection through FEHBP (Federal Employees Health Benefits Program) with different rules.

sections.heading: When COBRA Actually Makes Sense
sections.content:
Despite the high cost, COBRA is genuinely the right call in specific situations.

You're mid-treatment: cancer treatment, pregnancy, surgery recovery, physical therapy for an injury. Switching plans mid-treatment means changing networks, potentially changing providers, and resetting deductibles. The continuity value can easily exceed $200-300/month in premium difference, especially if you'd hit specialists or facilities that would be out-of-network on a new plan.

You've met your deductible: if it's October and you've already paid $3,000 toward your $3,500 deductible, the remaining $500 exposure for the year is cheap insurance on COBRA versus starting over at zero on a marketplace plan. Run the math for the specific calendar year period.

You expect to land a new job quickly: if you're confident you'll have employer coverage again in 60-90 days, COBRA might be cleaner than starting a new marketplace plan and then canceling it. Though the gap period on a marketplace plan is fine too — you can enroll and cancel when your new employer coverage starts.

You have a complex health situation where network continuity matters enormously: if your care team is all on your current plan and switching networks would require finding all new providers for ongoing issues, that's a real cost that a premium comparison doesn't capture.

sections.heading: What Happens When COBRA Ends
sections.content:
The end of COBRA is itself a qualifying event for marketplace Special Enrollment. When your 18 or 36 months expire, you have 60 days to enroll in a marketplace plan without waiting for open enrollment. This is the designed transition — COBRA bridges you to a new plan.

Don't let COBRA expire without having your next plan ready. The 60-day SEP after COBRA loss is generous, but you don't want to be uninsured while you figure it out. The month before your COBRA end date, start your marketplace research.

Also: you can voluntarily drop COBRA before it expires. That's also a qualifying event for marketplace SEP. So if you're on COBRA, find a significantly cheaper marketplace option, and want to switch — you can do that. Voluntarily terminating COBRA opens your marketplace window. But it has to be voluntary — you can't miss payments to force termination and claim it was involuntary loss.

The COBRA-to-Medicare transition is worth noting separately. If you're approaching 65 while on COBRA, Medicare enrollment timing is critical. COBRA does not delay Medicare enrollment the way employer coverage does. If you're eligible for Medicare Part A (free) and don't enroll, you can face late enrollment penalties on Part B later. The two systems interact in ways that require careful coordination if you're in this window.

faq.question: How long do I have to elect COBRA after losing my job?
faq.answer: You have 60 days to elect COBRA. The 60-day clock starts from the later of two dates: when your coverage actually ends or when you receive the COBRA election notice from your former employer. If you elect on day 59, your coverage is retroactive to the day it ended — you're not uninsured during the gap, though you'd owe the back-premiums.

faq.question: Does COBRA cover dependents?
faq.answer: Yes, and your dependents can elect COBRA independently of you. Each qualified beneficiary — you, your spouse, each dependent child — can elect or decline COBRA individually. If you decline COBRA but your spouse and children want to keep the coverage, they can elect it without you.

faq.question: Can I be denied COBRA coverage?
faq.answer: The employer can deny COBRA if your separation was due to gross misconduct — a high bar that goes beyond ordinary termination for cause. You can also lose COBRA eligibility if you fail to pay premiums on time, become covered under another group health plan, or become entitled to Medicare. Outside of those situations, the continuation right is federally guaranteed.

faq.question: Is COBRA coverage exactly the same as my old plan?
faq.answer: Yes, in the sense that it's the same plan document, same network, same benefits structure, and same pharmacy coverage. You're not getting a watered-down version — you're getting exactly the same plan your employer offered. The only difference is you're now paying the full cost yourself. If the plan changes during your COBRA period (the employer changes the group plan), those same changes apply to you.

faq.question: Can I switch from COBRA to a marketplace plan before COBRA expires?
faq.answer: Yes. Voluntarily dropping COBRA is a qualifying life event that opens a 60-day marketplace Special Enrollment Period. If you find a marketplace plan that's significantly cheaper — particularly if you qualify for income-based subsidies — you can drop COBRA and enroll. Just be sure to sequence it correctly so you don't have a coverage gap.

faq.question: What if I miss a COBRA payment?
faq.answer: There's a 30-day grace period for COBRA premium payments. If you miss a payment but pay within 30 days of the due date, coverage continues uninterrupted. If you miss the 30-day window, coverage terminates — and unlike the initial election, it's not retroactively reinstatable. Set up auto-pay or a calendar reminder. This is an area where administrative sloppiness can cost you coverage.

title: Health Savings Account (HSA) Complete Guide for 2026
metaDescription: The only triple-tax-advantaged account in the U.S. tax code. 2026 limits, investment strategy, best providers, and how to use your HSA as a retirement vehicle — not just a medical bill fund.
h1: Health Savings Account (HSA) Complete Guide for 2026
summary: There are a lot of tax-advantaged accounts. 401k, Roth IRA, FSA, 529. But the HSA is categorically different from all of them because of the triple tax advantag

sections.heading: What Makes an HSA Actually Different
sections.content:
There are a lot of tax-advantaged accounts. 401k, Roth IRA, FSA, 529. But the HSA is categorically different from all of them because of the triple tax advantage — the only account in the U.S. tax code with all three:

1. Contributions are tax-deductible (or pre-tax if through payroll)
2. Growth is tax-free
3. Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free
