For comparison: a Roth IRA has tax-free growth and withdrawals but contributions aren't deductible. A traditional 401k has deductible contributions and tax-free growth but withdrawals are taxed. An FSA has pre-tax contributions but use-it-or-lose-it rules and no investment growth.

The HSA has all three wins and no use-it-or-lose-it constraint. Money you don't spend rolls over forever. Contribute at 30, invest it, and withdraw at 70 for a knee replacement — still tax-free. That compounding tax-free growth over decades is extraordinary and almost nobody optimizes for it.

The catch: you need to be enrolled in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) to contribute. That's the access requirement, and it's real. But in 2026 it's easier to satisfy than ever — all ACA marketplace bronze plans and catastrophic plans are classified as HDHPs by default.

sections.heading: 2026 Contribution Limits and HDHP Requirements
sections.content:
The IRS sets HSA limits annually based on inflation adjustments. For 2026:

Self-only HDHP coverage: $4,400 maximum annual contribution
Family HDHP coverage: $8,750 maximum annual contribution
Catch-up contribution (age 55+): $1,000 additional per eligible person

So a married couple, both over 55, on a family HDHP could contribute $10,750 total in 2026. That's $10,750 of deductible contributions, growing tax-free, with tax-free withdrawals for medical costs. The math on this is enormous over time.

For your plan to qualify as an HDHP in 2026, it must meet minimum deductible thresholds:
- Self-only: deductible of at least $1,700
- Family: deductible of at least $3,400

And out-of-pocket maximums can't exceed:
- Self-only: $8,550
- Family: $17,100

The HDHP requirement is the one thing that makes people hesitate. High deductibles feel scary. But here's the reframe: the lower premium on an HDHP versus a low-deductible plan often exceeds or matches the deductible difference — especially when you factor in HSA contributions. You're not necessarily exposed to more risk; you're repositioning who holds the cash buffer.

sections.heading: How HSA Contributions Work
sections.content:
There are two ways to fund an HSA: through payroll deduction if your employer offers it, or by making your own direct contributions.

Payroll deduction is better. When contributions come through payroll, they're pre-tax — meaning they avoid federal income tax AND Social Security/Medicare taxes (FICA). That's an additional 7.65% savings compared to making a deductible contribution yourself outside of payroll. Over a career of maximum contributions, that FICA exemption adds up to thousands.

Direct contributions (outside payroll) are deductible on your federal taxes as an above-the-line deduction on Schedule 1. You don't need to itemize. But you don't get the FICA benefit. For self-employed people this is still the path, and it's still excellent — you're just leaving the FICA benefit on the table.

Contribution timing: you can make contributions anytime during the tax year, up to the tax filing deadline (April 15 of the following year). So you can contribute to your 2026 HSA as late as April 15, 2027. This gives you flexibility to figure out your full contribution after you know your year-end income.

Partial year eligibility is a real thing. If you enroll in an HDHP mid-year, you can contribute the full annual limit — but only if you remain HDHP-eligible through December 31 of the following year. This is called the last-month rule. If you switch off the HDHP before the testing period ends, you'll owe income tax plus a 10% penalty on the 'excess' contributions. Most people just prorate the year to be safe.

sections.heading: Qualified Medical Expenses — What Counts
sections.content:
The list of qualified medical expenses is broader than most people realize. IRS Publication 502 is the full reference, but the headline items:

Deductibles, copays, and coinsurance — yes. Prescription drugs — yes. Dental care including cleanings, fillings, crowns, orthodontia — yes. Vision care including exams, glasses, contacts — yes. Mental health services and therapy — yes. Chiropractic care — yes. Acupuncture — yes. Hearing aids — yes. Long-term care premiums (up to limits based on age) — yes. COBRA premiums while unemployed — yes. Medicare Part B, Part D, and Medicare Advantage premiums — yes (not Medigap though).

What doesn't count: health insurance premiums in general (with the Medicare exception noted above), cosmetic procedures, gym memberships, vitamins and supplements unless prescribed, and over-the-counter medications that aren't prescribed.

Wait — actually, that last one changed. The CARES Act made over-the-counter medications and menstrual care products HSA-eligible without a prescription starting in 2020 and permanently. So now Tylenol, Claritin, antacids — all qualify. Keep your receipts.

Critically: there's no time limit on reimbursement. You can pay a medical expense out of pocket in 2026, keep the receipt forever, let your HSA grow invested for 20 years, and then withdraw in 2046 to reimburse yourself for that 2026 expense. This is called the 'shoebox strategy' and it's completely legal and powerful. The IRS just says you need documentation of the original expense — date, amount, what it was for.

sections.heading: Investing Your HSA — The Part Most People Skip
sections.content:
Most people use their HSA like a checking account. Money goes in, bills come out, nothing is invested. That's leaving enormous value on the table.

The math: if you contribute $4,400/year for 30 years and invest it earning 7% (a modest index fund assumption), you end up with about $440,000 tax-free. If instead you just kept cash in the HSA, you'd have $132,000. The difference is more than $300,000. All tax-free for medical expenses.

Most major HSA providers now offer investment options. Some are good. Some are terrible — high-fee mutual funds, limited investment menus, or restrictions that require a minimum cash balance before you can invest anything.

The best approach: think of your HSA as two accounts in your head. The 'current year medical expenses' bucket — maybe $1,500-2,000 in cash to cover routine costs. And the 'long-term wealth' bucket — everything else invested in low-cost index funds.

Some people invest 100% of their HSA and pay current medical expenses out of pocket, filing away the receipts for future tax-free reimbursement. This is the aggressive-optimization version and it makes sense if you can afford to pay current expenses from other funds.

Investment options by provider vary significantly. Fidelity stands alone at the top of the market in 2026 — commission-free trading, access to Fidelity index funds with zero expense ratios, no maintenance fees, and 2.19% interest on uninvested cash. The gap between Fidelity and lower-tier providers isn't small. If you're going to maximize the investment potential of your HSA, provider selection matters.

sections.heading: Best HSA Providers in 2026
sections.content:
Not all HSA providers are equal. The fee structures, investment menus, and interest rates vary enough to meaningfully affect your outcomes over time. Here's the honest breakdown of the top three.

Fidelity HSA: Flat out the best for investors. No monthly maintenance fees. No minimum balance. No minimum to invest. Access to Fidelity's zero-expense-ratio index funds, individual stocks, and ETFs through a full brokerage account. Cash earns 2.19% — higher than every other major provider. No investment account fee. For someone optimizing the HSA as a long-term investment vehicle, Fidelity is the default choice. The only downside: Fidelity isn't available as an employer-sponsored HSA option everywhere, so if you want Fidelity, you might need to open it individually.

Lively HSA: Best overall for most people in the employer-sponsored context. No fees for the base account. FDIC-insured cash. Investment options through Schwab (full brokerage) or Devenir managed funds. The catch: if your account balance is under $3,000 in cash, there's a $24/year fee for the Schwab investing option. Lively has a clean interface, mobile app, and easy receipt management that makes the day-to-day less annoying. Good choice if your employer offers it as a benefit option.

HSA Bank: One of the largest HSA providers by assets under management. No monthly maintenance fees (recently removed them). Investment through Devenir managed funds or self-directed ETF trading. Fee on investments ranging 0.10%-0.35% of AUM, waived when cash balance exceeds $7,500/quarter. Not as clean as Fidelity for the full-investment approach, but solid and widely available.

Provider advice: if your employer offers an HSA with matching contributions, use that account regardless of provider — the match is free money that beats any fee structure differential. Once you're no longer at that employer, roll the balance to Fidelity. HSA rollovers are allowed once per year without tax consequences.

sections.heading: HSA as a Retirement Account
sections.content:
Here's the reframe that changes how you think about HSAs.

After age 65, you can withdraw from your HSA for any reason without penalty. If you withdraw for a non-qualified expense, you just pay ordinary income tax — exactly like a traditional IRA. So at 65+, your HSA effectively becomes a traditional IRA for any purpose, plus retains the tax-free benefit for medical expenses specifically.

Given that Medicare premiums, out-of-pocket costs, and long-term care represent some of the largest expenses in retirement — Fidelity estimates the average 65-year-old couple needs $315,000 in today's dollars for retirement healthcare costs — an HSA that you've invested aggressively for 20-30 years is one of the best retirement assets you can hold.

HSA and Medicare: once you enroll in Medicare (any part), you can no longer contribute to an HSA. This is a hard stop. But you can continue to use HSA funds for qualified expenses, including Medicare Part B premiums, Part D premiums, and Medicare Advantage premiums — just not Medigap premiums. The money you've accumulated keeps working; you just can't add more.

Strategic implication: if you can afford to delay Medicare enrollment (which requires also not taking Social Security before 65), you can continue contributing to your HSA on an employer HDHP up to 65. Every extra year of contributions and tax-free compounding has value.

sections.heading: Common HSA Mistakes to Avoid
sections.content:
Using it as a checking account: spending everything that goes in instead of investing the excess. Kills the long-term compounding.

Not keeping receipts: the shoebox strategy only works if you have documentation. IRS doesn't require you to submit receipts to the HSA provider, but if you get audited and claim a 2026 medical expense for a 2038 withdrawal, you need proof. Take photos of every receipt and store them in a dedicated folder — digital or physical, doesn't matter.

Missing the contribution deadline: you have until April 15 of the following year to contribute for the current tax year. People frequently miss this and leave deductible money on the table.

Withdrawing for non-qualified expenses before 65: you pay income tax plus a 20% penalty. That's brutal. Not worth it. The only exception is if you lose HDHP coverage — you stop contributing but the money already in there stays and continues to grow tax-free. Just leave it.

Forgetting about your old HSA: if you switched jobs and had an HSA with a former employer's plan, that money is still yours and still tax-advantaged. Roll it to Fidelity. Even a $2,000 balance invested in index funds is worth managing properly.

Contributing when ineligible: if you're enrolled in Medicare or covered by a second plan that disqualifies HDHP status (like an FSA at your spouse's employer), you're not eligible to contribute. Contributing when ineligible triggers taxes and penalties on the excess amount.

faq.question: Can I have an HSA if my employer doesn't offer one?
faq.answer: Yes. You can open an HSA at any eligible custodian — Fidelity, Lively, HSA Bank, others — as long as you're enrolled in a qualifying HDHP. You don't need employer sponsorship. You make contributions directly and take the above-the-line deduction on your taxes. You just miss the FICA savings that come with payroll deduction.

faq.question: What happens to my HSA if I switch to a non-HDHP plan?
faq.answer: The money in your HSA is yours permanently. You stop being eligible to make new contributions the day you're no longer covered by a qualifying HDHP, but the existing balance stays and can still be used tax-free for qualified medical expenses. The account doesn't close or get forfeited — it just becomes a spending account rather than an accumulation account.

faq.question: Can my spouse and I each have our own HSA?
faq.answer: Yes, but contribution limits apply to the household, not each individual. If you're on a family HDHP, the combined contributions to both spouse HSAs can't exceed $8,750 in 2026. You can split that however you want between the two accounts. Each spouse over 55 can also make their own $1,000 catch-up contribution — so a married couple both over 55 could contribute $10,750 total.

faq.question: Is the HSA deduction available in all states?
faq.answer: Federally, yes. But California and New Jersey don't recognize HSA tax-exempt status at the state level — residents in those states pay state income tax on HSA contributions and earnings. The federal tax treatment still applies, so it's still worth using, but the state benefit is absent. If you live in CA or NJ, the math changes but the HSA is still worth having.

faq.question: Can I use my HSA for my family's medical expenses?
faq.answer: Yes — even if only one spouse is enrolled in the HDHP, you can use HSA funds for any qualified medical expense for yourself, your spouse, and your tax dependents. So if your spouse is on your HDHP family plan, or if you have children on the plan, all of their qualified expenses can be paid from your HSA.

faq.question: What counts as a qualifying HDHP for HSA purposes?
faq.answer: In 2026, a qualifying HDHP must have a minimum deductible of $1,700 for self-only coverage or $3,400 for family coverage. Out-of-pocket maximums can't exceed $8,550 (self) or $17,100 (family). The plan also can't cover any expenses before the deductible is met, with the exception of preventive care — ACA preventive services must be covered at no cost even on HDHPs.

title: Short-Term Health Insurance: When It Makes Sense and When It Doesn't
metaDescription: Short-term health insurance is cheap, fast, and controversial. Here's what the federal 4-month cap means, which states have banned it, what pre-existing conditions have to do with it, and the specific situations where it's actually the right call.
h1: Short-Term Health Insurance: When It Makes Sense and When It Doesn't
summary: Short-term health insurance — formally called Short-Term Limited Duration Insurance (STLDI) — is exactly what it sounds like.

sections.heading: What Short-Term Health Insurance Is
sections.content:
Short-term health insurance — formally called Short-Term Limited Duration Insurance (STLDI) — is exactly what it sounds like. A health plan designed for temporary coverage gaps, sold on shorter terms than standard annual insurance, with fewer regulatory requirements than ACA-compliant plans.

It's not designed to be a primary coverage strategy. The plans are built to fill defined gaps: between jobs, waiting for an employer plan to start, aging off a parent's plan, or bridging to Medicare. Used that way, with realistic expectations about what they cover, they serve a legitimate purpose.

Used as a primary long-term coverage strategy — which some insurers actively encouraged for years before federal intervention — they're a disaster for anyone who gets sick. Pre-existing condition exclusions, annual and lifetime benefit caps, limited preventive coverage, no maternity coverage, no mental health coverage in most cases. If you're healthy and have no claims, you'll never notice the difference. If you get cancer six months in, you'll learn what 'not designed for primary coverage' actually means.

sections.heading: The Federal 4-Month Cap: What Changed in 2024
sections.content:
For years, federal rules allowed short-term plans to last up to 364 days with renewals up to 36 months total. The Trump administration in 2018 expanded that from 90 days to 364 days. The Biden administration reversed course.

In March 2024, the Departments of Treasury, HHS, and Labor finalized a rule limiting STLDI plans to:
- Maximum 3-month initial term
- Maximum 4 months total duration including any renewals
- No 'stacking' consecutive policies from the same insurer or affiliated insurer

This rule applies to plans sold or issued on or after September 1, 2024. So if you're shopping for a short-term plan in 2026, the federal maximum is 4 months, period. No exceptions at the federal level.

There's a wrinkle worth noting: as of early 2026, enforcement has been inconsistent at the federal level under the current administration. Some insurers are still selling longer-term plans in states that don't have their own stricter rules, citing uncertainty about enforcement priorities. This is a gray area that could clarify — or not — during the plan year. If you buy one expecting 4-month federal cap protection to limit your commitment, verify the specific policy terms rather than assuming.

The 4-month federal cap doesn't prevent you from buying a new short-term plan from a different insurer after your first one ends — but the new insurer can treat the gap as a fresh application, meaning pre-existing conditions acquired during the first plan would be newly excludable.

sections.heading: States That Have Banned or Heavily Restricted Short-Term Plans
sections.content:
Federal rules set the floor. States can go stricter, and many have.

As of 2026, approximately 14-15 states plus the District of Columbia have banned short-term health plans entirely or made them unavailable in practice:

California: banned.
New York: banned.
New Jersey: banned.
Massachusetts: effectively not available — state regulations make them non-viable.
Washington: banned.
Oregon: banned for durations over one month with strict coverage requirements.
Connecticut: banned.
Hawaii: banned.
Vermont: limited to 3 months, no renewals, no plans offered in practice.
New Mexico: limited to 3 months, no renewals, no plans actually available.
Illinois: banned as of January 1, 2025.
Colorado: banned.
Maryland: banned.
Rhode Island: banned.
District of Columbia: banned.

If you're in one of these states, short-term coverage isn't an option. Full stop. Your alternatives are ACA marketplace plans, Medicaid (if income-eligible), COBRA continuation, or — in limited cases — employer group coverage.

In the remaining states, STLDI plans are available but vary significantly in terms of duration limits, required benefit standards, and allowed exclusions. Some states require short-term plans to cover pre-existing conditions or include essential health benefit equivalents. Always check your specific state's rules, not just the federal framework.

sections.heading: Pre-Existing Condition Exclusions — The Big Risk
sections.content:
This is where short-term plans differ most fundamentally from ACA-compliant coverage, and it's where people get hurt.

ACA-compliant plans (marketplace, employer group, Medicaid) cannot deny coverage or charge more based on pre-existing conditions. Period. That protection doesn't exist on short-term plans.

A typical STLDI plan will exclude:
- Any condition you've been diagnosed with or treated for in the past 2-5 years (sometimes up to 10 years)
- Any condition for which you've sought medical advice, diagnosis, care, or treatment in the lookback period
- Any condition that a 'reasonably prudent person' should have sought treatment for — even if you didn't

That last one is the sneaky clause. You had some chest pain six months ago, never went to a doctor, didn't think much of it. Then on a short-term plan you have a heart attack. The insurer reviews your records, finds a single complaint about chest tightness in your chart from a year ago, and denies the claim. This happens.

The insurer also applies these exclusions at claim time, not at enrollment. You buy the plan, pay the premiums, and then discover at the moment you need coverage most whether your condition is excluded. The medical underwriting that ACA plans don't do, STLDI plans do — aggressively.

If you have any ongoing health conditions, recent medical history, or anticipate needing anything beyond basic acute care, short-term coverage is not appropriate coverage. This isn't hedging. It's the structure of the product.

sections.heading: What Short-Term Plans Typically Cover (and Don't)
sections.content:
Coverage varies significantly by plan and insurer, but the typical STLDI structure includes:

Usually covered: doctor visits for new illnesses or injuries, emergency room visits, hospitalization for accidents or new illness, urgent care, some prescription drugs for acute conditions, basic diagnostic testing for new conditions.

Typically NOT covered: pre-existing conditions (as described above), mental health and substance abuse treatment (most plans), maternity care and pregnancy, preventive care like annual physicals and cancer screenings, pediatric services (dental, vision), and any ACA-mandated essential health benefits.

Benefit caps are common — many short-term plans cap benefits at $250,000-$500,000 per year or per benefit period. A serious illness — leukemia, major cardiac event, premature birth — can blow through those caps. ACA plans have no annual or lifetime caps on essential health benefits.

Premiums are significantly cheaper: a healthy 30-year-old might pay $90-150/month for a short-term plan versus $300-450 on a bronze ACA plan. The difference is real. But understand precisely what you're buying. The lower premium reflects lower coverage, not a bargain on the same product.

sections.heading: When Short-Term Coverage Is Actually the Right Call
sections.content:
Used appropriately, short-term health insurance fills legitimate gaps. Here are the scenarios where it genuinely makes sense.

Job transition with a defined timeline: you left one job and your new employer plan starts in 60 days. You're healthy, you have no ongoing conditions, you just need catastrophic protection during the gap. A 60-day short-term plan at $120/month is a reasonable bridge. You're not looking for comprehensive coverage — you're looking to not be completely uninsured if you break your arm.

Aging off a parent's plan at 26: if you're a healthy 26-year-old who aged off your parent's coverage mid-year and the next ACA open enrollment is months away, a short-term plan bridges the gap to your SEP or the next enrollment period. Same logic applies — you're young, healthy, and managing a defined short-term gap.

Missed open enrollment with no SEP: if you missed the ACA marketplace open enrollment window and don't have a qualifying life event for a Special Enrollment Period, you're stuck. Without COBRA eligibility or employer coverage, a short-term plan may be your only option for some portion of the year. Not ideal but it's something.

Bridging to Medicare at 64: if you retire at 64 and COBRA's 18-month max ended before Medicare eligibility, or if COBRA is prohibitively expensive, a short-term plan for the final months before 65 can bridge the gap — assuming you're in a state where it's available and your health profile makes the exclusions manageable.

Graduation and job search: a new graduate with no employer plan and limited income, searching for a job with benefits. Short-term coverage at low premiums during the active job search, with the understanding it's temporary and they'll switch to employer coverage the moment it's available.

sections.heading: The States Where Short-Term Makes No Sense at All
sections.content:
If you live in California, New York, New Jersey, Illinois, Washington, Massachusetts, or any of the other states that have banned or severely restricted STLDI plans, stop reading this section. These plans aren't available to you.

The policy logic in those states is that short-term plans undermine the ACA risk pool by siphoning off young, healthy enrollees who would otherwise buy ACA plans and subsidize sicker members. It's a legitimate policy debate, but the practical outcome is that in those states, if you need coverage you're on the ACA marketplace, on Medicaid, or on COBRA. There's no STLDI option.

The irony: residents of those states who fall in the coverage gap — too much income for Medicaid, ACA premiums too high after subsidy reduction — have fewer options than residents of states with functional STLDI markets. For a 35-year-old in California making $70,000 who missed open enrollment and has no employer coverage, the answer is: wait for SEP or pay full ACA premiums. That's hard.

sections.heading: How to Buy Short-Term Coverage If You Need It
sections.content:
If you're in a state where it's available and the use case fits, here's the practical approach.

Shop through aggregators: eHealth, HealthMarkets, and similar brokers aggregate STLDI plans from multiple carriers. You can compare premiums, benefit levels, deductibles, and exclusion lookback periods side by side. Carriers like Pivot Health, UnitedHealthcare, and National General are major STLDI providers in states where the plans are allowed.

Read the exclusion language carefully. The lookback period for pre-existing conditions matters — a 2-year lookback is meaningfully different from a 5-year lookback. If you had any treatment in the recent past, understand exactly how the plan defines and applies exclusions before buying.

Check the benefit cap. Don't buy a plan with a $100,000 annual cap if you're concerned about catastrophic events. If the point is catastrophic protection during a gap, make sure the cap is high enough to actually cover a catastrophe.

Verify the term length and renewal rules. The federal 4-month cap applies, but verify in your specific state and with your specific carrier. Some plans auto-renew with new underwriting (meaning new exclusions applied at renewal). Know what you're signing up for.

Don't confuse short-term plans with indemnity plans, critical illness policies, or accident-only plans — these are all separate products with their own coverage structures and limitations. An accident-only plan doesn't cover illness. A fixed indemnity plan pays a set dollar amount per covered service, not actual cost. These fill different niches and are sometimes sold alongside STLDI plans as 'bundles.'

sections.heading: Short-Term vs ACA Bronze Plan: The Real Comparison
sections.content:
Let's put actual numbers on this.

Healthy 32-year-old, non-smoker, no pre-existing conditions, in a state where STLDI is available, income too high for meaningful ACA subsidy.

Short-term plan: $130/month. $5,000 deductible. $100,000 annual benefit cap. Exclusions for pre-existing conditions. No maternity, no mental health, no preventive care. 3-month term renewable to 4 months federal max.

ACA bronze HDHP (marketplace): $340/month. $7,000 deductible. No annual cap on essential health benefits. Covers pre-existing conditions. Covers maternity, mental health, preventive. HSA-eligible. Annual plan — no renewal concerns.

The premium difference: $210/month, $2,520/year. That's real money.

The risk difference: on the short-term plan, a leukemia diagnosis or a complicated pregnancy or a mental health crisis is potentially uncovered. On the bronze ACA plan, all of those are covered after the deductible. The $2,520 premium difference is essentially insurance against the scenarios the short-term plan doesn't cover.

For a healthy person using the plan as a 3-month gap bridge, the short-term plan wins on cost and the lack of ACA coverage features doesn't matter for that 90-day window. For anyone using it as primary long-term coverage who then develops a serious condition — the $2,520 annual savings vs potentially $100,000+ in uncovered medical bills is the calculation that matters.

faq.question: Can I use short-term insurance during ACA open enrollment?
faq.answer: You can use short-term insurance at any time — it has no enrollment periods. You can also simultaneously be shopping ACA plans during open enrollment while covered by a short-term plan, and then cancel the short-term plan when your ACA coverage starts January 1. That said, in the 14+ states where STLDI is banned, this isn't an option at all.

faq.question: Does short-term insurance cover COVID-19 and respiratory illnesses?
faq.answer: It depends on the plan and your history. If you have no prior COVID history and get newly infected during the plan term, most STLDI plans would cover it as a new acute illness. But if you have a documented history of COVID complications, long COVID, or respiratory issues that predate the plan, the exclusion language could apply. Read your specific policy's pre-existing condition definition and lookback period.

faq.question: Does short-term insurance affect my ACA marketplace eligibility?
faq.answer: Short-term coverage is not considered 'qualifying health coverage' under the ACA, so being on a short-term plan doesn't disqualify you from ACA marketplace enrollment or subsidies. When you lose short-term coverage — because it ends — that may or may not be a qualifying life event for marketplace SEP depending on whether it's considered a 'loss of coverage.' Check with your state exchange; rules vary.

faq.question: Is short-term insurance worth it for a 2-week gap between jobs?
faq.answer: Probably not. For a 2-week gap, the administrative hassle of enrolling in STLDI likely isn't worth it, and you could just choose to be uninsured for 14 days with the understanding that a genuine emergency would be covered under COBRA (if you elect it retroactively within 60 days). For any gap over 30 days, having at least catastrophic coverage makes more sense financially.

faq.question: Can short-term insurance deny a claim after I've already paid premiums?
faq.answer: Yes, and this is the core risk. Medical underwriting on STLDI happens at claim time, not at enrollment. The insurer reviews your medical history when you file a claim and determines whether the condition was pre-existing under their definition. If it was, the claim is denied regardless of how many premiums you paid. This is fundamentally different from ACA plans, which guarantee issue and cannot deny claims based on pre-existing conditions.

faq.question: What happens if I need a specialist or prescription on a short-term plan?
faq.answer: For new conditions, specialists and prescriptions are generally covered at your plan's benefit level — after deductible and subject to any out-of-pocket limits. But prescription formularies on STLDI plans are often limited, particularly for specialty drugs. A brand-name drug for an acute condition might be covered; an expensive specialty medication for a condition that arguably predates the plan might not be. Verify your specific plan's pharmacy coverage and formulary before relying on it.

title: Medicare Advantage Plans with Gym Membership: SilverSneakers, Renew Active, and One Pass Explained
metaDescription: Which Medicare Advantage plans include free gym memberships in 2026? Full breakdown of SilverSneakers, Renew Active (UHC), and One Pass — what you get, what it costs, and how to check if your plan qualifies.
h1: Medicare Advantage Plans with Gym Membership: SilverSneakers, Renew Active, and One Pass Explained
summary: Here's something that surprises a lot of people when they first switch into Medicare Advantage: the plan might be paying for your gym membership.

sections.heading: The Gym Benefit Most Medicare Enrollees Don't Know They Have
sections.content:
Here's something that surprises a lot of people when they first switch into Medicare Advantage: the plan might be paying for your gym membership. Not reimbursing part of it. Paying for it entirely. Free access to thousands of fitness locations — Planet Fitness, Anytime Fitness, LA Fitness, Life Time, YMCAs, and more — just because of the insurance card in your wallet.

Most people sit on this benefit for years without using it. They either don't know it exists, don't know how to activate it, or got a vague explanation during open enrollment that didn't fully click. That's money left on the table. A standard gym membership runs $30 to $80 a month depending on where you live. If your plan includes a fitness benefit, that's $360 to $960 per year in value you're already paying for.

But not every Medicare Advantage plan includes a fitness benefit. And not every fitness benefit is the same. Three programs dominate the market — SilverSneakers, Renew Active, and One Pass — and they work through different insurers, cover different locations, and offer different perks. If you're picking a plan or trying to figure out what you already have, this is everything you need to know.

sections.heading: SilverSneakers: The Biggest Network, the Most Plans
sections.content:
SilverSneakers is the oldest and most widely available senior fitness program in Medicare Advantage. It's operated by Tivity Health and partners with thousands of fitness locations across all 50 states. As of 2026, the network includes more than 25,000 locations — which is genuinely enormous. Anytime Fitness, Planet Fitness, LA Fitness, 24 Hour Fitness, Gold's Gym, community rec centers, YMCAs — most of the gyms you'd actually consider using are in the network.

What you get with SilverSneakers isn't just swipe-in gym access. The membership includes:

— Unlimited access to any SilverSneakers partner location nationwide
— Instructor-led group fitness classes specifically designed for older adults
— On-demand and live-stream workout videos through the SilverSneakers app
— SilverSneakers FLEX, which covers fitness classes at non-gym venues like parks, community centers, and pools
— A mobile app with workout tracking and health challenges

The in-person classes are worth calling out specifically. These aren't generic aerobics. They're built around the physical realities of older bodies — chair-based options, low-impact cardio, balance training, strength work with resistance bands. If you haven't been to a gym in years or you're managing joint problems, these classes are a realistic entry point.

Which plans include SilverSneakers? The list is long. AARP/UnitedHealthcare used to offer SilverSneakers but switched to Renew Active (their own program) in 2019. The plans that still carry SilverSneakers as of 2026 include Aetna Medicare Advantage plans, Humana Medicare Advantage in many markets, Blue Cross Blue Shield Medicare Advantage plans in many regions, Cigna Medicare Advantage, and Kaiser Permanente in markets where they operate. Anthem and many regional Blue plans also offer it.

The caveat: plan availability varies massively by county. A plan that includes SilverSneakers in Florida might not offer it in the same insurer's plan in Ohio. This is one of those things where you genuinely have to check your specific plan, not just assume the insurer offers it.

How to check: Go to tools.silversneakers.com and click "Check Eligibility." Enter your Medicare ID number or insurance card information and it'll tell you within seconds whether you're enrolled and which locations are near you. You can also call the number on the back of your insurance card.

sections.heading: Renew Active: UnitedHealthcare's In-House Fitness Program
sections.content:
When UnitedHealthcare dropped SilverSneakers in 2019, they didn't just remove a benefit — they replaced it with something they own and control. Renew Active is UHC's proprietary fitness and wellness program, exclusive to their Medicare Advantage plans. If you have any AARP Medicare Advantage plan through UHC, Renew Active is what you have.

On paper, Renew Active is competitive with SilverSneakers. The network includes thousands of locations — 24 Hour Fitness, Anytime Fitness, LA Fitness, Planet Fitness, and YMCA locations participate in many markets. You also get access to Life Time fitness centers in some plans, which matters because Life Time is premium-tier — normally $70 to $120 per month. Getting that through a Medicare Advantage plan is legitimately valuable.

What makes Renew Active different from SilverSneakers is the brain health component. UHC built in Renew Active Brain Health, which offers a suite of cognitive engagement tools — puzzles, memory exercises, online courses through a platform called BrainHQ, which has some legitimate research behind it. The program also includes online hobby and social classes, designed to address the isolation problem that hits a lot of retirees harder than physical decline does.

Renew Active also includes a personalized fitness plan feature — you take an assessment when you enroll and get a recommended workout program based on your current fitness level and goals. Whether you actually follow it is on you, but the infrastructure exists.

A few things to know about Renew Active that trip people up:

First, the included gym locations vary by plan type and region. Premium Renew Active plans (typically the higher-tier UHC offerings) include Life Time and other premium gyms. Standard Renew Active plans may have a more limited network. Read your plan's Evidence of Coverage to see exactly which locations are included before you assume your preferred gym qualifies.

Second, there are spending allowances on some plans. Some UHC Medicare Advantage plans include a fitness allowance — a dollar amount you can spend toward gym fees, fitness equipment, or wellness purchases. These allowances have been getting trimmed. In 2026, UHC cut OTC and wellness allowances on many non-SNP plans, so don't assume this year's allowance matches what you had in 2025. Check your 2026 Evidence of Coverage.

Third, enrollment in Renew Active isn't automatic. You need to activate the benefit. UHC's website has a Renew Active portal where you register, choose your gym, and get your membership started. This takes about ten minutes but you have to actually do it.

sections.heading: One Pass: The Newer Contender with Life Time as Its Anchor
sections.content:
One Pass is a fitness benefit program operated by Rally Health (a UHC subsidiary, confusingly) and distributed through several Medicare Advantage plans. It's newer than SilverSneakers and has a smaller network, but it punches above its weight in a specific way: Life Time Fitness is a cornerstone partner.

Life Time is legitimately one of the best gym chains in the country. These are full-service clubs — resort-style pools, pickleball courts, group fitness studios with real instructors, nutrition programs, spa facilities. A Life Time membership typically costs $70 to $200 per month depending on location and tier. Some One Pass enrollees get access to Life Time at no additional cost through their plan. That's the headline.

Beyond Life Time, One Pass includes a network of other partner gyms — Anytime Fitness, Crunch Fitness, YMCA locations, and others depending on the market. The app lets you search by location and see exactly which facilities participate.

One Pass also includes an at-home fitness component: access to digital workouts you can do without a gym. This matters for people in rural areas or anyone going through a period where getting to a gym isn't realistic — recovery from surgery, winter months in northern states, whatever.

Which plans offer One Pass? This changes frequently. As of 2026, One Pass is available through some Humana plans, some Cigna plans, and certain regional insurers. The distribution isn't as universal as SilverSneakers, which is why it flies under the radar. The One Pass website (onepassfit.com) has a tool to check if your plan qualifies.

Worth noting: some plans offer One Pass as a standard benefit and some offer it as an upgrade or add-on with a small monthly cost. The difference matters. If you're in a zero-premium Medicare Advantage plan, a $15/month gym add-on might still represent savings versus paying retail — but it's not "free" in the way the headline benefit is.

sections.heading: Other Fitness Programs Worth Knowing
sections.content:
SilverSneakers, Renew Active, and One Pass are the big three, but they're not the only options. A few others show up in plan documents and are worth understanding.

Silver&Fit is offered through some Medicare Advantage plans — notably certain Humana, Aetna, and regional Blue plans — and works similarly to SilverSneakers with a nationwide gym network and home fitness kit option. The home fitness kit is actually one of Silver&Fit's selling points: enrollees can request exercise DVDs, fitness equipment, or streaming subscriptions as an alternative to in-person gym access. For people who prefer working out at home, this is legitimately useful.

AMSF (American Specialty Health Fitness) administers Silver&Fit and some other fitness programs behind the scenes. If you see ASHF mentioned in your plan documents, this is what it refers to.

Fitness benefit through OTC allowance: Some plans don't have a named fitness program but instead include a general OTC/wellness allowance that can be applied toward gym fees, fitness equipment, or wearable devices. These allowances got cut significantly across many plans in 2026 — UHC, Aetna, and Elevance all reduced OTC allowances on non-SNP plans — but they still exist on some plans and can fund gym access indirectly.

What's NOT covered: Original Medicare (Parts A and B with no Advantage plan) does not cover gym memberships, fitness classes, or any exercise-related expenses. Zero. This is a Medicare Advantage-exclusive benefit. If you have traditional Medicare plus a Medigap supplement, you have no fitness benefit unless your state or insurance company offers something separately.

sections.heading: How to Check If Your Plan Includes a Gym Benefit
sections.content:
The fastest way to find out what you have is to call the member services number on your insurance card and ask directly: "Does my plan include a fitness benefit, and if so, which program?" That call takes three minutes.

If you want to do it yourself before calling:

For SilverSneakers: Go to silversneakers.com and click the "Check My Eligibility" button. Enter your Medicare number (the red, white, and blue card) or your insurance card information. The tool is fast and accurate.

For Renew Active: Log into myuhc.com or call 1-844-459-7554. Renew Active is only through UnitedHealthcare plans.

For One Pass: Go to onepassfit.com and use their eligibility checker.

For Silver&Fit: Go to silverandfit.com and check eligibility there.

During plan shopping (open enrollment is October 15 to December 7 for the following year): When you're comparing plans on Medicare.gov's Plan Finder, look in the "Extra Benefits" section of each plan's detail page. Fitness benefits are listed there, though the description can be vague. Always cross-reference with the plan's Summary of Benefits document, which you can download directly from the Plan Finder. That document spells out exactly what's included.

One trap to avoid: the fitness benefit listed in a plan's marketing materials isn't always the same as what's in the Evidence of Coverage. Marketing shows the best case; the EOC shows the actual terms. Always read the EOC if a benefit matters to you.

sections.heading: The Real Dollar Value of These Benefits
sections.content:
Let's be specific about what this is actually worth so you can weigh it properly against other plan differences.

SilverSneakers gives you unlimited access to 25,000+ locations. If you use a gym even twice a week, you'd normally pay $30 to $60 per month for a standard gym membership — $360 to $720 per year. Some markets run higher. A Gold's Gym or LA Fitness in a major metro can run $40 to $80/month. The on-demand classes and FLEX classes add value on top of that. Conservative estimate: $400 to $800 per year in value for regular users.

Renew Active with Life Time access is the high-end case. Life Time memberships retail at $70 to $200 per month. If your Renew Active plan includes Life Time and you use it, you're looking at $840 to $2,400 per year in value. Even if you use a more modest gym in the network, you're getting $400 to $720 per year for free.

One Pass: Similar range to SilverSneakers for standard gym access, potentially higher if Life Time is included in your market.

The catch: these benefits only have value if you actually use them. Plans know that a significant percentage of enrolled members never activate their fitness benefit. That's why they can offer it — the average cost to the plan is lower than the retail value because utilization isn't 100%. You using the benefit actively is, in a very literal sense, getting more than the average enrollee from the same premium.

For comparison: a plan with a $20 lower monthly premium than a competitor might actually cost you more if the competitor's plan includes a fitness benefit you'd actually use. $20/month is $240/year. A gym membership is $400 to $720/year. The math favors the plan with the better fitness benefit if you're going to use it.

Health value matters too, and it's harder to quantify. Regular exercise reduces hospitalizations, falls, cardiovascular events, and depression. Medicare Advantage plans offer these benefits partly because healthier members cost them less. You benefit from the program; the plan benefits from lower claims. It's one of the few genuinely aligned incentives in health insurance.

sections.heading: Which Plans to Look at in 2026 for Fitness Benefits
sections.content:
General guidance, not a specific recommendation — plan availability varies too much by county to say "get Plan X" universally. But here's what to look for:

For SilverSneakers specifically: Aetna Medicare Advantage plans are a reliable source of SilverSneakers in 2026. Humana includes it in many markets. Cigna/Evernorth Medicare Advantage plans often include it. Many regional Blue Cross Blue Shield Medicare Advantage plans carry it. Scan the plan details for the SilverSneakers logo or the phrase "fitness benefit included."

For Renew Active: Any AARP Medicare Advantage plan through UnitedHealthcare includes Renew Active. There are no exceptions — it's baked into every UHC MA plan as a standard feature. The question is whether your specific plan tier includes Life Time access.

For Life Time access specifically: Life Time partners with Renew Active (UHC), One Pass, and SilverSneakers at some locations. If proximity to a Life Time is important to you, search their website (lifetime.life/medicare) to see which program applies to your plan.

For the best overall fitness benefit package: Higher-tier Medicare Advantage plans (the ones with premiums of $20 to $50/month rather than $0) often include better fitness benefits — more premium gym networks, higher OTC allowances, and more classes. The zero-premium plans still usually have fitness benefits but they may be more basic.

One underrated approach: call the gym you actually want to use and ask which Medicare fitness programs they participate in. Then work backwards — find plans in your area that include that program. It's counterintuitive (most people pick the plan first), but if the gym matters to you, start there.

faq.question: Does regular Medicare (Parts A and B) cover gym memberships?
faq.answer: No. Original Medicare does not cover gym memberships, fitness classes, or exercise-related expenses of any kind. Fitness benefits are only available through Medicare Advantage plans (Part C). If you have traditional Medicare with a Medigap supplement, you have no fitness benefit through Medicare.

faq.question: How do I activate my SilverSneakers benefit?
faq.answer: Go to silversneakers.com, click 'Check Eligibility,' and enter your Medicare or insurance card information. If you're eligible, you can register directly on the site and get a digital membership card. You can also call 1-888-423-4632 to activate by phone. The benefit is not automatic — you must register.

faq.question: Can I use Renew Active at Life Time Fitness?
faq.answer: It depends on your specific plan and your local Life Time location. Higher-tier UnitedHealthcare Medicare Advantage plans in certain markets include Life Time access through Renew Active. Check your plan's Evidence of Coverage or call 1-844-459-7554 to confirm. Life Time also lists participating Medicare programs at lifetime.life/medicare.

faq.question: What's the difference between SilverSneakers and Silver&Fit?
faq.answer: SilverSneakers and Silver&Fit are separate programs offered through different plans. SilverSneakers (Tivity Health) has a larger gym network — over 25,000 locations. Silver&Fit (American Specialty Health) has a smaller gym network but includes a home fitness kit option where you can request exercise equipment or DVDs if you prefer working out at home. Your plan will specify which one you have.

faq.question: My plan changed for 2026. Did I lose my gym benefit?
faq.answer: Possibly. Several major insurers cut OTC and wellness allowances in 2026, and some plans changed their fitness benefit terms. Check your 2026 Annual Notice of Change (ANOC) — it was mailed to you in September 2025 and lists all changes from 2025. If your plan dropped or modified the fitness benefit, you had a chance to switch during open enrollment (Oct 15–Dec 7). If you missed it, contact your plan to understand current options.

faq.question: Are there age or health requirements to use SilverSneakers or Renew Active?
faq.answer: No. Any enrolled member whose plan includes the benefit can use it regardless of age, health status, or fitness level. The programs are specifically designed to be accessible for people with limited mobility, chronic conditions, or no recent exercise history. You do not need a doctor's referral to use these fitness benefits.

title: Medicare Advantage for Diabetics: C-SNPs, CGM Coverage, and the Best Plans in 2026
metaDescription: If you have diabetes and you're on Medicare, the plan you pick matters more than almost any other decision you'll make. Full guide to Chronic Special Needs Plans, CGM coverage, the $35 insulin cap, and how to find the best Medicare Advantage plan for diabetes management in 2026.
h1: Medicare Advantage for Diabetics: C-SNPs, CGM Coverage, and the Best Plans in 2026
summary: Pick the wrong Medicare Advantage plan when you have diabetes and you can end up paying $200 to $400 per month more than you need to — just on insulin, test strips, and doctor visits.

sections.heading: Why Plan Choice Matters More for Diabetics Than Almost Anyone Else
sections.content:
Pick the wrong Medicare Advantage plan when you have diabetes and you can end up paying $200 to $400 per month more than you need to — just on insulin, test strips, and doctor visits. Pick the right one and those same expenses drop close to zero.

That's not an exaggeration. The difference between a generic Medicare Advantage plan and a plan specifically designed for diabetics (called a Chronic Special Needs Plan, or C-SNP) can be dramatic in terms of out-of-pocket costs, network access to endocrinologists, CGM coverage, and care coordination. Most people with diabetes don't know C-SNPs exist. And even among those who know, many end up in a generic MA plan because they didn't understand the trade-offs.

This guide covers everything that actually matters: what C-SNPs are and how to get into one, the current CGM coverage rules under Medicare, how the $35 insulin cap works (and where it has limits), what to look for in an endocrinologist network, and which plan features make the biggest real-world difference for someone managing diabetes day to day.

sections.heading: What Is a C-SNP and Who Qualifies
sections.content:
Special Needs Plans (SNPs) are a category of Medicare Advantage plans designed for people with specific health situations. There are three types: Dual SNPs (D-SNPs) for people on both Medicare and Medicaid, Institutional SNPs (I-SNPs) for people in nursing facilities, and Chronic Special Needs Plans (C-SNPs) for people with specific chronic conditions.

Diabetes C-SNPs are the most common type of chronic SNP. They're designed specifically for Medicare beneficiaries with diabetes — both Type 1 and Type 2, and in some cases for pre-diabetics or those with multiple related conditions like chronic kidney disease combined with diabetes.

To enroll in a diabetes C-SNP, you need a confirmed diagnosis of diabetes from a qualifying provider. Most plans require documentation — typically a letter or records from your treating physician. Some plans do the verification themselves after you enroll; others require documentation upfront. Either way, you can't just wander into a C-SNP without a qualifying condition. But if you have diabetes, getting into one is usually not complicated.

What makes C-SNPs different from standard Medicare Advantage plans:

— Lower or zero cost-sharing for diabetes-related services: doctor visits, lab work, specialist referrals, and supplies are often heavily subsidized
— Care coordination: C-SNPs are required by CMS to provide an individualized care plan and care coordination support for each member
— More comprehensive drug formularies for diabetes medications, including newer drug classes like GLP-1 agonists (Ozempic, Wegovy, Jardiance, Farxiga)
— Often include disease management programs — nurse coaching, diabetes education classes, telehealth with diabetes educators
— Network optimized for diabetes care: endocrinologists, ophthalmologists, nephrologists, and podiatrists are prioritized in the plan's network

The limitation: C-SNPs aren't available everywhere. They're heavily concentrated in urban and suburban markets. Rural beneficiaries often have no C-SNP available in their county. If that's your situation, look for a standard Medicare Advantage plan with the strongest diabetes benefits — more on that below.

Major carriers offering diabetes C-SNPs in 2026 include Humana, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, Wellcare, and several regional managed care organizations. Use Medicare.gov's Plan Finder, filter for Special Needs Plans, and select Chronic Condition to see what's available in your county.

sections.heading: CGM Coverage Under Medicare in 2026
sections.content:
Continuous Glucose Monitors used to be a coverage nightmare under Medicare. The rules were restrictive, the durable medical equipment classification created cost-sharing headaches, and a lot of people with Type 2 diabetes couldn't qualify at all. That changed — significantly — and in 2026 coverage is broader than it's ever been.

Here's where things stand: Medicare currently covers CGMs for beneficiaries who use insulin in any amount and in any frequency. The old rule required intensive insulin therapy (multiple daily injections) and four or more fingerstick readings per day. That requirement is gone. If you use insulin at all — even once-daily basal insulin — you can qualify for CGM coverage under Medicare.

The specific CGMs covered under Medicare in 2026 include:
— Dexcom G6 (covered as a therapeutic CGM)
— Dexcom G7 (covered)
— Abbott FreeStyle Libre 2 (covered)
— Abbott FreeStyle Libre 3 (covered)
— Eversense implantable CGM (covered)

These are covered under Medicare Part B as durable medical equipment, not under Part D. That distinction matters because Part B typically has 80% coverage after the Part B deductible (leaving you responsible for 20%). However, most Medicare Advantage plans cap your out-of-pocket on Part B-covered DME, and C-SNPs often have $0 or very low cost-sharing for CGM supplies specifically.

For Type 1 diabetics who were already using CGMs: your coverage path is essentially the same — Part B covers the receiver/reader and transmitter, Part D may cover some sensors depending on classification. Check your specific plan's formulary because CGM supplies are sometimes split across Part B and Part D coverage depending on the device.

For Type 2 diabetics not on insulin: this is where Medicare coverage still falls short. If you're managing diabetes with oral medications only (metformin, SGLT-2 inhibitors, etc.) and no insulin, CGMs are generally not covered under Medicare — not Part B, not Part D. Some C-SNPs include CGM access as a supplemental benefit for non-insulin users, so if you're in this category, specifically ask whether the plan covers CGMs for non-insulin T2D management. It's worth asking directly rather than assuming.

Out-of-pocket reality for CGMs in 2026: With standard Medicare Advantage and no supplemental coverage, a CGM like the FreeStyle Libre 3 costs roughly $70 to $120 per month out of pocket for the sensors (after the Part B 20% coinsurance or plan cost-sharing). C-SNPs with $0 cost-sharing for diabetes DME can bring this to nothing. That's a $840 to $1,440 per year difference — not trivial.

sections.heading: The $35 Insulin Cap: How It Works and Where It Has Gaps
sections.content:
The $35 monthly insulin cap is one of the genuine wins from the Inflation Reduction Act, and it's permanent as of 2026. Here's exactly what it covers and what it doesn't, because there's real confusion about this.

What's covered: Under Medicare Part D, all covered insulin products are capped at $35 per month regardless of which coverage phase you're in — initial coverage, the coverage gap, or catastrophic coverage. The deductible for insulin is also eliminated. You pay a maximum of $35 per month even if you haven't met your Part D deductible. This applies to all insulin types: rapid-acting, long-acting, pre-mixed.

As of 2026, there's a specific additional note: Fiasp and Novolog (two commonly used rapid-acting insulins) have a 2026 Part D copay capped at $29.75 under the IRA's drug price negotiation program. That's the first class of drugs where direct CMS price negotiation actually hit the patient's wallet, and it hit the right direction.

What's NOT covered by the $35 cap:
— Insulin used with an insulin pump may be classified differently (as a pump supply under Part B) and the $35 cap doesn't apply to that pathway
— GLP-1 agonists like Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Jardiance are NOT insulin and are NOT subject to the $35 cap. These are often the drugs that actually determine whether someone's diabetes is controlled, and they're expensive — Ozempic retails at $900+ per month without coverage
— Non-insulin diabetes medications have no general cap

The GLP-1 situation is genuinely painful. These drugs are increasingly the standard of care for Type 2 diabetes and can run $500 to $900 per month without coverage. Part D formularies vary enormously in how they handle GLP-1s — some cover them with reasonable copays, some put them on high tiers with $100+ monthly costs, some require prior authorization that's difficult to obtain.

If you use Ozempic, Mounjaro, Wegovy, or any GLP-1: the single most important thing to check when comparing plans is whether your specific medication is on the plan's formulary, at what tier, and what the monthly cost-sharing is. This can vary by $100 to $400 per month across plans for the same drug. Use Medicare's Plan Finder drug comparison tool and enter your specific medications to get accurate cost projections before you pick a plan.

For people on Medicare who use GLP-1s for weight loss specifically (not diabetes): Medicare Part D covers GLP-1s when prescribed for diabetes or cardiovascular disease but does not cover them when prescribed solely for weight loss. This is a CMS coverage rule, not an insurer decision. Some Medicare Advantage plans offer supplemental obesity benefits, but coverage of injectable weight loss drugs specifically is still inconsistent.

sections.heading: Endocrinologist Networks and Specialist Access
sections.content:
Your primary care doctor manages a lot of your diabetes care, but endocrinologists matter — especially for Type 1, or for Type 2 patients with complex management needs, multiple comorbidities, or access to newer insulin therapies and CGM technology. The question of whether your endocrinologist is in-network is one of the most important things to check before picking a plan, and people get surprised by this constantly.

Here's how Medicare Advantage networks work with specialists: each plan has a defined network of providers. If your endocrinologist is in-network, you pay the plan's cost-sharing rate. If they're out-of-network, you either pay significantly more (in a PPO plan) or aren't covered at all (in an HMO plan). Changing plans can mean losing access to a specialist you've worked with for years.

For diabetics, the relevant specialist types to verify for in-network status:
— Endocrinologist (primary)
— Ophthalmologist or optometrist (diabetic eye exams, retinopathy screening)
— Nephrologist (kidney disease is a major diabetes complication)
— Podiatrist (diabetic foot care, neuropathy)
— Certified Diabetes Care and Education Specialist (CDCES, formerly CDE) — these aren't always in-network databases, sometimes covered as part of Diabetes Self-Management Training

C-SNPs are required by CMS to have adequate specialist networks for their qualifying condition. This doesn't guarantee your specific doctor is in-network, but it does mean the plan is contractually obligated to have endocrinologists available. Standard MA plans have no such requirement and can have sparse specialist networks in smaller markets.

Diabetes Self-Management Training (DSMT) is a Medicare-covered benefit that most people with diabetes don't use — up to 10 hours in the first year of diagnosis and 2 hours per year thereafter, covered under Part B. These sessions with a certified diabetes educator are genuinely useful for learning carb counting, medication management, CGM interpretation, and lifestyle strategies. Make sure your plan doesn't impose additional barriers to accessing DSMT beyond the standard Medicare rules.

Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) is also a Part B benefit for people with diabetes — sessions with a registered dietitian, covered at no charge after you meet your Part B deductible. Again, check that your plan doesn't create additional hoops.

For telehealth: post-pandemic, most Medicare Advantage plans have robust telehealth options. Many endocrinologists now see patients via telehealth, which is particularly useful for routine CGM data review and insulin adjustment conversations that don't require an in-person exam. C-SNPs tend to have better-developed telehealth and remote monitoring infrastructure specifically for diabetes management.

sections.heading: What to Actually Compare When Evaluating Plans for Diabetes
sections.content:
When you sit down with Medicare.gov's Plan Finder or work with a Medicare broker, here's the specific checklist that matters for diabetes management. These are the variables that actually move the needle on annual costs.

Formulary check for your exact medications: Enter every medication you take — not just insulin, but oral diabetes drugs, blood pressure medications, statins, everything. The total annual cost estimate the Plan Finder generates is the single most important number. Don't compare plans without running this.

CGM cost-sharing: Look for CGM coverage under Part B (DME) cost-sharing. A plan with $0 CGM cost-sharing versus one with 20% coinsurance is a $840+ per year difference for active CGM users.

Specialist copays: What do you pay per endocrinologist visit? $0, $10, $40? If you see an endocrinologist quarterly (4 visits/year), a $40 specialist copay is $160/year. A $0 copay plan saves that entire amount.

Annual out-of-pocket maximum (MOOP): Medicare Advantage plans must have an annual out-of-pocket maximum. In 2026, the maximum allowed MOOP for in-network services is $9,350 for standard plans and $13,300 for combined in-and-out-of-network. C-SNPs often have lower MOOPs than standard plans because CMS recognizes their members have higher expected costs. A lower MOOP is better protection against a bad year.

Care coordination and disease management programs: Does the plan include a diabetes management program? Nurse hotlines? Remote monitoring? For C-SNPs, this is required. For standard MA plans, it varies. These programs have real value — studies consistently show that structured diabetes management programs reduce hospitalization rates.

Dental and vision: Diabetics are at elevated risk for dental disease (linked to blood sugar control) and vision complications. Plans that include comprehensive dental and vision benefits are particularly valuable for people managing diabetes. Compare what's actually included — not just "dental coverage" but whether cleanings, x-rays, and basic restorative care are covered and at what cost-sharing level.

sections.heading: If There's No C-SNP in Your Area: What to Look For Instead
sections.content:
A lot of beneficiaries in rural counties, small metros, and certain states won't have a diabetes C-SNP available. If that's you, here's how to maximize your position in a standard Medicare Advantage plan.

Look for plans with robust Part D formularies for newer diabetes drug classes. SGLT-2 inhibitors (Jardiance, Farxiga), GLP-1 agonists (Ozempic, Trulicity), and DPP-4 inhibitors (Januvia) are now standard diabetes care. A plan whose formulary puts these on a $0 or $10 tier versus a $50 to $100 tier represents hundreds of dollars in annual difference.

Prefer PPO over HMO if your doctors are established. PPO plans let you see out-of-network providers at higher cost-sharing, which provides a safety net if your endocrinologist eventually leaves the network. HMO plans give zero coverage for out-of-network care (outside emergencies), which creates real risk if your specialist network changes.

Look at plans from insurers with strong diabetes management track records: Humana's star ratings have historically reflected strong chronic care management. Aetna CVS has invested heavily in pharmacy-integrated care which benefits people on complex medication regimens. Both operate in most major markets.

Ask your endocrinologist which plans their office participates in. Their staff deals with authorizations daily and will know which plans create problems (excessive prior auth requirements, coverage denials on CGMs, difficulty getting specialty referrals) and which ones are straightforward. That ground-level intelligence is worth more than any star rating.

faq.question: What is a C-SNP and how is it different from a regular Medicare Advantage plan?
faq.answer: A Chronic Special Needs Plan (C-SNP) is a Medicare Advantage plan designed specifically for people with one or more qualifying chronic conditions, including diabetes. Compared to a standard MA plan, C-SNPs have lower cost-sharing for condition-related care, required care coordination, more robust specialist networks, and often better formulary placement for diabetes medications. You must have a confirmed diagnosis to enroll.

faq.question: Does Medicare cover CGMs for Type 2 diabetics who don't use insulin?
faq.answer: Standard Medicare (Part B) does not cover CGMs for people managing Type 2 diabetes with oral medications only. You must use insulin to qualify for CGM coverage under Part B. However, some C-SNPs and select Medicare Advantage plans include CGM access as a supplemental benefit for non-insulin users. Ask plans specifically about this if it applies to you.

faq.question: Is the $35 insulin cap available on Medicare Advantage plans?
faq.answer: Yes. The $35 per month insulin cap under the Inflation Reduction Act applies to all Part D coverage, including Medicare Advantage plans with Part D (MA-PD plans). All insulin types are covered at $35/month with no deductible. This is a federal requirement — plans cannot charge more for insulin.

faq.question: Does Medicare cover Ozempic or Mounjaro?
faq.answer: Medicare Part D covers GLP-1 agonists like Ozempic and Mounjaro when prescribed for diabetes management or cardiovascular risk reduction. Coverage is not guaranteed for weight loss as the sole indication. Cost-sharing varies significantly by plan — check the specific formulary for your medications using Medicare's Plan Finder. The $35 insulin cap does not apply to GLP-1s; they are not insulin.

faq.question: How do I find C-SNP plans available in my county?
faq.answer: Go to Medicare.gov's Plan Finder tool. Enter your ZIP code and select 'Medicare Advantage Plan' as the plan type. Then filter by 'Special Needs Plans' and select 'Chronic Condition' as the SNP type. The results will show available C-SNPs in your area. Note that C-SNPs are not available in all counties — rural areas often have no C-SNP options.

faq.question: Can I switch to a Medicare Advantage plan specifically to get better diabetes coverage?
faq.answer: Yes, during the Annual Open Enrollment Period (October 15 to December 7) you can switch from any Medicare plan to any other plan for coverage starting January 1. If you have a new or changed diabetes diagnosis, check whether you qualify for a Special Enrollment Period. If you're already in Medicare Advantage, you can also use the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) to switch to a different MA plan once.

title: Medicare Changes 2026: Everything That's Actually Different This Year
metaDescription: Real 2026 Medicare numbers from CMS: Part B is $202.90, Part D out-of-pocket hits $2,100, IRMAA brackets shifted, IRA drug prices took effect, and major carriers pulled back plans in hundreds of counties. Here's what changed and what it means.
h1: Medicare Changes 2026: Everything That's Actually Different This Year
summary: Every fall, Medicare updates its numbers and most years it's modest — a slight premium increase, a small deductible adjustment, nothing that fundamentally changes the picture.

sections.heading: More Changed This Year Than Most People Realize
sections.content:
Every fall, Medicare updates its numbers and most years it's modest — a slight premium increase, a small deductible adjustment, nothing that fundamentally changes the picture. 2026 is different. The number of things that actually changed — structurally, not just inflationary — is unusual.

The Part D benefit was redesigned under the Inflation Reduction Act. Negotiated drug prices under the IRA took effect for the first time. Major insurers pulled out of hundreds of counties — up to a million enrollees may have had to find new plans this open enrollment. Star ratings methodology shifted again. And Part B premiums jumped by nearly $18 per month, the second-largest dollar increase in the program's history.

If you or someone you know is on Medicare and didn't review their coverage during open enrollment, they might be in for some surprises. And if you're approaching Medicare eligibility in the next year or two, understanding what the program actually looks like right now — not the 2023 version you read about somewhere — matters for your planning.

Here's the complete breakdown.

sections.heading: Part B Premium: $202.90 Per Person Per Month
sections.content:
The standard Medicare Part B premium in 2026 is $202.90 per month. That's an increase of $17.90 from 2025's premium of $185.00 — a 9.7% jump.

For context: the 2026 Social Security COLA (cost-of-living adjustment) was 2.5%. Part B's premium increase ate up most of it for many beneficiaries. The Center for Retirement Research at Boston College calculated that higher Medicare premiums will consume more than 25% of the Social Security COLA for the median beneficiary in 2026.

The Part B annual deductible also increased — to $283 in 2026 from $257 in 2025, a $26 increase.

Why did Part B go up this much? CMS pointed to higher spending projections for physician services and outpatient care. Part B covers outpatient services, preventive care, and physician visits, and utilization has been rising. There's also a structural issue: when a high-cost drug or treatment gets approved and prescribed at scale (as happened with Alzheimer's drugs in recent years), the costs flow through Part B and affect premiums broadly.

For people paying IRMAA surcharges — more on those below — the base premium increase compounds with the surcharges. Someone in the second IRMAA bracket went from paying $263.70 per month to $284.10 per month for Part B alone. Multiply by 12 and by two people if you're a couple: the math adds up fast.

sections.heading: Part A: Hospital Deductible Climbs to $1,736
sections.content:
Medicare Part A (hospital insurance) has its own cost increases in 2026 that get less attention than Part B but matter if you're hospitalized.

The Part A inpatient hospital deductible for 2026 is $1,736 — up $60 from $1,676 in 2025. This deductible applies per benefit period, not per year. If you're admitted to the hospital, leave, and are readmitted more than 60 days later, you pay the deductible again. People who have multiple hospitalizations in a year can face this more than once, which is one of the reasons Medigap supplements are valuable.

Coinsurance for extended hospital stays in 2026:
— Days 1-60: covered after the deductible
— Days 61-90: $434 per day coinsurance
— Days 91+: $868 per day (lifetime reserve days)

For skilled nursing facilities, the 2026 daily coinsurance for days 21-100 is $217.00. Days 1-20 are covered in full (after a qualifying hospital stay). Days beyond 100 are not covered by Medicare at all.

The Part A premium: most people don't pay a Part A premium because they've worked 40+ quarters (10 years) of Medicare-covered employment. If you have 30-39 quarters of work, the 2026 Part A premium is $285/month. Fewer than 30 quarters, it's $518/month. This affects people who immigrated later in life or had significant self-employment income gaps.

sections.heading: Part D: The $2,100 Out-of-Pocket Cap Changes Everything
sections.content:
This is the single most consequential Medicare change in at least a decade for people with high drug costs, and it's worth understanding fully.

Starting January 1, 2026, there is a hard cap on out-of-pocket spending for Part D prescription drugs at $2,100 per year. Once you've spent $2,100 on covered medications, you pay nothing for the rest of the year. Zero. No more catastrophic phase where you still owe a percentage. No more drug donut hole. The benefit is simple: spend $2,100 and you're done.

In 2025, the cap was $2,000. The $100 increase for 2026 reflects an annual adjustment tied to average drug cost increases, as established by the IRA. The cap will continue to adjust annually, but it stays capped — the old structure where out-of-pocket costs were theoretically unlimited in the catastrophic phase is gone.

Who this benefits most: people on expensive specialty medications — oncology drugs, multiple sclerosis treatments, rheumatoid arthritis biologics, hepatitis C treatments. Before the cap, someone on a $15,000/month specialty drug could face thousands in out-of-pocket costs even in the coverage gap. Now, $2,100 and you're protected.

Also changed in 2026: the structure of who pays in catastrophic coverage. Under the old design, once you hit the catastrophic threshold, you paid 5% of drug costs. That 5% was eliminated. Manufacturers now cover a larger share of costs through the redesigned Manufacturer Discount Program, and plans cover more. Beneficiaries pay nothing past $2,100.

The IRA drug price negotiations: Beginning January 1, 2026, Medicare's negotiated prices for the first ten selected drugs took effect. These are drugs whose manufacturers agreed to Maximum Fair Prices (MFPs) under CMS's negotiation process. The first negotiated drugs include Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Januvia, Farxiga, Entresto, Enbrel, Imbruvica, Stelara, and Fiasp/Novolog. For patients on these medications, actual Part D costs fell — in some cases dramatically. Jardiance and Januvia in particular are widely used for diabetes; the negotiated pricing represents real savings on common medications.

The average base monthly Part D premium is projected at $38.99 for 2026, up from $36.78 in 2025. Premium increases have been moderate given the benefit redesign — CMS's risk model adjustments helped prevent the benefit changes from spiking premiums excessively.

sections.heading: IRMAA 2026: The Income Brackets That Determine What You Actually Pay
sections.content:
IRMAA — Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount — is the system by which higher-income Medicare beneficiaries pay more for Parts B and D. It's one of those Medicare details that can blindside retirees who had a high-income year without realizing the two-year lookback would hit them later.

Here's how 2026 IRMAA works: your 2026 IRMAA surcharge is based on your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI). Not 2025. Not 2026. What you reported on your 2024 tax return. SSA calculates it and adds the surcharge automatically to your premium.

The 2026 IRMAA thresholds for Part B (per month per person):
— $0 to $109,000 (single) / $0 to $218,000 (married filing jointly): $202.90 (standard)
— $109,001 to $136,000 / $218,001 to $272,000: $284.10
— $136,001 to $163,000 / $272,001 to $326,000: $365.60
— $163,001 to $192,000 / $326,001 to $384,000: $447.10
— $192,001 to $500,000 / $384,001 to $750,000: $530.90
— Above $500,000 / Above $750,000: $614.90 (wait — CMS 2026 fact sheet shows maximum at $689.90 for the highest bracket)

For Part D, IRMAA surcharges range from $14.50 to $91.00 per month on top of your plan premium.

A married couple at the first surcharge threshold ($218,001 MAGI in 2024) each pays $284.10 instead of $202.90 — that's $81.20 more per person per month, or $1,948.80 more per year per couple just on Part B.

Why IRMAA catches people: you retire in 2025, your income drops dramatically — but your 2026 IRMAA is based on 2024 income when you were still working. You can appeal this using CMS Form SSA-44 if you've had a life-changing event (retirement, divorce, death of spouse, work stoppage). The appeal isn't guaranteed but it's absolutely worth doing. IRMAA appeals are processed by SSA, not CMS.

The IRMAA cliff is real and it's brutal. One dollar over a threshold and you owe the full surcharge for the entire year. This is why income planning in the two years before Medicare eligibility is genuinely valuable — Roth conversions, capital gains timing, RMD management.

sections.heading: Medicare Advantage in 2026: Carriers Pulling Back, Fewer Plans
sections.content:
The Medicare Advantage market underwent a significant contraction in 2026. This is the story that got the most press during open enrollment, and for good reason — hundreds of thousands of enrollees had to find new plans.

The scale of pullbacks:
— UnitedHealthcare exited 109 counties, affecting approximately 180,000 enrollees
— Humana exited 194 counties, reduced from 48 states to 46 states
— Aetna/CVS Health exited 100 counties, reduced from 44 states to 43 states

Benefits got cut too. Aetna, Elevance, and UnitedHealthcare materially reduced OTC and wellness allowances on non-Special Needs Plans. The generous supplemental benefits that drove Medicare Advantage growth over the last decade — flex cards, dental allowances, transportation benefits — got trimmed across the board.

Why did this happen?
