Medicare Advantage plans were getting squeezed from multiple directions simultaneously: utilization rates exceeded projections post-pandemic (people used more care), coding practices were scrutinized and partially rolled back, CMS reduced risk adjustment payments, and the new Part D benefit design shifted more cost to plans. The result was that many plans weren't profitable in certain markets, particularly rural ones, and carriers made the rational business decision to exit.

For enrolled members: anyone in an exited plan received an automatic Special Enrollment Period. They weren't left without coverage — they could enroll in a new plan or return to Original Medicare. But many had to find new doctors, rebuild care relationships, and navigate a stressful transition.

The total number of available plans fell slightly — from 5,633 in 2025 to approximately 5,600 in 2026 — but plan availability remains dense in major metro areas. The story is really about specific counties, often rural and lower-income ones, where competition thinned.

Star ratings in 2026: CMS toughened the cut-points for reaching higher star ratings. Eighteen MA-PD contracts earned 5 stars — fewer than in recent peak years. The average enrollment-weighted star rating across all plans was 3.98, up marginally from 3.95. The practical effect: fewer plans offer the 5-star Special Enrollment Period that allows mid-year plan changes.

sections.heading: What Didn't Change (That People Think Changed)
sections.content:
A few things worth clarifying because the confusion around them costs people money.

Medicare eligibility age: still 65. Still hasn't changed. The conversation about raising it to 67 has been active in policy circles for years and has gone nowhere. If you're turning 65 and enrolling in Medicare in 2026, nothing about the eligibility rules changed.

Initial enrollment window: still the seven-month period centered on your 65th birthday month (three months before, the month of, three months after). Still the same rules about late enrollment penalties for Part B and Part D if you miss it without qualifying employer coverage.

Part D late enrollment penalty: still 1% of the national base beneficiary premium per month you went without creditable drug coverage. Still permanent (it applies for as long as you have Part D). The base premium for the penalty calculation in 2026 is $38.99.

Medicare supplement (Medigap) open enrollment: still only guaranteed during the six-month period after you first enroll in Part B at 65. After that, insurers can medically underwrite you. This hasn't changed and it matters enormously for people approaching Medicare — your Medigap window is a one-time, use-it-or-lose-it opportunity.

Four-part structure: Medicare is still Parts A, B, C, D. The basic architecture hasn't changed. What has changed is the financial terms within that structure and the market dynamics of Part C (Medicare Advantage).

sections.heading: What's Coming Next: 2027 and the Continuing IRA Rollout
sections.content:
2026 isn't the end of the ACA/IRA changes rolling through Medicare. Here's what's already known about what's coming:

More IRA drug negotiations: CMS is required to negotiate prices for additional drugs each year — 15 more drugs for 2027, expanding annually. The pipeline of negotiated drugs will grow, which should gradually reduce Part D costs for commonly used medications. The downside: manufacturers have filed lawsuits challenging the negotiation process, and the outcome of those cases could affect future rounds.

Medicare Advantage market stabilization or further contraction: the 2026 pullbacks were largely a response to 2022-2024 profitability issues. Some analysts expect the market to stabilize as plans reprice to reflect true utilization. Others expect further exits in 2027 as plans adjust to the new Part D landscape. If you're in Medicare Advantage, annual plan review during open enrollment is more important than it's been in any prior year.

Part B premium trajectory: projections suggest Part B will continue increasing faster than general inflation. Drug approvals (particularly high-cost Alzheimer's and oncology drugs billed through Part B) continue to be a driver. Planning for ongoing Part B premium increases matters for retirement budgets.

IRMAA adjustments: brackets are adjusted annually for inflation. Based on recent trends, expect the income thresholds to increase about 3% annually. This doesn't change the fundamental structure but does mean people near bracket edges should monitor their income positioning year by year.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Part B premium for 2026?
faq.answer: The standard Part B premium is $202.90 per month in 2026 — an increase of $17.90 from $185.00 in 2025. The Part B annual deductible is $283. Higher-income beneficiaries pay IRMAA surcharges on top of the standard premium, ranging from $284.10 to $689.90 per month depending on income.

faq.question: What is the Part D out-of-pocket cap for 2026?
faq.answer: The Part D out-of-pocket maximum is $2,100 for 2026. Once you've spent $2,100 on covered medications in a year, your cost for covered drugs is $0 for the rest of the year. This cap was established by the Inflation Reduction Act. In 2025 the cap was $2,000; it adjusts annually based on average drug cost increases.

faq.question: Which drugs have IRA negotiated prices in 2026?
faq.answer: Ten drugs have Maximum Fair Prices effective January 1, 2026: Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Januvia, Farxiga, Entresto, Enbrel, Imbruvica, Stelara, and Fiasp/Novolog. Beneficiaries on these medications pay the negotiated price rather than the previous list price, resulting in lower out-of-pocket costs in most cases.

faq.question: Why did so many Medicare Advantage plans leave counties in 2026?
faq.answer: Major insurers including UnitedHealthcare, Humana, and Aetna/CVS pulled out of hundreds of counties for 2026 due to financial pressures: higher-than-projected utilization, reduced CMS risk adjustment payments, and the cost impact of the new Part D benefit redesign. Plans exited markets where they couldn't operate profitably. Rural and lower-income counties were disproportionately affected.

faq.question: When does my 2026 IRMAA kick in and how is it calculated?
faq.answer: Your 2026 IRMAA is based on your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI). SSA uses your 2024 tax return to calculate whether you owe a surcharge. If you retired in 2025 and your income dropped significantly, you can appeal using Form SSA-44 for a 'life-changing event' adjustment. IRMAA surcharges apply to both Part B premiums and Part D premiums.

faq.question: Did Medicare eligibility age change in 2026?
faq.answer: No. Medicare eligibility still begins at age 65. There have been ongoing discussions in Congress about raising the eligibility age but no legislation has passed. The standard enrollment rules and penalties remain unchanged.

title: Medicare vs. Marketplace Insurance: A Real Cost Comparison for Early Retirees Ages 62-64
metaDescription: If you retire before 65, you're stuck on ACA Marketplace coverage until Medicare kicks in. How does the cost actually compare? Side-by-side analysis for early retirees 62-64 — premiums, deductibles, subsidies, coverage quality, and the year you cross over.
h1: Medicare vs. Marketplace Insurance: A Real Cost Comparison for Early Retirees Ages 62-64
summary: You retire at 62. You feel great about it. Then someone asks: what are you doing about health insurance?

sections.heading: The Three Years Nobody Plans For
sections.content:
You retire at 62. You feel great about it. Then someone asks: what are you doing about health insurance?

You can't get Medicare until 65. That's not a rumor — it's the law. So there's a three-year gap where you're neither covered by an employer plan nor eligible for Medicare, and you need to figure out something else. For most early retirees, that something else is the ACA Marketplace. And the cost of that coverage — especially if your income lands in the wrong spot or if the enhanced subsidies expire — can genuinely threaten early retirement math.

This is a comparison most people don't do rigorously before they retire early. They have a general sense that Marketplace coverage is "expensive" and Medicare is "not that bad" but they haven't actually run the numbers side by side. Here are the numbers.

sections.heading: ACA Marketplace Costs for Ages 62-64: What You're Actually Paying
sections.content:
The ACA allows insurers to charge older enrollees up to three times the premium of a 21-year-old for the same plan. This is the age rating rule, and it means premiums increase significantly with age in the years just before Medicare. By 62-64, you're in the highest-cost band.

2026 benchmark premiums (second-lowest silver plan, before subsidies) for early retirees:
— Age 62: approximately $1,072 per month
— Age 63: approximately $1,102 per month
— Age 64: approximately $1,120 per month

These are national averages. Actual premiums vary by state, county, insurer, and plan. Some states run 20-30% higher (New York, Connecticut, Alaska). Some run lower. But these figures represent what a typical early retiree is looking at before subsidies.

On an annual basis: that's $12,864 to $13,440 in unsubsidized benchmark premiums at age 62-64. For a couple both in this age range, double it — $25,728 to $26,880 per year just in health insurance premiums before any cost-sharing.

And then there's the deductible. Marketplace silver plans in 2026 typically carry deductibles of $2,000 to $4,000 per person, with family deductibles of $4,000 to $8,000. Gold plans have lower deductibles ($500 to $1,500) but higher premiums. Bronze plans are cheaper in premium but carry deductibles of $6,000 to $8,000 per person — essentially catastrophic coverage.

The total potential exposure (premiums + deductible) for a single 63-year-old on a silver plan: $13,224 premium plus up to $3,000 deductible = $16,224 before out-of-pocket max kicks in. The out-of-pocket maximum on silver plans is often $8,000 to $9,000 per person. Worst case: over $21,000 for one bad health year.

sections.heading: The Subsidy Situation in 2026: This Is the Part You Have to Understand
sections.content:
The ACA's premium tax credits (subsidies) can dramatically change the calculus. If you qualify, they can turn a $1,100/month premium into a $200/month premium. But the subsidy structure has a cliff that early retirees fall off all the time, and 2026 has a specific wrinkle that deserves careful attention.

How subsidies work: the ACA caps your premium contribution at a percentage of your Modified Adjusted Gross Income, benchmarked to the second-lowest silver plan in your area. If your income is below the cap, the federal government covers the rest. The enhanced subsidies passed in 2021 (American Rescue Plan) reduced the income ceiling — for several years, even people above 400% of the Federal Poverty Level got some subsidy. Those enhanced subsidies were extended through 2025 but their status for 2026 and beyond is uncertain at the time of writing, depending on Congressional action.

What that uncertainty means in dollars: a 60-year-old couple with $85,000 household income paid approximately $7,225 per year for ACA Marketplace coverage in 2025 under enhanced subsidies. Without those subsidies, the same couple faces $31,762 per year — a 340% increase. If you are an early retiree planning your income around ACA subsidies, you need to know whether you're planning around 2025 rules or 2026 rules because the difference is enormous.

The subsidy cliff for 2026 at standard (non-enhanced) rules: subsidies phase out at 400% of the Federal Poverty Level. In 2026, 400% FPL is roughly $62,880 for a single person and $85,320 for a couple. One dollar above that threshold and you lose all subsidy. At 63 years old, that means going from subsidized premiums of perhaps $500/month to unsubsidized premiums of $1,100/month — a $7,200 annual cliff for $1 of extra income.

Income management for early retirees is genuinely important here: people retiring early with significant IRA balances face a choice about how much to draw from traditional IRA accounts versus Roth accounts versus taxable investments. Drawing from Roth accounts or taxable accounts (harvesting only capital gains) can keep MAGI low enough to qualify for subsidies. Every dollar of traditional IRA withdrawal counts against your subsidy.

The practical recommendation: if you're planning early retirement before 65, model your Marketplace coverage costs under both enhanced-subsidy and standard-subsidy scenarios and have a plan for each. Don't assume the more favorable rules persist.

sections.heading: Medicare Costs at 65: The Comparison Point
sections.content:
Now the other side of the comparison. What does Medicare actually cost?

For someone turning 65 who enrolls in standard Medicare:
— Part B: $202.90 per month (standard, no IRMAA)
— Part A: $0 for most people (if you worked 40+ quarters)
— Medigap Plan G premium: varies by age and state, but roughly $100 to $200 per month at age 65 for most of the country. Some areas run higher.
— Part D: $0 to $50 per month depending on plan

Total monthly cost for comprehensive Medicare coverage at 65 (no IRMAA): approximately $310 to $450 per month. Annual: $3,720 to $5,400.

Compare that to unsubsidized ACA Marketplace at 64: $1,120 per month in premiums alone — $13,440 per year — before any cost-sharing.

The gap is massive. Medicare is dramatically cheaper than unsubsidized Marketplace coverage for the same person, year over year. This is part of why Medicare is financially valuable and why delaying it — which is a different question entirely — has real costs.

For someone getting subsidized Marketplace coverage: the comparison is closer. A heavily subsidized early retiree might pay $200 to $500 per month on the Marketplace, which is in the same range as Medicare. The differences then come down to coverage quality, network breadth, and predictability of costs — more on that below.

Medicare Advantage at 65 can be even cheaper: many $0-premium Medicare Advantage plans exist in 2026. Your only out-of-pocket is the Part B premium ($202.90) that you'd pay anyway. But $0-premium MA plans have cost-sharing when you use services and narrower networks than Medigap — so the true cost comparison depends on how much care you use.

sections.heading: Coverage Quality: Marketplace vs. Medicare Side by Side
sections.content:
Premiums are only part of the story. What do you actually get for the money?

Network breadth: ACA Marketplace plans, particularly Silver plans, often have narrower networks than people expect. Many use HMO or EPO designs that exclude out-of-network care entirely except in emergencies. PPO plans with broader networks exist but carry higher premiums. Original Medicare (Parts A and B) has the broadest network of any insurance product in America — virtually every hospital and physician who accepts patients accepts Medicare. Every Medicare Advantage plan is more restricted than Original Medicare, but still generally broader than Marketplace HMOs.

Specialist access: Medicare beneficiaries generally have better access to specialists than Marketplace enrollees with HMO plans. Referrals are required in many Marketplace HMO plans; Original Medicare has no referral requirement. Medicare Advantage varies.

Annual out-of-pocket maximum: ACA Marketplace plans in 2026 have an out-of-pocket maximum of $9,450 for self-only coverage and $18,900 for family coverage. Medicare Advantage plans have a maximum of $9,350 for in-network services. Original Medicare has no out-of-pocket maximum, which is why Medigap exists.

Dental and vision: neither Marketplace nor Original Medicare generally covers routine dental and vision. Some Marketplace plans include limited dental/vision benefits; many Medicare Advantage plans include them. Both are gaps that require either a standalone plan or out-of-pocket spending.

Prescription coverage: ACA Marketplace plans include prescription coverage as an essential health benefit. Medicare Part D is separate and must be enrolled in separately (or via MA-PD). The Part D $2,100 out-of-pocket cap in 2026 is better protection for high drug spenders than most Marketplace out-of-pocket maximums, which typically count drugs toward the overall maximum but don't cap drug costs separately.

Preventive care: both Marketplace and Medicare cover recommended preventive services at $0 cost-sharing. The specifics vary — Medicare covers more geriatric-specific screenings; Marketplace covers some services Medicare doesn't and vice versa.

Mental health: ACA Marketplace plans are required to cover mental health on parity with physical health. Medicare covers mental health but at Part B rates (80% after deductible). Medicare Advantage often has better mental health cost-sharing than Original Medicare alone.

sections.heading: The Crossover Decision: When Should You Delay Retirement to Stay on Employer Coverage?
sections.content:
This comes up constantly and the answer is genuinely personal — but there are scenarios where the math is clear.

Scenario A: You're 62, have substantial IRA savings, and your income on paper (after careful management) will be under $50,000. Enhanced subsidies are available. Your benchmark Marketplace premium would be around $1,072/month but your actual cost might be $300 to $500 after subsidies. You'd spend $3,600 to $6,000 per year on Marketplace coverage for three years, then transition to Medicare at 65. Total Marketplace spend: $10,800 to $18,000. This is manageable and early retirement makes financial sense.

Scenario B: You're 62, receive pension income of $75,000 per year, and enhanced subsidies are not available or you're above 400% FPL at standard rules. Your Marketplace premium is $1,072 to $1,120/month with no subsidy — $12,864 to $13,440 per year. Times three years: $38,592 to $40,320 in premiums before any cost-sharing. Plus deductibles and cost-sharing if you use care. This is a real number that could affect whether early retirement makes sense.

Scenario C: You're 63, your employer offers health coverage but you're contemplating retirement. The employer plan costs you $200/month ($2,400/year). Marketplace would cost $800/month ($9,600/year). Working two more years saves you $14,400 in health insurance costs alone, plus any additional pension or 401k accumulation. The breakeven calculation should include healthcare explicitly.

The general rule: early retirement healthcare costs are a major wildcard that financial plans often underestimate. If you can retire at 65 instead of 62 with adequate financial security, the coverage transition is clean. If you need to retire before 65, understand the Marketplace costs under realistic income assumptions before you pull the trigger.

COBRA as a bridge: if you leave employer coverage, COBRA lets you stay on the plan for 18 months. The catch: you pay the full premium — employee share plus employer contribution — which is typically $600 to $1,400/month for single coverage and $1,500 to $2,800/month for family. COBRA can be more expensive than Marketplace. Compare before assuming COBRA is the default.

Retiree coverage from employer: some large employers and unions offer retiree health coverage that bridges to Medicare. If you have this, treasure it — it's increasingly rare and genuinely valuable.

sections.heading: The Year You Turn 65: Getting the Transition Right
sections.content:
The Medicare enrollment process has rules that trip people up, and a mistake in the transition from Marketplace to Medicare can cost money.

Your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) for Medicare is the seven-month window around your 65th birthday: three months before your birthday month, your birthday month, and three months after. Enroll during the first three months (before your birthday month) and coverage starts on your birthday. Enroll in the month of or after your birthday and coverage is delayed one to three months.

If you're on Marketplace coverage: you can drop your Marketplace plan when Medicare starts. You get a Special Enrollment Period for Marketplace changes based on the loss of your Marketplace coverage, which you can use to end coverage. The timing matters — you don't want a gap, and you don't want to double-pay.

The Part B late enrollment penalty: if you miss your IEP and don't have qualifying employer coverage, you owe a 10% permanent surcharge on Part B premiums for every 12 months you delayed. Being on ACA Marketplace does NOT count as qualifying coverage that waives the penalty. Marketplace is individual insurance, not employer group coverage. If you delayed Medicare enrollment because you were on Marketplace rather than employer coverage, you may owe the penalty.

Medigap and open enrollment: your guaranteed-issue Medigap window is the six months after you first enroll in Part B. During this window, any insurer must sell you any Medigap plan without medical underwriting. After this window closes, insurers can deny you coverage or charge you more based on health. This is a one-time opportunity and it coincides with the Medicare transition from Marketplace. Don't blow past it without making a deliberate decision about Medigap.

faq.question: Can I get Medicare before age 65?
faq.answer: Medicare eligibility before 65 is limited to specific situations: people who have received Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI) for 24 months, people with ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis), and people with End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) requiring dialysis or transplant. Standard early retirees cannot access Medicare before 65 regardless of income or employment status.

faq.question: What happens to my ACA Marketplace plan when I turn 65?
faq.answer: When you become eligible for Medicare at 65, you should transition to Medicare. You can continue Marketplace coverage but you cannot receive ACA premium tax credits (subsidies) once you're eligible for Medicare Part A premium-free. Additionally, Marketplace coverage isn't equivalent to Medicare, which has its own benefits and protections. Time your Marketplace termination with your Medicare start date to avoid gaps or double coverage.

faq.question: Does ACA Marketplace count as creditable coverage for Medicare purposes?
faq.answer: Marketplace coverage counts for some Medicare purposes but not all. It counts as creditable prescription drug coverage for Part D purposes, meaning you won't owe a Part D late enrollment penalty for time on Marketplace. However, it does NOT waive the Part B late enrollment penalty — only active employer group coverage does that. If you delayed Part B enrollment because you were on Marketplace, you may owe the permanent Part B penalty.

faq.question: What is the cheapest way to cover health insurance from age 62 to 65?
faq.answer: It depends heavily on your income. If you can keep your MAGI low through Roth withdrawals, taxable investment drawdown, and strategic IRA management, ACA subsidies can make Marketplace coverage very affordable — potentially $0 to $200/month. If your income exceeds subsidy thresholds, costs rise sharply. COBRA from prior employer coverage is usually more expensive than Marketplace for individual coverage. Spousal employer coverage, if available, is often the least expensive bridge option.

faq.question: How much more expensive is health insurance at 64 versus 30 under the ACA?
faq.answer: The ACA's age rating rules allow insurers to charge a 64-year-old up to three times the premium of a 21-year-old for the same plan. In practice, this means a benchmark silver plan that costs a 21-year-old $350/month will cost a 64-year-old approximately $1,050 to $1,120/month in the same market, before subsidies.

title: How to Budget for Medicare in Retirement: A Real Number Breakdown
metaDescription: What does Medicare actually cost per year in retirement? Total annual spending by scenario, IRMAA planning, Medigap premium trajectory, Part D costs, and how Roth conversion strategy affects everything. The numbers a retirement planner would actually use.
h1: How to Budget for Medicare in Retirement: A Real Number Breakdown
summary: Ask a retirement planner what to budget for healthcare in retirement and you'll get a range: $300,000 to $315,000 for a 65-year-old couple, lifetime.

sections.heading: The Number Most Retirement Plans Get Wrong
sections.content:
Ask a retirement planner what to budget for healthcare in retirement and you'll get a range: $300,000 to $315,000 for a 65-year-old couple, lifetime. That number comes from Fidelity's annual estimates and similar actuarial projections and it's useful as a lifetime framing.

But it doesn't help you budget for Tuesday.

What you actually need to know is: what do I spend on Medicare in year one of retirement? Year five? What happens if my income goes over the IRMAA threshold? What am I looking at if my Medigap insurer raises premiums 8% per year? What's the realistic worst case?

This is a working budget document, not a marketing summary. Real 2026 numbers. Three scenarios: lean (healthy, modest income, good choices), moderate (typical), and fat (high income, complex needs, or bad luck). All based on current CMS data.

sections.heading: The Baseline: What Medicare Costs in Year One at 65
sections.content:
Let's start with what you actually pay when you turn 65 and first enroll in Medicare. We'll build from here.

Scenario assumptions: single person, age 65, MAGI of $85,000 (just under the IRMAA threshold of $109,000 for 2026), retiring with Medicare for the first time. Taking Original Medicare (A+B) plus Medigap Plan G plus Part D.

Part A: $0 per month. You worked 40+ quarters. Premium-free.
Part B: $202.90 per month. That's $2,434.80 per year.
Part B deductible: $283 per year. (Paid once, then 80/20 split applies.)
Medigap Plan G: approximately $120 to $160 per month at age 65 for a non-smoker in most markets. Let's use $140/month. That's $1,680 per year.
Part D: $20 to $40 per month for basic coverage in 2026. Let's use $30/month (you're on generic medications, no specialties). That's $360 per year.

Base annual Medicare cost, Scenario 1 (lean): Part B $2,434.80 + Part B deductible $283 + Medigap $1,680 + Part D $360 = $4,757.80 per year. Call it $4,800 to round up.

For a married couple: double the Part B, Medigap, and Part D. The Part A deductible applies per person per benefit period but doesn't show up if neither is hospitalized. Couple's lean scenario: approximately $9,600 per year in Medicare costs, assuming no major health events.

This is the baseline most people don't believe when they first hear it. After paying $1,200 to $1,500 per month on employer coverage, suddenly paying $400 to $450 per month for comprehensive Medicare feels like a different world. And it is.

sections.heading: IRMAA: The Invisible Tax That Wrecks Retirement Income Plans
sections.content:
IRMAA is where Medicare budgeting gets complicated. It's the part of the system that's least intuitive, most impactful at higher income levels, and most underplanned.

Reminder: IRMAA is a surcharge on Part B and Part D premiums for beneficiaries with income above $109,000 (single) or $218,000 (married). The income used is your MAGI from two years prior. In 2026, that's your 2024 return.

Here's what IRMAA actually does to your Medicare budget across income levels for a single person in 2026:

Income $0-$109,000: Part B = $202.90/month. Annual = $2,434.80.
Income $109,001-$136,000: Part B = $284.10/month. Annual = $3,409.20. Delta: +$974.40/year.
Income $136,001-$163,000: Part B = $365.60/month. Annual = $4,387.20. Delta: +$1,952.40/year.
Income $163,001-$192,000: Part B = $447.10/month. Annual = $5,365.20. Delta: +$2,930.40/year.
Income $192,001-$500,000: Part B = $530.90/month. Annual = $6,370.80. Delta: +$3,936/year.
Income above $500,000: Part B = $689.90/month (2026 maximum). Annual = $8,278.80. Delta: +$5,844/year.

Add Part D IRMAA on top: $14.50 to $91.00/month additional.

For a married couple at $250,000 income (each paying the second IRMAA bracket): each pays $284.10/month in Part B. Together that's $568.20/month, $6,818.40/year. Plus Part D IRMAA surcharges per person. Their total Medicare costs jump to $13,000 to $15,000 per year versus $9,600 for the same couple below the threshold.

The cliff: $218,001 versus $218,000 as a couple triggers the first IRMAA bracket. That $1 of income costs you roughly $2,000 per year. This is why retirement income planning — specifically around capital gains, RMD timing, and Roth conversions — matters so much in the years before and during Medicare.

IRMAA appeals: if you had a one-time income spike in 2024 (sale of property, large distribution, severance package) that pushed you into IRMAA territory for 2026, you can appeal. CMS Form SSA-44 covers several qualifying life-changing events including work reduction, divorce, and death of a spouse. Retirement itself counts. The appeal process typically resolves in 60 to 90 days. Document the income change clearly.

sections.heading: Medigap Premium Trajectory: What You're Actually Buying Over 20 Years
sections.content:
Medigap (Medicare supplement insurance) is one of the most misunderstood products in retirement planning. People think of it as a monthly premium they pay now. It's actually a decades-long financial commitment that needs to be modeled over a 15 to 25 year horizon.

Medigap premiums increase for two reasons: general rate increases (the insurer raises rates across the board) and attained-age rating (your premium increases as you get older, because you're in a progressively higher-cost actuarial group). Not all Medigap plans use attained-age rating — some use issue-age or community rating — but even fixed-rate plans experience general premium increases.

Historical Medigap premium inflation: approximately 5% to 8% per year on average, though this varies significantly by insurer and region. Some years are higher; some plans are more stable than others.

What that trajectory looks like for Plan G:
— Age 65: $140/month ($1,680/year)
— Age 70: $175-190/month ($2,100-2,280/year) at 5% annual increases
— Age 75: $220-250/month ($2,640-3,000/year)
— Age 80: $280-320/month ($3,360-3,840/year)
— Age 85: $350-400+/month ($4,200-4,800+/year)

This is rough — your insurer and state will determine actual numbers — but the trajectory is real. By age 80, Medigap premiums for a Plan G could be 100% to 150% higher than what you paid at 65. In retirement income planning, Medigap should be modeled with a 6% annual increase assumption, not a flat number.

Medigap Plan F vs. Plan G: Plan F is the most comprehensive (covers the Part B deductible, which Plan G doesn't). But Plan F is only available to people who turned 65 before January 1, 2020. New Medicare enrollees in 2026 can only get Plan G, Plan N, or lower-coverage plans. Plan G is now the de facto comprehensive option.

Plan N as an alternative: Medigap Plan N has lower premiums than Plan G — typically 20% to 30% less — but requires copays for office visits (up to $20) and emergency room visits (up to $50). For healthy, low-utilization retirees, Plan N can be a substantial premium saver. The trade-off: if you're seeing doctors frequently, those copays accumulate. Model this against your expected utilization.

Medicare Advantage as a Medigap alternative: many people use $0-premium Medicare Advantage instead of Medigap specifically to avoid the Medigap premium burden. The trade-off is different coverage structure — cost-sharing when you use services, narrow networks, and annual plan instability (as we saw with the 2026 county exits). MA versus Medigap is one of the foundational Medicare decisions and the right answer depends on your health status, geography, and risk tolerance.

sections.heading: Part D and Prescription Drug Costs: Building the Realistic Budget
sections.content:
Part D budgeting requires the most personalization of any Medicare cost projection because it depends entirely on what drugs you take.

Here's the framework for building an accurate Part D cost projection in 2026:

Step 1: List every medication with its exact name and dosage. Both brand and generic if applicable.

Step 2: Go to Medicare.gov's Plan Finder. Enter your medications. Run the annual cost estimate for the top three plans in your area. The Plan Finder shows your total annual drug cost — premium plus cost-sharing — for each plan. This is the number that matters.

Step 3: Check whether any of your drugs are on the IRA negotiated price list. The ten negotiated drugs (Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Januvia, Farxiga, Entresto, Enbrel, Imbruvica, Stelara, Fiasp/Novolog) have reduced costs in all Part D plans in 2026.

Step 4: Factor in the $2,100 out-of-pocket cap. If your drug costs would exceed $2,100 without the cap, you know your maximum exposure. If you're on a specialty drug costing $5,000/month retail, your out-of-pocket under Part D is capped at $2,100 for the year — compared to potentially unlimited exposure in prior years.

Typical Part D annual costs by medication profile in 2026:
— Generics only (common chronic disease drugs): $400 to $800 per year including premium
— Mix of generics and one or two brand-name drugs: $800 to $1,800 per year
— One specialty drug (oncology, biologics, MS treatment): capped at $2,100 per year including premium
— Multiple specialty drugs: still $2,100 maximum (the cap protects high-cost patients)

The Part D late enrollment penalty: if you delayed Part D enrollment without creditable coverage, you pay 1% of the national base premium ($38.99 in 2026) per month you were uncovered. Someone who went 24 months without coverage owes a permanent 24% surcharge on the base premium — roughly $9.36/month additional, forever. It's not a huge dollar amount but it accumulates over decades. Avoid it by enrolling when first eligible even if you don't take prescriptions.

sections.heading: Total Annual Medicare Budget by Scenario: The Working Numbers
sections.content:
Let's build three complete annual Medicare budget scenarios for 2026. All for a single 70-year-old (five years into Medicare, so Medigap has escalated a bit).

Lean Scenario: Income $60,000. On generics only. No IRMAA. Medigap Plan G, premiums have grown to $165/month. Part D basic plan at $25/month.
— Part B: $2,434.80
— Medigap: $1,980
— Part D premium: $300
— Drug copays (generics): $400
— Total annual Medicare cost: $5,114.80

Moderate Scenario: Income $130,000 (first IRMAA bracket). Medigap Plan G at $170/month. Part D plan with one brand-name drug (Jardiance, which is now negotiated).
— Part B with first-bracket IRMAA: $284.10/month = $3,409.20/year
— Part D IRMAA: $14.50/month = $174/year
— Medigap: $2,040/year
— Part D premium: $360/year
— Drug cost-sharing (Jardiance negotiated, plus generics): $900/year
— Total annual Medicare cost: $6,883.20

High Scenario: Income $175,000 (third IRMAA bracket). Medigap Plan G at $185/month. On Eliquis and Xarelto (both IRA negotiated drugs) plus two other brand-names.
— Part B with third-bracket IRMAA: $447.10/month = $5,365.20/year
— Part D IRMAA: $46.50/month = $558/year
— Medigap: $2,220/year
— Part D premium: $400/year
— Drug cost-sharing (hitting $2,100 cap): $2,100/year
— Total annual Medicare cost: $10,643.20

High Scenario with Major Health Event: Add a hospitalization in the same year. Medigap Plan G covers the Part A deductible ($1,736), all hospital coinsurance, and all Part B coinsurance above the deductible (after the single annual Part B deductible of $283). With Medigap, catastrophic hospital costs are essentially covered — that's what you're buying with the Medigap premium.

Without Medigap (Medicare Advantage instead): the calculus is different. A Medicare Advantage plan with $0 premium but $30/day hospital coinsurance and a $3,000 in-network out-of-pocket maximum might cost you $3,000 in a hospitalization year versus essentially nothing with Medigap Plan G. But in a healthy year, you save $2,000 on Medigap premiums. Whether you come out ahead over time depends on your utilization pattern. Healthy people tend to do better with MA; people with chronic conditions or who use significant care often do better with Medigap.

sections.heading: Roth Conversion Strategy and IRMAA: The Most Valuable Planning Move
sections.content:
If you're retiring at 62 to 65 and have a mix of traditional IRA/401k and Roth accounts, the years between retirement and Medicare enrollment (or early Medicare enrollment) are often the best window for Roth conversions. Here's the specific logic:

When you retire and stop drawing a salary, your taxable income drops — sometimes dramatically. You're in a lower tax bracket than you've been in years. But required minimum distributions (RMDs) start at age 73, and those distributions are fully taxable. If you haven't converted traditional IRA money to Roth by then, RMDs will push your income up — and that income hits the IRMAA lookback two years later.

Example: you retire at 63. Income drops to $70,000 from investment and pension income. You have $800,000 in a traditional IRA. If you do nothing and let that account grow until RMDs kick in at 73, your RMD at 73 might be $30,000 to $40,000 per year on top of your other income. Depending on other income sources, that could push you into IRMAA territory for Medicare premiums you pay at age 75.

Alternatively: in the years from 63 to 72, you convert $20,000 to $30,000 per year from traditional to Roth, keeping your income just below the IRMAA threshold. You pay current income tax on the conversion (at today's rates, which may be lower than future rates), and you reduce future RMDs. Roth distributions in retirement don't count toward MAGI — so when you're 75 and pulling from Roth accounts, those dollars don't trigger IRMAA.

The math requires a tax professional and your specific numbers. But the framework is: every dollar you convert from traditional IRA to Roth below the IRMAA threshold today potentially saves you $80 to $160 in IRMAA surcharges per year in future Medicare costs, for the rest of your life. Plus the tax bracket differential if rates increase.

Critical timing: IRMAA uses a two-year lookback. Roth conversions done in 2024 affect 2026 IRMAA. So if you're planning to control 2028 IRMAA, you need your income to be in the right range in 2026. Plan two to three years ahead, not one.

Capital gains management: long-term capital gains are included in MAGI for IRMAA purposes. Retirees with taxable investment accounts should consider the IRMAA impact of realizing large gains. Bunching gains in a lower-income year versus spreading them can make a several-thousand-dollar difference in Medicare premiums.

Qualified Charitable Distributions (QCDs): for people who are charitably inclined and over 70½, QCDs allow direct transfers from an IRA to charity that are excluded from taxable income. A $20,000 QCD reduces MAGI by $20,000 — potentially keeping you in a lower IRMAA bracket. As of 2026, the annual QCD limit is $108,000. This is an underused tool.

sections.heading: Building a 20-Year Medicare Budget Projection
sections.content:
Here's how to actually build a working Medicare budget projection for retirement planning purposes. This is the framework a fee-only financial planner would use.

Inputs you need:
— Current age and expected retirement age
— Expected annual income in retirement by source (Social Security, pension, IRA distributions, Roth, taxable investments, rental income)
— Current prescription medications and whether any are specialty drugs
— Geographic market (Medicare costs vary significantly by state)
— Health status and family history (affects Medigap utilization estimates)

Year-by-year projections to build:

Part B premium: start at $202.90 for 2026. Use a 4% to 6% annual increase assumption. In 10 years at 5% annual increase, Part B is approximately $330/month per person.

Medigap premium: start at your initial premium (age 65 quote from your state). Use a 5% to 7% annual increase assumption. Model this compound growth over your projection period.

Part D: start with your current drug cost estimate. Add 3% annual increase for formulary changes and drug pricing. Factor in the $2,100 cap if you're on specialty medications.

IRMAA: project your expected MAGI in each year of retirement. Identify years where income spikes occur (RMDs starting at 73, property sales, Roth conversions). Apply the appropriate IRMAA bracket for the two-year-lagged income.

Dental and vision: budget separately. These are the biggest gaps in Medicare coverage. A realistic dental budget for a retiree without a dental plan is $1,500 to $3,000 per year, accounting for occasional crowns, root canals, or extractions. Vision: $300 to $600 per year for exams and glasses.

Long-term care: separate from Medicare. Medicare does not cover custodial long-term care (nursing home, assisted living for non-skilled care). This is a major financial exposure that requires separate planning — LTC insurance, hybrid life insurance, or self-funding.

Healthcare inflation: NCOA data shows Medicare beneficiaries spent an average of $6,330 out of pocket in 2022. Projections suggest lifetime Medicare spending for a 65-year-old couple is $275,000 to $313,000 in today's dollars. These numbers should be modeled with a 4% to 5% healthcare inflation assumption, not a general 3% inflation assumption.

The bottom line: Medicare is not free, it's not fixed-cost, and it's not simple. A 65-year-old couple starting Medicare today should budget $9,600 to $12,000 per year in baseline Medicare costs, scaling to $15,000 to $20,000 per year by their late 70s as premiums increase, and higher still if IRMAA applies or health needs grow. Getting the plan choices right in year one — especially the Medigap decision, which you largely can't undo — is worth more than almost any other retirement financial decision.

faq.question: What is the average total Medicare spending per year in retirement?
faq.answer: NCOA data shows the average Medicare beneficiary spent $6,330 out-of-pocket on all healthcare costs in 2022, including premiums, cost-sharing, and non-covered services. For 2026, realistic annual Medicare cost estimates range from about $4,800 to $5,500 per year for a healthy 65-year-old on standard Medicare with Medigap Plan G and modest drug costs. Couples should roughly double those figures. High-income beneficiaries with IRMAA surcharges or complex health needs can spend $10,000 to $15,000+ per year.

faq.question: How much does Medigap Plan G cost in 2026?
faq.answer: Medigap Plan G premiums at age 65 typically range from $100 to $200 per month ($1,200 to $2,400 per year) for non-smokers in most markets, with significant variation by state. Some high-cost markets (like New York or Florida) run higher. Premiums increase with age — expect 5% to 7% annual increases as a planning assumption. By age 80, premiums are often 100%+ higher than your age-65 rate.

faq.question: What MAGI do I need to stay under to avoid IRMAA in 2026?
faq.answer: For 2026, IRMAA surcharges kick in at MAGI above $109,000 for single filers and above $218,000 for married filing jointly. This is based on your 2024 MAGI. Staying under these thresholds means paying the standard Part B premium of $202.90/month. Exceeding them by even $1 triggers the full surcharge for the next bracket, which can cost $974 to $5,844 more per year per person depending on income level.

faq.question: Should I do Roth conversions to reduce Medicare costs?
faq.answer: For many retirees, yes — Roth conversions in the years before or early in Medicare enrollment are one of the highest-value financial planning moves available. Roth distributions don't count toward MAGI, so they don't trigger IRMAA surcharges. By converting traditional IRA funds to Roth in lower-income years (typically 62-72), you reduce future RMDs that could push you into IRMAA territory at 73+. The math requires your specific numbers — work with a fee-only financial planner or CPA.

faq.question: Is Medicare Advantage cheaper than Medigap over time?
faq.answer: It depends on your utilization. Medicare Advantage plans often have $0 or very low premiums compared to Medigap, which saves $1,200 to $2,400+ per year in premiums. However, MA plans have cost-sharing when you use services (copays, coinsurance up to the annual out-of-pocket maximum). Healthy, low-utilization retirees often pay less overall with MA. Retirees with chronic conditions, frequent hospitalizations, or complex care needs often pay less overall with Medigap despite higher premiums.

faq.question: What does Medicare not cover that I need to budget for separately?
faq.answer: Medicare's largest coverage gaps are: routine dental care (cleanings, x-rays, fillings, major restorative work), routine vision care (exams, glasses, contacts), hearing aids and exams, long-term custodial care (nursing home, assisted living not requiring skilled care), and most care received outside the U.S. Budget $1,500 to $3,000 per year for dental, $300 to $600 for vision, and make a separate long-term care plan. Dental and vision are the most common unexpected expenses for new Medicare enrollees.

title: Medicare Advantage Plans with OTC Benefits
metaDescription: How Medicare Advantage OTC benefit allowances work, what you can actually buy, which carriers are most generous, how to use the card before it expires, and what's changing in 2026.
h1: Medicare Advantage Plans with OTC Benefits
summary: How Medicare Advantage OTC benefit allowances work, what you can actually buy, which carriers are most generous, how to use the card before it expires, and...

sections.heading: What the OTC Benefit Actually Is
sections.content:
About four out of five Medicare Advantage plans offer some kind of over-the-counter benefit — a quarterly or monthly allowance loaded onto a prepaid card that you can spend on health-related products at participating retailers. It's not flashy, and carriers sometimes bury it in the plan brochure, but for a lot of beneficiaries this is real money. We're talking $50 to $200 per quarter depending on the plan, and some D-SNP plans go higher than that.

The OTC benefit isn't part of Original Medicare. You won't find it in Parts A or B. It's a supplemental benefit — meaning plans choose to offer it, and they design the rules around it. CMS allows this under what's called the Special Supplemental Benefits for the Chronically Ill (SSBCI) authority, along with older Value-Based Insurance Design (VBID) model waivers that are being phased out after 2025.

Here's the thing a lot of people don't realize until it's too late: the allowance doesn't roll over. Spend it or lose it. We've talked to beneficiaries who had $150 just sitting on their card at the end of the quarter because they didn't know what was eligible. That's $600 a year walking out the door.

The mechanics vary by carrier. Some plans load the allowance monthly, some quarterly. Some plans limit you to specific product categories. Others give you a broader wallet — the so-called OTC Plus card — that covers things like exercise equipment or even utility bills (though those expanded categories are getting tighter in 2026). You need to read your specific plan's Evidence of Coverage to know exactly what your card can do, because it genuinely varies that much.

sections.heading: What You Can Actually Buy
sections.content:
The classic OTC benefit covers standard drugstore health items — the stuff you'd normally pay out of pocket without thinking twice. Think Band-Aids, aspirin, antacids, cold medicine, allergy pills, vitamins, dental care products, first aid supplies. CVS makes it easy by putting blue shelf tags on eligible items. Walmart has a scanner you can use in-store, or you can check the app to see if something is covered before you put it in your cart.

Specific categories that are almost universally covered across plans:

First aid and wound care. Bandages, gauze, antiseptic, medical tape — all standard. Pain relievers. Both brand and generic, OTC versions only — so Tylenol yes, prescription ibuprofen no. Allergy and cold medications. Antihistamines, decongestants, cough suppressants. Digestive health. Antacids, fiber supplements, stool softeners. Dental products. Toothpaste, toothbrushes, mouthwash, denture care. Vision care. Reading glasses — yes, some plans cover OTC readers. Eye drops. Contact solution on some plans. Vitamins and supplements. Multivitamins, calcium, vitamin D, fish oil — most plans cover these though the specific list varies. Diabetes monitoring. Some plans include glucose monitors, test strips, lancets under OTC.

What you can't buy: prescription drugs (obviously), food (unless your plan specifically has a healthy food card, which is a separate benefit), cosmetics, personal hygiene that isn't health-related, and alcohol.

The OTC Plus benefit — offered by some Humana, Aetna, and UnitedHealthcare plans, particularly D-SNPs — can extend coverage to things like fitness trackers, blood pressure monitors, thermometers, and TENS units. A handful of plans have included utility bill credits and grocery allowances in the same wallet, but CMS is tightening those rules for 2026, requiring members to have a qualifying chronic condition to access the non-OTC categories.

Bottom line: if you have diabetes, heart disease, COPD, or another chronic condition, your OTC wallet might be considerably more flexible. Worth asking your carrier specifically.

sections.heading: Dollar Amounts by Carrier — What to Expect
sections.content:
CMS doesn't set a minimum OTC allowance. Plans decide. And the spread is wide.

On the lower end, you'll see $25 to $50 per quarter — that's $100 to $200 annually. These are typically basic HMO plans in competitive markets where carriers are managing costs aggressively. On the higher end, especially in D-SNP plans designed for dual-eligible beneficiaries, you can see $150 to $200 per quarter or more — some plans have offered up to $500 quarterly for certain SSBCI-qualifying members, though those plans got scarce heading into 2026.

For 2025, here's roughly where the major carriers land based on plan comparisons:

UnitedHealthcare AARP plans: Generally $75 to $150 per quarter on standard MA plans. D-SNP plans offer an upgraded Extra Supports Wallet combining OTC, food, and utility credits — total value depends on qualifying conditions. Humana: Ranges widely, $50 to $200 per quarter depending on the specific plan. Humana Achieve, Value, and Choice plans tend to fall in the lower range; their Gold Plus plans often run higher. Aetna: Standard MA plans often $75 to $125 per quarter. D-SNP plans get the OTC Wallet upgrade, which can reach $200+ depending on chronic condition status. BCBS plans (varies by state): Blue Cross Blue Shield of Michigan, for example, loads Advantage Dollars quarterly. Specific amounts vary by plan but commonly range from $50 to $150. Cigna/Evernorth: Competitive in urban markets, typically $50 to $100 per quarter on standard plans. WellCare and Centene-owned plans: Often higher OTC benefits — WellCare has offered $100 to $200 quarterly — because they typically serve lower-income markets where supplemental benefits matter more.

Important caveat heading into 2026: many carriers cut or restructured their supplemental benefits due to CMS rate pressure and the phase-out of the VBID model. Oliver Wyman analysis from late 2024 found that over 10.5 million beneficiaries with OTC allowances in 2024 saw the amounts decrease for 2025. The trend is continuing. If you're comparing plans for next enrollment, check the Annual Notice of Change carefully — your quarterly amount may have dropped from what you're used to.

The Better Medicare Alliance's 2026 data shows the percentage of individual MA plans offering OTC items declining slightly year over year. Not going away, but getting less generous across the board.

sections.heading: Where to Use the Card
sections.content:
Most OTC benefit cards work at a network of participating retailers that your carrier has set up. The big ones: CVS Pharmacy, Walgreens, Walmart, Rite Aid, Dollar General, Family Dollar, Kroger (and affiliated grocery chains), and Target in some plans. Online ordering through CVS.com and Walmart.com is available for most plans — this is actually the most convenient way to shop, especially if you have mobility issues or live in an area without easy access to retail.

CVS is by far the most integrated partner. They've built out OTC benefit shopping extensively — their website has a dedicated benefit shopping section, their app lets you scan items, and the blue shelf tags in-store mark eligible products. If your plan uses CVS as its primary OTC retailer, which many do, this is seamless.

Some plans also have their own online OTC catalog — basically a web store where you pick items and they ship to your door. Aetna, UnitedHealthcare, and Humana all have this for at least some plan types. You get a list of approved products, add to cart, and it ships free. For homebound or rural beneficiaries this is genuinely useful.

Phone ordering exists too. Most carriers have an OTC benefit phone line where you can place orders by calling and having them mailed. It's slower and more limited but it works for people who don't do online shopping.

At retail checkout: present your OTC card like a debit card. No PIN required in most cases. The register automatically identifies eligible items — anything that doesn't qualify gets flagged, and you'd need to pay out of pocket for those. You can't use the OTC card to pay for non-eligible items, so you may need a second form of payment if your cart is mixed.

sections.heading: Expiration Rules and How Not to Lose Your Balance
sections.content:
This is where people consistently get burned. The OTC allowance expires. Period.

For quarterly allowances: whatever you don't use by the last day of the quarter is gone. March 31, June 30, September 30, December 31. That's it. Your carrier does not roll it over. They don't give you a grace period. They definitely don't refund it. The money just disappears and your balance resets to zero.

For monthly allowances: same deal but on a monthly cycle. That's actually worse in some ways because you have a shorter window to spend, though the amounts are typically smaller.

Some plans do allow a rollover for a defined number of months — this is plan-specific and relatively uncommon. If rollover is a priority for you, ask your carrier directly and get it confirmed in writing because plan brochures aren't always clear about this.

Practical strategies to not lose your balance:

Set a calendar reminder for the last week of each quarter. Check your balance online or via the carrier's app — most plans let you see your remaining balance in real time. Stock up. OTC items have a long shelf life. Aspirin, vitamins, first aid supplies, dental products — none of this goes bad quickly. If you have $75 left on March 28, go buy $75 worth of vitamins and cold medicine you'll use over the next several months. Shop online with free shipping. If you can't get to a store before the deadline, the carrier's online OTC catalog usually processes and ships even if you order late in the quarter — but confirm this with your carrier because order cutoff dates vary. Gift and share within household. Some plans allow you to use your OTC benefit to buy items for a household member — check your plan documents because this varies. Help family members shop. If your kids or grandkids need health items and you have balance to burn, some plans permit this.

The one move a lot of beneficiaries miss: CPAP supplies. If you use a CPAP, check whether your plan's OTC benefit covers CPAP masks, filters, or cleaning supplies. Some do. That's a meaningful expense you can shift off your own wallet.

sections.heading: What's Changing for 2026
sections.content:
This is worth paying attention to if you're doing plan comparisons.

CMS finalized significant changes to how supplemental benefits work starting in 2026. The VBID (Value-Based Insurance Design) model program is ending, and the path for expanded supplemental benefits — which is what gave many plans the flexibility to offer broad OTC Plus wallets covering food and utilities — is shifting entirely to the SSBCI (Special Supplemental Benefits for the Chronically Ill) framework.

What this means practically: starting in 2026, to access spending on healthy food, utility bills, or other non-standard OTC categories, you generally need to have a qualifying chronic health condition. Plans will still offer the OTC card, but the scope of what you can spend it on may be more restricted for members without documented chronic conditions.

UnitedHealthcare sent out specific FAQs to members explaining this shift — their Extra Supports Wallet, which combines food, OTC, and utility credits, will require qualifying conditions for the food and utility portions starting in 2026. The basic OTC portion remains accessible.

In practice, most beneficiaries with chronic conditions (diabetes, heart disease, COPD, etc.) will still qualify for expanded benefits. The change mostly affects healthier MA enrollees who were benefiting from broad food allowances.

Across the board, the trend is: less generous, more targeted. Plans are under margin pressure from CMS rate adjustments, and supplemental benefits are one of the first levers they pull. If your 2025 plan had a strong OTC benefit, review the Annual Notice of Change before assuming 2026 will be the same.

Best move: use the Medicare Plan Finder at medicare.gov to compare OTC benefit amounts side by side during the Annual Enrollment Period. Filter by the supplemental benefits you care about. The dollar amounts are shown, and you can see exactly what categories each plan covers.

faq.question: What happens to my OTC balance if I don't use it before the quarter ends?
faq.answer: It's gone. Unused OTC allowances expire at the end of each benefit period — typically quarterly on the last day of March, June, September, and December. Your carrier doesn't roll it over, credit it to next period, or refund it. Set a calendar reminder in the last week of each quarter and spend it down on items you'll use.

faq.question: Can I use my OTC card for groceries?
faq.answer: Not with a standard OTC card. Some plans — particularly D-SNP and certain SSBCI plans — offer a separate healthy food benefit or a combined wallet that includes grocery allowances. But standard OTC benefits cover health and wellness products, not general groceries. In 2026, food allowances are restricted to members with qualifying chronic conditions.

faq.question: Which Medicare Advantage plan has the highest OTC benefit?
faq.answer: It varies by zip code and plan type. D-SNP plans for dual-eligible beneficiaries typically offer the most generous allowances — some reaching $150 to $200+ per quarter. WellCare and Centene-affiliated plans have been competitive on OTC amounts. The only way to see exact dollar amounts for plans available in your area is to use medicare.gov's Plan Finder and look at the supplemental benefits detail for each plan.

faq.question: Can I use my OTC card at Amazon?
faq.answer: Some plans have partnered with Amazon for OTC benefit purchases, but this isn't universal. Check your carrier's specific OTC benefit retailer list. CVS, Walmart, and Walgreens are the most consistently accepted retailers. If Amazon isn't listed, try the carrier's own online OTC catalog for home delivery.

faq.question: What if I buy something with my OTC card that isn't covered?
faq.answer: The register will reject it. OTC benefit cards are coded to only authorize eligible product categories — the checkout system automatically declines items that don't qualify. You'll need a second form of payment for anything not covered. This is actually useful because it prevents accidental spending on ineligible items.

faq.question: Do OTC benefits count toward my deductible?
faq.answer: No. OTC benefit allowances are supplemental — they're completely separate from your medical coverage and deductibles. The allowance is a spending credit on a prepaid card, not an insurance payment. Using your OTC card doesn't affect your plan's cost-sharing in any way.

faq.question: I have CPAP — can I use my OTC card for supplies?
faq.answer: Sometimes, yes. Some plans include CPAP supplies — filters, masks, cleaning supplies — in their OTC eligible product list. Check your plan's specific OTC catalog or call your carrier. If it's covered, this can save meaningful money given how frequently CPAP supplies need replacing.

title: Switching Medicare Advantage Plans: When and How
metaDescription: A plain-English guide to AEP, OEP, and SEP — when you can switch Medicare Advantage plans, the step-by-step process, what happens to your current coverage, and the underwriting trap waiting for anyone who wants to go back to Original Medicare with a Medigap plan.
h1: Switching Medicare Advantage Plans: When and How
summary: A plain-English guide to AEP, OEP, and SEP — when you can switch Medicare Advantage plans, the step-by-step process, what happens to your current coverage, and...

sections.heading: The Three Windows When You Can Actually Switch
sections.content:
Most of the year you're locked in. Medicare Advantage has three enrollment periods that let you make changes, and they have different rules, different options, and different consequences.

Annual Enrollment Period (AEP) runs October 15 through December 7 every year. This is the big one. During AEP you can do essentially anything: switch from one MA plan to another, switch from MA to Original Medicare, switch from Original Medicare to MA, change your Part D drug plan, or drop Part D entirely. Changes made during AEP take effect January 1. There's no limit on how many changes you can make before December 7 — your last election wins. So if you enroll in Plan A on October 20 and then change your mind and enroll in Plan B on November 15, Plan B is what takes effect in January.

Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (MA OEP) runs January 1 through March 31. This is a more limited window — you get one change, and that's it. During MA OEP, if you're already enrolled in a Medicare Advantage plan, you can switch to a different MA plan or drop MA and go back to Original Medicare. What you can't do during OEP is switch from Original Medicare to MA — that would require AEP or a qualifying Special Enrollment Period. One change only. Use it wisely.

Special Enrollment Periods (SEPs) can happen any time during the year if you have a qualifying life event. These are the escape hatches. You move out of your plan's service area, your plan gets terminated, you get a chronic condition that qualifies you for a Special Needs Plan, you enter or leave a nursing facility, your plan loses its star rating — these all trigger an SEP. The rules changed meaningfully in 2025 for low-income and dual-eligible beneficiaries specifically.

For dual-eligible beneficiaries (those on both Medicare and Medicaid) starting in 2025: CMS eliminated the old quarterly SEP and replaced it with a monthly SEP, but with new restrictions. Full-benefit dual-eligible members can now change plans monthly in a more flexible way, but the prior model of just switching any MA plan every quarter is gone. The monthly SEP for duals now specifically covers dropping MA to return to Original Medicare with a PDP (once per year) and, for full-benefit D-SNP enrollees, switching between aligned or integrated plans.

sections.heading: Step-by-Step: How to Actually Switch Plans
sections.content:
Switching sounds complicated. It isn't if you follow the sequence.

Step 1: Confirm you're in an eligible enrollment window. If it's not AEP (Oct 15–Dec 7), OEP (Jan 1–Mar 31), or a qualifying SEP, you generally can't switch. Call 1-800-MEDICARE to confirm whether a specific SEP applies to your situation.

Step 2: Compare your options before you pull the trigger. Don't just look at premium. Total cost of ownership matters more — premiums plus expected out-of-pocket for your actual utilization. Use medicare.gov's Plan Finder, enter your zip code, and enter your current medications. The tool calculates estimated annual costs across all plans in your area including drug costs.

Things to specifically compare: your doctors' network participation in the new plan (call the plan directly — the online directories aren't always current), formulary tier for your medications in the new plan vs. current, and any supplemental benefits that matter to you like dental, vision, OTC, or transportation.

Step 3: Enroll in the new plan. You don't have to call your old plan to cancel — enrollment in a new plan automatically disenrolls you from the old one. You can enroll online at medicare.gov, by calling the new plan directly, by calling 1-800-MEDICARE, or by working with a licensed insurance agent or SHIP counselor.

Step 4: Confirm the transition. You'll get an enrollment confirmation from the new plan. Hold onto it. If you don't receive confirmation within a week, follow up.

Step 5: Verify your coverage start date and make sure your doctors have the new insurance information. During the transition period — especially in January when AEP changes take effect — providers sometimes still have the old plan on file and bill incorrectly. Carry your new card as soon as you have it and update your care team.

Step 6: Handle your Part D if applicable. If you're switching to a plan that includes drug coverage, your old standalone Part D plan gets terminated automatically. If you're switching MA plans and neither has Part D, or you're moving to Original Medicare, you may need to enroll in a standalone Part D plan separately during the same enrollment window.

sections.heading: What Happens to Your Current Coverage During the Switch
sections.content:
Coverage doesn't gap as long as you're switching from one plan to another. Here's what actually happens.

For AEP switches taking effect January 1: your old plan covers you through December 31. Your new plan starts January 1. There's no gap. If you have a procedure or hospitalization on December 28, that's your old plan. January 2, that's your new plan. Your new member ID card should arrive before January 1 — if it doesn't, call the new plan and ask for confirmation of enrollment, which providers can use.

For OEP switches taking effect the first of the following month: if you switch on February 10, your new coverage starts March 1. Your old plan covers you through February 28.

For SEP switches: effective dates vary by the type of SEP. Moving out of a service area typically gives you a new plan starting the month after you notify Medicare. Plan termination situations have their own timelines.

Drug coverage continuity: if you're mid-refill cycle when the switch happens, your new plan's formulary applies starting on the effective date. If your medication is on the new plan's formulary at a different tier, you pay the new cost-sharing rate. If it's not on the formulary at all, you may need to request an exception or transition supply. CMS requires plans to provide a 30-day emergency supply of drugs not on the formulary during plan transitions — use this if you need it while pursuing an exception.

Prior authorizations don't transfer. If you had a prior auth for a specialist, procedure, or medication under your old plan, the new plan starts fresh. You'll need to request new prior authorizations if required. This is a real operational headache and one of the most common complaints when people switch plans mid-treatment.

sections.heading: The Medigap Underwriting Trap — Read This Before You Leave MA
sections.content:
This is the part that catches people off guard and it's genuinely serious.

When you first turn 65 and enroll in Medicare Part B, you have a six-month guaranteed issue window to buy any Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policy in your state with no medical underwriting. No health questions. No denials. No higher premiums for pre-existing conditions. This window exists exactly once for most people, and most people on Medicare Advantage have already used it up — or let it expire without buying a Medigap plan.

Here's what that means: if you're on a Medicare Advantage plan and you decide you want to go back to Original Medicare with a Medigap supplement — maybe your MA plan's network got worse, maybe you need a specialist out of network, maybe you moved — in 46 states, the Medigap insurer can reject you based on your health history, or charge you substantially higher premiums. Permanently.

You have cancer? Denied. Heart disease? Denied. Diabetes? Maybe declined or heavily rated. The insurer can look at your entire medical history and decide they don't want to cover you, or they'll charge you so much that the supplement isn't economically viable.

Exceptions — situations where you keep guaranteed issue rights even after MA:

If you tried MA for the very first time and it's within 12 months of your first MA enrollment, you can drop the plan and buy any Medigap with guaranteed issue. This is the trial right. You get it once, and only for plans you're enrolling in for the first time.

If your MA plan is leaving your area or losing its Medicare contract, you get a guaranteed issue SEP.

If you moved outside your plan's service area.

If your plan committed fraud or misrepresentation that materially affected your enrollment decision.

That's about it for federal rights. The states with stronger protections: Connecticut, Massachusetts, Maine, and New York require guaranteed issue Medigap enrollment on at least some rolling basis. A handful of other states have birthday rules (California, Idaho, Illinois, Louisiana, Missouri, Nevada, Oklahoma, Oregon) that give you a window around your birthday to switch Medigap plans without underwriting — though the scope varies. Utah and Virginia updated their rules in 2025.

If you're considering leaving MA for Original Medicare and you've had any significant health issues, talk to a SHIP counselor or a licensed broker before making the switch. The underwriting situation can make the move financially punishing. This is the single biggest reason people stay on Medicare Advantage plans they dislike — the exit cost is too high.

sections.heading: Common Reasons People Switch and Whether They Should
sections.content:
Let's talk about the actual scenarios.

Doctor left the network. This is the most common trigger. Your primary care doctor or a specialist you rely on dropped the plan, and suddenly you're facing out-of-network costs or finding a new provider. This is a legitimate reason to switch — but do your homework before assuming the new plan has your doctor in-network. Provider directories are notoriously inaccurate. Call the provider's office directly and ask if they're currently accepting patients under the specific plan you're considering. Not 'do you take Humana' — which specific Humana plan.

Premiums went up significantly. Check the Annual Notice of Change every fall. Some plans raise premiums substantially year over year. If your plan's premium jumped $40/month, shopping alternatives is smart. But factor in the full picture — a $0 premium plan with a $6,700 out-of-pocket maximum is not automatically better than a $80/month plan with a $3,400 maximum if you have ongoing medical needs.

Drug went off formulary. This happens. If a medication you depend on is no longer covered by your plan's formulary, or got moved to a tier that makes it unaffordable, switching plans during AEP specifically to get better drug coverage is entirely rational. Use the Plan Finder's drug cost calculator.

Moved to a new area. Service areas are geographically defined. If you move out of your plan's service area, you qualify for an SEP and need to enroll in a new plan that covers your new location. Or, if you're moving to a rural area with limited MA options, you may want to consider Original Medicare plus a Medigap supplement if you can still get guaranteed issue.

Got frustrated with network restrictions. If you've had prior auth denials, difficulty seeing specialists, or just don't want the managed care model anymore — that's valid. But see the underwriting section above before you bail. The return trip to Medigap might not be possible or affordable.

sections.heading: SEPs You Might Not Know You Qualify For
sections.content:
Beyond the obvious ones, there are special enrollment periods that beneficiaries regularly miss.

Five-Star SEP: CMS rates Medicare Advantage plans on a 1 to 5 star scale. If you're in a lower-rated plan, you can switch to a 5-star plan once per calendar year, any time of year. Not many plans hit 5 stars, but the ones that do tend to be worth knowing about. Check the Plan Finder and filter for 5-star ratings.

SNP Chronic Condition SEP: If you're newly diagnosed with a chronic condition that qualifies you for a Chronic Special Needs Plan (C-SNP) — conditions like diabetes, cardiovascular disease, COPD, chronic heart failure, end-stage renal disease — you can enroll in a C-SNP at any time. These plans are designed around your specific condition and often have better specialist access and coordinated care for it.

Institutional / Nursing Home SEP: If you move into a nursing facility or are hospitalized and then discharged to skilled nursing, you typically qualify for an SEP to switch plans or enroll in an Institutional SNP (I-SNP). Similarly, moving out of a nursing facility triggers an SEP.

Low-Income SEP: If you qualify for Extra Help on drug costs or newly gain Medicaid coverage, you get SEP rights. As noted above, for 2025 the quarterly SEP for duals was replaced with a new monthly SEP structure — more flexible for some, more restricted for others depending on plan type.

COBRA exhaustion: if your COBRA coverage expires and you missed your initial Medicare enrollment window, you generally get a SEP to enroll. This isn't specifically an MA switching SEP but it's relevant for people coming into Medicare who want MA from day one.

Benchmark SEP: If your MA plan's premium increases to more than zero (i.e., a plan that was previously premium-free now charges a premium), you get a SEP to switch. This is obscure but real.

faq.question: Can I switch Medicare Advantage plans more than once a year?
faq.answer: During AEP (October 15 to December 7), yes — as many times as you want before the period closes. But during OEP (January 1 to March 31), you only get one change. Outside of those periods, switching requires a qualifying Special Enrollment Period event.

faq.question: What's the difference between AEP and OEP?
faq.answer: AEP (Annual Enrollment Period, Oct 15–Dec 7) is the annual window where anyone with Medicare can make any change — switch MA plans, drop MA, add MA, change Part D. OEP (Open Enrollment Period, Jan 1–Mar 31) is limited: only one change, and only for people already enrolled in an MA plan. You can't join MA for the first time during OEP.

faq.question: If I switch plans, will my doctors still be covered?
faq.answer: Not automatically. Every Medicare Advantage plan has its own provider network, and your doctors may or may not be in-network on the new plan. Don't rely solely on the online provider directory — they're often outdated. Call your provider's office directly and confirm they're accepting your specific plan before you enroll.

faq.question: Can I go back to Original Medicare from Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: Yes, during AEP or OEP. But if you want to add a Medigap supplement when you go back to Original Medicare, you may face medical underwriting in most states. If you have significant health conditions, insurers can deny you coverage or charge substantially higher premiums. Four states (CT, MA, ME, NY) require some guaranteed issue access. Check your state's rules before making the switch.

faq.question: What is the trial right for Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: If you're new to Medicare Advantage — enrolling in an MA plan for the first time — and you decide within 12 months that you want to go back to Original Medicare, you have a guaranteed issue right to purchase any Medigap policy in your state with no health questions. This trial right exists once, for your first MA plan. Use it if you want out during that window.

faq.question: My plan is leaving my area. What do I do?
faq.answer: You qualify for a Special Enrollment Period and can switch to any MA plan in your new area, or return to Original Medicare. CMS requires plans to give advance notice of terminations — you should receive a letter explaining your options and the timeline. If you don't receive one and you're hearing rumors about your plan, call Medicare directly at 1-800-MEDICARE.

faq.question: Does switching Medicare Advantage plans affect my Social Security benefits?
faq.answer: No. Medicare Advantage plan enrollment and Social Security are separate. However, if you have your Part B premium deducted from your Social Security payment, a change in plan (particularly going to a plan with a Part B premium buy-down) may affect the net amount withheld. This typically takes a few months to update.

title: Medicare for Retired Federal Employees (FEHB)
metaDescription: How FEHB and Medicare coordinate for federal annuitants, when Medicare Part B is actually worth paying for, the FEHB suspension option, what OPM says about the dental and vision gap, and how the new PSHB program changes the math for postal workers.
h1: Medicare for Retired Federal Employees (FEHB)
summary: How FEHB and Medicare coordinate for federal annuitants, when Medicare Part B is actually worth paying for, the FEHB suspension option, what OPM says about the...

sections.heading: The FEHB-Medicare Situation Most Federal Retirees Face
sections.content:
Federal retirement benefits are genuinely good. The Federal Employees Health Benefits (FEHB) program is one of the most comprehensive employer-sponsored health insurance programs in the country — over 130 plan options in 2026, covering everything from catastrophic costs to prescription drugs. So when federal retirees hit 65 and Medicare comes into the picture, the question isn't 'do I need Medicare' — it's 'does Medicare make my existing coverage better, and what does it cost me to find out.'

The short answer: FEHB alone is sufficient at 65. You don't have to enroll in Medicare Part B. Unlike most Americans who need Medicare as their primary coverage at 65, federal retirees with FEHB already have comprehensive insurance. The government doesn't penalize you for declining Part B and keeping just FEHB active.

But 'sufficient' and 'optimal' are different things. The question is whether the $185.00 monthly Part B premium in 2025 (or more, if you're subject to IRMAA surcharges based on income) generates enough coverage improvement to be worth the additional cost — especially because Part B costs compound over retirement. Over ten years, $185/month is $22,200 at current rates, before any premium increases.

The calculus changed over the past several years because of a significant evolution in how FEHB plans coordinate with Medicare. Plans that qualify as Employer Group Waiver Plans (EGWPs) can essentially cover your Part B cost-sharing gaps — meaning the combination of Medicare paying first and FEHB paying second results in close to zero out-of-pocket for hospitalizations and many medical expenses. When that works, the combination is powerful. When it doesn't, you're paying for two layers of coverage that don't synergize cleanly.
