Medigap loss ratios. Required by CMS under the ACA, carriers must maintain a minimum 65% loss ratio on individual Medigap policies (meaning at least 65 cents of every premium dollar goes to claims). Some states require 75% or higher. If a carrier's loss ratio is consistently in the 70-75% range, they're running efficiently. If it's 55%, they're either over-reserving or harvesting profit, and a correction is coming.

The single best tracking tool I've seen for normal people: Medicare.gov's Medigap comparison tool shows current rates from participating carriers. Screenshot it annually. Not a complete rate history, but over two or three years you can build your own trend data if your state doesn't publish it cleanly.

sections.heading: When to Switch: The Math You Actually Have to Run
sections.content:
Switching Medigap plans isn't automatically a good idea just because a competitor is cheaper today. You have to think about three things: current savings, future trajectory, and underwriting risk.

Let's do a real example. Say you're 70, on Plan G with Carrier A, paying $195/month. Carrier B offers the same Plan G at $158/month. That's $37/month savings — $444/year. Sounds obvious, right?

Not necessarily. First, look at Carrier B's rate history. If they've been averaging 9% annual increases versus Carrier A's 5%, Carrier B will overtake Carrier A within three or four years. You'd save money short-term and pay more long-term. Run the math out at least five years, ideally ten.

Second — and this is the one that trips people up — you need to pass underwriting to switch. Medigap has no guaranteed issue outside of specific qualifying events. Most states, if you're outside your open enrollment window, require medical underwriting. That means if you have diabetes, heart disease, COPD, recent cancer, recent hospitalization... you might not qualify. Or you'll qualify with a rated premium that's higher than what you're paying now.

The exception to this is the states with birthday rules or other continuous open enrollment protections, which we cover in depth in the next article. But in the 40-something states without those protections, switching requires passing a health questionnaire. If you're healthy, great. Do the math and switch if it makes sense. If you have chronic conditions, switching may be impossible or worse than staying put.

Third thing to consider: the plan letter itself. Are you considering switching plan letters (say, from F to G, or from G to N) as part of the switch? If so, you're also changing benefits, not just carriers. Plan N has lower premiums than Plan G but comes with copays ($20 office visit, $50 ER visit that waives if admitted) and excess charge exposure if doctors don't accept Medicare assignment. The math has to include potential out-of-pocket changes, not just premium.

A switching trigger I'd personally use: if Carrier B's five-year projected cost is more than $600-800 lower than Carrier A, and I'm healthy enough to clear underwriting, I'd look hard at switching. Under $600 in projected savings? The administrative hassle, the underwriting risk, and the possibility I'm wrong about rate trajectories make it not worth it.

sections.heading: The Birthday Rule States: A Free Window Most People Don't Use
sections.content:
A handful of states have passed what's commonly called the birthday rule — a guaranteed-issue window once a year, timed to your birthday, that lets you switch to an equal or lesser Medigap plan without medical underwriting. No health questions. No denial risk. Just shop, compare, pick.

This is huge. It completely changes the switching math because it removes the underwriting risk that otherwise locks people into their current carrier even when better options exist.

California created the birthday rule and it's been in effect since 2000. Oregon followed. Then Missouri, Illinois, Idaho, Louisiana, Nevada, and a few others. The list has been growing — several states passed birthday rule legislation between 2020 and 2024. By the time you're reading this, check your current state's status directly, because the map changes.

How the window works varies slightly by state. California gives you 30 days starting on your birthday. Oregon gives you 30 days as well. Some states extend it to 60 days. During that window, you can switch to any Medigap plan at the same level or lower — same plan letter or step down — without answering health questions. What "same or lower" means is defined differently in each state, but typically it means you can't use the birthday rule to upgrade from Plan N to Plan G. You can go from G to G, or G to N, but not N to G.

What's the actual strategy here? In states with the birthday rule, you should be comparing rates every year in the month or two before your birthday. Set a calendar reminder. Literally put it in your phone. Run a current quote comparison. If a competitor is meaningfully cheaper AND has a stable rate history, switch. You've got a clean shot at it once a year.

A few nuances worth knowing: some carriers in birthday rule states will try to get you to answer health questions anyway — either out of ignorance of the rule or hoping you'll answer before realizing you don't have to. You don't have to. Cite the statute. If they push back, your state insurance department has a complaint line.

Also: the birthday rule is specifically for plan-to-plan switches. It doesn't help you if you're trying to switch from Medicare Advantage back to Original Medicare plus Medigap. That's a different pathway with different rules.

sections.heading: High-Deductible Plan G: The Rate Increase Hedge Nobody Talks About
sections.content:
Let me throw one more thing in here because it gets underused. High-Deductible Plan G (HD-G) exists. The 2024 deductible is $2,800. You pay out-of-pocket until you hit that number, then HD-G covers what standard Plan G would cover. In exchange, your monthly premium is dramatically lower — often $40-70/month versus $140-200 for standard Plan G.

If you're 65 and healthy, you could be putting $100-130/month in savings versus standard Plan G. Over twelve months that's $1,200-1,560. Unless you have a really bad year medically, you're likely coming out ahead or close to even. And the premium is substantially lower, which means the annual rate increase — even if it's the same percentage — hits a much smaller base. 8% on $55/month is $4.40. 8% on $165/month is $13.20.

The catch: if you have a bad medical year, you're exposed to the full $2,800 deductible. And you need to be disciplined about setting aside what you're saving in premiums rather than spending it. But for the right person — healthy, financially stable, not prone to catastrophizing every medical bill — HD-G is a genuinely smart way to keep costs lower and reduce the compounding effect of annual rate increases.

It's also still a real Medigap plan with guaranteed renewability. Your carrier can't cancel you for getting sick.

sections.heading: What to Do Right Now if Your Rate Just Increased
sections.content:
Step one: don't panic-cancel anything. You need coverage while you're researching alternatives.

Step two: pull your state's insurance department website and look up your carrier's rate filing history. You want to see the past three to five years of increases. If this is a one-time spike on an otherwise stable history, that's different than a carrier that's been raising 9-12% for four consecutive years.

Step three: run current quotes from at least three other carriers. Medicare.gov's plan finder is a starting point. A licensed broker who works across multiple carriers is better — they can show you more options and pull rate histories you might not find on your own.

Step four: check your state for birthday rule protection or any other guaranteed-issue windows. If you're in California, Oregon, Missouri, Illinois, or another birthday rule state, check when your window opens.

Step five: if you're considering switching and you're not in a guaranteed-issue state, be honest with yourself about your health. Not what you think your health is. What an underwriter's questionnaire will say about your health. Hospitalizations in the past year? Chronic conditions on medication? Recent diagnoses? These can all affect your ability to switch.

Step six: if switching makes financial sense and you can clear underwriting, do it. Don't wait for next year. The carrier isn't going to refund this year's increase while you think about it.

One more thing. If you get a rate increase notice and the carrier doesn't explain why, you can ask. Call the customer service line, ask for the actuarial justification for the rate change. They have to have one — it's in the rate filing. They won't always give you a useful answer, but asking puts them on notice that you're paying attention. Sometimes that alone prompts a retention offer. Rare, but it happens.

faq.question: How often do Medigap premiums go up?
faq.answer: Most carriers adjust Medigap premiums annually, typically effective January 1. Some carriers adjust mid-year in certain states. You'll receive a written notice at least 30 days before any premium change — usually it arrives in October or November for a January increase. The size of the increase varies by carrier, plan letter, state, and pricing method, but 4-10% annual increases are common, with some carriers going higher.

faq.question: Can my Medigap carrier cancel my policy if I get sick?
faq.answer: No. Medigap is guaranteed renewable, meaning as long as you pay your premiums on time, the carrier cannot cancel your policy because of health status, claims volume, or any medical reason. They can raise your rates — they do that collectively across all policyholders in your state — but they can't single you out or cancel you individually.

faq.question: Is Plan F going up faster than Plan G?
faq.answer: Generally yes, and this is likely to continue. Plan F closed to new enrollees on January 1, 2020. No one born after January 1, 1955 can enroll, so the pool is permanently aging with no new younger enrollees coming in. An aging pool files more claims, which pushes rates up faster. Plan G has a broader, more diverse pool with new 65-year-olds entering every month. If you're still on Plan F, run a comparison with Plan G carriers — the premium difference plus the Part B deductible you'd now be responsible for under G might still net out in G's favor over five years.

faq.question: What's the difference between a rate increase and an age-based increase?
faq.answer: On attained-age plans, both happen simultaneously. Your premium goes up because you aged one year (the age component) and also because the carrier adjusted rates for medical inflation and claims experience (the inflationary component). On issue-age plans, you only get the inflationary component — no age-based bump. On community-rated plans, everyone pays the same regardless of age, so there's no age component at all, just the shared inflationary adjustment.

faq.question: Do I have to answer health questions to switch Medigap plans?
faq.answer: In most states, outside of guaranteed-issue periods, yes. Medical underwriting is required for most Medigap switches. States with birthday rules (California, Oregon, Missouri, Illinois, Idaho, Nevada, Louisiana, and others) provide an annual guaranteed-issue window around your birthday where no underwriting is required for equal-or-lesser plan switches. A few other states have their own open enrollment protections. Check your specific state.

faq.question: Which carriers have the lowest rate increase history?
faq.answer: This changes by state and varies year to year, so there's no universal answer. AARP/UnitedHealthcare and Mutual of Omaha tend to appear frequently as more stable pricers in many markets, but that's not universal. USAA has a strong history in states where it's available. Smaller regional carriers can be excellent or terrible — you have to pull their specific state filing history. Never choose based on current premium alone.

keyTakeaways: Medigap rate increases are driven by three compounding forces: medical inflation, aging pool dynamics, and carrier profitability decisions — all stack on top of each other.
Plan F pools are permanently aging since the plan closed to new enrollees in 2020, meaning F rate increases will likely outpace Plan G increases indefinitely.
The pricing method — attained-age, issue-age, or community-rated — matters more for long-term cost than today's premium. Always identify which method your carrier uses.
Rate history is public record in every state. Pull it. Five years of filings shows you the trend — single-year data is meaningless.
Switching requires passing underwriting in most states, so health status is a critical variable in the switching decision.
Birthday rule states (California, Oregon, Missouri, Illinois, and others) give you a free, annual guaranteed-issue window to switch without medical questions.
High-Deductible Plan G can hedge against rate increase compounding by keeping the premium base low — 8% on $55 hurts far less than 8% on $165.

title: Birthday Rule Medigap States: Your Free Switch Window Explained
metaDescription: Several states give Medicare beneficiaries a guaranteed-issue window around their birthday to switch Medigap plans without health questions. Here's which states have it, how the timing works, and how to actually use it to lower your premiums.
h1: Birthday Rule Medigap States: Your Free Switch Window Explained
summary: Several states give Medicare beneficiaries a guaranteed-issue window around their birthday to switch Medigap plans without health questions.

sections.heading: The Problem the Birthday Rule Solves
sections.content:
Here's a situation that plays out thousands of times a year. Someone turns 65, enrolls in Medigap during their guaranteed-issue window, picks a carrier based on who was cheapest that month. Fast forward three years. Same benefits. Premium is now $40/month higher. A competitor is offering the exact same Plan G — standardized, identical benefits — for $35 less. Should be an easy switch, right?

In most states, it's not. Outside of specific qualifying events, Medigap has medical underwriting. So even though you've been paying your premiums faithfully for three years, even though your benefits haven't changed, even though the competitor's plan is literally the same product — you have to fill out a health questionnaire. And if you've been diagnosed with anything since you first enrolled (type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea, high blood pressure on medication, you name it), you might get denied. Or rated. Which means the switch either doesn't happen or costs you more.

The birthday rule was designed specifically to break this trap. It creates a predictable, annual window where you can shop and switch without underwriting. No health questions. No denial risk. Just competition doing what competition is supposed to do — keeping premiums honest.

California pioneered this in 2000. It took a while for other states to catch on, but the pace has picked up significantly over the past five years. The map keeps changing — check current state status before making decisions.

sections.heading: Which States Have the Birthday Rule Right Now
sections.content:
As of 2024-2025, the following states have some form of birthday rule or annual guaranteed-issue window for Medigap:

California — the original, in effect since 2000. 30-day window starting on your birthday. Any equal-or-lesser plan, no underwriting.

Oregon — modeled on California's version. 30-day window from birthday. Same equal-or-lesser rule applies.

Missouri — passed its birthday rule in 2021. 30-day window. Carriers operating in Missouri are required to accept the switch.

Illinois — enacted in 2021. 45-day window from birthday — slightly more generous than California's 30 days.

Idaho — joined the birthday rule states. 30-day window.

Louisiana — 30-day window starting on birthday.

Nevada — 30-day window. Nevada also has some additional Medigap protections worth reviewing if you're a resident.

Kentucky — passed birthday rule legislation, effective for enrollees.

Maryland — enacted protections that function similarly to a birthday rule.

A few important caveats. First, the list above reflects the state of the law as of early 2025. Several additional states have had birthday rule bills in various stages of legislation. States do add protections, and the trend has been toward more of them, not fewer. Check your state insurance department or call a SHIP counselor for current status.

Second, even within birthday rule states there are variations. Not all carriers operating in a birthday rule state are subject to the same requirements in all circumstances. Some states exempt certain plan types or certain carrier categories. The general rule holds — but the fine print matters.

Third, if you live in a community-rated state — Connecticut, Massachusetts, Minnesota, New York, Maine, New Jersey, Washington, Arkansas, Vermont — the birthday rule is less relevant because you typically have broader open enrollment or pricing protections already in place. In New York, for example, Medigap is year-round guaranteed issue for most plans.

sections.heading: How the 30-Day Window Actually Works
sections.content:
Let's say you're in California. Your birthday is April 14. Your 30-day window opens April 14 and closes May 13. During that window, you can apply to any carrier licensed to sell Medigap in California, for any plan letter at or below your current plan (same letter is most common), and they cannot require you to answer health questions or deny you based on medical history.

What "equal or lesser" means in practice: if you're on Plan G, you can switch to Plan G with any other carrier, or step down to Plan N, Plan K, Plan L, or any lower-tier plan. What you can't do is use the birthday window to upgrade — you can't go from Plan N to Plan G, for instance.

Application timing is important. You don't have to complete the switch within the 30 days — you have to apply within the 30 days. Your new coverage can take effect the following month after your birthday if you're starting the process close to the end of the window. But don't wait until day 28 to start shopping. Give yourself the full month.

The process: contact carriers or a broker (a broker can do this faster, comparing multiple carriers simultaneously), get current quotes, pick one, submit the application before your window closes. Most applications are processed within a week or two. Once approved, you set a termination date with your old carrier that aligns with your new coverage start date. Don't terminate old coverage before new coverage is confirmed active — that's how people accidentally end up in a gap.

A note on carriers that push back: some carriers — either out of ignorance of the birthday rule or as a retention tactic — will ask health questions anyway. In California, this is illegal during the window. If a carrier asks health questions after you've invoked the birthday rule, tell them you're within your California birthday rule window. If they persist, file a complaint with the California Department of Insurance. This isn't theoretical. It happens, and the complaint process exists for exactly this reason.

sections.heading: The Shopping Strategy Inside the Window
sections.content:
Having a guaranteed-issue window is only useful if you actually use it to compare. A lot of people set it and forget it — they renew with their existing carrier because it's the path of least resistance, even when a meaningfully cheaper option exists. That's money left on the table.

Here's what a smart birthday window strategy looks like.

Two months before your birthday: pull current quotes from at least four to five carriers. Don't just look at premium. Look at the rate increase history for each carrier in your state. A carrier $20 cheaper today with a history of 9% annual increases is worse than a carrier $8 cheaper with a history of 5% increases — if you're planning to stay on the plan for five or more years. Run the five-year math.

One month before your birthday: narrow to your top two options. Verify the carriers are licensed and in good standing with your state insurance department. Check their AM Best rating — at minimum you want a B+ or better, and A or A+ is strongly preferred for a carrier you're planning to stay with for years.

First week of your birthday window: apply. Don't wait. You want maximum processing time before the window closes. Submit your application. Keep a copy of the application with the date on it.

After confirmation of new coverage: set the termination date with your old carrier. Many people forget this step and end up paying premiums to two carriers simultaneously for a month. Your new carrier can sometimes help coordinate termination, but don't assume they will — call the old carrier yourself.

A few things to shop beyond premium: does the carrier offer electronic claims filing? Is their customer service reachable? Some smaller carriers have better rates but genuinely terrible service when you actually need to file a claim. Check reviews, but weight recent ones more heavily than old ones — carrier quality can shift when they change ownership or management.

Also worth knowing: some carriers offer household discounts of 5-7% if two people in the same household are enrolled. If you're married or have a domestic partner also on Medigap, this can change the math between carriers. Ask explicitly about household discounts when getting quotes — not all carriers advertise them.

sections.heading: States Without the Birthday Rule: What Are Your Options?
sections.content:
If you're in a state without a birthday rule and not in one of the community-rated states, your options for switching are more limited but not zero.

Guaranteed-issue qualifying events exist outside birthday windows. These include: losing other creditable coverage (like an employer plan or union coverage), moving out of a service area (more relevant for Medicare Advantage but can create Medigap opportunities), losing Medicare Advantage plan (you get a guaranteed right to go back to Original Medicare plus Medigap in this case), or your current Medigap carrier going bankrupt or leaving your market.

These are real options but relatively rare. Most people in non-birthday-rule states who want to switch are doing it voluntarily because premiums are too high — and that requires passing underwriting.

If you're in a non-birthday-rule state, the underwriting question is: how healthy are you? Broadly speaking, the conditions that are most likely to cause problems in underwriting include: any cancer diagnosis or treatment in the past two to five years (varies by carrier and state), heart attacks or strokes, COPD or emphysema requiring oxygen, end-stage renal disease, active chemotherapy or radiation, AIDS or HIV diagnosis. Conditions that are often manageable but worth knowing about: well-controlled type 2 diabetes (some carriers accept it with a rating), sleep apnea treated with CPAP (many carriers accept), controlled hypertension (usually fine), stable high cholesterol (usually fine).

The smart move in a non-birthday-rule state: every few years, if you're still healthy, get a competitive quote and actually apply. Don't just shop — submit the application and see if you qualify. You're under no obligation to switch until you accept coverage. If you get approved, then decide whether the savings justify it. If you get denied, you've lost nothing — your current plan continues.

One more thing: Alaska, which passed a birthday rule in 2022. It's worth mentioning separately because it surprised a lot of people. If you're an Alaskan Medigap beneficiary, you have the window — use it.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes People Make With the Birthday Window
sections.content:
Mistake one: not knowing it exists. I'm not being sarcastic — surveys consistently show that the majority of beneficiaries in birthday rule states have never heard of the birthday rule. If you're reading this, you're ahead of most.

Mistake two: waiting until the last week to start shopping. The 30-day window sounds like plenty of time, but if you start on day 25, you might not have confirmation of new coverage before the window closes. Start early.

Mistake three: switching carriers without checking rate history. A $25/month cheaper plan from a carrier that increases 11% per year is a trap. Run the five-year projection.

Mistake four: trying to use the birthday window to upgrade plan letters. You can't. Equal or lesser only. If you want to upgrade from Plan N to Plan G, you either need to pass underwriting or wait for a qualified guaranteed-issue event.

Mistake five: canceling existing coverage before new coverage is confirmed. This happens. Someone submits an application, feels confident it'll go through, calls their old carrier and terminates coverage, then the new carrier has a processing delay. Even in a guaranteed-issue window, administrative delays happen. Don't terminate until you have a policy number and confirmed effective date from the new carrier.

Mistake six: not checking if a household discount applies. As mentioned earlier, this can significantly change the math. If you're both on Medigap and you've never asked about household pricing, ask now.

Mistake seven: not setting a calendar reminder for next year. The birthday window recurs annually. If this year's shopping didn't find a better option, next year might. Set the reminder. Every year.

sections.heading: How Brokers Fit Into the Birthday Rule Strategy
sections.content:
A licensed broker who specializes in Medicare can run birthday window quotes across multiple carriers simultaneously, knows the rate histories in your state, and can manage the application process. They're paid by carriers via commission — you don't pay them directly — so there's no direct cost to you for using one.

The caveat: brokers are incentivized by commission rates, which vary by carrier. A broker who pushes you toward one carrier without explaining why, or who doesn't show you rate histories, or who can't name the top two or three alternatives and explain why they're recommending one over the others — that's a broker whose advice you should weight accordingly.

A good broker will show you their work. They'll show you the rate histories, explain the pricing method, and give you a five-year cost projection. They'll also tell you honestly if there's not a better option right now — because a client who trusts them is worth more over time than one $25/month commission.

You can also work directly with carriers — call them during your birthday window, ask for a rate quote, and apply directly. More work, but eliminates the broker incentive question.

SHIP counselors (State Health Insurance Assistance Programs) are another option. They're free, they're not affiliated with any carrier, and they can explain your state's birthday rule specifics and help you compare options. Find your local SHIP at shiptacenter.org. They don't process applications but they're excellent for education and unbiased guidance.

faq.question: What is the birthday rule for Medigap?
faq.answer: The birthday rule is a state-level protection that gives Medicare beneficiaries a guaranteed-issue window — typically 30 days — starting on their birthday, during which they can switch to an equal or lesser Medigap plan with any licensed carrier in the state without medical underwriting. No health questions, no denial based on health history. States with birthday rules include California, Oregon, Missouri, Illinois, Idaho, Louisiana, Nevada, Kentucky, and others.

faq.question: Can I switch to a higher plan letter during my birthday window?
faq.answer: No. The birthday rule applies to equal or lesser plan letters only. You can switch from Plan G to Plan G (different carrier), or from Plan G to Plan N, but not from Plan N to Plan G. If you want to upgrade plan letters, you either need to pass medical underwriting or qualify for a different guaranteed-issue event.

faq.question: Does the birthday rule apply to Medicare Advantage plans?
faq.answer: No. The birthday rule specifically applies to Medigap (Medicare Supplement) plans. Medicare Advantage has its own separate enrollment rules including Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7) and Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 - March 31). They're different systems with different rules.

faq.question: My carrier is asking me health questions during my birthday window. What do I do?
faq.answer: Tell them you're within your state's birthday rule window and that you're entitled to guaranteed-issue enrollment. Cite the specific law if you know it (in California it's Insurance Code Section 10192.6). If they continue asking health questions, contact your state department of insurance to file a complaint. This is a known issue and the consumer protections are enforceable.

faq.question: How long do I have to apply during the birthday window?
faq.answer: Most states give 30 days from your actual birthday date. Illinois extends this to 45 days. Your application must be submitted within that window — coverage doesn't have to be active, but the application needs to be in. Don't wait until the last few days. Start shopping at least 30-45 days before your birthday so you have the full window to actually apply.

faq.question: If I live in New York, do I need the birthday rule?
faq.answer: Probably not — New York has year-round guaranteed issue for Medigap, meaning you can switch plans any time without underwriting. Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Minnesota have similar broad protections. Community-rated states generally have stronger consumer protections that make the birthday rule somewhat redundant.

keyTakeaways: The birthday rule gives beneficiaries in qualifying states a guaranteed-issue window to switch Medigap plans without medical underwriting — typically 30 days starting on your birthday.
States with birthday rules as of 2025 include California, Oregon, Missouri, Illinois, Idaho, Louisiana, Nevada, Kentucky, Maryland, and Alaska.
The window only allows switches to equal or lesser plan letters — you cannot use it to upgrade from Plan N to Plan G.
Start shopping 30-45 days before your birthday so you have the full window to apply and process coverage.
Never cancel existing coverage before new coverage is confirmed with a policy number and effective date.
Rate history matters more than current premium — check five years of state-filed rate increases before switching carriers.
Community-rated states (New York, Connecticut, Massachusetts, etc.) often have broader protections that make the birthday rule less necessary.
SHIP counselors can provide free, unbiased guidance specific to your state's birthday rule rules and current market options.

title: Medicare for Self-Employed: What You Need to Know
metaDescription: No employer HR department to handle your Medicare enrollment. No group plan transition. Just you, the deadlines, and a lot of rules nobody explains clearly. Here's how to navigate Medicare when you're self-employed — Part B timing, HSA conflicts, premium deductions, and more.
h1: Medicare for Self-Employed: What You Need to Know
summary: No employer HR department to handle your Medicare enrollment.

sections.heading: The Self-Employed Medicare Problem Nobody Warns You About
sections.content:
If you've spent your career working for a company with more than 20 employees, Medicare enrollment is relatively simple. You turn 65, you're still working, you have employer group coverage, you delay Part B, your employer plan is primary. When you retire or leave, you have a Special Enrollment Period. The system mostly works.

If you're self-employed? The system works very differently. And the people who design Medicare enrollment rules didn't exactly optimize for freelancers, sole proprietors, S-corp owners, or any of the other flavors of self-employment that tens of millions of Americans use to structure their work.

Here's the core problem. The Special Enrollment Period that lets working people delay Part B — avoiding the late enrollment penalty — only exists if you have creditable coverage through an employer group health plan. And the key word is employer. Your own coverage as a self-employed person usually doesn't qualify as employer group coverage in the way the statute means. Whether it does depends entirely on how your business is structured and how you obtain health insurance.

Get this wrong and you end up with a Part B late enrollment penalty that lasts for life. We're talking a 10% premium increase for every 12-month period you delayed enrollment without qualifying coverage. If you delay five years past your initial enrollment window, that's a 50% permanent premium surcharge. On 2024 Part B premiums of $174.70/month for most people, a 50% surcharge is an extra $87/month. Every month. For the rest of your life.

So the stakes are real. Let's go through the situations you're most likely to be in.

sections.heading: Marketplace Plans and the Medicare Transition: Timing Is Everything
sections.content:
A lot of self-employed people under 65 buy coverage through the ACA Marketplace — either directly or through a state exchange. This is creditable coverage. It covers you. But it does not exempt you from Medicare enrollment deadlines the way employer group coverage does.

When you turn 65, your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP) is a 7-month window: 3 months before the month you turn 65, the month itself, and 3 months after. If you're on a Marketplace plan and you don't enroll in Part B during this window, you will face the late enrollment penalty unless you have qualifying employer group coverage.

Marketplace coverage is not qualifying employer group coverage for Part B deferral purposes. Full stop. This trips people up constantly, because the coverage is good coverage, it's creditable for drug coverage (Part D), and it works just fine in your daily life. But Medicare doesn't treat it as a basis to defer Part B without penalty.

So if you've been self-employed and buying Marketplace coverage, your Medicare transition looks like this: turn 65, enroll in Part B during your IEP, drop (or keep, depending on circumstances) your Marketplace plan. The Marketplace plan likely has a premium tax credit if your income is in the right range — but once you're enrolled in Medicare, you're no longer eligible for premium tax credits on a Marketplace plan. You need to disenroll from the Marketplace plan within 30 days of your Medicare effective date to avoid the credit clawback on your taxes.

Here's the sequencing that works: enroll in Part A first (it's usually free — premium-free Part A if you have 40+ quarters of Social Security credits). Simultaneously or immediately after, enroll in Part B. Set your Medicare effective date. Call your Marketplace plan and provide your Medicare effective date — they'll terminate your coverage effective that date. Apply for Medigap during your 6-month Medigap open enrollment window (starts the month you're both 65 and enrolled in Part B). Add Part D if you're not getting drug coverage through Medigap.

If you want Medigap, the 6-month window is the only guaranteed-issue opportunity you get without a birthday rule or another qualifying event. Don't miss it.

sections.heading: The HSA Trap: This One Can Cost You Real Money
sections.content:
A lot of self-employed people use Health Savings Accounts. They're genuinely good tools — tax-deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses. Triple tax advantage. Makes sense, especially when you're paying your own premiums out of pocket.

But here's the collision that Medicare causes with HSAs, and it's not well publicized.

You cannot contribute to an HSA after you're enrolled in any part of Medicare. Any part — Part A, Part B, Part D, any of it. The day you enroll in Medicare, your ability to make new HSA contributions ends. Contributions you make after your Medicare enrollment date are subject to income tax and a 6% excise penalty. That's a nasty surprise come tax time.

And it gets more complicated. Social Security auto-enrolls you in Part A when you start collecting Social Security benefits. If you're 65 and collecting Social Security, you're likely already enrolled in Part A — which means HSA contributions you're making might already be disqualified. Check this.

Furthermore: Part A enrollment is sometimes retroactive. When you enroll in Social Security at 65 or later, Part A can be made retroactive up to six months. So if you enrolled in Social Security in October and elected Part A, Medicare might backdate your Part A to April. Any HSA contributions you made April through October are now disqualified retroactively. This happens. You need to know this.

The practical implication: if you're planning to keep contributing to your HSA, delay Social Security (which delays automatic Part A enrollment) and delay your Medicare enrollment. Some people do this intentionally — they're still working, they want the HSA contributions, and they have employer group coverage (or in the case of larger S-corps with adequate structure, potentially qualifying business coverage) that lets them defer Medicare. But this requires careful planning, and the definition of 'qualifying employer group coverage' for self-employed individuals is very specific.

The cleanest rule: stop HSA contributions at least six months before you plan to enroll in Medicare. That buffer accounts for potential retroactive Part A dating.

sections.heading: The Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction and Medicare Premiums
sections.content:
Here's one of the few places Medicare actually works in your favor if you're self-employed. Medicare premiums are deductible as self-employed health insurance premiums under IRC Section 162(l).

This means if you're self-employed and paying Medicare premiums — Part B, Part D, Medigap — these are deductible as an above-the-line deduction on your personal federal tax return. You don't have to itemize. It directly reduces your adjusted gross income. For someone in the 22% or 24% bracket, deducting $3,000/year in Medicare premiums (Part B plus Medigap) saves $660-720 in federal income taxes annually.

A few conditions: you must have net self-employment income to claim this deduction. If your business had a loss, you can't deduct more than your net profit from self-employment (and other eligible earnings). You also can't claim this deduction for any month you were eligible for employer-subsidized health coverage — including through a spouse's employer. If your spouse has an employer plan that you could enroll in, you can't claim the self-employed health insurance deduction for that same month.

The deduction covers: Part B premiums, Part D premiums, Medicare Advantage premiums, Medigap premiums. It does not cover premiums paid with pre-tax dollars from another source. Most Medicare premiums are paid post-tax, so this typically isn't an issue.

The impact on estimated taxes: if you're making quarterly estimated tax payments, factor in this deduction when calculating your payments. A lot of self-employed Medicare enrollees forget to reduce their estimated tax payments when they start paying Medicare premiums — they end up overpaying quarterly and getting a refund instead of the cash flow benefit in real time.

Also worth knowing: IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) surcharges are also deductible. If your income is high enough that you're paying higher Part B and Part D premiums due to IRMAA, those elevated premiums are still deductible as self-employed health insurance. Every dollar of the surcharge is deductible.

sections.heading: What Counts as Creditable Coverage When You're Self-Employed
sections.content:
This is the question that has the most legal and financial consequence for self-employed people. What coverage lets you defer Medicare Part B without penalty?

The statutory test is: coverage through an active employer group health plan based on current employment (you or a spouse). The phrase 'employer group health plan' has a technical meaning under Medicare statutes and regulations — it's coverage through a group health plan sponsored by an employer or employee organization (like a union) with 20 or more employees.

For self-employed individuals, the analysis depends on business structure:

Sole proprietors generally do not have employer group coverage. Their health insurance is individual insurance, even if purchased through a business account. Individual insurance — including ACA Marketplace plans, even high-quality BCBS PPOs — does not qualify for Part B deferral. If you're a sole proprietor with no other qualifying coverage source, enroll in Part B at 65.

S-Corp or C-Corp owners with employees may qualify. If your corporation has a legitimate group health plan with 20+ employees (including yourself and your spouse), and you're covered under that plan, it might qualify. The key threshold is 20 employees — below that, Medicare becomes primary regardless of the group plan. This is a real cliff: 19 employees and your group plan doesn't protect you from Part B obligations. 20 employees and different rules apply. Get this wrong and you owe late enrollment penalties.

Partners in partnerships — similar analysis. If the partnership maintains a group health plan with 20+ employees and you're covered, potentially qualifying. If it's a small partnership with fewer than 20 employees, probably not.

The practical advice: before you decide to delay Medicare enrollment as a self-employed person, get a written opinion from your plan administrator or a qualified Medicare attorney confirming whether your coverage constitutes a qualifying employer group health plan. Don't guess. The penalty for guessing wrong is permanent.

sections.heading: Social Security, Medicare, and Self-Employment Tax: The Full Picture
sections.content:
This section is for the people who want to understand how everything fits together. Skip it if you already have a tax person who handles this. Read it if you're DIY-ing your finances.

As a self-employed person, you pay self-employment tax (SE tax) of 15.3% on net earnings up to the Social Security wage base ($168,600 in 2024), then 2.9% above that. The 2.9% above the wage base is entirely the Medicare payroll tax portion. So you're already paying into the Medicare system in a meaningful way.

When you turn 65 and enroll in Medicare, Part A is premium-free if you have 40 or more quarters of covered earnings (which means you've paid SE tax on sufficient earnings over your career). If you have fewer than 40 quarters, you'll pay a premium for Part A — up to $505/month in 2024. This is relatively rare but worth knowing.

Part B, everyone pays. The standard 2024 premium is $174.70/month. Higher-income individuals pay more via IRMAA surcharges based on MAGI from two years prior. If your self-employment income was high two years ago and has dropped significantly (common for retirees who've wound down a business), you can appeal IRMAA using Form SSA-44 and provide evidence of the income change. This is worth doing — IRMAA can add hundreds of dollars per month.

Social Security timing: most self-employed people have more flexibility over when they receive income, which gives more control over IRMAA brackets. If you can strategically time business income recognition, distributions from retirement accounts, or Roth conversions relative to your Medicare enrollment and IRMAA lookback years, there's real money to manage here. This is beyond DIY territory — it's worth a conversation with a tax advisor who understands both Medicare and self-employment before you pull any of these levers.

sections.heading: The Enrollment Checklist for Self-Employed Individuals
sections.content:
Three months before you turn 65:

Determine whether your current health coverage qualifies as employer group coverage for Part B deferral purposes. If not (and for most sole proprietors, it doesn't), plan to enroll in Part B during your IEP.

Stop new HSA contributions if you plan to enroll in Medicare. If you want to keep contributing, understand exactly when your Medicare enrollment is effective and stop contributions before that date. Six-month buffer recommended.

Decide between Original Medicare plus Medigap plus Part D, versus Medicare Advantage. Self-employed people who travel frequently for business often prefer Original Medicare plus Medigap because of the nationwide provider network. Medicare Advantage plans have network restrictions that can be inconvenient if you're working in multiple markets.

At 65:

Enroll in Part A and Part B through SSA.gov or your local Social Security office. If you're already receiving Social Security, you may be automatically enrolled — verify.

After Part B is active:

You have 6 months of guaranteed-issue Medigap enrollment. Don't waste this window. Compare Plan G options across carriers, check rate histories, apply before the 6-month window closes.

Enroll in Part D (prescription drug coverage) during your IEP if you're not getting drug coverage through an MA plan or Medigap. Delaying Part D without creditable drug coverage creates a separate late enrollment penalty of 1% of the national base beneficiary premium per month of delay — also permanent.

Terminate Marketplace coverage effective the date Medicare begins. Report the change to the Marketplace to avoid premium tax credit clawback.

Adjust quarterly estimated tax payments to reflect the self-employed health insurance deduction for Medicare premiums.

Each year in October/November:

Review Medicare Advantage or Part D plan options during Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7) if you're on Medicare Advantage. If you're on Original Medicare plus Medigap, review Part D plans for formulary or premium changes.

faq.question: Can I delay Part B if I'm self-employed and still working?
faq.answer: Only if you have coverage through an employer group health plan — meaning a group plan sponsored by a business with 20 or more employees. For most sole proprietors, S-corp owners with few employees, and freelancers with individual or Marketplace coverage, the answer is no. You must enroll in Part B at 65 or face a permanent late enrollment penalty of 10% per 12-month period of delay.

faq.question: Are Medicare premiums tax-deductible for self-employed people?
faq.answer: Yes. Medicare Part B, Part D, Medicare Advantage, and Medigap premiums are all deductible as self-employed health insurance premiums under IRC Section 162(l). This is an above-the-line deduction — you don't need to itemize. The deduction is limited to your net self-employment income for the year and is unavailable for months when you were eligible for employer-sponsored coverage through a spouse.

faq.question: When should I stop contributing to my HSA before enrolling in Medicare?
faq.answer: Stop at least six months before your planned Medicare enrollment date. This buffer accounts for potential retroactive Part A dating — when you enroll in Social Security, Part A can be backdated up to six months, which would retroactively disqualify HSA contributions made during that period. Contributions made after your Medicare effective date (even retroactively determined) are subject to income tax and a 6% excise penalty.

faq.question: I've been buying Marketplace coverage. Does that count as creditable coverage to avoid the Part B penalty?
faq.answer: For Part D drug coverage purposes, yes — ACA Marketplace plans are creditable for Part D. For Part B deferral purposes, no. Marketplace coverage does not qualify as employer group health coverage under Medicare law. If your only coverage at 65 is a Marketplace plan, you must enroll in Part B during your Initial Enrollment Period to avoid the late enrollment penalty.

faq.question: What is IRMAA and how does it affect self-employed people?
faq.answer: IRMAA (Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount) is a surcharge on Part B and Part D premiums paid by higher-income Medicare beneficiaries. It's based on your MAGI from two years prior. In 2024, individuals with MAGI above $103,000 pay additional amounts ranging from roughly $70 to $419/month extra on top of standard Part B premiums. Self-employed people have more control over income timing, which creates planning opportunities around IRMAA brackets. If your income dropped significantly, you can appeal IRMAA using Form SSA-44.

keyTakeaways: Marketplace coverage does not qualify you to defer Part B — you must enroll at 65 or face a permanent late enrollment penalty unless you have qualifying employer group coverage.
Stop HSA contributions at least six months before Medicare enrollment to avoid retroactive disqualification from Part A backdating.
Medicare premiums (Part B, Part D, Medigap, MA) are fully deductible as self-employed health insurance premiums — an above-the-line deduction that reduces AGI.
The self-employed Part B deferral exception requires coverage through an employer group plan with 20+ employees — most sole proprietors and small business owners don't qualify.
The 6-month Medigap guaranteed-issue window starts when you're both 65 and enrolled in Part B — this is your best shot at comprehensive supplemental coverage without underwriting.
IRMAA surcharges use MAGI from two years prior — self-employed people have strategic opportunities to manage income in IRMAA lookback years.
Terminate Marketplace coverage effective the date Medicare begins to avoid premium tax credit clawback on your federal return.

title: COBRA and Medicare: How They Work Together (And the Trap That Costs People Thousands)
metaDescription: COBRA and Medicare seem like a natural pairing — leave a job, keep your old insurance. But if you're Medicare-eligible, the interaction is almost exactly backwards from what most people expect. Here's the trap, the penalty, and when COBRA actually makes sense.
h1: COBRA and Medicare: How They Work Together (And the Trap That Costs People Thousands)
summary: COBRA and Medicare seem like a natural pairing — leave a job, keep your old insurance.

sections.heading: The COBRA-Medicare Trap: How It Works and Why It's Counterintuitive
sections.content:
You leave a job at 66. You've been on your employer's group health plan. HR hands you a COBRA packet — 18 months of continuation coverage. You think: great, I'll take COBRA while I figure out Medicare. Familiar coverage, same network, keeps things simple. What could go wrong?

A lot, actually. Here's the thing that trips people up constantly: if you're already entitled to Medicare when you take COBRA, Medicare is primary and COBRA is secondary. Not the other way around. COBRA pays what Medicare doesn't — after Medicare has already paid its share.

That sounds fine until you realize the downstream consequence. If COBRA is secondary and Medicare is primary, and you're enrolled in Medicare Part A but you never enrolled in Part B because you figured COBRA had you covered... you have a problem. Medicare Part B covers outpatient services — doctor visits, labs, imaging, physical therapy, most things that aren't a hospital admission. Without Part B, Medicare isn't paying those claims. And COBRA won't pay primary on services that Medicare is supposed to cover. So you end up with claims that neither plan pays. You get the bill.

And then it gets worse. You've been off your Initial Enrollment Period. Maybe it's been two years of COBRA while you thought you were covered. When you finally enroll in Part B, CMS looks back and says: why didn't you enroll on time? Answer: COBRA. CMS says: COBRA doesn't count as a basis for deferring Part B when you're already Medicare-entitled. Penalty: 10% per 12-month period of delay. Two years late? 20% permanent Part B premium surcharge. For life.

This scenario plays out for thousands of people every year. It's one of the most common and most expensive Medicare enrollment mistakes.

sections.heading: Employer Size Changes Everything: The 20-Employee Rule
sections.content:
The way COBRA and Medicare interact depends heavily on the size of the employer you're leaving. This is the rule that most people don't know until they need to know it — by which point it's sometimes too late.

For employers with 20 or more employees: while you're actively employed and covered by the group plan, employer coverage is primary and Medicare is secondary. This is the scenario that allows working people over 65 to legitimately defer Medicare enrollment without penalty. The employer plan is primary, it's doing the heavy lifting on claims, and CMS recognizes it as a valid basis for deferral.

When you leave that employer and take COBRA — even though you still have technically the same insurance — the picture flips. Now you're no longer actively employed. Medicare becomes primary. COBRA becomes secondary. The deferral protection disappears the moment you're no longer actively working.

For employers with fewer than 20 employees: Medicare is always primary, even while you're still actively employed. Small employer group coverage is secondary from the start. In this scenario, if you're 65 and working for a 15-person company, you needed to enroll in Part B when you turned 65. If you didn't, the penalty clock may have already started — regardless of COBRA status.

The 20-employee threshold is measured as a function of actual working days — specifically, whether the employer had at least 20 employees for each working day in at least 20 calendar weeks in the current or preceding year. This is a defined test, and it matters. If a small employer is near this threshold, the analysis gets technical.

The point: COBRA's status as primary or secondary coverage is determined by employment status (active vs. former employee), not by the existence of the COBRA coverage itself.

sections.heading: The Part B Penalty: Permanent, Compounding, Unavoidable
sections.content:
Let's spend a minute on exactly what the Part B late enrollment penalty looks like in real dollars, because abstract percentages don't fully convey the impact.

The standard 2024 Part B premium is $174.70/month. If you delayed enrollment for one full 12-month period (without qualifying coverage), your penalty is 10%. That's $17.47/month added permanently to your premium. Not for a year. Not for ten years. Forever. Every month until you die, you pay an extra $17.47.

Delayed two years (24 months): 20% penalty. Extra $34.94/month. Permanently.
Delayed three years: 30% penalty. Extra $52.41/month. Permanently.
Delayed five years: 50% penalty. Extra $87.35/month. Permanently.

And Part B premiums increase over time. Your penalty is a percentage of the current premium — so as premiums go up, the dollar amount of your penalty goes up with them. If the standard premium rises to $220/month in a few years (not unreasonable given historical trends), a 20% penalty becomes $44/month. Still forever.

Over a 20-year retirement, a 20% Part B penalty costs roughly $8,000-10,000 in excess premiums, depending on premium growth. A 50% penalty over 20 years can cost $20,000+ in excess premiums. This is not small money.

The Part B penalty applies to any period without creditable employer group coverage. COBRA, as we've established, doesn't count when you're Medicare-entitled and not actively employed. The penalty accrues during those months.

Can you appeal the penalty? Sometimes. CMS accepts appeals if you can document that you genuinely had creditable employer group coverage during the period in question. If your employer's HR department told you COBRA counted and you enrolled in Part B late based on that advice, CMS may (not will — may) grant an exception. But this requires documentation, an appeal process, and isn't guaranteed. Don't count on it. Enroll on time.

sections.heading: When COBRA Actually Makes Sense (The Legitimate Use Cases)
sections.content:
COBRA isn't useless when you're Medicare-eligible. There are specific situations where it provides genuine value. The key is understanding what it can and can't do.

The dental and vision gap. Original Medicare doesn't cover dental or vision care — no cleanings, no fillings, no glasses, no eye exams. Medicare Advantage plans sometimes include these benefits, but not always, and the coverage can be limited. If your old employer plan had solid dental and vision coverage, a short COBRA bridge while you sort out Medicare and find standalone dental/vision plans might make sense. COBRA for dental and vision is a very different calculation than COBRA as your primary medical coverage — your exposure is much lower because Medicare is handling medical costs.

The spouse who's under 65. If you're 65 and Medicare-eligible but your spouse is 60, they can't enroll in Medicare yet. Your employer plan was probably covering both of you. When you leave the job, your spouse loses that coverage. COBRA lets your spouse continue on the old employer plan for up to 36 months (the extended COBRA period for a qualifying event like loss of coverage due to the covered employee's Medicare enrollment). This is a real and legitimate use case — COBRA bridges your spouse's coverage until they reach Medicare age or find another option.

Short-term transition with specific providers. If you're mid-treatment with a specific specialist who's in your employer network but hasn't yet confirmed they take Medicare assignment, a one-month to two-month COBRA bridge while you verify and complete active treatment is defensible. Just know that Medicare is still primary during this time, and ensure you enroll in Part B promptly.

High-cost prescriptions currently on the employer formulary. If you have expensive drugs that your employer plan covers well and you're mid-investigation on what your Medicare drug coverage will look like, a very short COBRA bridge while you sort out Part D can save you from a coverage gap on critical medications. This should be measured in weeks, not months.

What COBRA is never the right answer for: replacing Part B. The moment you're not actively employed and you're Medicare-eligible, get Part B. COBRA cannot legally serve as the primary coverage for someone Medicare-entitled who's not actively employed. Using it as a substitute for Part B creates the penalty trap we described above.

sections.heading: The Special Enrollment Period When COBRA Ends
sections.content:
Here's the piece of COBRA-Medicare interaction that works in your favor, if you use it correctly.

If you had actual employer group coverage based on your own active employment (not COBRA, but the real employer plan while you were still working), you qualify for a Special Enrollment Period to sign up for Part B when that employment ends — or when the coverage ends, whichever comes later. This SEP lasts eight months and is completely penalty-free, even if you're past your Initial Enrollment Period.

But — and this is critical — the SEP does not extend into COBRA. If you take COBRA after leaving your job, your eight-month SEP starts running from the day you lose the employer group coverage or stop working, whichever is later. The SEP does not pause while you're on COBRA. It runs concurrently.

This means: if you take COBRA and then wait until COBRA ends to enroll in Part B, you've likely already burned your penalty-free SEP. The eight months are gone. You're now enrolling late, without qualifying coverage for the COBRA period, and the penalty applies.

The right move: when you leave your job and your employer group coverage ends, start your SEP clock. Enroll in Part B promptly — ideally in the first month or two of your SEP. Take COBRA for dental, vision, or spouse coverage if it makes sense. But get Part B enrolled during the SEP window.

A lot of SHIP counselors say to enroll in Part B the month your active employment ends, full stop. Don't overthink it. The SEP gives you a penalty-free window, but the best use of that window is to enroll immediately, not to use it as permission to wait.

sections.heading: Practical Steps When You're Leaving a Job and You're Medicare-Eligible
sections.content:
Step one, before you leave: confirm your employer plan's status relative to Medicare. Is it a 20+ employee group plan (making it primary while you're employed)? If yes, you have an SEP protection when you leave. If it's a small employer plan, Medicare has been primary the whole time — get your Part B situation sorted immediately.

Step two, at the moment you leave or your last day of active employment: your eight-month SEP starts. Mark that date. Count out eight months. That's your deadline for penalty-free Part B enrollment.

Step three: enroll in Part B as soon as practical — ideally within the first month of your SEP. Don't wait for COBRA decisions to enroll in Part B. They're separate decisions.

Step four: evaluate COBRA specifically for what it can do that Medicare can't. Dental, vision, spouse coverage, medication bridge. If your employer plan had good dental and you haven't found a standalone dental plan, COBRA for the dental benefits alone might be worth short-term cost. Calculate actual COBRA premiums (you pay the full premium — employer and employee portion — plus up to 2% admin fee). Dental-specific COBRA premiums are often much lower than the full medical COBRA premium.

Step five: once Part B is active, you have 6 months of Medigap guaranteed-issue enrollment. This is your window to lock in a Medigap plan without underwriting. Use it.

Step six: if you took COBRA and your coverage is ending, don't think a new SEP opens. It generally doesn't. The SEP is tied to the end of active employment coverage, not COBRA coverage. If you burned your SEP by waiting, you're likely in the General Enrollment Period (January 1 - March 31, with coverage effective July 1) — and you owe the penalty.

Step seven: if you're in the penalty situation, consult a SHIP counselor or Medicare attorney. There are some appeal pathways if you received demonstrably incorrect information from an employer or insurance carrier. Not guaranteed, but worth pursuing if the misinformation is documented.

sections.heading: How to Read an Explanation of Benefits When Both Plans Apply
sections.content:
If you end up in a situation where both Medicare and COBRA are active (the correct scenario being Medicare primary, COBRA secondary), understanding how claims process is important so you don't leave money on the table.

Claims must be submitted to Medicare first. Your provider submits to Medicare. Medicare pays its share and generates an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) showing: what was billed, what Medicare allowed, what Medicare paid, and what's left. That remaining amount — the Medicare coinsurance, copays, deductible — is what goes to COBRA.

Your COBRA plan then receives the Medicare EOB and the claim, evaluates it against its own benefits, and pays what it will pay of the remainder. For most former group plans, the COBRA coverage will pick up most or all of the Medicare cost-sharing. That's actually a decent setup financially — if your employer plan was good, the combination of Medicare primary plus COBRA secondary can provide nearly complete coverage.

The problem: providers don't always know to bill Medicare first. Some providers have a default billing process that just goes to whatever insurance you give them. If you hand them your COBRA card first, they might bill COBRA primary. This causes the claim to get bounced back, delayed, or incorrectly processed. When you present insurance, present your Medicare card first. Be explicit: Medicare is my primary insurance. Here is my Medicare ID. Here is my COBRA as secondary.

Also: some providers don't accept Medicare assignment. In that case, Medicare still processes the claim and pays its allowed amount, and you may have excess charges (amounts above Medicare's approved rate). Your COBRA plan may or may not cover those excess charges depending on the plan terms. Original Medicare plus a good Medigap plan with excess charge coverage is often cleaner than the Medicare-plus-COBRA dance.

faq.question: If I'm on COBRA, can I delay enrolling in Medicare Part B?
faq.answer: Almost certainly not without penalty. COBRA coverage does not qualify as employer group health coverage for purposes of deferring Part B enrollment. If you're Medicare-eligible and not actively employed, COBRA is secondary to Medicare — not a substitute for it. Delaying Part B while on COBRA generates the Part B late enrollment penalty of 10% per 12-month period, permanently.

faq.question: Does COBRA extend my Special Enrollment Period?
faq.answer: No. Your eight-month SEP begins when your active employment ends (or your employer group coverage ends, whichever is later). COBRA does not pause or extend the SEP. If you take COBRA and wait until it expires to enroll in Part B, you've likely already missed your SEP window and will owe a late enrollment penalty.

faq.question: Can COBRA be useful even if I'm Medicare-eligible?
faq.answer: Yes — in specific, limited ways. COBRA can provide dental and vision coverage that Medicare doesn't offer. It can continue coverage for a spouse under 65. It can provide a short-term bridge for specific medications while you finalize Part D enrollment. What COBRA cannot do is substitute for Part B as primary medical coverage when you're Medicare-entitled and not actively employed.

faq.question: What if my employer told me COBRA would protect me from the Part B penalty?
faq.answer: Unfortunately, this is wrong advice that HR departments give fairly often — and 'my HR told me so' is not a defense that reliably works with CMS. If you received this advice in writing and enrolled in Part B late based on it, you have grounds for a penalty appeal. Contact your SHIP counselor or a Medicare attorney and bring any documentation of the advice you received. Appeals are not guaranteed to succeed, but documented misinformation is your strongest argument.

faq.question: My spouse is 62 and I'm 65. How does COBRA help us?
faq.answer: Your spouse can continue on your former employer's COBRA for up to 36 months (the extended COBRA period triggered by your Medicare enrollment as the covered employee). This bridges your spouse's coverage until they're eligible for Medicare, find other coverage, or you find a better alternative like marketplace coverage. This is a legitimate and common use of COBRA when ages don't align.

keyTakeaways: COBRA is secondary to Medicare when you're Medicare-eligible and no longer actively employed — it cannot substitute for Part B coverage.
The employer size rule is critical: employers with 20+ employees make group coverage primary while you're actively working; fewer than 20 employees and Medicare is always primary.
The Part B late enrollment penalty is 10% per 12-month period of delayed enrollment — permanent, with no expiration or phase-out.
Your 8-month Special Enrollment Period starts when active employment ends, not when COBRA ends — COBRA runs concurrently with your SEP, not after it.
COBRA has legitimate uses: dental/vision gap coverage, spouse coverage for a partner under 65, and short-term prescription bridges.
When both Medicare and COBRA are active, always present Medicare first — it is primary, and providers must bill it first.
Enroll in Part B the month you leave your job if Medicare-eligible — don't wait for COBRA decisions to make the Part B decision.

title: Medicare Abroad: Does It Cover You Outside the US?
metaDescription: The short answer is almost never — but there are real exceptions worth knowing, including Medigap foreign travel benefits, Medicare Advantage emergency coverage, and what expats and snowbirds actually need to stay protected.
h1: Medicare Abroad: Does It Cover You Outside the US?
summary: The short answer is almost never — but there are real exceptions worth knowing, including Medigap foreign travel benefits, Medicare Advantage emergency coverage, and what expats and snowbirds actua...

sections.heading: The Hard Truth About Original Medicare Overseas
sections.content:
If you're planning a trip to Italy, a year in Costa Rica, or a retirement in Portugal, here's the answer you need to know upfront: Original Medicare — Parts A and B — does not cover medical care received outside the United States and its territories. Full stop.

And when Medicare says 'United States and territories,' that means the 50 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, the U.S. Virgin Islands, Guam, American Samoa, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Anywhere else on the globe: no coverage. Not even if you're an American citizen in an American-owned hospital in a foreign country. Not even if you're on a cruise ship in international waters, generally — though there's a narrow exception for ships within six hours of a US port that we'll cover.

This isn't a technicality or a loophole — it's the foundational design of the program. Medicare was built around domestic healthcare infrastructure. It pays providers in the Medicare system. There are no contracts with hospitals in Spain or pharmacies in Thailand. The claims submission system doesn't work internationally. The whole architecture assumes you're getting care domestically.

The practical consequence: if you get hit by a car in France, have a heart attack in Japan, or need emergency surgery in Mexico, Medicare pays nothing. Zero. You're paying out of pocket or relying on whatever travel insurance, Medigap foreign travel benefit, or other coverage you had the foresight to arrange.

For occasional travelers, this is manageable with supplemental travel insurance. For snowbirds who spend five or six months a year in another country. For expats who've retired abroad. For people who take extended international trips. This is a potentially catastrophic coverage gap that requires deliberate planning.

sections.heading: The Three Narrow Exceptions in Original Medicare
sections.content:
To be complete, there are three situations where Medicare may cover care outside the US. They're narrow, they're specific, and most people will never encounter them — but they exist.

Exception one: emergency care in Canada or Mexico when you're traveling between the lower 48 states and Alaska. If you're driving through Canada on your way from Seattle to Anchorage and you have a medical emergency in British Columbia, Medicare may cover the care. The key conditions: you're traveling a direct route between states (or between the continental US and Alaska), the emergency occurs while you're in Canada or Mexico because it's the most accessible location for care, and a Canadian or Mexican hospital is closer than any US hospital. This exception is geographic and situational — it applies to the transit route, not to travel to Canada or Mexico as a destination.

Exception two: ships within territorial waters. If you're on a cruise ship and a medical emergency occurs while the ship is within 6 hours of a US port, Medicare may cover the care. Beyond 6 hours from port? No coverage. This rule is why cruise ship medical facilities often just stabilize patients and make for port — not necessarily because it's medically optimal but because that's how the billing works.

Exception three: there's no third broad exception. Some sources list 'if a foreign hospital is closer than a US hospital' as a general rule, but that's really a subset of the Canada/Mexico transit exception and it's geographically limited. Don't bank on it for international travel generally.

These three exceptions combined cover a small fraction of the scenarios where international care actually becomes necessary. Most Medicare beneficiaries traveling internationally need coverage beyond Original Medicare.

sections.heading: Medicare Advantage Emergency Coverage Abroad: Better, But Not a Safety Net
sections.content:
Medicare Advantage plans are required to cover emergency and urgently needed care anywhere in the US. But internationally, the rules are looser and the coverage varies significantly by plan.

By law, Medicare Advantage plans must cover emergency care outside the US if the plan is an HMO or PPO. 'Emergency' has a specific definition in this context — it's the same standard as domestic emergency coverage: a condition that a reasonable person would expect to be life-threatening or seriously harmful without immediate treatment. Urgently needed care (not an emergency, but care for a condition that can't wait for you to return home) may or may not be covered depending on the plan.

The coverage amount and cost-sharing vary. Some MA plans have robust international emergency benefits with low out-of-pocket exposure. Others have minimal coverage or high coinsurance for international claims. The maximum out-of-pocket under MA doesn't always apply internationally in the same way it applies domestically.

And there's the claims processing problem. When you have a medical emergency in another country, you typically pay out of pocket first and submit for reimbursement later. MA plans have claims submission requirements, documentation requirements, timelines. If the foreign hospital doesn't speak your insurer's language (literally and figuratively), getting properly documented claims submitted can be a significant administrative undertaking after the fact.

For someone on Medicare Advantage who travels internationally occasionally, read your Evidence of Coverage carefully. Look specifically at the sections covering international emergency care and what the plan pays. If the coverage is thin or the cost-sharing is high, supplement it with travel insurance for the specific trip.

For someone on MA who travels extensively or spends significant time abroad, MA may not be the right structure. Original Medicare plus Medigap gives you more predictable international coverage through the Medigap foreign travel benefit.

sections.heading: Medigap Foreign Travel Emergency Benefit: The Best Medicare Coverage You Have Abroad
sections.content:
Here's where there's actually good news for people who travel. Several Medigap plan letters include a foreign travel emergency benefit. Specifically, plans C, D, F, G, M, and N all include this benefit. Plans A, B, K, and L do not.

The benefit works like this: after a $250 per-year deductible, Medigap covers 80% of medically necessary emergency care outside the US during the first 60 days of each trip. The lifetime maximum is $50,000.

Let's unpack that. The $250 deductible is per calendar year, not per trip. Once you've hit it, you don't pay it again that year regardless of how many trips you take. The 80% coverage kicks in, meaning you're responsible for 20% of covered costs up to the lifetime limit. The $50,000 lifetime maximum is shared across all foreign travel emergencies over the life of your Medigap policy.

A few important nuances. 'Emergency' in this context means unexpected illness or injury that requires immediate treatment — the same standard as other emergency definitions. Routine care while traveling (a scheduled checkup with a doctor abroad, picking up a maintenance prescription, elective procedures) is not covered. The foreign travel benefit is specifically for emergencies.

The 60-day trip duration limit matters for extended travelers. If you're on a 90-day trip, the foreign travel benefit only applies for the first 60 days. Day 61 through 90, you're without the Medigap international coverage unless you have other coverage in place.

The $50,000 lifetime maximum sounds like a lot. And for many emergencies, it is — a cardiac event, a broken hip, even an appendectomy in Western Europe can often be handled within $50,000. But a complex hospitalization, a major surgery, or air medical evacuation can exceed $50,000 quickly. Medevac alone — airlifting you from a rural location to an appropriate facility — can run $50,000-150,000 depending on distance and circumstances. The Medigap benefit is a meaningful safety net, not a complete solution.

For most people with Medigap C, D, F, G, M, or N who take occasional international trips of less than 60 days, this benefit provides solid baseline coverage. The gap to worry about is the 20% coinsurance above the $250 deductible, and events that exceed $50,000.

sections.heading: Expat Options: When You're Not Just Visiting
sections.content:
Retiring abroad is a legitimate and growing phenomenon. Portugal, Mexico, Costa Rica, Belize, Spain, Italy, Thailand — Medicare beneficiaries are living meaningful portions of their retirement outside the US. The coverage question for expats is more complex than for occasional travelers.

First, you must maintain Medicare Part B to keep your Medigap or Medicare Advantage coverage. If you drop Part B because you're living abroad, you lose Medigap. When you return to the US years later and want to re-enroll, you'll face the Part B late enrollment penalty for every year you were out, plus you have to wait for the General Enrollment Period (January-March, coverage starting July 1). This is a significant financial penalty for returning expats who dropped Medicare.

Most expats who retire abroad should maintain Part B and Medigap G — even if they're not using it much — precisely because they might return to the US for medical care. If you're living in Portugal but come back to the US for a major surgery or cancer treatment (using the superior domestic network), your Medicare and Medigap are there. This is a real use case. Many expats do exactly this.

For the medical care they receive while abroad, expats need one of three solutions:

Option one: international health insurance. Companies like Cigna Global, Aetna International, and BUPA International offer plans specifically for expats. These are genuine medical insurance products designed for people living internationally long-term. They cover routine care, not just emergencies. They have real networks in many countries. They're not cheap — premiums for a 65+ enrollee can run $200-500/month depending on coverage level — but they're designed for the expat use case.

Option two: local health insurance in the country of residence. Many popular expat destinations have accessible health systems — Mexico, Costa Rica, Portugal, Thailand. Some have public systems that expats can access for a nominal contribution. Some have private insurance markets with premiums dramatically lower than US equivalents. A healthy 65-year-old can often get solid private health coverage in Costa Rica for $150-200/month. Quality varies, and you need to research each country specifically.

Option three: self-pay with an emergency fund. Some expats in countries with very low medical costs (Thailand, Mexico, some Eastern European countries) choose to self-pay for routine and moderate-cost care, carry the Medigap foreign travel benefit for true emergencies, and maintain a dedicated medical emergency fund for the gap. This works better in some countries than others — Thailand's private hospital network, for example, is genuinely excellent and inexpensive by US standards.

For most expats, the right answer is a combination: maintain US Medicare and Medigap G for US-based care and the emergency travel benefit, layer international health insurance or local coverage for the primary day-to-day medical care in your country of residence.

sections.heading: Snowbird Strategies: Six Months Abroad, Six Months Home
sections.content:
