The plan reviews whether the service is medically necessary and meets their coverage criteria before agreeing to pay. It applies in MA plans but not in original Medicare — which is one of the fundamental trade-offs of the two coverage types.

faq.question: How long does prior authorization take in Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: Plans must respond to standard PA requests within 7 calendar days and expedited (urgent) requests within 72 hours. These are federal requirements. If your situation is time-sensitive — you're hospitalized, in active treatment, or a delay would seriously jeopardize your health — explicitly request an expedited review. If the plan misses these deadlines, that itself is grounds for a complaint to CMS.

faq.question: What happens if my prior authorization is denied?
faq.answer: First, have your doctor request a peer-to-peer review — a direct clinical conversation between your doctor and the plan's medical reviewer that resolves many denials quickly. If that doesn't work, file a formal Level 1 internal appeal within 60 days. If that fails, it escalates to an Independent Review Entity. The key statistic: over 80% of appealed denials get overturned. Most people don't appeal — they should.

faq.question: Does prior authorization apply in emergencies?
faq.answer: No. Federal law prohibits Medicare Advantage plans from requiring prior authorization for emergency services. You can go to any emergency room — in-network or out-of-network — in a genuine emergency and your plan must cover it at in-network cost-sharing rates. Post-stabilization continuing care is where PA can re-enter the picture, but actual emergency care is protected.

faq.question: Which Medicare Advantage plans have the lowest prior authorization denial rates?
faq.answer: Denial rates vary significantly by insurer. Under the 2026 CMS rules, plans must publish aggregate denial rate metrics. Historically, smaller regional plans and Kaiser Permanente have had lower denial rates, though Kaiser also had lower overturn rates on appeal. National insurers like UnitedHealthcare, Humana, Aetna, and Centene have variable rates by market and plan type. Check the plan's publicly reported metrics during open enrollment.

faq.question: What changed with prior authorization rules in 2026?
faq.answer: Key 2026 changes: approved authorizations must be honored for the full course of treatment (can't be pulled mid-stay), appeals process definitions were tightened, and plans must publish PA requirement lists plus 8 denial rate metrics. A proposed definition of 'internal coverage criteria' was not finalized — CMS deferred that after receiving over 33,000 public comments. Some transparency requirements were suspended pending further review.

faq.question: Can a Medicare Advantage plan deny a service my doctor ordered?
faq.answer: Yes, and they do — about 3.4 million times in 2024. But denials can be wrong, and are wrong at high rates when challenged. A plan can deny a service if it doesn't meet their medical necessity criteria or coverage policies. However, those policies must be consistent with Medicare's coverage rules — plans can't impose stricter standards than CMS allows. If they do, that's what the appeals process is for.

keyTakeaways: Medicare Advantage plans made 53 million prior authorization determinations in 2024 — 6.4% (roughly 3.4 million) were denied.
Over 80% of appealed denials get overturned — but only 11.5% of denials are ever appealed.
Standard PA decisions require 7 calendar days; expedited (urgent) decisions require 72 hours.
Emergency care is exempt from prior authorization — plans cannot require PA for genuine emergencies.
2026 CMS rules require plans to honor approved authorizations for the full course of treatment.
Peer-to-peer review between your doctor and the plan's medical reviewer often resolves denials faster than formal appeals.
Plans must now publish their complete PA requirement lists and aggregate denial rate metrics as of 2026.
The proposed definition of 'internal coverage criteria' was not finalized in 2026 — leaving the most controversial PA practices partially unaddressed.

title: Can You Have Medicare Advantage and Medigap?
metaDescription: The short answer is no — but the full story involves an underwriting trap that catches a lot of people, specific trial rights that expire, and a one-way door you might not be able to get back through. Read this before you switch.
h1: Can You Have Medicare Advantage and Medigap?
summary: The short answer is no — but the full story involves an underwriting trap that catches a lot of people, specific trial rights that expire, and a one-way door you might not be able to get back through.

sections.heading: The Short Answer: No
sections.content:
You cannot have Medicare Advantage and Medigap at the same time. This isn't a gray area, not a technicality, not something that varies by state. It's federal policy and it's absolute.

Medicare Advantage and Medigap are fundamentally incompatible because they work on different premises. Medicare Advantage replaces original Medicare — the plan pays instead of Medicare. Medigap supplements original Medicare — it covers the gaps (deductibles, copays, coinsurance) in original Medicare's coverage structure. If you're in Medicare Advantage, original Medicare isn't your primary coverage, so there are no gaps for Medigap to fill. The insurer can't even legally sell you a Medigap policy if you're in Medicare Advantage.

If you try to buy Medigap while enrolled in Medicare Advantage, the sale is supposed to be blocked. An ethical agent won't sell it to you. If someone does sell you both and you're paying premiums on a Medigap policy you can't use, that's a regulatory violation — and unfortunately it happens. People sometimes keep paying Medigap premiums for years after switching to MA without realizing the policy is worthless in that scenario.

So: pick one path. Original Medicare + Medigap (often called the supplement route) or Medicare Advantage (the managed care route). They don't combine. And the decision you make has long-term consequences that aren't immediately obvious.

The rest of this article is about those consequences — specifically about the underwriting trap, the trial right window, and why switching from Medicare Advantage back to Medigap is much harder than switching from Medigap to Medicare Advantage.

sections.heading: Understanding the Two Paths: What Each Actually Is
sections.content:
Before digging into the switching problem, let's make sure the two options are clear.

Original Medicare (Parts A and B) is the federal government insurance program. Part A covers hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, some home health, and hospice. Part B covers outpatient care, doctor visits, preventive services, and medically necessary services. Together they cover a lot — but not everything. There are deductibles, coinsurance rates, and no out-of-pocket maximum. The Part A deductible alone is $1,676 per benefit period in 2026.

Medigap (Medicare Supplement Insurance) is private insurance that layers on top of original Medicare to cover those cost-sharing gaps. Plans are standardized — Plan G in one state covers the same benefits as Plan G in another state. Different insurers compete on price and service quality, not on benefits (benefits are fixed by letter). With a good Medigap plan like Plan G, you can end up with near-zero out-of-pocket costs beyond your monthly premiums.

The downside: Medigap premiums. They run anywhere from $100 to $300+ per month depending on your age, location, gender, and smoking status. That's on top of your Part B premium.

Medicare Advantage (Part C) is a private alternative to original Medicare. You get your Medicare benefits through the private insurer instead of the government. Plans often include Part D prescription coverage (making them MA-PD plans). They frequently include supplemental benefits — dental, vision, hearing, fitness, meal delivery — that Medigap and original Medicare don't cover. Many MA plans have $0 premium.

The trade-offs: network restrictions (HMO and PPO networks), prior authorization requirements, and the variability year-to-year in benefits and costs. And the one that matters most for this conversation: if you're in Medicare Advantage and want to switch back to original Medicare and get Medigap, you may not be able to — at least not at standard rates.

sections.heading: The Underwriting Trap: Why Switching Back Is Hard
sections.content:
This is the thing that catches people. And it catches a lot of people.

When you first turn 65 and enroll in Medicare, you have a 6-month Medigap Open Enrollment Period. During this window — starting the month you're both 65 and enrolled in Medicare Part B — you have a federally guaranteed right to buy any Medigap plan offered in your state, at standard rates, with no medical underwriting. You can't be denied. You can't be charged more because of health conditions. This window is gold.

After that window closes, you're in the normal market for Medigap in most states. Normal market means medical underwriting. Insurers can ask about your health history, request medical records, and either deny you coverage or charge you significantly higher premiums based on pre-existing conditions. A 70-year-old with diabetes, heart disease, and a recent hospitalization trying to buy Medigap? They may be denied by every insurer or offered rates that are prohibitively expensive.

Here's how this creates the trap:

Someone turns 65. They see the $0 premium Medicare Advantage plans. They think: great, I'll try this and if I don't like it I'll switch back to original Medicare and get Medigap. They enroll in MA.

A year or two later, they've developed health issues. They've been using the MA plan heavily. They realize the prior authorizations are frustrating, the network is limiting their specialist access, or they travel and can't use their plan out of area. They want to switch to original Medicare + Medigap.

They disenroll from Medicare Advantage. Fine — you can always go back to original Medicare during the annual enrollment period. But now they try to buy Medigap. They're 68, have a few diagnoses on their record, and they have no guaranteed issue right.

Result: denied. Or approved with a waiting period on pre-existing conditions. Or approved at rates that don't make financial sense.

The switch from original Medicare to Medicare Advantage is easy. The switch back is hard. This asymmetry is the single most important thing to understand before enrolling in Medicare Advantage for the first time.

sections.heading: Trial Rights: The One Window That Protects You
sections.content:
Federal law carves out specific situations where you get a guaranteed right to buy Medigap even after your initial enrollment window — regardless of your health status. These are called Medicare trial rights or guaranteed issue rights, and they're specific and time-limited.

The first-time trial right is the most important one. If you enrolled in Medicare Advantage for the first time when you became eligible for Medicare (at 65 or when you first enrolled in Part B), and you decide within 12 months that it's not working for you, you have a guaranteed issue right to buy Medigap. The clock starts when you joined Medicare Advantage. You have 12 months. If you disenroll from MA within that window and apply for Medigap, you cannot be denied based on health status.

This 12-month window exists precisely because of the asymmetry problem described above. It gives first-timers a one-year test drive of Medicare Advantage without permanently closing the Medigap door. But — and this is critical — once that 12 months is up, the trial right expires. If you stay in Medicare Advantage past your 12-month mark, you lose that protection.

The return trial right applies to people who dropped Medigap to join MA for the first time. If you had Medigap, dropped it to try Medicare Advantage, and within 12 months decide to go back to original Medicare, you have a guaranteed right to get your old Medigap policy reinstated — as long as the same insurer still offers that same plan in your state. You have 63 days from the date your MA plan coverage ends to apply.

This is specifically for first-time MA enrollment. If you've been in Medicare Advantage for several years, switched back to original Medicare previously, and then tried MA again, you don't have this protection a second time. It's a one-time right.

Other guaranteed issue situations exist too:
- Your Medicare Advantage plan is leaving your area or ending coverage
- You move out of your plan's service area
- Your plan loses its Medicare contract
- You leave employer/union group coverage

In all these cases, you have a 63-day window to apply for Medigap with guaranteed issue rights. Don't miss the window.

sections.heading: What Happens When People Don't Know This
sections.content:
Honestly? It's a mess. And it's a predictable mess because the marketing for Medicare Advantage plans is often excellent and the disclosure of these trade-offs is often inadequate.

Here's a real scenario that plays out constantly: Someone retires at 65 with a $0-premium Medicare Advantage plan. They feel good, they're not using much healthcare. The plan seems great. Five or six years pass. Now they're 71. Their health has changed. They've had a hospitalization, maybe a cardiac diagnosis, maybe cancer treatment. The MA plan's prior authorization requirements have become a constant source of frustration. A friend tells them about a great Medigap plan.

They call an agent. The agent runs them through the underwriting application. Their health history disqualifies them or the premiums quoted are $400/month with exclusions. The original enrollment window was six years ago and they didn't use their 12-month trial right. They're stuck in Medicare Advantage.

This isn't the plan being malicious. They enrolled, the plan paid their claims, they used the insurance as intended. The problem is they didn't understand the trade-off they were making at enrollment — that choosing MA over Medigap initially could be a one-way door.

State-based protections: Some states have enacted additional protections that give Medicare beneficiaries more flexibility to switch between Medicare Supplement plans or from MA to Medigap without underwriting. California, Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, Missouri, New York, Oregon, and a few others have continuous open enrollment protections or guaranteed issue rights that go beyond federal minimums. If you live in one of these states, your switching options are less constrained. But in most states, you're working with the federal baseline.

sections.heading: The Right Way to Think About This Decision
sections.content:
Given everything above, how should someone approaching Medicare actually think about this choice?

Age and health at enrollment matter enormously. If you're 65 and healthy, you have the most options. If you're 65 and have significant pre-existing conditions, you might still be able to get Medigap during your guaranteed issue window — and locking in Medigap while you can might be the financially rational choice even if the premiums seem high, because you may not be able to get it later.

Longevity assumptions. Medigap tends to make more financial sense for people who will use significant healthcare over time. Medicare Advantage tends to look better for relatively healthy people who won't hit the catastrophic coverage scenarios. The problem is that health trajectories are unpredictable.

The $0 premium isn't free. This is one of the most misunderstood aspects of Medicare Advantage. $0 premium plans are real — you don't pay a monthly premium beyond your Part B premium. But you pay in other ways: copays per service, deductibles, coinsurance, and potentially significant out-of-pocket costs if you have a bad health year. The maximum out-of-pocket in MA plans can be $5,000-$10,000+ per year. With a good Medigap plan, your out-of-pocket risk is essentially zero beyond premiums.

Lifestyle fit. Do you travel internationally frequently? Original Medicare has very limited international coverage; Medigap Plan G or F includes foreign travel emergency coverage. Do you spend part of the year in another state? Medicare Advantage is geographic — you need to use the plan's network. Medigap works anywhere Medicare is accepted in the US.

My honest take after spending time with this system: the right answer depends entirely on your specific health situation, financial picture, travel habits, and risk tolerance. There's no universal correct answer. But the asymmetry — easy to go into MA, potentially impossible to come back out — means you should make the initial decision with full knowledge of what you're choosing, not just based on the $0 premium headline.

sections.heading: If You're Already in Medicare Advantage and Want Out
sections.content:
Let's say you're reading this and you're already in Medicare Advantage. Maybe you've been in it for a few years. What are your actual options?

First — check your state. As mentioned, some states (California, New York, Connecticut, Maine, Missouri, Oregon, etc.) have broader guaranteed issue rights. If you're in one of those states, the federal limitation may not apply or may be significantly loosened. Check with your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) — they're free, unbiased, and know your state's specific rules.

Second — check your timing. If you enrolled in MA within the last 12 months for the first time, your trial right is still active. Use it before it expires.

Third — if your plan is leaving the market or significantly changing benefits, that's a guaranteed issue triggering event. Watch for those Annual Notice of Change letters in September/October.

Fourth — apply for Medigap anyway and see what happens. Underwriting decisions aren't always predictable. Some conditions aren't rated as heavily as others. Some insurers are more liberal than others. In some states, even without guaranteed issue rights, you can apply — the worst that happens is a denial or a higher rate.

Fifth — consider whether your current situation is actually as bad as you think. If you're in a good MA plan with low prior authorization friction and your doctors are in-network, switching to original Medicare just for the theoretical freedom isn't necessarily rational. The dissatisfaction usually comes from specific problems: prior authorization denials, network limitations, cost surprises. Some of those can be addressed by changing MA plans (every October 15 to December 7, you can switch MA plans without underwriting) rather than leaving the system entirely.

faq.question: Can I have both Medicare Advantage and a Medigap policy?
faq.answer: No. Federal rules prohibit this combination. Medicare Advantage replaces original Medicare, so there are no gaps for Medigap to fill — the policies are structurally incompatible. An insurer cannot legally sell you a Medigap policy while you're enrolled in Medicare Advantage. If you're somehow paying premiums on both, the Medigap policy is effectively useless and you should contact your state insurance department.

faq.question: Can I switch from Medicare Advantage back to Medigap?
faq.answer: Switching back to original Medicare is always possible during the Annual Enrollment Period (Oct 15-Dec 7) or Special Enrollment Periods. But then buying Medigap requires passing medical underwriting in most states. If you're within your 12-month first-time trial right window, you have guaranteed access to Medigap regardless of health. Outside of that window and without another triggering event, insurers can deny you based on health conditions.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Advantage trial right?
faq.answer: The first-time trial right gives you 12 months to decide whether Medicare Advantage is right for you. If you enrolled in MA when you first became eligible for Medicare and decide within 12 months it isn't working, you can disenroll, return to original Medicare, and buy Medigap with guaranteed issue rights — no underwriting, no denials based on health status. After 12 months, this protection expires.

faq.question: Which states allow switching from Medicare Advantage to Medigap without underwriting?
faq.answer: Several states have enacted protections beyond federal minimums. California, Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, Missouri, New York, Oregon, and Washington provide various forms of continuous or annual open enrollment rights for Medigap. The specifics vary by state. Contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) — a free counseling service — to understand your specific state's rules.

faq.question: Is it better to have Medicare Advantage or Medigap?
faq.answer: There is no universal answer. Medigap offers more predictable costs and greater flexibility in providers — you can see any Medicare-accepting provider nationwide. Medicare Advantage often offers $0 premiums and supplemental benefits like dental and vision, but comes with network restrictions, prior authorization requirements, and variable year-to-year benefits. The key risk with MA is the underwriting trap if you want to switch to Medigap later.

faq.question: What happens to my Medigap policy if I switch to Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: Your Medigap policy doesn't automatically cancel — you need to actively cancel it. More importantly, if you're switching to MA for the first time, you have a 12-month trial right to come back and potentially get your Medigap policy reinstated. If the same insurer still offers that same plan in your state and you return within 12 months, you have a guaranteed right to reinstatement. After 12 months, that right expires.

keyTakeaways: You cannot have Medicare Advantage and Medigap simultaneously — they are mutually exclusive by federal law.
Switching from Medigap to Medicare Advantage is easy; switching back from MA to Medigap often requires passing medical underwriting.
The underwriting trap is real: if you develop health conditions in Medicare Advantage, you may be denied or rated too high when trying to buy Medigap later.
The first-time 12-month trial right gives you a guaranteed path back to Medigap — but it expires exactly 12 months after joining MA for the first time.
Some states (CA, NY, CT, ME, MO, OR and others) have stronger switching protections than federal minimums — know your state's rules.
Guaranteed issue triggering events include: plan leaving your area, moving out of service area, losing employer coverage, and your plan losing its Medicare contract.
You have 63 days from the end of MA coverage to apply for Medigap under a guaranteed issue right.
If you're approaching 65 and deciding between paths, make the choice with full awareness of the asymmetry — it's much easier to enter MA than to exit it.

title: Best Medicare Part D Plans for Expensive Medications
metaDescription: The $2,100 out-of-pocket cap changed everything for high-cost drug users. Combine that with 10 newly negotiated IRA prices, the right plan-finding strategy, and manufacturer assistance — and you can dramatically cut what you pay for expensive medications.
h1: Best Medicare Part D Plans for Expensive Medications
summary: The $2,100 out-of-pocket cap changed everything for high-cost drug users.

sections.heading: The Landscape Just Changed — In Your Favor
sections.content:
If you're on expensive medications and you've been in Medicare Part D for a few years, you remember the coverage gap. The donut hole. You'd hit a spending threshold, coverage would partially disappear, and suddenly you were paying 25% of your expensive drug's full list price out of pocket. For someone on a specialty biologic running $5,000/month, that math got ugly fast.

The Inflation Reduction Act killed the donut hole. It's gone as of 2025. That's not a minor tweak — it's a fundamental restructuring of how Part D works. And it stacked with two other major changes: a hard out-of-pocket cap (now $2,100 in 2026, indexed annually) and, brand new this year, negotiated Medicare prices on 10 high-utilization drugs.

For people on the most expensive medications, these changes are significant. Not "you might save a few dollars" significant — I mean people who were paying $6,000 or $8,000 or more per year on specialty drugs before 2025 who are now looking at a $2,100 annual maximum. That's a real change in financial exposure.

But — and this matters — the cap only helps if you're running your medications through Medicare Part D. There are decisions you make about which plan to enroll in, which pharmacy to use, and whether to use assistance programs that can either capture these benefits or accidentally route around them. Getting this wrong costs real money.

Let me walk through the current landscape, the 10 negotiated drugs, how to find the best plan for your specific situation, and what assistance programs stack on top of Medicare when the $2,100 cap isn't enough.

sections.heading: The $2,100 Cap: How It Actually Works
sections.content:
The Part D out-of-pocket maximum is $2,100 in 2026, adjusted from $2,000 in 2025 based on CMS's annual update formula. Once you hit $2,100 in true out-of-pocket spending (TrOOP), your plan covers 100% of covered drug costs for the rest of the calendar year. No more charges. January 1 it resets.

TrOOP — true out-of-pocket — is the key concept. Not everything you spend on drugs counts toward TrOOP. Specifically:

What counts: your deductibles, copays, coinsurance on covered drugs when you're paying in the initial coverage phase or post-cap catastrophic phase.

What also counts: payments made on your behalf by certain assistance programs — specifically the Low Income Subsidy (LIS/Extra Help) and, in some cases, qualifying patient assistance program contributions. This is a complicated area where the rules have been updated by the IRA.

What does NOT count: third-party discount cards like GoodRx (more on this in a separate article), out-of-pocket spending on non-covered drugs, and premium costs.

The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (MPPP) — also called the Smoothing program — was introduced in 2025 and is worth knowing about. Instead of potentially paying a large portion of your $2,100 cap early in the year when you fill expensive drugs in January, the MPPP lets you spread your total out-of-pocket costs across 12 monthly payments. You still pay $2,100 total, but in $175/month installments rather than potentially $800 in January and $200/month after. For cash-flow purposes this matters for a lot of people.

Enrollment in MPPP is voluntary and you need to contact your plan to set it up for the plan year. Most plans offer it; check with yours.

sections.heading: The 10 IRA-Negotiated Drugs: What You Actually Save
sections.content:
This is genuinely historic. For the first time, CMS has directly negotiated drug prices with manufacturers, and those negotiated prices took effect January 1, 2026. Ten drugs in this first round. Here's what happened to prices:

Eliquis (apixaban) — blood clot prevention and treatment, widely prescribed. List price was $521 per month. Negotiated Medicare price: $231 per month. That's a 56% reduction. Nearly 9 million Medicare beneficiaries use Eliquis. If you're one of them, your plan's drug cost just dropped substantially, which should flow through to lower cost-sharing before you hit your cap.

Jardiance (empagliflozin) — diabetes, heart failure, chronic kidney disease. List price $573/month. Negotiated price: $197/month. 66% reduction.

Xarelto (rivaroxaban) — blood clot treatment and prevention. List price $517/month. Negotiated price: $197/month. 62% reduction.

Farxiga (dapagliflozin) — diabetes, heart failure, chronic kidney disease. List price $556/month. Negotiated price: $178.50/month. 68% reduction.

Januvia (sitagliptin) — diabetes management.

Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan) — heart failure.

Enbrel (etanercept) — rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis.

Imbruvica (ibrutinib) — certain blood cancers.

Stelara (ustekinumab) — Crohn's disease, psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis.

NovoLog (insulin aspart) — diabetes insulin.

CMS estimated that out-of-pocket costs for these 10 drugs will decline by about 50% on average for original Medicare beneficiaries in certain standalone PDPs compared to 2025. For MA-PD plans, impact varies by how the plan had already been pricing these drugs.

Important caveat: the negotiated prices apply specifically to Medicare, not to your cost-sharing necessarily. Your actual copay or coinsurance depends on how your plan tiers the drug. A plan that puts Eliquis on Tier 3 (preferred brand) versus Tier 4 (non-preferred) will have different member cost-sharing even though the underlying plan cost is based on the negotiated price. Plan formulary and tier placement still matter enormously.

sections.heading: Specialty Tier Drugs: The Most Expensive Category
sections.content:
Specialty drugs — Tier 5 in most plans, sometimes called specialty or high-cost tier — are where the real money is. These are the biologics, cancer treatments, MS medications, rheumatoid arthritis drugs, and other complex therapies that can list at $5,000 to $50,000+ per month before discounts.

Before the IRA changes, specialty tier drugs could be a financial catastrophe. Someone on a biologic for RA or MS could blast through the initial coverage phase in January, hit the coverage gap, and face enormous out-of-pocket costs. The $2,100 cap fundamentally changes this.

In 2026, your maximum specialty drug exposure is $2,100 for the entire year across all covered drugs. If your specialty medication costs $8,000/month, you pay your plan's cost-sharing structure until you hit $2,100 in TrOOP, then it's covered 100%. You'll likely hit that cap in February or March on a truly high-cost specialty drug.

However, not all specialty drugs are covered by Medicare Part D. Part D covers drugs you administer yourself — pills, self-injection, self-infused drugs. Drugs administered in a doctor's office or infusion center are usually covered by Part B (Medicare's medical benefit), not Part D. If your expensive drug is infused in a clinic, the Part D cap doesn't apply — Part B's 20% coinsurance structure applies instead, which has no annual cap (this is where Medigap matters for original Medicare users).

For MA-PD plans, specialty drugs administered in-office may be covered under the plan's Part B equivalent benefit, and the plan's overall maximum out-of-pocket may provide some protection — but the specific structures vary significantly by plan.

Step therapy is a significant issue for specialty drugs. Many plans require you to try and fail on cheaper medications before they'll cover the specialty drug your doctor initially prescribed. If your rheumatologist wants to prescribe a biologic, but the plan requires you to try methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and a non-biologic DMARD first, you could be looking at months of step therapy before reaching the drug your doctor actually recommended. Step therapy requirements vary by plan and by drug — check formulary requirements before enrolling.

sections.heading: How to Find the Best Plan for Your Specific Drugs
sections.content:
This is where the rubber meets the road. General advice about Medicare Part D is worth something, but the only way to find the best plan for your situation is to run your specific medications through the comparison tool.

Medicare's Plan Finder at medicare.gov/plan-compare is the authoritative tool. You enter your specific drugs, dosages, and preferred pharmacy, and it shows you every plan available in your area with estimated annual costs based on your drug list. Don't skip this step — plan performance varies dramatically based on your specific drug list. A plan that's great for someone on Jardiance and a generic blood pressure drug might be terrible for someone on Enbrel.

What to look for beyond premium:

Formulary coverage. Is your drug on the formulary at all? Check this first. A plan that doesn't cover your drug is essentially useless for that medication regardless of everything else.

Tier placement. Higher tiers mean higher cost-sharing. The same drug can be Tier 3 (preferred brand, maybe 25% coinsurance) in one plan and Tier 4 (non-preferred brand, maybe 40% coinsurance) in another. Given the $2,100 annual cap, this affects how quickly you reach the cap and what your cost structure looks like month by month.

Pharmacy network. Preferred pharmacies in a plan's network often have lower cost-sharing than non-preferred network pharmacies. Some plans have very good pricing at specific pharmacy chains (CVS, Walgreens, Costco) and mediocre pricing at others. Mail-order pharmacies often have the lowest cost-sharing and are worth using for maintenance medications.

Deductible structure. The maximum Part D deductible is $615 in 2026. Some plans waive the deductible for Tier 1 and Tier 2 drugs (generics and preferred generics). If all your drugs are Tier 3+, you'll likely hit the deductible regardless.

Coverage during the deductible phase. During the deductible phase, you pay full discounted drug cost. Plans that have negotiated preferred pricing or that apply cost-sharing rather than full drug cost during the deductible phase can make a significant difference for high-cost drugs.

Run the numbers, not the brochure. The Plan Finder's estimated annual cost estimate is the best single number to compare across plans for your drug list.

sections.heading: Manufacturer Patient Assistance Programs: The Other Lever
sections.content:
Even with the $2,100 cap, $2,100 is real money for a lot of Medicare beneficiaries. The good news: for many high-cost specialty drugs, manufacturer patient assistance programs (PAPs) exist — and some of them can help even for Medicare beneficiaries, though the rules are complex.

Historically, manufacturer copay assistance programs were largely unavailable to Medicare beneficiaries because CMS considered them a form of inducement that could distort drug selection. But for certain qualifying programs — particularly programs that are available to all eligible patients regardless of insurance and that aren't tied to the manufacturer's own drug — restrictions have loosened somewhat.

Under the IRA's structure, starting in 2025, drug manufacturers are required to contribute to the cost of drugs when beneficiaries are in the catastrophic phase (after hitting the $2,100 cap). This is built into the Part D program structure now, not a separate assistance program you apply for.

For drugs before you hit the cap:

State Pharmaceutical Assistance Programs (SPAPs) exist in about 25 states and help Medicare beneficiaries with drug costs. Some provide direct financial assistance; others wrap around Medicare Part D to reduce cost-sharing. Check your state's program — funding and eligibility vary.

Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy) is a federal program that provides substantial assistance with Part D premiums, deductibles, and cost-sharing for beneficiaries with limited income and assets. The income limit is approximately 150% of the federal poverty level. If you qualify — and a lot of people who could qualify don't apply — Extra Help essentially eliminates most Part D cost-sharing. Apply through Social Security.

NeedyMeds, RxAssist, and similar nonprofit databases track manufacturer PAPs and other assistance programs. For some non-Medicare-covered drugs or drugs in special circumstances, these can identify assistance options.

Biosimilar alternatives deserve a mention here. For several high-cost biologics — Humira, Remicade, Enbrel, Stelara — biosimilar versions are now available. Plans may tier biosimilars more favorably than the reference biologic, and your doctor may be able to switch you to a biosimilar with equivalent clinical effectiveness at significantly lower plan cost (and therefore potentially faster TrOOP accumulation or lower cost-sharing). Ask your doctor specifically whether a biosimilar is appropriate for your condition.

sections.heading: The Best Strategy by Drug Type
sections.content:
Let me give you a practical breakdown by situation rather than keeping this entirely abstract.

If you're on one of the 10 IRA-negotiated drugs (Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Farxiga, Januvia, Entresto, Enbrel, Imbruvica, Stelara, NovoLog): The negotiated prices took effect January 1, 2026. Your plan's cost-sharing should reflect lower underlying drug costs. Run the Plan Finder with your specific drug and compare plans — the impact varies by how each plan has structured their formulary for these drugs. The $2,100 cap still applies on top of negotiated prices, but you may reach it more slowly.

If you're on a high-cost specialty biologic not yet in the negotiated list: Your primary protection is the $2,100 annual cap. You'll likely hit it in Q1 if you're on something like Humira or a cancer drug. Plan selection matters less for annual total once you're going to hit the cap, but it matters for month-by-month cash flow. Consider the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan to smooth payments. Also check whether a biosimilar is appropriate for your condition.

If you're on multiple expensive brand-name drugs (but not specialty tier): Focus heavily on tier placement in Plan Finder. The $2,100 cap may or may not apply depending on total spending. Plans with lower preferred pharmacy cost-sharing and more of your drugs at preferred tiers will minimize monthly out-of-pocket before potentially capping.

If you're primarily on generics: Focus on plans with low or no deductible for Tier 1/2 drugs, low generic copays, and a preferred pharmacy network that includes convenient locations. The specialty drug features don't matter as much for you.

If you have low income: Apply for Extra Help immediately if you haven't already. The income threshold is higher than many people realize (about $22,000 for an individual in 2026). Extra Help can eliminate deductibles and reduce cost-sharing to nominal amounts — it's the most powerful drug cost assistance available and it's underutilized.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Part D out-of-pocket maximum for 2026?
faq.answer: The Part D out-of-pocket maximum (TrOOP threshold) is $2,100 in 2026, up from $2,000 in 2025. Once you hit $2,100 in true out-of-pocket spending on covered drugs, your plan pays 100% for the rest of the year. This cap replaced the old 'donut hole' coverage gap structure, which was eliminated as of 2025 under the Inflation Reduction Act.

faq.question: Which 10 drugs have negotiated Medicare prices in 2026?
faq.answer: The 10 drugs with IRA-negotiated prices effective January 1, 2026 are: Eliquis (apixaban), Jardiance (empagliflozin), Xarelto (rivaroxaban), Farxiga (dapagliflozin), Januvia (sitagliptin), Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan), Enbrel (etanercept), Imbruvica (ibrutinib), Stelara (ustekinumab), and NovoLog (insulin aspart). Discounts range from roughly 50% to 68% off list prices, with nearly 9 million beneficiaries expected to see savings.

faq.question: How do I find the best Medicare Part D plan for my medications?
faq.answer: Use Medicare's Plan Finder at medicare.gov/plan-compare. Enter your specific drugs, dosages, and preferred pharmacy. The tool calculates estimated annual costs across all available plans in your area. Compare total estimated annual costs, not just premiums. Check formulary coverage, tier placement, and preferred pharmacy networks for your specific medications.

faq.question: What is Extra Help and do I qualify?
faq.answer: Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy) is a federal program that helps Medicare beneficiaries with limited income and assets pay for Part D costs — premiums, deductibles, and copays. For 2026, the income limit is approximately 150% of the federal poverty level (roughly $22,000 for an individual). If you qualify, it can reduce your drug costs dramatically. Apply through Social Security (ssa.gov) or by calling 1-800-772-1213.

faq.question: Are specialty drugs covered under Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Specialty drugs you administer yourself — self-injected biologics, oral specialty drugs — are generally covered under Part D. Drugs administered in a doctor's office or infusion clinic are typically covered under Medicare Part B, not Part D. This distinction matters because the $2,100 Part D cap doesn't apply to Part B drugs — those are subject to Part B's 20% coinsurance structure (with no annual cap in original Medicare).

faq.question: What is step therapy and how does it affect specialty drug access?
faq.answer: Step therapy requires you to try and fail on less expensive medications before your plan will cover the specialty drug your doctor originally prescribed. For biologics, this might mean trying conventional drugs (like methotrexate) before a biologic is approved. Requirements vary by drug and plan. Check formulary requirements before enrolling in a plan, and know that you have appeal rights if step therapy requirements are clinically inappropriate for your condition.

faq.question: What is the Medicare Prescription Payment Plan?
faq.answer: The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan (sometimes called the Smoothing program) was introduced in 2025 and allows you to spread your annual Part D out-of-pocket costs across 12 equal monthly payments. You don't pay less — the total is still up to $2,100 — but instead of potentially paying a large portion of that early in the year when you fill expensive drugs, you pay $175/month throughout the year. You must opt in through your plan.

keyTakeaways: The donut hole is gone. As of 2025, Medicare Part D has a hard $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap (TrOOP) — the most significant structural change to Part D in decades.
Ten drugs have IRA-negotiated Medicare prices effective January 1, 2026, with discounts of 50-68% off list prices — including Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Farxiga, and NovoLog.
The $2,100 cap only protects you if drugs run through your Part D plan — GoodRx cash purchases don't count toward TrOOP.
Medicare's Plan Finder (medicare.gov/plan-compare) is the essential tool — run your specific drug list before choosing or sticking with any plan.
Drugs administered in a doctor's office are usually Part B, not Part D — the Part D cap doesn't apply to them.
Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy) can eliminate most Part D cost-sharing for qualifying beneficiaries — income limit is ~$22,000 for individuals in 2026.
The Medicare Prescription Payment Plan lets you spread the $2,100 cap across 12 monthly installments — useful for cash-flow management.
Biosimilar alternatives for high-cost biologics may offer equivalent clinical effectiveness at lower plan cost and more favorable tier placement.

title: Medicare Part D and GoodRx: Can You Use Both?
metaDescription: Yes, you can use GoodRx with Medicare — but not at the same time on the same fill. The bigger issue is that GoodRx purchases don't count toward your TrOOP cap. Here's the math on when GoodRx wins, when it hurts you, and how to think about it.
h1: Medicare Part D and GoodRx: Can You Use Both?
summary: Yes, you can use GoodRx with Medicare — but not at the same time on the same fill.

sections.heading: The Question Everyone's Getting Wrong
sections.content:
Go to any Medicare Q&A forum and you'll see the question: "Can I use GoodRx if I have Medicare?" The answers are usually technically correct but practically incomplete. Yes, you can use GoodRx. But the more important question is: should you, and when?

Here's the thing that most explanations miss: the decision to use GoodRx instead of your Medicare Part D coverage isn't just about which is cheaper today. It's about whether cheaper today costs you more over the year by slowing your progress toward the $2,100 TrOOP cap. And for certain drugs and certain situations, that math matters a lot.

GoodRx beats insurance copays somewhere around 37% of the time according to their own data. That means roughly a third of the time, their cash price is genuinely lower than your plan's cost-sharing for that drug. For some low-cost generics, GoodRx can be dramatically cheaper than even Tier 1 generic copays. For high-cost brand drugs, your Part D plan's cost-sharing is often substantially lower than the GoodRx cash price.

But the cash vs. insurance decision is never just "which is cheaper on this fill." Once you understand TrOOP and how the $2,100 cap works, the decision framework changes — and in some cases, using GoodRx when it looks cheaper is actually the financially worse decision over the full year.

Let me walk through the mechanics, the math, and the specific scenarios where each option makes sense.

sections.heading: The Fundamental Rule: You Pick One, Not Both
sections.content:
The first thing to understand is that you cannot use both Medicare and GoodRx on the same prescription fill. This isn't a policy quirk — it's structural. When a pharmacist processes a claim, it either goes through insurance or it doesn't. You can't run it through Medicare and also apply a GoodRx coupon. The systems are mutually exclusive.

At the pharmacy counter, you (or your pharmacist) make a choice for each prescription:

Option A: Run it through your Medicare Part D plan. The pharmacy submits a claim to your insurer. You pay whatever your plan's cost-sharing is (deductible, copay, coinsurance). That amount counts toward your TrOOP.

Option B: Pay cash using GoodRx (or another discount card). The prescription is processed as a cash transaction. You pay the GoodRx price. That amount does NOT count toward your TrOOP, does not count toward your deductible, and has zero effect on your Part D benefit status.

You make this choice separately for each prescription, each fill. You could theoretically run your Eliquis through Part D and use GoodRx for a cheap antibiotic. The decisions are independent per fill.

One more thing worth knowing: if you present a GoodRx coupon, you need to explicitly tell the pharmacist to process it as cash, not through your insurance. The pharmacist will sometimes default to running it through your Medicare plan unless you specify otherwise. And if you later decide you want that fill to count toward your TrOOP, you generally cannot retroactively reprocess it through insurance — the cash transaction is final.

sections.heading: When GoodRx Is Actually Cheaper
sections.content:
Let's be honest about when GoodRx wins on price, because it does — in specific circumstances.

Generic drugs are the clearest case. A lot of generic medications cost $4-$15 at the pharmacy with GoodRx. Your Part D plan might have a $10 or $15 Tier 1 copay for the same drug. If GoodRx gets it to $4 and your copay is $10, you save $6. On a 30-day supply of a cheap generic like metformin, lisinopril, or atorvastatin, GoodRx can be meaningfully cheaper than your plan's cost-sharing.

Drugs not on your formulary. If your Part D plan doesn't cover a specific drug — it's simply not on the formulary — your only covered options are either an appeal/exception request or switching to an alternative drug. GoodRx fills the gap. You won't get any plan benefit anyway, so paying the GoodRx cash price is just the reality of that situation.

Drugs in the deductible phase. In the early part of the year before you've met your Part D deductible (up to $615 in 2026), you're paying the full discounted drug cost on covered drugs. For some drugs, the GoodRx price may be lower than even the plan's deductible-phase cost. This is more common than people realize.

Drugs with very high Part D cost-sharing. Some non-preferred brand or specialty drugs have 30-40%+ coinsurance in the initial coverage phase. For moderately priced brand drugs, GoodRx can occasionally beat that. Not common for truly expensive drugs, but worth checking.

How to check: Medicare's Plan Finder shows your plan's cost for each drug, and GoodRx.com shows cash prices at pharmacies near you. Run both numbers on any medication where you're unsure. Takes two minutes.

sections.heading: When GoodRx Hurts You (The TrOOP Problem)
sections.content:
This is the part of the GoodRx-Medicare story that doesn't get enough attention.

When you use GoodRx instead of your Part D plan, those dollars don't count toward your TrOOP. They don't count toward your deductible. Your $2,100 annual cap counter stays at zero for those purchases, as if you never bought the drug.

For people on cheap generics who will never come close to $2,100 in annual drug spending, this is mostly irrelevant. If you're spending $200/year on drugs no matter what, the TrOOP cap isn't going to help you, so using GoodRx for the cheapest price each time is perfectly sensible.

But if you're on expensive drugs — anything that might accumulate toward $2,100 — using GoodRx slows or stops your TrOOP accumulation. Here's a simple example:

Say you're on Eliquis plus a few other medications and you'd hit the $2,100 cap by May if you ran everything through Part D. After May, everything is free for the rest of the year. That's potentially $1,000-$2,000 in savings in the back half of the year.

But if you use GoodRx for Eliquis some months — even if it's $20 cheaper per fill than your Part D cost-sharing — you delay hitting the cap. Maybe instead of hitting it in May you hit it in July. Two extra months of cost-sharing before the cap, potentially hundreds of dollars more. The $20 you saved per fill on GoodRx might have cost you $300 in delayed cap savings.

The math genuinely reverses when you're likely to hit the $2,100 cap. In that scenario, maximizing TrOOP accumulation early is the optimal strategy — which means running expensive drugs through Part D even when GoodRx is slightly cheaper on a given fill.

GoodRx even acknowledges this on their own site: if you expect to reach your out-of-pocket cap, favor running drugs through Part D to accelerate toward the cap. Their business model relies on you using their product when it makes sense, and they're honest that for high-utilization Medicare patients, it often doesn't.

sections.heading: The Smart Framework: How to Decide for Each Drug
sections.content:
Rather than a blanket rule — always use GoodRx, never use GoodRx — the right answer depends on your specific situation. Here's a practical framework:

Step one: Estimate your annual drug spending through Part D. Add up approximate costs for all your medications using the Plan Finder's annual estimate. If that total is well under $2,100, you're unlikely to cap out. If it's close to or over $2,100, you're probably hitting the cap.

Step two: For each individual drug, compare the GoodRx price vs. your Part D cost-sharing at a preferred pharmacy.

Step three: Apply the rule based on your cap status:

If you won't hit the cap: Take the cheaper option on each fill. GoodRx when it's lower, Part D when it's lower. You're optimizing per-fill cost, not annual total.

If you will hit the cap: Use Part D for all significant drugs, especially expensive ones. You want to accumulate TrOOP as quickly as possible. The GoodRx savings on individual fills will likely be smaller than the benefit of hitting the cap sooner and having more months of free coverage.

Special case — cheap generics you're certain won't affect cap timing: If you're going to hit the cap regardless, whether you use GoodRx for your $8 metformin doesn't matter much either way. Small generics are low-stakes either way.

Special case — drug not on your formulary: GoodRx is often your best option, though also look at your plan's exceptions process, step therapy alternatives, and whether a formulary alternative is clinically appropriate.

Pharmacy matters too. GoodRx prices vary by pharmacy — sometimes dramatically. A drug that's $25 at CVS with GoodRx might be $12 at Costco or $9 at an independent pharmacy. The GoodRx app lets you compare prices at nearby pharmacies. Some pharmacies also have their own discount programs (Walmart's $4 generic list, Mark Cuban's Cost Plus Drugs) that can compete with or beat GoodRx for certain medications.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Strategy: Getting the Best Price Either Way
sections.content:
Whether you're using Part D or GoodRx, pharmacy selection is an underappreciated lever.

For Part D claims, preferred pharmacies in your plan's network typically have lower cost-sharing than non-preferred pharmacies. The same drug might cost $15 at a preferred pharmacy and $40 at a non-preferred in-network pharmacy under the same plan. When you're comparing plans in the Plan Finder, make sure you select your actual preferred pharmacy — the cost estimates change.

Mail-order pharmacies for maintenance medications (drugs you take chronically, refilled every 90 days) typically offer the lowest cost-sharing in most Part D plans. A 90-day supply by mail often costs the same as a 60-day supply at retail, effectively giving you a month free. For expensive brand drugs, mail-order can accelerate TrOOP accumulation by bundling 90 days at once.

For GoodRx prices, the variation by pharmacy is real. Their tool searches across all pharmacies in your area. Costco Pharmacy, Sam's Club Pharmacy, independent pharmacies, and Mark Cuban's Cost Plus Drugs (costplusdrugs.com) consistently appear near the bottom of the price range for generic medications. Costco doesn't require a membership for pharmacy purchases.

Cost Plus Drugs is worth mentioning specifically. For certain generic drugs — particularly generics of older medications — Cost Plus prices can be dramatically lower than even GoodRx, because they operate on a transparent markup model (cost + 15% + $3 dispensing fee). For some generics, they're under $5 for a 90-day supply. They don't accept insurance at all — everything is cash — so the same TrOOP logic applies. Use Cost Plus for generics where you're certain you won't approach the annual cap.

Local independent pharmacies sometimes participate in preferred pharmacy networks and can negotiate competitive pricing. Don't overlook them — they often have better service and can sometimes match or beat chain pricing with your plan's preferred rate.

sections.heading: What Changes in 2026 for This Decision
sections.content:
The IRA-negotiated drug prices that took effect January 1, 2026 affect the GoodRx-vs-Part-D calculus for specific drugs.

For the 10 negotiated drugs — Eliquis, Jardiance, Xarelto, Farxiga, Januvia, Entresto, Enbrel, Imbruvica, Stelara, NovoLog — the Medicare negotiated price is now much closer to or potentially below what GoodRx can access through commercial discount channels.

GoodRx operates by negotiating with pharmacy benefit managers and leverages commercial rebates and contracts. The Medicare negotiated prices go through different channels — they're the direct government-negotiated Maximum Fair Price. For some of these drugs, the GoodRx cash price may now be higher than your Part D cost-sharing on the negotiated price. That's a significant shift for drugs like Eliquis, where the Medicare negotiated price of $231/month may flow through to lower patient cost-sharing that GoodRx can't beat.

For drugs NOT in the negotiated list — including most brand-name drugs and specialty biologics — the GoodRx vs. Part D comparison remains as it was: check both, apply the TrOOP framework, choose accordingly.

The $2,100 cap in 2026 ($100 more than 2025) represents a minor inflationary adjustment. CMS indexes the cap annually to program growth. Don't expect the cap to stay flat — it will gradually increase over time. The core logic of running high-cost drugs through Part D to hit the cap remains intact at $2,100.

sections.heading: The Honest Bottom Line on GoodRx and Medicare
sections.content:
GoodRx is a legitimate tool. It's not a scam. For the right person using it in the right situation, it saves real money. The problem is that the marketing framing — 'save big on prescriptions!' — doesn't come with the asterisk that Medicare beneficiaries need: this only makes sense in specific circumstances and can actually cost you money if you're on an expensive drug regimen.

The people who benefit most from GoodRx alongside Medicare:

Those on cheap generics where Part D cost-sharing is higher than the cash price. The $6 savings on metformin adds up if you're taking several cheap generics.

Those filling drugs that aren't on their Part D formulary and who are unlikely to hit the $2,100 cap through other drugs anyway.

Those who don't have Part D at all — people who missed enrollment, are in a coverage gap, or are on a limited income Medicaid/Medicare overlap situation. (Though if you have no Part D and can qualify for Extra Help, get Extra Help — it's dramatically better than GoodRx for most situations.)

The people who get hurt by using GoodRx:

Those on expensive brand or specialty drugs who would benefit from accelerating toward the $2,100 cap. Using GoodRx delays cap arrival and costs more over the year.

Those who don't realize that GoodRx purchases don't count toward TrOOP at all — they think they're saving money now and building toward the cap simultaneously. They're not.

My practical recommendation: run every new prescription through the Plan Finder and GoodRx before the first fill. Five minutes of comparison saves money. For generics that are clearly going to be cash-cheaper and won't affect your cap, use GoodRx. For anything expensive or anything that contributes meaningfully to your annual drug spending, run it through Part D. And every fall during open enrollment, re-evaluate your plan against your current drug list — that's when the real money decisions happen.

faq.question: Can Medicare beneficiaries use GoodRx?
faq.answer: Yes, Medicare beneficiaries can legally use GoodRx. But you cannot use GoodRx and your Medicare Part D coverage on the same prescription fill — you must choose one or the other at the pharmacy counter for each prescription. When you use GoodRx, you're paying cash and your Part D benefit isn't used for that transaction.

faq.question: Does GoodRx count toward my Medicare Part D deductible?
faq.answer: No. When you pay cash using GoodRx, that purchase does not count toward your Part D deductible, and it does not count toward your TrOOP (true out-of-pocket) spending limit. Your progress toward the $2,100 annual cap is zero for any prescription filled with GoodRx instead of your Part D plan.

faq.question: When should I use GoodRx instead of Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Use GoodRx when: (1) the GoodRx price is lower than your Part D cost-sharing AND you're unlikely to hit the $2,100 annual out-of-pocket cap, (2) the drug isn't on your Part D formulary, or (3) you're filling cheap generics that won't meaningfully affect your cap trajectory. Avoid using GoodRx for expensive drugs when you're likely to reach the $2,100 cap — those dollars should run through Part D to accelerate reaching the cap and triggering zero cost-sharing.

faq.question: When should I use Medicare Part D instead of GoodRx?
faq.answer: Use Part D when you're on expensive drugs and are likely to hit the $2,100 TrOOP cap during the year. Every dollar paid through Part D counts toward the cap; every GoodRx dollar doesn't. If running a drug through Part D means you hit the cap in May instead of July, you get two extra months of 100% coverage — which usually outweighs the per-fill savings from GoodRx.

faq.question: What is TrOOP in Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: TrOOP stands for True Out-of-Pocket spending. It's the running total of qualifying drug cost-sharing that counts toward your annual Part D out-of-pocket maximum ($2,100 in 2026). When TrOOP hits $2,100, your plan covers 100% of covered drug costs for the rest of the year. Only spending through your Part D plan counts toward TrOOP — GoodRx cash purchases, non-covered drugs, and premium costs do not count.

faq.question: Is GoodRx better than Medicare Part D?
faq.answer: Neither is universally better — it depends on your drug costs and whether you'll hit the annual cap. GoodRx can be cheaper per fill for some generic drugs. But for anyone spending enough on medications to approach the $2,100 annual cap, Part D is typically the better overall financial strategy because hitting the cap triggers free coverage for the rest of the year. Compare both options for each of your medications and apply the cap framework.

faq.question: Can I switch between GoodRx and Medicare Part D for the same prescription?
faq.answer: You can make a different choice each time you refill. One month you might use Part D, next month GoodRx, depending on price comparisons. However, retroactively switching an already-processed GoodRx cash purchase to Part D (to get TrOOP credit) generally isn't possible. The decision at the pharmacy counter is final for that transaction.

keyTakeaways: You can use GoodRx with Medicare, but not on the same fill — you choose one or the other per prescription.
GoodRx purchases do NOT count toward your Part D TrOOP or deductible — this is the critical fact most people miss.
GoodRx wins on cheap generics where its cash price beats your plan's copay and you won't hit the annual cap.
Part D wins when you're on expensive drugs likely to hit the $2,100 annual cap — every Part D dollar counts toward the cap, no GoodRx dollar does.
The $2,100 Part D cap (2026) means after that threshold, your plan covers drugs 100% — maximizing TrOOP accumulation can save hundreds in the back half of the year.
Tell the pharmacist explicitly to process as cash when using GoodRx — they may default to your insurance.
GoodRx prices vary by pharmacy; Costco, Cost Plus Drugs, and independent pharmacies often have lower cash prices than chains.
Review your Part D plan annually using Medicare's Plan Finder — plan performance changes year to year and is drug-list specific.

title: Health Insurance for Young Adults: Options After 26
metaDescription: Aging off your parents' health plan at 26? Here's every option you actually have — marketplace plans, employer coverage, Medicaid, catastrophic plans, and what short-term coverage can (and can't) do for you.
h1: Health Insurance for Young Adults: Options After 26
summary: Aging off your parents' health plan at 26? Here's every option you actually have — marketplace plans, employer coverage, Medicaid, catastrophic plans, and what short-term coverage can (and can't) do for you.

sections.heading: The Day the Clock Runs Out
sections.content:
Nobody warns you about this one. You're 25, still on your parents' plan, life is fine — and then you turn 26 and the ACA says sorry, you're on your own now. It's one of the more abrupt transitions in adult life, and the window to fix it is real. Miss it and you're uninsured until Open Enrollment rolls back around.

Here's what actually happens with the timing, because this trips people up constantly. If your parents have employer-based coverage, your coverage ends on your birthday or at the end of the month you turn 26 — depends on the employer's plan. Some cut you off the day you turn 26. Others let you run through the end of that month. Check the Summary of Benefits and Coverage your parents' HR department can pull.

Marketplace plans work differently. If your parents bought coverage through HealthCare.gov, you get to stay covered through December 31st of the year you turn 26, regardless of when your birthday falls. That's a significantly better deal — a full year's buffer if you're born in January.

The Special Enrollment Period is 60 days. Both before and after you lose coverage. So if your employer plan is cutting you off April 30, you've got from March 1 through June 29 to enroll somewhere new. But here's the thing most people get wrong: you can enroll before you lose coverage and have your new plan start the first day of the following month. You don't have to wait until coverage actually lapses. Don't wait. The paperwork takes longer than you think.

sections.heading: Employer Coverage: Your First Move
sections.content:
If you've got a job that offers benefits, this is probably your best option. Full stop. Employer-sponsored insurance is almost always cheaper than buying on your own because your employer is subsidizing a big chunk of the premium — usually 70-80% of it. You're seeing the employee share on your paycheck, not the full cost.

Aging off your parents' plan counts as a qualifying life event, which means you get a Special Enrollment Period with your employer too. You don't have to wait for your company's annual open enrollment window. Tell HR what's happening, get the forms, and get moving within 30 days (some employers have a 31-day window; confirm yours).

When evaluating employer plans, stop staring at the monthly premium and start doing the actual math. You need three numbers: premium (what you pay per month), deductible (what you pay before insurance kicks in), and out-of-pocket maximum (the most you'd ever pay in a year). A plan with a $200/month premium and a $7,000 deductible can cost you dramatically more than one with a $350/month premium and a $2,500 deductible — especially if you ever use the damn thing.

HMO vs. PPO matters too. HMOs are cheaper but you need referrals to see specialists and coverage outside the network is basically nonexistent. PPOs cost more but give you flexibility. At 26, if you're generally healthy and just want catastrophic protection, an HMO bronze plan is often the play. If you have ongoing prescriptions or specialist relationships, PPO is worth the extra money.

Also check if your employer offers an HSA-eligible high-deductible health plan. In 2026, you can contribute up to $4,300 per year into an HSA as an individual. That money goes in pre-tax, grows tax-free, and comes out tax-free for medical expenses. If you're healthy and young, this is a legitimate wealth-building tool, not just insurance.

sections.heading: Marketplace Plans: The ACA Route
sections.content:
If your job doesn't offer benefits or you're freelancing or doing the whole gig economy thing, the ACA marketplace at HealthCare.gov is your main alternative. And in 2026, this is genuinely more complicated than it's been in years.

Here's the problem: the enhanced premium tax credits that kept marketplace plans affordable since 2021 expired at the end of 2025. Congress didn't extend them. So premiums on marketplace plans jumped significantly for a lot of people in 2026 — some saw increases of 20-30% or more. If you were paying $80/month last year because of enhanced subsidies, that number might be considerably higher now.

That said, if your income is between 100% and 400% of the federal poverty level, you still qualify for some premium tax credits. In 2026, 100% FPL for a single person is roughly $15,650. The math gets complicated fast, and the Kaiser Family Foundation subsidy calculator is the fastest way to see your actual number.

The metal tiers on marketplace plans are: Bronze (lowest premium, highest deductible — you're basically buying catastrophic coverage), Silver (middle), Gold (higher premium, lower out-of-pocket), and Platinum (highest premium, lowest cost when you use it). For healthy 26-year-olds, Bronze or a catastrophic plan is often the right call. For people with ongoing health needs, Silver or Gold is usually worth the premium difference.

Marketplace plans are available in every state. About half the states run their own exchange — California, New York, Massachusetts, etc. The rest use the federal HealthCare.gov platform. Both work the same way for enrollment purposes.

One thing that'll trip you up: if your employer offers coverage, even coverage that's technically unaffordable by ACA standards, you may be blocked from receiving marketplace subsidies. The 'affordability' threshold in 2026 is whether the employee-only premium for the lowest-cost plan offered exceeds roughly 9.02% of your household income. If it does, you can claim marketplace subsidies instead. If it doesn't, even if the actual coverage isn't great, you generally can't.

sections.heading: Medicaid: More People Qualify Than Realize
sections.content:
Medicaid covers a massive chunk of Americans and a lot of people who could qualify don't apply because they assume they make too much. Check before you assume.

In the 41 states (plus DC) that expanded Medicaid under the ACA, single adults qualify at incomes up to 138% of the federal poverty level. For 2026, that's roughly $20,120 for an individual. You're making around $1,677 a month or less? Medicaid is likely available to you and it's nearly free — sometimes completely free depending on your state.

The non-expansion states are a different problem. If you live in one of the remaining states that didn't expand Medicaid (a shrinking list, but still), you may fall into what's called the coverage gap — you make too much for traditional Medicaid but too little to qualify for meaningful ACA subsidies. It's one of the genuinely broken parts of American healthcare and there's no elegant solution if you're in it. Check your state specifically at medicaid.gov.

Beyond standard income-based eligibility, former foster care youth have special rules. If you were in foster care and had Medicaid coverage on your 18th birthday, you can qualify for Medicaid at any income level up to age 26, regardless of which state you currently live in. That's a significant protection that a lot of people in that situation never know about.

Application is usually through your state Medicaid agency or through the ACA marketplace — they'll screen for Medicaid eligibility automatically when you apply for coverage. Processing time varies wildly by state. Some states do it in days; others take weeks. Apply immediately when you lose coverage.

sections.heading: Catastrophic Plans: The Honest Safety Net
sections.content:
Catastrophic health plans deserve more attention than they get. Under 30? You're automatically eligible. No exemption needed, no paperwork beyond normal enrollment. You can just pick one.

In 2026, catastrophic plans got expanded access — adults of any age who don't qualify for premium tax credits (either because their income is below 100% FPL or above 400% FPL) can now get a hardship exemption and buy catastrophic coverage. The Trump administration expanded this access as part of broader marketplace rule changes.

Here's what a catastrophic plan actually is: very low monthly premium, extremely high deductible. The 2026 out-of-pocket maximum on catastrophic plans is $10,600 for individuals. That's your deductible on most claims. But — and this is important — catastrophic plans cover three primary care visits per year before the deductible, and all ACA-required preventive care at no cost. So you're not completely without coverage.

Think of it as 'get hit by a bus' insurance. You're not using this thing for routine care. You're buying protection against a hospitalization or a major diagnosis that would otherwise financially ruin you. At 26, if you're healthy, don't take prescriptions, and rarely see doctors, a catastrophic plan at maybe $80-150/month in most markets is legitimate coverage.

Starting in 2026, catastrophic plans are also now HSA-compatible, which is a new development. You can pair the catastrophic plan with an HSA and build up that emergency fund while having coverage. That's actually a pretty solid setup for a young healthy person.

The downside is obvious — you pay everything out of pocket until you hit $10,600. If you need ongoing prescriptions, mental health care, or regular specialist visits, a catastrophic plan will cost you more in the long run than a silver or gold plan with a higher premium.

sections.heading: Short-Term Coverage: The Gap Filler
sections.content:
Short-term health insurance is the option everyone has an opinion about and most of those opinions are partially right.

The reality: short-term plans are cheap. Often $100-200/month for a healthy 26-year-old. They can activate as fast as the next day. If you're staring at a 45-day gap between your parents' plan and your new employer's benefits kicking in, a short-term plan is a reasonable bridge.

But don't call them health insurance in the full sense of the word, because they aren't. Short-term plans don't cover the ACA's essential health benefits. That means no mental health coverage, limited prescription coverage, no maternity care, and critically — no pre-existing conditions. If you got diagnosed with anything before applying, they'll deny the claim related to it. Read the exclusions. Read them twice.

Duration rules changed under the Trump administration. In 2026, federal rules now allow short-term plans up to 364 days with renewals up to 3 years in states that permit it. But about 15 states — California, New York, New Jersey, Massachusetts, and others — have banned or severely restricted short-term plans. If you live in one of those states, this option is just off the table.

When does a short-term plan make sense? When your gap is genuinely short (weeks to 2-3 months) and you're healthy with no ongoing medical needs. When the alternative is being completely uninsured. When you genuinely can't afford ACA marketplace rates and aren't Medicaid-eligible. Short-term plans exist for situations like that and in those situations, they serve a real purpose.

When does a short-term plan NOT make sense? When you'd be using it for more than six months. When you take medications regularly. When you have any chronic conditions. In those scenarios, bite the bullet and get real coverage, even if it's more expensive.

sections.heading: The Actual Decision Framework
sections.content:
So you're 26, your parents' plan is dropping you, and you need to make a call. Here's how to think through it without losing your mind.

First question: does your employer offer benefits? If yes, almost always take them. Calculate the full cost (premium + expected out-of-pocket) but in most cases employer coverage wins.

Second question: what's your income? Under roughly $20K? Check Medicaid first. Between $20K-$65K-ish? Run the ACA marketplace calculator — subsidies may make marketplace plans surprisingly affordable even post-enhanced-credit-expiration. Over that? You're likely paying full freight on marketplace plans, which makes employer coverage even more attractive by comparison.

Third question: how's your health? Regular prescriptions, chronic conditions, mental health care? Lean toward gold or silver plans with lower deductibles even if the premium stings. Basically healthy, rarely see doctors? Bronze or catastrophic is legitimate.

Fourth question: is this a temporary situation? Starting a job in 90 days? Short-term coverage for the gap is fine. Permanently freelancing with no employer option? Short-term plans are not a long-term solution — you need real coverage.

Don't let perfect be the enemy of enrolled. The worst outcome is being uninsured because you're waiting for a better option that isn't coming. Even a plan you're not thrilled about is infinitely better than nothing when something actually goes wrong.

And use your Special Enrollment Period. Once that 60-day window closes after you lose coverage, you're locked out until January unless you have another qualifying life event. Set calendar reminders. Seriously.

faq.question: When exactly does my health insurance end when I turn 26?
faq.answer: It depends on the type of plan. If your parents have employer-sponsored insurance, your coverage ends on your 26th birthday or at the end of that month — check with the employer's HR department. If your parents have a marketplace (ACA) plan, you're covered through December 31st of the year you turn 26, regardless of your birthday month.

faq.question: How long do I have to enroll in new health insurance after turning 26?
faq.answer: You have a 60-day Special Enrollment Period. That window opens 60 days before you lose coverage and closes 60 days after coverage ends. You can and should enroll before your coverage actually lapses — your new plan can start the first day of the month after you lose coverage.

faq.question: Do I qualify for Medicaid after aging off my parents' plan?
faq.answer: Maybe. In the 41 states (plus DC) that expanded Medicaid, you qualify if your income is below 138% of the federal poverty level — roughly $20,120 for a single person in 2026. Non-expansion states have stricter rules. Apply through your state Medicaid office or HealthCare.gov, which screens for Medicaid eligibility automatically.

faq.question: What's a catastrophic health plan and should I get one?
faq.answer: Catastrophic plans have low premiums (often $80-150/month for a healthy 26-year-old) but a very high deductible — $10,600 in 2026. You automatically qualify if you're under 30. They're best for genuinely healthy people who want protection against a major medical event but rarely use healthcare. They're not good if you have prescriptions or ongoing care needs.

faq.question: Can I stay on my parents' health insurance past 26?
faq.answer: Not under federal ACA rules — 26 is the cutoff. A few states have extended this to 29 or 30 (New York, New Jersey, Florida, and others allow parents to keep adult children on employer plans to certain older ages). Check your specific state law if you're hoping for an extension.

faq.question: What if I can't afford health insurance after 26?
faq.answer: Check Medicaid first — more people qualify than realize. Then check the marketplace and run the subsidy calculator. If you genuinely can't afford any of those, a catastrophic plan may be your cheapest ACA-compliant option. Short-term plans exist as a last resort for gaps, but they're not real health insurance — they exclude pre-existing conditions and skip essential health benefits.

keyTakeaways: Employer-sponsored coverage through your own job is almost always the cheapest option — getting dropped from your parents' plan qualifies you for a Special Enrollment Period with your employer.
The 60-day Special Enrollment Period starts before you lose coverage — you don't have to wait until the plan actually lapses to enroll somewhere new.
Medicaid covers single adults earning up to roughly $20,120 in 2026 in expansion states — check before assuming you don't qualify.
Catastrophic plans are automatically available to anyone under 30 and have low premiums, but come with a $10,600 deductible — only right for genuinely healthy people.
Short-term insurance is cheap and fast but excludes pre-existing conditions and essential health benefits — fine for a short gap, not a long-term solution.
Enhanced ACA premium tax credits that kept marketplace costs low through 2025 have expired — marketplace premiums are higher in 2026 for many people.

title: How to Appeal a Health Insurance Denial
metaDescription: Your insurance company denied a claim. Here's exactly how to fight back — the internal appeal process, external review, what to include, real success rates, and when to escalate to your state insurance commissioner.
h1: How to Appeal a Health Insurance Denial
summary: Your insurance company denied a claim.

sections.heading: The Denial Letter Is Not the End
sections.content:
About 19% of in-network claims submitted through ACA marketplace insurers were denied in 2023. Let that sit for a second. Nearly 1 in 5. And initial claim denial rates hit 11.8% across all insurance types in 2024, up from 10.2% just a few years earlier. The insurers are denying more, not less.

But here's the part that'll make you furious: fewer than 1% of people whose claims get denied actually appeal. And of the ones who do appeal properly? About 82% get the denial overturned. Eighty-two percent. The appeal process isn't a formality designed to make you feel heard before they say no again — it genuinely works when you use it.
