Before enrolling in any telematics program, ask your insurer specifically whether bad driving data can result in a premium increase. Get the answer in writing if possible.

faq.question: Does my credit score affect my auto insurance rates?
faq.answer: In most states, yes — insurers use a credit-based insurance score (different from your FICO score but correlated with it) to help set rates. This isn't technically a discount, but improving your credit can meaningfully lower your auto insurance premium. California, Hawaii, Massachusetts, and Michigan prohibit the use of credit in auto insurance pricing.

faq.question: How do I get the military discount if I'm a veteran but not active duty?
faq.answer: Different carriers define eligibility differently. GEICO extends its military discount to active duty, National Guard, reserve, and retired military — veterans qualify. USAA is open to veterans as well as active military. When shopping, specifically ask about veteran eligibility as distinct from active duty eligibility — the discount amounts may differ.

faq.question: Is it worth switching insurers to get new customer discounts even if I lose loyalty discounts?
faq.answer: Often yes — research consistently shows that insurance companies offer better rates to new customers than to long-term ones. Shopping and switching every two to three years can save $300-500 annually on average. The loyalty discount you lose is usually smaller than the new customer discount you gain. But factor in any mid-policy cancellation fees and the hassle factor.

keyTakeaways: Bundling home and auto insurance with one company is typically the largest single discount, ranging from 10-25% depending on the carrier.
Telematics programs (Snapshot, Drivewise, DriveEasy) offer the highest potential savings — up to 40% — but involve sharing driving behavior data with your insurer.
Payment method discounts (pay-in-full, paperless, autopay) stack easily and require no behavior change — worth 8-20% combined on most policies.
Discounts are multiplicative, not additive — three 10% discounts save roughly 27%, not 30%.
Good student discounts require annual proof of GPA — set a calendar reminder to submit your transcript each year or you may lose the discount without knowing.
Insurers do not automatically apply all discounts you qualify for — request a discount audit annually.
Pay-per-mile insurance (Metromile/Lemonade, Allstate Milewise) can save 50%+ for drivers under 5,000-6,000 miles per year.

title: Home Warranty vs Home Insurance: What They Cover, What They Cost, and Which One You Actually Need
metaDescription: Home insurance and home warranties are not the same thing. One covers disasters. One covers breakdowns. Both cost money and both have gaps — here's what you're actually paying for and when each is worth it.
h1: Home Warranty vs Home Insurance: What They Cover, What They Cost, and Which One You Actually Need
summary: New homeowners almost always buy both because their real estate agent recommended it and their lender required one of them.

sections.heading: The Confusion That Costs Homeowners Real Money
sections.content:
New homeowners almost always buy both because their real estate agent recommended it and their lender required one of them. But most people couldn't tell you which one covers what. They have two cards in their kitchen drawer and they're not totally sure which one to call when the water heater dies.

Home insurance is mandatory if you have a mortgage. Your lender requires it. It protects against catastrophic, unpredictable events — fires, storms, theft, liability for someone getting hurt on your property. The bank's collateral (your house) has to be insured.

Home warranties are optional. Nobody requires them. They're service contracts that cover the cost of repairing or replacing systems and appliances when they break down from normal wear and tear. The HVAC, the plumbing, the electrical, the refrigerator, the dishwasher.

The gap between the two is real and it bites people. Home insurance will not pay to replace your 15-year-old water heater that finally gave out. That's a maintenance issue, not a covered peril. A home warranty might cover that water heater — if you have the right plan and the right coverage terms, and if the breakdown qualifies under their contract language.

Understanding what each covers — and where neither covers you — is basically a requirement of homeownership.

sections.heading: What Home Insurance Actually Covers
sections.content:
Standard homeowners insurance (typically an HO-3 policy) covers four things:

Dwelling coverage: Physical damage to the structure of your house from covered perils. Covered perils on a standard HO-3 are 'open perils' — everything is covered unless specifically excluded. Excluded perils typically include floods, earthquakes, normal wear and tear, pest damage, and intentional damage. So if a tree falls on your roof: covered. If your roof just wears out after 20 years: not covered.

Other structures: Detached garage, fence, shed — usually covered at 10% of dwelling coverage by default.

Personal property: Your stuff — furniture, electronics, clothes, jewelry (with limits). Usually covered at 50-75% of dwelling coverage. Replacement cost vs actual cash value matters here — actual cash value deducts for depreciation, so a 5-year-old laptop gets settled for maybe $200 even if a comparable new one costs $1,200.

Liability: Someone slips on your icy driveway and sues you. Your dog bites a neighbor. Your kid breaks a window at a friend's house. Standard policies carry $100,000 in liability coverage; $300,000 is common; umbrella policies extend further.

Additional living expenses: If your home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss and you need to stay in a hotel while repairs happen, these costs are covered. Usually capped at 20-30% of dwelling coverage.

What home insurance does NOT cover: Normal wear and tear, mechanical or electrical breakdown, flooding (needs a separate flood insurance policy), earthquakes (needs a separate endorsement or policy), sewer/drain backup (usually an add-on), mold if it develops gradually from ongoing moisture, pest infestations, cosmetic damage.

sections.heading: What a Home Warranty Actually Covers
sections.content:
A home warranty is a service contract — not insurance — covering mechanical failure of your home's systems and appliances due to normal wear and use.

Systems plans typically cover:
- HVAC (heating and air conditioning)
- Electrical system
- Plumbing system (pipes, toilets, faucets)
- Water heater
- Ductwork

Appliance plans typically cover:
- Refrigerator
- Dishwasher
- Oven/range/cooktop
- Washer and dryer
- Built-in microwave
- Garbage disposal

Combo plans cover both systems and appliances.

Optional add-ons (extra cost) commonly include: pool and spa equipment, well pump, septic system, second refrigerator, standalone freezer, roof leak repair, central vacuum.

What home warranties do NOT cover: Pre-existing conditions (issues that existed before the policy started — this is heavily contested in claims). Cosmetic damage. Code violations — if your HVAC fails and the replacement must meet current code, the code upgrade costs are usually excluded. Damage from improper installation or maintenance. Outdoor plumbing. Most structural components.

The 'pre-existing conditions' issue is the biggest source of home warranty disputes. The company sends a technician. The technician says the failure was due to a pre-existing condition. Claim denied. This happens more often than it should.

sections.heading: Cost Breakdown: What You're Actually Paying
sections.content:
Home insurance costs:
National average: $1,759/year in 2024 for a median-value home ($300,000 dwelling coverage). But this varies wildly by state.

Florida: $3,000-10,000+/year. Hurricane and flood risk drives premiums through the roof. Some markets near impossible to insure.
Oklahoma/Kansas: $2,500-4,000/year. Tornado risk.
Texas: $2,000-5,000/year depending on coastal vs inland.
California: $1,200-3,000+/year, with wildfire-prone areas essentially uninsurable through private market — many homeowners ending up in the FAIR Plan.
Midwest/Northeast non-coastal: $1,200-2,000/year in many markets.

Deductibles: Standard home insurance deductibles are $500-2,500. Wind/hail deductibles in storm-prone states can be a percentage of dwelling value — 1-2% means a $5,000-10,000 deductible on a $500K home before your hurricane claim pays anything.

Home warranty costs:
Annual premium range: $300-600/year for basic plans. Comprehensive plans with systems + appliances can run $600-1,000/year.

Service call fee: Every time a technician comes out, you pay a service call fee — typically $75-150 per visit. This is on top of your annual premium. If you have three service calls in a year at $100 each, that's $300 in service fees alone on top of your $500 premium.

Top warranty companies and approximate costs:
- American Home Shield: $30-60/month ($360-720/year). One of the largest, most reputable.
- Choice Home Warranty: $30-50/month ($360-600/year). Popular but more customer service complaints.
- First American Home Warranty: $25-50/month.
- 2-10 Home Buyers Warranty: $20-45/month. Also covers structural defects for new construction.
- Select Home Warranty: $30-45/month with aggressive promotional pricing.

Service call fees at these companies: $65-100 per visit typically.

sections.heading: Where They Overlap — and Where Neither Covers You
sections.content:
There are scenarios where both could theoretically apply — and scenarios where neither does.

Water damage: If a pipe bursts suddenly, home insurance typically covers the resulting water damage (ruined floors, damaged walls). But the pipe repair itself? Not covered by home insurance. Your home warranty might cover the pipe repair. So you're potentially filing with both: warranty for the broken pipe, insurance for the resulting damage to your home.

HVAC failure: If your AC system fails from normal wear, home insurance doesn't cover it — it's not a covered peril, it's maintenance. A home warranty covers this (usually). But if a covered storm event damages your HVAC unit, home insurance applies, not the warranty.

Appliance damage from a covered peril: House fire damages your appliances. Home insurance personal property coverage handles replacement. Your warranty is irrelevant.

Where neither covers you: Gradual damage. Mold that developed because of ongoing moisture. A sinking foundation from soil conditions. Pest damage. Normal wear and gradual deterioration of the roof (as opposed to storm damage). These are the largest gaps, and they're real. Roofs are a particular headache — insurance covers storm damage, warranty rarely covers roofs well, and gradual aging (the roof is just old) is nobody's problem but yours.

sections.heading: When a Home Warranty Is Worth It
sections.content:
A home warranty makes financial sense in specific situations. Not always. Not automatically.

Older home with aging systems and appliances. If the HVAC is 12 years old, the water heater is 10 years old, and the dishwasher is 8 years old — they're all approaching end-of-life. A $500/year warranty that might cover a $4,000 HVAC replacement is a reasonable bet. The expected value calculation works in your favor when everything is old.

You just bought a home and don't have a repair emergency fund. A home warranty is essentially a low-friction way to cover unexpected repair costs during the period when you've just dumped your savings into a down payment and closing costs. Even if it's not the most efficient use of money long-term, the cash flow protection has real value.

Limited knowledge of home systems. If you genuinely don't know an HVAC technician and wouldn't know a fair price from a rip-off, a home warranty handles vendor coordination and negotiation for you. The contractor comes out and the dispute over the repair cost is between them and the warranty company, not you.

Seller-provided warranty on a newly purchased home. Many sellers provide a one-year home warranty as part of the sale. This costs them nothing extra (typically $300-500) and provides a year of coverage. Accept it — free is free. Re-evaluate at renewal whether you'd pay for it yourself.

When a home warranty probably isn't worth it: New construction with new systems and appliances under manufacturer warranties. You have a healthy repair fund (emergency savings can self-insure the same risk). You have good contractor relationships and know how to get fair pricing. You've had a warranty for three years and used it zero times with no major system failures on the horizon.

sections.heading: The Service Contract Fine Print That Kills Claims
sections.content:
Home warranties are notoriously contentious. The fine print matters enormously.

Pre-existing conditions exclusion: This is the #1 claim denial reason. Warranty companies will send a technician, the tech finds the failure was a pre-existing condition (sometimes genuinely, sometimes questionably), and the claim is denied. When buying a warranty, ask specifically how they define and handle pre-existing conditions.

Improper maintenance exclusion: If your HVAC failed because the filter wasn't changed, the warranty company may deny the claim. You're supposed to maintain your systems. 'Maintain' can be defined loosely in disputes.

Capacity limits: Many warranties have per-item caps. If your HVAC replacement costs $7,000 and your warranty's HVAC cap is $3,000, you're paying $4,000 out of pocket. Read the caps before you buy.

Parts vs labor: Some warranties cover parts but not labor, or vice versa. Read what's included.

Code upgrade exclusion: Replacing your old HVAC system may require upgrading to current building codes. Many warranties exclude code upgrade costs. This can add thousands to a replacement project that you're still on the hook for.

Forced-air vs mini-split: Coverage definitions matter. Some warranties cover 'central air conditioning systems' and may not cover ductless mini-split systems. If your home uses non-standard systems, verify coverage explicitly.

Reputable companies have clearer contracts and better customer service ratings. American Home Shield, despite its higher cost, has better claim resolution rates than many budget providers. Check BBB ratings and consumer review aggregators before choosing a warranty company — there are significant quality differences.

sections.heading: Best Home Warranty Companies: A Realistic Assessment
sections.content:
American Home Shield (AHS): The market leader for a reason. Largest network of contractors, clearest contract language, best for older homes. They offer three plans: ShieldSilver (systems only, ~$30-45/month), ShieldGold (systems + appliances, ~$40-60/month), ShieldPlatinum (comprehensive, ~$55-75/month). Service call fee $100-125. They're not cheap but they're reliable.

Choice Home Warranty: Often cheaper than AHS. Two plans: Basic and Total. Basic covers most appliances, Total adds systems. Premium approximately $36-54/month, service call fee $65-85. Consistently has more complaints about claim denials than AHS. The savings might not be worth the risk.

AFC Home Club: Good reputation, good for high-cost repair caps. Technician choice option — you can use your own contractor and they reimburse. This is a meaningful differentiator for people with trusted contractors.

First American Home Warranty: Solid middle option. Reasonable premiums, decent contractor network.

2-10 Home Buyers Warranty: Best for new construction — they also offer structural defect warranties (10 years on structural, 2 years on systems, 1 year on workmanship). Unique product in the market.

Select Home Warranty: Very cheap, very aggressive promotional pricing. But the claim experience is significantly worse than the top-tier options. 'You get what you pay for' applies here.

Key metric to check: Look up the company's ratio of complaints to policies on the BBB and check state insurance department complaint data. Warranty company quality varies more than almost any other insurance-adjacent product.

sections.heading: Choosing Coverage That Makes Sense Together
sections.content:
Here's the practical framework:

Home insurance: Not optional if you have a mortgage. Optimize it like any insurance product — right coverage amounts, right deductible for your risk tolerance and cash reserves, right endorsements for your specific exposures (flood if you're in a flood zone, earthquake if you're in earthquake country, jewelry rider if you have valuables above standard limits).

Home warranty: Optional. The decision tree:
- New construction? Probably skip it — manufacturer warranties cover most issues.
- All-new systems? Probably skip it for a few years.
- Mix of old systems? Calculate expected replacement costs vs annual premium + service fees. If an HVAC replacement is overdue and costs $5,000-8,000 in your market, a $600/year warranty looks cheap.
- Cash-rich with an emergency fund? Self-insure if you want — the warranty's value is smoothing cash flow and handling vendor coordination, not necessarily total financial protection.

The honest math: Home warranties pay out more often than traditional insurance because the insured events (appliance breakdowns) happen far more frequently than catastrophic home damage. But the warranty companies price for this — service call fees are significant, caps limit payouts, exclusions are numerous. Consumer advocates estimate that home warranties return roughly 50-60 cents of value per dollar of premium on average, compared to 70-80 cents for most property and casualty insurance. That's not inherently bad — insurance is always buying probability management — but it means the value proposition depends heavily on your specific situation.

faq.question: Does home insurance cover appliance breakdowns?
faq.answer: No. Standard home insurance covers sudden and accidental damage from covered perils — fire, storms, theft. It does not cover appliances breaking down from normal wear and tear. If your refrigerator stops working because it's 12 years old, that's a maintenance issue, not a covered insurance claim. That's exactly what a home warranty is designed for.

faq.question: Does a home warranty cover pre-existing conditions?
faq.answer: Generally no — pre-existing conditions are the most common exclusion and the most common reason for claim denials. If a system or appliance had an issue before your warranty coverage started, the company can deny the claim. The key question when buying a warranty is how they define 'pre-existing' and what their process is for determining it. Better companies use known-failure analysis rather than denying anything that's not brand-new.

faq.question: Is a home warranty worth it for a new home?
faq.answer: Usually not for the first few years. New construction typically comes with builder warranties and manufacturer warranties on systems and appliances. After 5-7 years, when systems start approaching middle age, a home warranty becomes more worth evaluating. The exception: if you're buying a new home and the builder includes a 2-10 Home Buyers Warranty (or similar structural warranty), that's different from an appliance warranty and worth having.

faq.question: Can you have both home insurance and a home warranty?
faq.answer: Yes — and for most homeowners with a mortgage, you'll have both. They cover different risks. Home insurance covers catastrophic perils (fire, storms, theft, liability). Home warranty covers mechanical breakdown of systems and appliances. Having both means you have coverage for most of the financial risks of homeownership — though both have exclusions and gaps.

faq.question: What's the average home insurance deductible?
faq.answer: Most homeowners policies have a flat deductible of $500-2,500 for standard claims. In hurricane-prone states, wind and hail deductibles are often percentage-based — 1-2% of dwelling coverage value. On a $400,000 home, a 2% wind/hail deductible means you pay the first $8,000 of any wind damage claim before insurance kicks in.

faq.question: Do home warranties cover roof replacement?
faq.answer: Roof coverage varies widely by company and plan. Most standard home warranties do not cover roof replacement. Some offer limited roof leak repair as an add-on. For full roof replacement due to age and wear, home insurance is also unlikely to pay — insurance covers storm damage, not gradual aging. A 20-year-old roof that simply needs replacement is an out-of-pocket expense. This is one of the biggest coverage gaps in homeownership.

keyTakeaways: Home insurance covers sudden catastrophic events (fire, storms, theft, liability) and is required by mortgage lenders. Home warranties cover mechanical breakdown of systems and appliances and are optional service contracts.
Home insurance costs $1,500-3,000+/year nationally with wide variation by state — Florida, Texas, and California are significantly more expensive.
Home warranties cost $300-600/year plus $75-150 per service call, with comprehensive plans reaching $600-1,000/year.
Pre-existing conditions are the most common reason for home warranty claim denials — read the contract language carefully before purchasing.
Home warranties make the most financial sense for older homes with aging systems (HVAC, water heater, plumbing) approaching end-of-life.
Neither home insurance nor home warranty covers gradual wear, pest damage, or gradual roof deterioration — these are homeowner out-of-pocket expenses.
American Home Shield is the most reliable major warranty company despite higher cost; budget options like Select Home Warranty have significantly more complaint volume.

title: Health Insurance If You Work Part-Time: Every Option Explained
metaDescription: Part-time workers are largely on their own for health insurance — employers don't have to offer it, and most don't. Here's every real option: ACA marketplace, Medicaid, spouse's plan, COBRA, gig economy solutions, and what actually makes financial sense.
h1: Health Insurance If You Work Part-Time: Every Option Explained
summary: About 26 million Americans work part-time.

sections.heading: The Part-Time Health Insurance Problem Is Bigger Than Most People Realize
sections.content:
About 26 million Americans work part-time. The vast majority of them — roughly 80% — don't have access to employer-sponsored health insurance through their job. Some have coverage through a spouse or parent. A meaningful chunk have Medicaid. A lot of them are uninsured, gambling that nothing bad will happen.

The problem is structural. Employer-sponsored health insurance is tied to full-time employment as a matter of historical accident — it became a standard benefit in the post-WWII era when most jobs were full-time salaried positions. The gig economy, retail sector, service industry, and part-time professional world have all grown enormously since then. The insurance system hasn't caught up.

The Affordable Care Act fixed some of this. The ACA created individual marketplaces with income-based subsidies, mandated that large employers offer coverage to full-time employees, and expanded Medicaid. These changes helped millions of part-time and gig workers. But they didn't solve everything, and navigating the options is genuinely confusing.

If you work part-time and you're trying to figure out health insurance, you have more options than you probably think. Some are better than others. The right answer depends on your income, your state, your age, whether you have family members with coverage, and what your actual health needs are.

sections.heading: Does Your Employer Have to Offer You Coverage?
sections.content:
Short answer: probably not.

The ACA's employer mandate requires applicable large employers (50+ full-time equivalent employees) to offer affordable coverage to employees who work 30 hours/week or more. This is the ACA threshold — 30 hours per week. If you work 29 hours, the employer has no ACA obligation to cover you.

Note what this means: the law only requires employers to offer coverage, not that you take it. And it only requires offering it to employees at 30+ hours. If you're regularly working 28-29 hours, you're likely not covered by this requirement.

Employers with fewer than 50 full-time equivalent employees (small employers) have no mandate at all. They may choose to offer coverage — some do — but they're not legally required to.

What about the '30 hours' rule in practice? Employers know about this threshold. Some deliberately keep part-time employees below 30 hours to avoid the mandate. This is legal. It's also very common in retail, food service, and hospitality. If you're in one of these sectors at under 30 hours, you're almost certainly on your own for insurance.

Some large employers do cover part-time workers below 30 hours voluntarily. Starbucks famously offers benefits to employees working 20+ hours per week. Costco offers coverage to part-timers. These are exceptions, not the rule. Worth asking your HR department specifically — 'What is the minimum hours requirement for health insurance eligibility?' Get the answer in writing.

sections.heading: ACA Marketplace: The Primary Option for Most Part-Time Workers
sections.content:
The ACA marketplace (healthcare.gov, or state exchanges in states that run their own) is where most part-time workers without other coverage options should start.

How subsidies work: Premium tax credits (subsidies) are available to households with income between 100% and 400% of the federal poverty level — and since 2021 (extended through 2025 under the Inflation Reduction Act), above 400% as well. The cap is now that you pay no more than 8.5% of your income on benchmark plan premiums regardless of how high your income is.

Income levels in 2024:
- 100% FPL (individual): ~$15,060/year
- 138% FPL (Medicaid threshold in expansion states): ~$20,783
- 150% FPL: ~$22,590
- 200% FPL: ~$30,120
- 250% FPL: ~$37,650
- 400% FPL: ~$60,240

At 200% FPL, you pay about 6.5-7% of income for the benchmark second-lowest-cost silver plan. The subsidy covers the rest. At 150% FPL, you pay about 0-3% of income. At 100-138% FPL, the plan is essentially free in most cases (with a small premium contribution).

Silver plans have enhanced cost-sharing reductions (CSR) if your income is below 250% FPL. This is huge and underutilized. A Silver plan on the marketplace at 150-200% FPL with CSR has dramatically reduced deductibles and out-of-pocket maxes compared to a standard Silver plan. An $8,700 maximum out-of-pocket might be reduced to $3,000 or even $1,500. This makes Silver the right tier for most low-to-moderate income part-time workers, not Bronze.

Open enrollment: November 1 - January 15 nationally (January 15 for February 1 coverage start). Special enrollment periods for qualifying life events: losing job-based coverage, moving to a new area, having a baby, getting married, etc.

Applying: healthcare.gov or your state's exchange. The application asks for your estimated annual income for the coming year. For part-time workers with variable income, estimate carefully — being off significantly can result in a subsidy reconciliation at tax time.

sections.heading: Medicaid Expansion: You Might Qualify and Not Know It
sections.content:
Medicaid is the joint federal-state program providing health coverage for low-income individuals. Under the ACA, Medicaid was expanded to cover adults with incomes up to 138% of the federal poverty level — about $20,783/year for an individual in 2024.

As of 2024, 40 states plus DC have adopted Medicaid expansion. The holdouts — states that have NOT expanded — are Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Idaho, Kansas, Mississippi, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Wisconsin, and Wyoming. If you live in one of these states and have low income, you may fall into the 'coverage gap' — income too high for traditional Medicaid but too low for ACA subsidies. This is genuinely bad and a real policy failure that hasn't been fixed.

In expansion states, Medicaid is often the best option for part-time workers with low income. Coverage is comprehensive, premiums are $0 or near-zero, cost-sharing is minimal. The trade-off is that not all doctors accept Medicaid, and networks can be narrower than private insurance.

Enrollment: Unlike ACA marketplace plans, Medicaid has no enrollment period — you can apply any time of year. If you become eligible due to reduced income at any point, apply immediately.

Medicaid income is counted monthly in most states. If your income drops because you cut hours, you may become Medicaid-eligible immediately. Don't wait.

Worth knowing: CHIP (Children's Health Insurance Program) covers children in families with income too high for Medicaid but unable to afford private insurance — up to 200-300% FPL depending on state. If you have children and you're part-time, check CHIP eligibility even if you don't qualify for Medicaid yourself.

sections.heading: Getting on a Spouse's or Partner's Plan
sections.content:
If you're married or have a domestic partner (some plans cover domestic partners), getting onto their employer-sponsored plan is often the best option — usually cheaper, better coverage than individual market.

Open enrollment: You can't just join a spouse's employer plan any time you want. Employer plans have open enrollment once a year, typically in fall for January 1 coverage. BUT — losing your own coverage is a qualifying life event that allows a 'special enrollment period' of 30-60 days (depending on the employer plan). If you quit a full-time job to go part-time and lose your coverage, that's a trigger to enroll in your spouse's plan immediately.

Cost: The premium for adding a spouse to an employer plan can range from $200-600/month depending on the employer and plan tier. But employer-subsidized rates are usually still better than individual market rates, even with subsidies.

ACA interaction: If your spouse's employer plan is considered 'affordable' (defined as costing less than 9.12% of household income for employee-only coverage in 2024), you don't qualify for ACA marketplace subsidies. The ACA affordability test is applied to the employee-only cost, not the family cost — this is sometimes called the 'family glitch' and it was partially fixed by the Biden administration in 2022, but it's complex. If you're considering your options, run the numbers both ways.

sections.heading: COBRA: The Bridge Option Nobody Loves But Everyone Needs to Know About
sections.content:
COBRA (Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act) lets you continue your employer health insurance coverage after you leave a job, reduce your hours, or otherwise lose employer-sponsored coverage. You keep the same plan, same network, same benefits.

The catch: you pay the full premium plus a 2% administrative fee. When you had employer coverage, your employer was probably paying 60-80% of the premium. On COBRA, that comes entirely out of your pocket.

Typical COBRA costs:
- Individual coverage: $400-700/month
- Family coverage: $1,200-2,000/month
- Self + spouse: $800-1,400/month

These numbers are brutal. That's why COBRA is usually a bridge, not a destination.

COBRA duration: Up to 18 months after job loss or reduction in hours (26 months for disability, 36 months for other qualifying events like divorce or death of covered employee). You have 60 days from notification to elect COBRA, and then another 45 days to pay the first premium.

When COBRA actually makes sense:
- You're mid-treatment for something significant and changing networks would disrupt care.
- You're close to meeting your deductible or out-of-pocket maximum and switching plans would reset the clock.
- You have a very short job gap and expect new employer coverage to start within a few months.
- ACA marketplace options in your area are limited or significantly more expensive than your subsidized COBRA premium.

For most part-time workers taking a pay cut to work fewer hours, COBRA is too expensive to maintain long-term. But knowing you have the 60-day election window is useful — if you go part-time and lose coverage, you don't have to decide immediately. You have 60 days to evaluate your options, and you can retroactively elect COBRA (with back premiums) if you end up with a significant medical expense during that window.

sections.heading: Gig Economy and Freelance Health Insurance Options
sections.content:
The gig economy created a new category of underinsured worker — self-employed, no employer, often variable income. The options are the same as any other individual without employer coverage, but the income variability makes navigation trickier.

ACA marketplace: Same as above, but income estimation is harder. Gig workers should estimate conservatively — it's better to slightly over-estimate income (resulting in a smaller subsidy and maybe a small tax credit at filing) than to significantly under-estimate (resulting in subsidy repayment at tax time). You can update your income estimate mid-year if your situation changes.

Self-employed health insurance deduction: If you're self-employed and profitable, 100% of health insurance premiums are deductible above the line on your federal tax return — before you calculate your adjusted gross income. This makes ACA marketplace coverage more affordable on an after-tax basis. On a $6,000 annual premium, a 22% marginal tax rate saves $1,320 in federal taxes.

Freelancers Union: Not a union in the traditional sense, but a membership organization that offers access to group health coverage options for freelancers. Plans vary by state. Worth checking if you're in a metro area with limited marketplace options.

Industry associations: Some industry trade associations negotiate group health rates for members. Worth investigating in your specific field.

Health sharing ministries: These are NOT insurance. They're cost-sharing arrangements between members, usually with religious requirements. They're exempt from ACA regulations, which means they can reject pre-existing conditions, have no cap on out-of-pocket costs, and may not cover medications or procedures they deem inconsistent with religious values. Some people use them and are satisfied. Many people have gotten badly burned when a large claim was refused. Only consider these with full understanding of what you're getting.

sections.heading: Short-Term Health Plans: The Cheap Option With Real Downsides
sections.content:
Short-term health insurance plans are available from private insurers outside the ACA marketplace. Premiums are significantly lower — often $100-200/month vs $300-500 on the marketplace.

Why they're cheaper: They're not ACA-compliant. They can and do:
- Deny coverage for pre-existing conditions
- Exclude coverage for specific conditions
- Cap total annual benefits (e.g., $1,000,000 limit)
- Not cover maternity care, mental health, substance abuse treatment, or prescription drugs
- Exclude coverage for conditions you didn't disclose at enrollment

Under Trump administration rules (2018) and again under Biden reversals and subsequent regulatory ping-pong, the duration and availability of short-term plans has varied. As of 2024, federal rules generally limit short-term plans to 4 months per enrollment with renewal limitations, though some states allow longer durations and others prohibit or restrict them.

Who might consider a short-term plan: A young, healthy person in a short bridge situation (waiting for new employer coverage to start, between seasonal jobs) who has no ongoing conditions and understands the coverage limitations. Nobody who has a chronic condition, takes regular medications, or might need mental health or maternity care.

The short-term plan problem: You don't know you needed comprehensive coverage until you need it. The 28-year-old with a short-term plan who gets diagnosed with cancer or has a serious accident discovers the limits of their coverage at the worst possible time. This is not a theoretical risk — it's documented, it's common, and the medical debt consequences can be severe.

sections.heading: Choosing the Right Option Based on Your Situation
sections.content:
There's no universal answer. Here's a decision framework:

First: Check Medicaid eligibility. If you're in an expansion state and your income is below 138% FPL (~$20,783 for an individual, ~$43,056 for a family of four), you likely qualify. Apply. It's free and comprehensive.

If income is above Medicaid threshold but below 400% FPL (or you're in a non-expansion state and income exceeds your state's Medicaid limit): Go to healthcare.gov or your state's marketplace. Run the numbers with your actual drug list and expected healthcare needs. Silver plans with cost-sharing reductions are usually the best value for income below 250% FPL.

If you have a spouse with employer coverage: Get on their plan during open enrollment or within 30-60 days of losing your own coverage. Compare the cost of adding yourself to their plan against your marketplace options.

If you're a gig worker: ACA marketplace with the self-employed premium deduction is usually the baseline. Research your state's marketplace options specifically and factor in the tax deduction.

If you're in a short-term bridge situation: COBRA for critical ongoing treatment continuity, ACA marketplace for everything else.

If you're young (under 26): You can stay on a parent's plan regardless of whether you live at home, are a student, or are financially independent. This is one of the ACA provisions that helps part-time young workers significantly.

Cost management regardless of option: HSA-compatible high-deductible plans (HSA plans) allow you to save pre-tax dollars for medical expenses. If you're healthy and can afford a higher deductible, an HSA plan can reduce your net healthcare costs meaningfully — the tax savings on HSA contributions are real money, and funds roll over year to year indefinitely.

sections.heading: The Hours Threshold Reality and How Employers Handle It
sections.content:
The ACA's 30-hour threshold is real, but it's only the starting point. Here's what actually happens in practice.

The ACA employer mandate: Large employers (50+ FTEs) must offer affordable coverage to employees working 30+ hours/week on average. They have up to 12 months of a 'measurement period' to determine which employees average 30+ hours before they're required to offer coverage. New employees typically get a 90-day waiting period before coverage can begin.

Employer-specific thresholds: Many employers set their own benefits thresholds independent of the ACA minimum. Some offer benefits at 20 hours (Starbucks). Some have 32 or 35 hours as their threshold even though the ACA only requires 30. The employer's plan document governs — read it or ask HR.

Common sector reality:
- Retail/food service: Benefits typically start at 30-35 hours, many intentionally schedule part-timers at 25-29 hours to avoid the mandate.
- Healthcare: Often covers part-timers at 20-24 hours because they want to attract and retain workers.
- Education: Part-time adjunct faculty are a notorious gap — often 'employees' in name but classified to avoid benefit obligations.
- Gig platforms (Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, Instacart): Workers classified as independent contractors, no employer health benefits obligation.

The contractor classification issue: Gig platform workers are independent contractors, not employees. This is legally contested in some states (California's AB5 is the most prominent example), but currently most gig workers are on their own for health insurance. This affects millions of people and the policy debate is ongoing.

faq.question: Does my employer have to offer me health insurance if I work part-time?
faq.answer: If you work 30+ hours per week and your employer has 50+ full-time equivalent employees, the ACA employer mandate requires them to offer you coverage. If you work under 30 hours, or if your employer has fewer than 50 FTE employees, there's no federal mandate. Some employers offer coverage to part-timers voluntarily — it's worth asking HR directly about the minimum hours threshold.

faq.question: I work 25 hours a week and make $28,000 per year. What's my best health insurance option?
faq.answer: At $28,000 (about 186% FPL for a single person in 2024), you're in the ACA marketplace sweet spot. You'd qualify for substantial premium tax credits. On the marketplace, look at Silver plans specifically — at your income level, Silver plans with cost-sharing reductions give you much lower deductibles and out-of-pocket costs than the Silver tier normally offers. Run your numbers at healthcare.gov to see specific plan options and costs in your area.

faq.question: Can I get on my spouse's health insurance if I go part-time?
faq.answer: Yes — losing your own employer coverage qualifies as a special enrollment period, typically giving you 30-60 days to enroll in your spouse's employer plan. Outside of that window, you'd have to wait for your spouse's employer's annual open enrollment period. Keep in mind that adding a spouse to an employer plan can cost $200-600/month depending on the employer.

faq.question: How does COBRA work if I go from full-time to part-time?
faq.answer: A reduction in hours that causes you to lose health coverage is a COBRA qualifying event. You'll receive a COBRA notice within 44 days and have 60 days to elect coverage. You'd pay the full premium plus a 2% admin fee — typically $400-700/month for individual coverage. COBRA is usually a bridge option, not a long-term solution for most part-time workers due to the cost.

faq.question: I'm a freelancer with variable income. How do I figure out my ACA subsidy?
faq.answer: Estimate your projected annual income as accurately as you can when you apply. For freelancers, erring slightly high is usually safer — if you over-estimate income you get a smaller subsidy but might get a credit at tax time. If you significantly under-estimate income you'll have to repay subsidies at tax time, which can be a painful surprise. You can update your income estimate on your marketplace account at any time during the year if your situation changes.

faq.question: Are health sharing ministries a legitimate alternative to health insurance for part-time workers?
faq.answer: They're a real option that some people use, but they come with major caveats. Health sharing ministries are not insurance, are not regulated as insurance, and are not required to cover pre-existing conditions, mental health, substance abuse, or other essential health benefits. Members have had large claims refused with no regulatory recourse. They're only worth considering if you're young, healthy, have no ongoing conditions, and fully understand that you're participating in a voluntary cost-sharing arrangement, not an insurance contract.

keyTakeaways: Employers are only required to offer health insurance to employees working 30+ hours per week under the ACA employer mandate, and only if they have 50+ full-time equivalent employees.
Medicaid covers individuals with income below 138% FPL in the 40 expansion states — apply immediately if you qualify, there's no enrollment period.

ACA marketplace Silver plans with cost-sharing reductions offer dramatically lower deductibles and out-of-pocket costs for people with income below 250% FPL — Silver is almost always better than Bronze at this income level.
COBRA preserves your current coverage but requires you to pay the full premium (typically $400-700/month for individual) — it's a bridge, not a long-term solution.
Getting on a spouse's employer plan when you lose coverage is often the best option, triggered by a special enrollment period that typically lasts 30-60 days.
Freelancers and self-employed workers can deduct 100% of health insurance premiums above the line on their federal tax return, making marketplace coverage meaningfully cheaper on an after-tax basis.
Workers under age 26 can remain on a parent's health plan regardless of their employment status, financial independence, or whether they live at home.

h1: Medicare Advantage Network: In-Network vs Out-of-Network Explained
metaDescription: HMO vs PPO network rules for Medicare Advantage plans, how to verify provider directories, ghost networks, what actually happens when you go out-of-network, and emergency care exceptions — explained plainly.
summary: Here's what nobody tells you when you're shopping Medicare Advantage: the premium is the bait, the network is the trap.

sections.heading: Why Your Medicare Advantage Network Is the Whole Game
sections.content:
Here's what nobody tells you when you're shopping Medicare Advantage: the premium is the bait, the network is the trap. A $0 premium plan sounds incredible — and it can be incredible — but if your cardiologist isn't in-network, you're either paying out-of-pocket rates that'll make your eyes water or you're finding a new cardiologist at 72. Neither of those is a great option.

Medicare Advantage (Part C) is sold by private insurers who contract with CMS to deliver your Medicare benefits. Because private companies run these plans, they get to build their own provider networks. They decide which doctors, hospitals, specialists, labs, and imaging centers are 'in-network.' And that decision matters more than almost anything else on the plan comparison sheet.

This isn't a minor bureaucratic detail. The entire cost structure of Medicare Advantage — the low premiums, the extra benefits like dental and vision, the out-of-pocket maximums — is built around the assumption that you'll stay in-network. Stray outside that network, depending on your plan type, and you either pay a lot more or you pay everything.

About 47 million Americans are enrolled in Medicare Advantage as of 2026, according to KFF. That's more than half of all Medicare beneficiaries. Most of them chose their plan based on premium and star rating. A lot fewer checked the network carefully before enrolling. And some of them found out the hard way what happens when they didn't.

So let's actually go through how this works. HMOs, PPOs, the difference between them, how to check if your doctors are really in-network (not just listed in a directory that hasn't been updated since 2023), what happens in emergencies, and what your rights are when the system fails you.

sections.heading: HMO Plans: You Stay in the Network or You Pay Full Price
sections.content:
Health Maintenance Organization plans are the most common type of Medicare Advantage. They're also the most restrictive.

With an HMO, you get a defined list of in-network providers. You pick a primary care physician (PCP) from that list. Your PCP coordinates your care. Want to see a specialist? In most HMOs, you need a referral from your PCP first. Want to see a specialist who isn't in the network? In most HMOs, that visit isn't covered at all — except in a genuine emergency.

This is where people get surprised. Not just 'pay a higher copay' surprised. 'The entire bill is yours' surprised.

HMOs can do this because CMS allows it. The rules say that Medicare Advantage plans must cover emergency and urgently needed care anywhere in the US, but for everything else, HMOs can — and do — require in-network providers. If you choose an HMO and then go see an out-of-network orthopedic surgeon for an elective knee replacement, the plan owes you nothing. That's not a technicality. That's the product you bought.

Why do people choose HMOs then? Because they're cheaper. HMO premiums are almost always lower than PPO premiums. In many markets, HMOs are the plans with the $0 premium. And for someone who has been seeing the same primary care doctor for 20 years and that doctor is in-network — fine. An HMO works great. The problem is when circumstances change. You move. Your doctor retires. You develop a condition that requires a specialist your HMO doesn't have. Now you're either switching plans during open enrollment or you're negotiating out-of-network costs.

HMO-POS (Point of Service) is a slight variation that sometimes lets you go out-of-network for a higher cost share. But don't assume your HMO is HMO-POS. Read the plan documents.

The referral requirement is its own headache. Most Medicare Advantage HMOs require referrals for specialty care. Some carve out certain specialties — gynecology, mental health, sometimes ophthalmology — as self-referral. But for the most part, you call your PCP, you explain what's going on, you get a referral, and then you see the specialist. That process takes time. If you have a condition that needs fast specialist attention, referral delays can matter.

Some HMOs have tightened their referral processes in recent years, requiring pre-authorization for an expanding list of procedures and specialist visits. CMS has pushed back on some of the more aggressive prior authorization practices, and the 2024 CMS final rule did tighten requirements around prior authorization timelines. But prior auth is still very much part of how HMOs control costs — and control access.

sections.heading: PPO Plans: More Flexibility, More Cost
sections.content:
Preferred Provider Organization plans are the other major flavor of Medicare Advantage, and they work fundamentally differently from HMOs.

With a PPO, you have in-network providers who cost less, and out-of-network providers who cost more — but you can see both. No referrals required. No gatekeeper PCP (though you can designate one if you want). You decide who you see and when. The plan pays something for out-of-network visits; it just pays less.

How much less? It varies dramatically by plan. Some PPOs have out-of-network cost sharing that's 20–30% higher than in-network. Some are structured so that in-network visits have a $30 copay and out-of-network visits have a 40% coinsurance with a separate, much higher out-of-pocket maximum. Read the summary of benefits carefully on this because 'out-of-network coverage' can range from genuinely useful to technically-exists-but-financially-brutal.

PPOs are popular with people who travel frequently, have homes in multiple states, or have established relationships with specialists they don't want to leave. Snowbirds — retirees who split time between, say, Minnesota and Florida — often gravitate toward PPOs specifically because HMOs don't travel well. Your Minnesota HMO has zero Florida network. Your Florida HMO has zero Minnesota network. A PPO at least offers some coverage when you're away from your primary residence.

The tradeoff is real though. PPO premiums are higher. In some markets, significantly higher. A plan comparison in a metro area might show a $0-premium HMO and a $75/month PPO covering the same geographic area. That $75/month is $900/year. Over a few years, that adds up. Whether the flexibility is worth it depends entirely on your situation — specifically whether you're actually going to use out-of-network providers, and if so, how often.

Private Fee-for-Service (PFFS) plans are a third type that technically allows any Medicare-accepting provider, but PFFS enrollment has been declining for years. They're a smaller slice of the market and worth knowing exist but not the main event.

Special Needs Plans (SNPs) — for people with chronic conditions, dual Medicare/Medicaid eligibility, or institutional care — have their own network rules and are generally more restrictive than standard PPOs but may include providers that specialize in their specific population.

sections.heading: How to Actually Check If Your Doctor Is In-Network
sections.content:
The plan's provider directory. That's the official answer. Every Medicare Advantage plan is required to maintain an online provider directory. You go to the plan's website, you search for your doctor by name or NPI number, and you see whether they're listed as in-network.

Except — and this is a real problem that CMS has documented repeatedly — those directories are often wrong.

CMS audits have found error rates in Medicare Advantage provider directories ranging from 25% to over 50% depending on the metric. Providers listed who have retired. Providers listed at practice addresses they left two years ago. Specialists listed as accepting new patients who haven't accepted new patients in three years. This is the 'ghost network' problem and it is genuinely widespread.

KFF and the HHS Office of Inspector General have both flagged provider directory accuracy as a systemic issue in Medicare Advantage. The 2023 OIG report found that Medicare Advantage beneficiaries trying to find mental health providers had particular difficulty — directories listed providers who weren't actually available, often by a lot.

So what do you actually do? Don't rely solely on the directory. Call. Pick up the phone and call your doctor's office directly. Tell them you're considering enrolling in [plan name] and ask if they currently participate in that network. Ask if they're accepting new patients on that plan. Ask if there are any restrictions or limitations. That five-minute phone call will save you from a lot of grief.

For hospitals, call the hospital's insurance verification line. Most large hospitals have dedicated staff who handle exactly this question. They can tell you whether the hospital has a current contract with a specific plan.

Also check whether individual physicians at a hospital are in-network separately from the hospital itself. This catches people off guard. You go to an in-network hospital for surgery. The facility is in-network. But the anesthesiologist who works in that facility is employed by a separate anesthesiology group — one that isn't contracted with your plan. The surgery is covered. The anesthesia bill hits you at out-of-network rates. This happens. It's a known problem. The No Surprises Act (effective 2022) provides some protections for surprise out-of-network bills in certain circumstances, particularly for emergency care and at in-network facilities, but navigating those protections isn't automatic.

The Medicare Plan Finder at medicare.gov is the starting point for comparing plans, but verify individual providers directly. When in doubt: call the plan, call the provider, and get it in writing (or at least get a name and date stamp your phone record).

If you're working with a broker or SHIP counselor, they can often help navigate provider directory verification, but the ultimate responsibility is on you to confirm before you enroll.

sections.heading: Ghost Networks: When the Directory Lies
sections.content:
Ghost networks deserve their own section because they're not a minor issue. They're a structural problem in Medicare Advantage that affects millions of beneficiaries.

A ghost network is a provider directory that lists physicians who either don't actually participate in the network, aren't accepting the plan's patients, have moved or retired, or list incorrect contact information. The result is that a beneficiary chooses a plan believing their doctor — or a specialist they need — is covered, only to discover after enrollment that coverage doesn't actually exist.

Why does this happen? A few reasons. Networks are large and constantly changing. Doctors join and leave networks. Practices close or move. Not all plans have robust processes for updating directories in real time. Some plans have been found to pad directories with providers who contracted with the plan years ago but rarely or never actually see plan members. And updating a massive provider database across hundreds of plans is genuinely hard to do accurately.

But that doesn't make it okay. CMS has issued numerous guidance documents and enforcement actions around directory accuracy. The 2022 and 2023 CMS final rules included provisions designed to tighten directory standards. Plans that fail CMS audits can face sanctions, marketing suspensions, or enrollment freezes. That regulatory pressure has improved things somewhat, but it hasn't solved the problem.

For mental health services specifically, the ghost network problem is acute. Mental health provider shortages are real across the country. Plans may list therapists and psychiatrists who are technically credentialed with the plan but have long waiting lists, have stopped accepting new patients, or aren't actively taking Medicare Advantage enrollments. Someone who enrolls in a plan believing they have mental health coverage and then can't find a single in-network therapist who's actually available — that person has been harmed by a ghost network.

Practical protection: before your Annual Enrollment Period (AEP) closes, do actual provider verification on the three to five providers that matter most to you. Not just 'check the directory.' Call them. Confirm participation. Confirm they're taking new patients on that specific plan. If you're already enrolled and discover a provider you relied on isn't actually in-network, you may qualify for a Special Enrollment Period — contact 1-800-MEDICARE and document everything.

If you encounter a ghost network situation after enrollment, file a complaint with Medicare and contact your State Insurance Department. CMS takes complaints seriously, and documented complaints contribute to audit triggers against plans with systemic directory problems.

sections.heading: What Happens When You Go Out-of-Network
sections.content:
Let's be concrete about what 'out-of-network' actually means for your wallet, because the plan documents can be hard to parse.

For HMO enrollees, out-of-network non-emergency care is generally not covered at all. You pay the provider's full billed charges. There's no plan reimbursement. If your HMO's network doesn't include a rheumatologist you need and you go see one anyway, you're paying that bill yourself, in full, at whatever rate the provider charges — which is almost certainly higher than any negotiated in-network rate.

For PPO enrollees, out-of-network care is covered but at a higher cost share. What that means specifically depends on your plan. Common structures include: a higher deductible that applies specifically to out-of-network services, higher coinsurance (say, 40% vs 20% in-network), higher copays, or a separate out-of-pocket maximum for out-of-network services that's distinct from — and higher than — the in-network maximum. Read your Evidence of Coverage (EOC) document for the exact numbers. The Summary of Benefits gives you the headline, but the EOC is where the details live.

One thing that often surprises people: out-of-network providers can balance bill you in Medicare Advantage. Original Medicare limits balance billing (providers can only charge up to 115% of the Medicare-approved rate if they're a Medicare non-participating provider). Medicare Advantage doesn't have the same protections for out-of-network care. An out-of-network provider can bill you their full charges, not just a percentage above some rate floor.

The No Surprises Act provides some protection against surprise bills for out-of-network care at in-network facilities and for emergency services, but it's not a blanket protection for all out-of-network care. If you proactively choose to go to an out-of-network provider for scheduled care, the No Surprises Act doesn't protect you from that bill.

For HMO members specifically: if your plan refuses to cover care you believe should be covered, you have appeal rights. You can file an internal appeal with the plan, and if that fails, an external appeal with an independent review organization. CMS publishes appeal rights prominently, and plans are required to notify you of your rights. The appeals process is real — beneficiaries win appeals regularly. Document everything, get denials in writing, and don't assume the first 'no' is final.

sections.heading: Emergency Care: The Exception That Protects You Everywhere
sections.content:
Here's the rule that applies to every Medicare Advantage plan regardless of type: emergency care must be covered anywhere in the United States. HMO, PPO, doesn't matter. If you have a medical emergency, your plan must cover it, and they can't charge you more for going to an out-of-network emergency room than they'd charge at an in-network facility.

This is federal law. CMS regulations explicitly require Medicare Advantage plans to cover emergency services without prior authorization, without requiring in-network providers, and at in-network cost sharing levels. A plan that tries to charge you more because you went to the 'wrong' emergency room during a genuine medical emergency is violating CMS rules and you should report it.

The definitional question — what counts as an 'emergency' — is more nuanced. Medicare uses a 'prudent layperson' standard. If a reasonable person with average medical knowledge would believe the situation required immediate medical attention, it's covered as emergency care. Chest pain, difficulty breathing, severe injuries, sudden neurological symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding — these clearly qualify. The plan cannot later deny coverage because the ER visit turned out not to be a heart attack. What matters is whether a reasonable person would have thought it was serious enough to require emergency care.

Urgently needed care is a related but different category. It covers care you need quickly when your regular in-network providers aren't available — like when you're traveling and develop an acute illness. Medicare Advantage plans are required to cover urgently needed care anywhere in the US, but the definition is narrower than emergency care. An urgent care visit while traveling for a sinus infection would typically qualify. A routine prescription refill at an out-of-state pharmacy wouldn't.

For travelers and snowbirds: understand the difference between emergency, urgently needed, and routine care. Emergency and urgently needed care travel with you anywhere in the US. Routine care — your regular primary care visits, ongoing specialist management, physical therapy — generally does not travel well with an HMO. If you're splitting time between states, seriously consider whether a PPO better fits your life.

For international travel: Medicare Advantage plans generally don't cover care outside the US, just like Original Medicare. Some Medigap plans (Plans C, D, F, G, M, N) cover foreign travel emergency care up to plan limits. If you travel internationally, you want either a Medigap plan with foreign emergency coverage or a separate travel health insurance policy.

sections.heading: Network Changes During the Plan Year
sections.content:
Your network can change after you enroll. This is one of the more unsettling realities of Medicare Advantage.

Plans renegotiate contracts with providers continuously. A hospital that's in your plan's network in January might not be in January of the following year. A primary care physician might retire, move to a different practice, or decide not to renew their participation agreement. A specialist group might renegotiate poorly and drop out of the network entirely.

CMS rules require plans to notify beneficiaries about significant mid-year network changes. If your plan terminates a provider contract, you're supposed to receive notice. But 'supposed to' and 'actually does' don't always match, and by the time you receive notice, your options may be limited.

For ongoing treatment mid-year, you may have continuity of care rights. If you're actively being treated by a provider who leaves the network, plans are generally required to allow you to continue that treatment in progress for a transitional period — typically up to 90 days or through the end of a course of treatment for acute conditions. Pregnant enrollees have specific protections. Terminally ill patients have specific protections. But routine ongoing care doesn't automatically get continuity rights.

If your provider leaves your network and you're not eligible for continuity of care, your options during the plan year are limited. You generally can't just switch plans mid-year because your doctor dropped out of the network — that alone doesn't qualify as a Special Enrollment Period. But if the plan notifies you of a significant network change, that notification itself may create an SEP that lets you switch to a different plan. Check with 1-800-MEDICARE or your SHIP counselor if this happens to you.

The Annual Enrollment Period — October 15 through December 7 — is when you review your plan for the coming year and make changes. Network changes often take effect January 1. The plan's Annual Notice of Change (ANOC), which arrives by September 30, tells you what's changing for the next year. Read it. Don't file it. If your plan is dropping providers you depend on, AEP is your window to switch.

faq.question: Can I see any doctor I want with Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: Depends on your plan type. PPO plans let you see any provider — you pay less for in-network and more for out-of-network. HMO plans generally require you to stay in-network except for emergencies. Going out-of-network with an HMO means the plan doesn't pay anything for that visit.

faq.question: What is a Medicare Advantage ghost network?
faq.answer: A ghost network is when a plan's provider directory lists doctors who aren't actually available — they've retired, moved, stopped accepting new patients, or never actively participated. CMS has documented this as a widespread problem. Always call providers directly to confirm participation before enrolling.

faq.question: Does Medicare Advantage cover emergency care out of network?
faq.answer: Yes. Federal law requires every Medicare Advantage plan to cover emergency care anywhere in the US at in-network cost sharing levels. You cannot be charged more for using an out-of-network ER during a genuine medical emergency.

faq.question: Do I need a referral with Medicare Advantage?
faq.answer: With an HMO, usually yes — you need a referral from your primary care physician to see a specialist. Some specialties may be carved out for self-referral. PPO plans don't require referrals. Check your specific plan's documents.

faq.question: What happens if my doctor leaves my Medicare Advantage network mid-year?
faq.answer: You may have continuity of care rights to continue active treatment for a transitional period (typically 90 days). For routine ongoing care, options mid-year are more limited. If the plan notifies you of a significant network change, you may get a Special Enrollment Period to switch plans.

faq.question: How do I verify my doctor is actually in-network?
faq.answer: Check the plan's online directory as a starting point, but don't stop there. Call your doctor's office directly and confirm they currently participate in the specific plan and are accepting new patients on that plan. Directory errors are common enough that a phone call is worth it before you enroll.

faq.question: Is Medicare Advantage good for people who travel a lot?
faq.answer: PPO plans are much better for frequent travelers than HMOs. PPOs cover out-of-network care at a higher cost share. HMOs generally only cover emergency and urgently needed care outside their service area. Snowbirds splitting time between states should strongly consider a PPO — or Original Medicare plus a Medigap plan.

keyTakeaways: HMO plans restrict you to in-network providers except for emergencies — out-of-network non-emergency care is typically not covered at all.
PPO plans let you use out-of-network providers at a higher cost share — more flexible but more expensive premiums.
Provider directories are frequently inaccurate. Always call your doctor directly to confirm in-network participation before enrolling.
Ghost networks — directories listing unavailable providers — are a documented systemic problem in Medicare Advantage.
Emergency care is covered anywhere in the US at in-network cost sharing regardless of plan type — this is federal law.
Your network can change during the plan year. Read your Annual Notice of Change every September.
HMOs often require referrals for specialist visits. PPOs generally do not.
The No Surprises Act provides some protection against unexpected out-of-network bills but doesn't cover all situations.
If you travel internationally, Medicare Advantage generally won't cover you — consider a Medigap plan with foreign emergency coverage or travel health insurance.

h1: Best Medigap Companies 2026: Ranked by Stability, Ratings, and Real-World Claims
metaDescription: Top Medigap insurance companies for 2026 ranked by AM Best financial strength, premium stability, claims experience, and customer service — including Mutual of Omaha, AARP/UHC, Cigna, Aetna, BCBS, Humana, and Bankers Fidelity.
summary: Here's something that doesn't get said enough in Medicare Supplement shopping guides: all Plan G policies cover the same things.

sections.heading: Why the Company Behind Your Medigap Plan Actually Matters
sections.content:
Here's something that doesn't get said enough in Medicare Supplement shopping guides: all Plan G policies cover the same things. Every single one. CMS standardizes Medigap benefits. Plan G from Company A and Plan G from Company B are legally required to cover the same benefits in exactly the same way. There's no 'better coverage' among Medigap plans of the same letter — just better or worse companies behind them.

So what actually differentiates Medigap companies? Four things.

First: premium pricing and how aggressively premiums increase over time. The premium you pay at 65 is not the premium you'll pay at 75. How a company manages rate increases — and how stable those increases have been historically — matters enormously over a 10-to-20-year coverage horizon.

Second: financial strength. Your Medigap insurer needs to be around and solvent to pay claims 15 years from now. AM Best ratings are the standard indicator here. A- or better is the floor for long-term confidence.

Third: claims experience — specifically how easy or painful it is to actually get a claim paid. Medigap is supposed to work seamlessly in the background, paying its share after Medicare pays first. Some companies do this more reliably than others.

Fourth: state availability and pricing methodology. Medigap is regulated at the state level, and some companies are strong in some states and weak in others. Pricing methodology (attained-age, issue-age, or community-rated) has huge long-term premium implications.

This guide ranks eight companies that consistently appear at or near the top of the Medigap market. The rankings are based on AM Best financial strength ratings, historical rate increase patterns, J.D. Power scores and consumer complaint data from state insurance departments, and the general reputation among SHIP counselors who work with Medicare beneficiaries every day. No single company is right for every person in every state — but knowing the landscape before you call an agent puts you in a much stronger position.

sections.heading: 1. Mutual of Omaha — The Benchmark
sections.content:
Mutual of Omaha consistently ranks at the top of most serious Medigap analyses, and the reputation is earned.

AM Best rating: A+ (Superior). That's about as good as it gets. Mutual of Omaha has been issuing Medicare Supplement policies since the program's early years, and the company has the financial track record to match its rating. For long-term policyholders who want confidence that the company will be there in 20 years, the A+ matters.

Premium stability: Mutual of Omaha has a history of relatively moderate rate increases compared to many competitors. That said, 'relatively moderate' isn't zero — all Medigap companies raise rates, and Mutual of Omaha is no exception. What they're known for is not spiking rates dramatically after the initial enrollment period, which is when some competitors' pricing becomes problematic.

Availability: Mutual of Omaha sells Medigap in most states. They're not available everywhere in every state, and their pricing varies significantly by zip code — but for pure national coverage footprint, they're among the broadest.

Product line: They sell Plans A, F (if grandfathered), G, G-HD (high deductible G), N, and several other letters. Plan G and Plan N are their main products for new enrollees since Plan F isn't available to people who became Medicare-eligible after January 1, 2020.

Claims experience: This is where Mutual of Omaha really earns its reputation. Their claims processing is generally clean — Medicare pays first, Mutual of Omaha picks up its portion without a lot of back-and-forth. For a standardized product like Medigap, this should be a baseline, but it isn't universal. Mutual of Omaha makes it easy.

Who it's best for: Someone prioritizing long-term financial stability and predictable rate behavior over getting the absolute lowest initial premium. The initial premium from Mutual of Omaha might not be the cheapest quote you get — there are smaller companies that undercut on price — but you're paying for track record.

sections.heading: 2. AARP / UnitedHealthcare — Scale and Accessibility
sections.content:
AARP-branded Medicare Supplement plans are underwritten by UnitedHealthcare, the largest private health insurer in the United States. The AARP brand brings something significant: community-rated pricing in many states, which means your premium doesn't increase based on your age alone.

AM Best rating: UnitedHealthcare's operating subsidiaries are rated A (Excellent). Not quite Mutual of Omaha's A+, but solidly in the 'you're not worried about insolvency' territory.

The community rating thing is worth understanding. Most Medigap plans use attained-age pricing — your premium goes up as you get older, on top of general rate increases. Community-rated plans charge the same premium to everyone in a geographic area regardless of age. For a 65-year-old, community-rated premiums are usually higher than attained-age premiums from competitors. For a 75-year-old, community-rated premiums are usually lower. The math depends heavily on how long you plan to stay on the plan.

UHC/AARP Medigap is available in all 50 states plus DC. That's a meaningful advantage for national coverage consistency. Some smaller regional carriers can match or beat AARP pricing in specific states but don't travel well.

The AARP membership requirement (currently around $16/year) is a minor cost of doing business. Don't let it be a decision factor.

One legitimate criticism of AARP/UHC: premium increases have been noticeable in recent years. The community-rated model can produce significant across-the-board increases when a plan's overall risk pool deteriorates. This has happened with some UHC plans in specific markets. It's worth checking state insurance department complaint ratios before enrolling.

J.D. Power scores: UHC Medicare Supplement tends to land in the middle of the pack — not the best, not the worst. Customer service can be inconsistent depending on the state and the specific issue. Claims processing is generally fine but has produced more complaints in some states than Mutual of Omaha.

Best for: People who want national availability, community-rated pricing (if available in their state), and the brand recognition of AARP backing them up. The scale of UHC is a genuine advantage when you're dealing with a claims dispute — a company that processes millions of Medicare Supplement claims knows the system.

sections.heading: 3. Cigna — Strong for Healthy Enrollees
sections.content:
Cigna (now part of The Cigna Group) has been growing its Medicare Supplement market share and is competitive in many states, particularly for healthier enrollees applying during their Initial Enrollment Period when no medical underwriting is required.

AM Best rating: A (Excellent). Solid, if not quite at the Mutual of Omaha level.

Pricing: Cigna frequently comes in competitively priced, sometimes significantly below the market leader in specific zip codes. For someone enrolling at 65 in good health who's going to be on the plan for years, the lower starting premium compounds into real savings — as long as future rate increases don't erase the advantage.

Rate increase history for Cigna has been a legitimate concern in some markets. There have been states where Cigna's initial low pricing was followed by above-average rate increases in subsequent years. This is the fundamental tension in Medigap shopping: the company with the best introductory offer isn't always the company with the best total cost over 10 years.

Digital experience: Cigna's online tools and member portal are generally modern and functional. If you're comfortable managing insurance digitally, Cigna's tech is competitive with the market.

Claims processing: Mostly smooth. Cigna processes Medicare Supplement claims with Medicare's crossover system, so most claims are paid automatically without you doing anything. The system works as expected for the majority of enrollees.

Availability: Good national presence, though like all Medigap carriers, there are some states and markets where Cigna isn't available or isn't competitive.

Best for: Healthy 65-year-old enrollees in states where Cigna is competitively priced who are willing to review their options at AEP if rates become a problem. (Note: Medigap doesn't require you to change during AEP — you can apply any time, but medical underwriting applies outside your guaranteed issue windows.)

sections.heading: 4. Aetna — Reliability with a Major Insurer's Resources
sections.content:
Aetna is another major national insurer with a solid Medicare Supplement presence, now operating under the CVS Health umbrella after the 2018 acquisition. The integration has been largely invisible to Medigap policyholders — the product still works the same way — but Aetna's financial backing and claims infrastructure are among the most robust in the market.

AM Best rating: A (Excellent).

Aetna's Medicare Supplement pricing tends to be in the competitive middle — not the cheapest, not the most expensive, and with a rate increase history that SHIP counselors generally describe as 'reasonable.' They're not known for dramatic spikes or for being the standout bargain. They're known for being a stable, competent large insurer.

For people who have existing relationships with Aetna from their working years — employer coverage, other products — there's some value in keeping everything in one place, though Medicare Supplement truly is a separate product with no meaningful tie-in to other Aetna products.

Aetna's Plan G and Plan N pricing is available in most states. They also offer a high-deductible Plan G option, which is worth considering for healthier Medicare beneficiaries who want catastrophic protection without paying for first-dollar coverage they may not use.

Customer service: Aetna's Medicare Supplement customer service reputation is decent. Not the standout best, but no horror stories either. The CVS Health integration has, if anything, improved the digital experience.

Best for: People who want a major insurer with proven claims infrastructure and moderate, predictable rate increases. Solid choice for someone who doesn't want to optimize aggressively but wants confidence in the company behind their policy.

sections.heading: 5. Blue Cross Blue Shield — The Local Variable
sections.content:
BCBS needs its own framing because it isn't one company. Blue Cross Blue Shield is a federation of independent plans — 34 separate licensees operating in different states and regions. This means 'BCBS Medigap' can be an excellent product in one state and mediocre in another, depending on which BCBS licensee you're dealing with.
