One thing often overlooked: gender pricing. Most states allow insurers to charge women less than men — typically 10–15% less. Tobacco surcharges run 15–50% depending on the carrier. If you quit smoking, tell your insurer — some will re-rate you after a year or two tobacco-free.

sections.heading: Guaranteed Issue Windows — The Timing That Changes Everything
sections.content:
Here's the thing that nobody emphasizes enough: when you enroll in Medigap matters almost as much as which plan you choose.

Your Medigap Open Enrollment Period runs for 6 months starting the month you turn 65 AND are enrolled in Medicare Part B. During this window, insurers cannot deny you coverage, charge you more for pre-existing conditions, or make you wait for coverage to start. That's guaranteed issue. Use it.

After that window closes, most states allow insurers to use medical underwriting. They can reject you, charge you more, or exclude coverage for pre-existing conditions. If you've had cancer, heart disease, COPD, diabetes complications — you might not be able to get Medigap at all. Or you'll pay significantly more.

Some states have more consumer-friendly rules. New York, Connecticut, Massachusetts, Maine, and a few others require guaranteed issue year-round regardless of enrollment period. If you live in one of those states, timing matters less. But if you're in Texas, Florida, Ohio, or most other states — the 6-month window is real and missing it can cost you a lot.

There are also Special Enrollment Periods that trigger guaranteed issue rights: losing employer coverage, your Medigap insurer going bankrupt, moving out of a plan's service area. But don't count on these. The initial window is your cleanest shot.

If you turned 65 and enrolled in Medicare Advantage instead of Medigap, you have a trial right — during the first 12 months you can switch back to Original Medicare and enroll in Medigap with guaranteed issue rights. After that 12 months, that specific protection expires in most states.

sections.heading: How to Actually Get the Best Rate
sections.content:
Step one: don't buy from whoever calls you first. Medicare Supplement plans are standardized by the federal government — every Plan G has identical benefits regardless of carrier. The only thing that differs is price and company financial stability.

This means you should shop aggressively. Use an independent broker who represents multiple carriers, not a captive agent who only sells one company's plans. A good broker will run quotes from 10-15 carriers simultaneously. In a competitive market, the spread between cheapest and most expensive for identical Plan G can be $80-$120/month. That's $1,000-$1,400/year. For identical coverage.

Check the carrier's rate history. Some carriers come in low to grab market share, then hike rates aggressively after a few years. Ask your broker about historical rate increases for any carrier you're considering. Some carriers have a track record of 3-4% annual increases. Others have been jumping 10-12% in some states. That matters over a 15-20 year horizon.

Look for household discounts. Many carriers offer 7-12% discounts when both spouses have policies with the same company. If you and your spouse are both turning 65, coordinate applications.

If you're in an attained-age state and your premium has gotten out of control, shop the market. You may be able to switch to a different carrier if you're healthy enough to pass underwriting. Some people do this every few years to restart at a lower rate — it's called rate shopping or plan hopping, and it's perfectly legal.

Finally: Medicare SELECT plans are a cheaper variant of Medigap that require you to use a network of hospitals and providers. Premiums can run 20-30% less than standard Medigap. Worth considering if you live in an area with strong in-network options and you're not a frequent traveler.

faq.question: What's the average cost of Plan G at age 65?
faq.answer: Nationally, Plan G premiums for a 65-year-old run roughly $130 to $320/month depending on state, gender, and insurer. The national average across all Medigap plans is around $149.50/month in 2026, but Plan G runs higher than that average because it's one of the most comprehensive options. In low-cost states like Tennessee or Indiana you might find Plan G for $130-$150. In Florida or New Jersey expect $230-$320.

faq.question: Do Medigap premiums increase every year?
faq.answer: In most states, yes. The majority of states use attained-age rating, where your premium increases as you get older — usually 2-4% annually on top of general medical cost increases. Community-rated states (New York, Washington, Massachusetts) don't increase premiums based on age, but rates can still rise due to healthcare inflation. Issue-age states lock your rate to your enrollment age, which helps but doesn't eliminate increases entirely.

faq.question: Can I switch from Plan N to Plan G later?
faq.answer: In most states, switching requires medical underwriting after your initial open enrollment period. You'd have to answer health questions and could be denied or charged more. If you're healthy, you can often switch without issue. In states with year-round guaranteed issue (New York, Connecticut, Massachusetts, Maine), you can switch anytime without underwriting. Check your state's rules before assuming you can upgrade coverage later.

faq.question: Why is Medigap so much cheaper in some states?
faq.answer: Combination of factors: cost of living, state regulations, level of competition among insurers, and the overall health profile of the Medicare population in that state. States with more competition among carriers tend to have lower premiums because companies are actively competing on price. States like Tennessee and Indiana consistently come up as affordable markets. Florida is expensive partly because it's a massive market with a lot of older enrollees who use more care.

faq.question: Does Plan N cover emergencies?
faq.answer: Yes, Plan N covers the Part A hospital deductible (currently $1,676 in 2026), all Part A coinsurance, and Part B coinsurance — but charges a copay up to $50 for ER visits that don't result in a hospital admission. If you go to the ER and get admitted, the $50 copay doesn't apply. For actual hospitalizations, Plan N covers your costs just as well as Plan G.

faq.question: What happens to my Medigap premium at 80?
faq.answer: In attained-age states, expect your premium to be 30-50% higher than what you paid at 65, sometimes more depending on the carrier and state. Someone paying $180/month at 65 in an attained-age state might be paying $280-$350 at 80. In community-rated states like New York, your premium at 80 is identical to what a 65-year-old pays today — which is one of the main arguments for buying in a community-rated state if you can.

keyTakeaways: Plan G premiums range from roughly $130/month in low-cost states to $380+ in New York at age 65, with costs rising significantly by age 80 in attained-age states.
Rating method — attained-age, issue-age, or community — is arguably more important than the initial premium because it determines how fast your costs grow.
Plan N saves $45-$80/month versus Plan G but charges up to $20 per doctor visit and up to $50 per ER visit that doesn't result in admission.
Your 6-month Medigap open enrollment period at 65 is the only time most states guarantee your right to buy coverage without medical underwriting.
Shopping multiple carriers for identical Plan G coverage can save $80-$120/month — the benefits are identical, only the price differs.
New York, Connecticut, and Massachusetts use community rating, meaning an 80-year-old pays the same as a 65-year-old — lower lifetime costs for people who stay on the same plan long-term.

h1: Social Security and Medicare: How They're Connected
metaDescription: Medicare Part B gets deducted from your Social Security check. IRMAA gets calculated from your Social Security records. Your claiming age affects how you pay. Here's everything that links the two programs.
summary: Most people think Social Security and Medicare are basically the same thing — part of some unified retirement benefit package that kicks in when you turn 65.

sections.heading: They Feel Like One Thing But They're Two Very Different Programs
sections.content:
Most people think Social Security and Medicare are basically the same thing — part of some unified retirement benefit package that kicks in when you turn 65. Makes sense. They usually arrive around the same time, both run through the federal government, and the bills and enrollment notices look vaguely similar.

But they're fundamentally separate programs with separate rules, separate funding, separate enrollment, and separate agencies administering them. Social Security is run by the Social Security Administration (SSA). Medicare is run by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). Different departments, different statutes, different eligibility rules.

The reason they feel connected is that they're deeply intertwined operationally. The SSA collects IRMAA surcharges on behalf of CMS. Your Part B premium gets deducted from your Social Security check if you receive both. The SSA uses your tax records (via the IRS) to determine your Medicare costs. And if you claim Social Security early, you can get automatically enrolled in Medicare in ways you might not expect.

Understanding exactly how they connect — and where they diverge — can save you real money and prevent some genuinely painful enrollment mistakes.

sections.heading: The Part B Deduction: How Your Medicare Bill Gets Paid
sections.content:
If you receive both Social Security retirement benefits and Medicare Part B at the same time, your Part B premium gets automatically deducted from your Social Security check. You don't get a bill from Medicare. The money just isn't in your check.

In 2026, the standard Part B premium is $202.90 per month. If your Social Security check is $1,800/month, you're actually receiving $1,597.10. That $202.90 is going directly to Medicare.

This seems obvious but people are constantly surprised by it when they first see their reduced check. The SSA will send you a notice each fall showing your new premium amount and the change to your benefit payment — but it's easy to miss or misunderstand.

What happens if you're subject to IRMAA (higher income surcharges)? Same deal — the additional amount also gets deducted from your Social Security check. So a high-income beneficiary paying $649.20/month for Part B in 2026 would see that full amount deducted from their check. If the SS check isn't big enough to cover the IRMAA amount — say someone who claimed a small spousal benefit — Medicare will send a separate quarterly bill.

What if you haven't claimed Social Security yet? Then there's nothing to deduct from. Medicare will bill you directly, typically through the Medicare Premium Bill (CMS-500). Payments go to the Medicare Premium Collection Center. You can set up auto-pay through Medicare.gov, which most people should do because missing a payment can cause a 90-day grace period and then premium surcharges.

If you're receiving Railroad Retirement Board benefits instead of Social Security, the same automatic deduction system applies — your Part B premium comes out of your RRB check.

sections.heading: Social Security Claiming Age vs. Medicare Enrollment Age
sections.content:
This is where people make expensive mistakes.

Medicare eligibility starts at 65. Full stop. It does not depend on when you claim Social Security. You can claim Social Security at 62, 65, 67, or 70 — Medicare eligibility is still 65.

But here's where it gets complicated: if you're already receiving Social Security benefits when you turn 65, you'll be automatically enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B. Medicare sends you a welcome package and red-white-blue card. You're in. If you don't want Part B (because you have employer coverage, for instance), you have to actively opt out.

If you haven't claimed Social Security yet when you turn 65, you do NOT get automatically enrolled. You have to sign up for Medicare yourself during your Initial Enrollment Period — the 7-month window around your 65th birthday (3 months before, your birth month, 3 months after). A lot of people who delay Social Security don't realize they still need to actively enroll in Medicare at 65. Forgetting this can result in late enrollment penalties.

The Part B late enrollment penalty is permanent: 10% added to your premium for every 12-month period you were eligible but didn't enroll. If you were eligible for 3 years and didn't enroll (and had no employer coverage excuse), your Part B premium goes up 30% — forever. That's not a one-time fine, it follows you.

So: you can absolutely delay Social Security to 70 to maximize your monthly benefit. But you still need to enroll in Medicare at 65 unless you have qualifying employer coverage. These are two completely separate decisions.

sections.heading: IRMAA: When Social Security Records Set Your Medicare Cost
sections.content:
IRMAA stands for Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount. It's a Medicare surcharge for higher-income beneficiaries, and the way it works is a perfect example of how intertwined Social Security and Medicare actually are.

Every year, CMS asks the IRS for your tax return information from two years prior. Based on your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) from that return, CMS determines whether you owe a Medicare surcharge. The SSA then administers the collection of those surcharges — adding them to your premium bill or deducting them from your Social Security check.

2026 IRMAA income thresholds are based on your 2024 MAGI. Here's what that looks like:

Single filers, 2024 MAGI up to $109,000: standard Part B premium $202.90/month.
$109,001-$137,000: $284.10/month.
$137,001-$171,000: $405.80/month.
$171,001-$205,000: $527.50/month.
$205,001-$499,999: $649.20/month.
$500,000 and above: $689.90/month.

For joint filers, the thresholds are double. Standard remains up to $218,000 MAGI.

That's nearly a $490/month difference between the lowest and highest Part B brackets. And because Medicare looks back two years, you can get hit with an IRMAA surcharge for income you earned when you were still working — even if your income dropped significantly once you retired.

This two-year lag is genuinely painful for people who retire at 63 or 64, had high income in their last working years, and then turn 65 to discover they owe IRMAA surcharges based on that pre-retirement income. The good news: you can appeal using SSA Form SSA-44 if you had a qualifying life-changing event (retirement, divorce, death of spouse, loss of income-producing property). SSA will use your more recent income data instead.

Part D (prescription drug coverage) has its own IRMAA surcharges using the same income brackets, ranging from $14.50 to $91.00/month added to your Part D plan premium.

sections.heading: The Delayed Social Security Strategy and How Medicare Fits
sections.content:
Delaying Social Security to 70 is often the right call financially. Your benefit grows 8% per year from full retirement age (66-67 depending on birth year) through age 70. That's a guaranteed, inflation-adjusted return that's hard to beat.

But if you're delaying Social Security, you need a plan for paying Medicare premiums because there's no SS check to deduct from.

Option one: set up direct Medicare billing. Medicare.gov lets you set up auto-pay from a bank account. Quarterly bills go to the Medicare Premium Collection Center. Easy, just easy to forget to set up.

Option two: if you're doing Roth conversions or managing income in the years before Medicare kicks in, you can actually structure things to minimize IRMAA in your early Medicare years. If you can keep MAGI below the IRMAA threshold at ages 63-64 (the years that feed into your first Medicare years at 65-66), you save on premium surcharges. Some financial planners call this the Medicare cliff — careful income management in your early 60s can pay off in lower premiums at 65-67.

One wrinkle: Social Security benefits themselves became partially taxable starting in 1984 and up to 85% of your benefit can be included in MAGI depending on your income. So waiting to claim Social Security actually reduces your MAGI in those waiting years, which can reduce IRMAA. Claiming early and having SS income count against your MAGI can, counterintuitively, raise your Medicare premiums.

This is genuinely complicated enough that it's worth running the numbers with a fee-only financial planner before deciding when to claim Social Security — especially if you have significant retirement account withdrawals, capital gains, or other income that will affect your MAGI.

sections.heading: When the Two Programs Diverge: Gaps That Trip People Up
sections.content:
Social Security disability (SSDI) eventually connects to Medicare, but not right away. Most people on SSDI wait 24 months after their disability determination date before Medicare coverage kicks in. During that gap — which can be brutal for people with serious conditions — the only options are marketplace coverage, employer COBRA, Medicaid (for those with low income), or specific state programs.

Exception: ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease) patients skip both the 5-month SSDI waiting period and the 24-month Medicare waiting period. Coverage is immediate upon SSDI approval. ESRD (end-stage renal disease) patients also qualify for Medicare before 65 without a 24-month wait under most circumstances.

End-stage renal disease Medicare eligibility starts the fourth month after beginning dialysis or the month of a kidney transplant, depending on circumstances. But ESRD Medicare does interact with employer coverage in complicated ways — there's a coordination period where employer insurance is primary.

The other divergence point is death. When a Social Security recipient dies, Medicare coverage ends. It doesn't transfer to a surviving spouse (unless the spouse is independently entitled to Medicare through their own age or disability). Social Security survivor benefits continue to the spouse, but Medicare coverage does not automatically extend to someone who doesn't otherwise qualify.

For divorced individuals: you may qualify for Social Security benefits based on an ex-spouse's work record (if married 10+ years). This has zero effect on Medicare. Your Medicare eligibility is based on your own work history or your current spouse's — not an ex-spouse's earnings record.

sections.heading: Practical Enrollment Coordination: What to Do and When
sections.content:
Here's a clean timeline for the most common situations:

Claiming Social Security before 65: You'll be auto-enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B at 65. Watch for your Medicare card in the mail about 3 months before your birthday. If you have employer coverage and don't need Part B, call SSA before your enrollment date to opt out. Otherwise you're enrolled and paying the premium.

Delaying Social Security past 65: Enroll in Medicare yourself during your Initial Enrollment Period. Go to ssa.gov or visit a Social Security office. Don't wait for the mail — nothing automatic happens if you haven't claimed SS. If you have employer coverage from active employment, you can delay Part B without penalty. Make sure you understand whether your employer plan qualifies as "creditable coverage" before making this decision.

Claiming Social Security after 65 but you already enrolled in Medicare: Medicare continues as-is. When your SS benefits start, the auto-deduction kicks in and your Part B premium disappears from what would otherwise be your check.

High-income retirees: Budget for IRMAA. The 2026 surcharges are based on 2024 income. Run the numbers now. If you had high income in 2024 due to a one-time event (property sale, Roth conversion, business sale), consider filing SSA-44 to appeal if that income won't continue.

The bottom line here is that Social Security and Medicare aren't one program — they just live next to each other and share a lot of infrastructure. Getting clear on how they interact, especially around the auto-enrollment triggers and IRMAA, is worth an hour of your time before you turn 65.

faq.question: Is Medicare taken out of Social Security automatically?
faq.answer: Yes, if you receive both Social Security and Medicare Part B at the same time, your Part B premium is automatically deducted from your Social Security check. In 2026 the standard Part B premium is $202.90/month. If you're subject to IRMAA surcharges, those are also deducted from your check. If you haven't claimed Social Security yet, Medicare bills you directly instead.

faq.question: Can I get Medicare if I haven't claimed Social Security?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Medicare and Social Security are separate programs. You can enroll in Medicare at 65 whether or not you've started Social Security. If you're delaying Social Security to maximize your benefit, you still need to enroll in Medicare during your Initial Enrollment Period at 65 (unless you have qualifying employer coverage), or you'll face late enrollment penalties.

faq.question: What is IRMAA and who has to pay it?
faq.answer: IRMAA is the Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount — a Medicare surcharge for people whose income exceeds certain thresholds. In 2026, single filers with MAGI above $109,000 pay more than the standard Part B premium of $202.90, with surcharges rising through six income brackets up to $689.90/month for those earning $500,000+. IRMAA is based on your income from two years prior — 2026 surcharges use your 2024 tax return.

faq.question: What happens to my Medicare if I claim Social Security early at 62?
faq.answer: You can claim Social Security at 62, but Medicare eligibility still starts at 65. You'll receive a reduced Social Security benefit for claiming early, but you won't have Medicare. When you turn 65, if you're already receiving Social Security, you'll be automatically enrolled in Medicare Parts A and B. You can opt out of Part B if you have other coverage, but you need to do it proactively.

faq.question: I retired this year with high income. Will I have to pay IRMAA?
faq.answer: If your 2024 income was high, your 2026 Medicare premiums could be affected by IRMAA even if you've since retired and your income dropped. However, you can appeal using SSA Form SSA-44 if you had a qualifying life-changing event — including retirement. Submit the form with documentation of your current income and SSA will use a more recent estimate instead of the 2024 return.

faq.question: Does delaying Social Security to 70 affect Medicare at all?
faq.answer: Delaying Social Security doesn't affect Medicare eligibility or the benefit itself. But it means no auto-deduction for Part B — you'll pay Medicare directly. It can also affect IRMAA: not claiming Social Security reduces your MAGI in those years, potentially keeping you below IRMAA thresholds. Once you do claim Social Security, the premium deduction kicks in automatically.

keyTakeaways: Medicare Part B premiums ($202.90/month in 2026) are automatically deducted from your Social Security check if you receive both at the same time.
Delaying Social Security past 65 doesn't delay Medicare — you still need to enroll yourself at 65 to avoid late enrollment penalties.
IRMAA surcharges are based on income from two years prior, administered through the SSA — high earners in 2024 will see higher Part B premiums in 2026.
You can appeal IRMAA using SSA Form SSA-44 if you had a qualifying life-changing event like retirement that reduced your current income.
ALS patients skip both the 5-month SSDI waiting period and the 24-month Medicare waiting period — coverage is immediate upon disability approval.
The two-year IRMAA lookback means strategic income management in your early 60s can directly lower your Medicare costs when you turn 65.

h1: Medicare for ALS Patients
metaDescription: ALS patients get Medicare immediately upon SSDI approval — no 24-month wait, no 5-month wait. What's covered, what equipment Medicare pays for, and how to navigate home health and hospice.
summary: For almost every other disability, the path to Medicare involves two waiting periods that stack on top of each other and can keep people without coverage for years.

sections.heading: The Rule That Changes Everything for ALS Patients
sections.content:
For almost every other disability, the path to Medicare involves two waiting periods that stack on top of each other and can keep people without coverage for years. First, a 5-month waiting period after your SSDI disability onset date before cash benefits start. Then, a 24-month waiting period after benefits start before Medicare kicks in.

So someone disabled at 45 from, say, a severe traumatic brain injury might wait nearly 30 months from the date their disability began before Medicare coverage starts. For conditions that progress slowly, that's cruel but survivable. People find alternatives — Medicaid, marketplace plans, COBRA.

For ALS, that timeline is medically unacceptable. ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Lou Gehrig's disease) progresses fast. Some patients decline from diagnosis to severe disability in 12-18 months. A 29-month combined wait would mean Medicare arriving after the disease has already devastated someone's ability to function.

Congress recognized this. The 5-month SSDI waiting period was eliminated for ALS patients in 2020 through the ALS Disability Insurance Access Act. The 24-month Medicare waiting period was also eliminated. Both. Gone.

The result: an ALS patient who gets approved for SSDI is entitled to Medicare Part A starting the very first month their SSDI benefits begin. No waiting. If your benefit month is July 2026, your Medicare starts July 2026.

This is one of the most meaningful exceptions in the entire Medicare statute. Understanding it — and getting SSDI applied for and approved quickly — is the first and most important step for anyone navigating ALS.

sections.heading: Getting SSDI Approved: The Clock Starts Here
sections.content:
Medicare coverage begins when SSDI cash benefits begin. So the faster you get SSDI approved, the faster Medicare starts. That sounds obvious but people often delay filing, either because they're still working, still hoping for recovery, or just don't know how fast the system can move.

Social Security has a compassionate allowances program that includes ALS as a condition that fast-tracks approval. When you file for SSDI with an ALS diagnosis, SSA is supposed to identify it as a compassionate allowance condition and prioritize processing. In practice, approvals still take weeks to months, not days — but it moves faster than standard disability claims, which can take 6-18 months to approve.

What you need to file: medical records confirming the ALS diagnosis (EMG test results, neurologist notes), documentation of your work history and recent earnings, and the SSA-16 application. You can file online at ssa.gov or in person. If you haven't seen a neurologist yet and are waiting on diagnosis confirmation, filing with whatever documentation you have and updating SSA as records come in is better than waiting.

Most ALS advocates recommend filing for SSDI as soon as you have a confirmed diagnosis — even if you're still working part-time or in an ALS walk. You can have some income and still be approved for SSDI if your condition meets the disability criteria. There's no penalty for filing early, and delaying filing delays your Medicare start date.

If you're denied initially (which happens), appeal immediately. The appeals process has multiple levels. The ALS Association and many state chapters have navigators who can help with the SSDI application and appeals at no cost. Use them.

sections.heading: What Medicare Actually Covers for ALS
sections.content:
Medicare for ALS patients is the same Medicare available to everyone over 65 — Parts A, B, and optionally D — but ALS creates specific, intensive coverage needs that are worth walking through in detail.

Part A covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care (after a qualifying hospital stay), home health services, and hospice. For ALS patients, the home health and hospice benefits become critically important as the disease progresses. There are no ALS-specific carve-outs here — it's standard Medicare Part A, but the utilization will likely be much higher than for typical Medicare beneficiaries.

Part B covers outpatient services — doctor visits, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, outpatient procedures, and durable medical equipment. For ALS, Part B's DME coverage is probably the most financially significant piece. We'll cover DME in detail in the next section.

Part B also covers respiratory therapy, which becomes essential as ALS affects breathing. Pulmonologist visits, pulmonary function testing, and follow-up management of breathing complications are all Part B covered.

Nutrition services and feeding tube management: as ALS affects swallowing, enteral nutrition (tube feeding) becomes necessary for many patients. Medicare Part B covers the supplies and enteral formula through the DME benefit once a physician certifies it's medically necessary.

Mental health services: depression is extremely common in ALS. Medicare covers psychiatric evaluations, psychotherapy, and medications. Social work services through home health can also be covered when they're part of a skilled care plan.

Part D covers prescription drugs. ALS doesn't have a cure, but medications like riluzole (Rilutek) — one of two FDA-approved ALS medications — and edaravone (Radicava) are Part D-covered drugs. The 2026 out-of-pocket cap on Part D is $2,100, which is significant for expensive ALS medications. Riluzole costs several hundred dollars monthly without coverage.

sections.heading: Durable Medical Equipment: The Big-Ticket Coverage
sections.content:
This is where Medicare's ALS coverage really matters financially. ALS progressively destroys motor neurons, eventually affecting mobility, communication, and breathing. The equipment needed at various stages is expensive — we're talking tens of thousands of dollars.

Medicare Part B covers 80% of approved DME costs after the Part B deductible. You're responsible for the remaining 20% coinsurance. That 20% on expensive equipment adds up fast, which is why a Medicare Supplement plan or Medicaid secondary coverage is important for ALS patients even if they have Medicare.

Manual and power wheelchairs: Medicare covers both when medically necessary for home use and prescribed by a physician. Power wheelchairs require additional documentation — a face-to-face exam, a written order, and often a home mobility assessment by a physical therapist. The process can take months. Start early, before mobility deteriorates to a crisis point.

Ventilators: probably the most expensive covered item. As ALS affects the diaphragm and respiratory muscles, most patients eventually need ventilatory support — starting with noninvasive options like BiPAP at night and progressing to more intensive support. Medicare covers non-invasive ventilators (NIV/BiPAP machines) and invasive ventilators (tracheostomy ventilators) as rental equipment. Medicare pays monthly rental as long as the equipment is medically necessary. These machines can cost $20,000-$50,000 to purchase outright; Medicare's rental model means coverage continues without a cost ceiling for as long as you need the machine.

Communication devices: ALS affects speech. Speech-generating devices (SGDs) — some of which are sophisticated computerized systems that track eye movement or facial muscle twitches to generate speech — are covered by Medicare as DME when speech impairment is documented. The approval process requires a speech-language pathology evaluation, but these devices can cost $5,000-$20,000 and Medicare's coverage is genuinely life-changing.

Other covered equipment: hospital beds (home use), patient lifts, suction machines, cough assist devices (mechanical insufflation-exsufflation, or MI-E), feeding pumps, and pressure-relieving mattresses. All require physician orders and documentation of medical necessity.

Supplies like catheters, wound care materials, and gastrostomy tube supplies are also covered under the Part B benefit.

sections.heading: Home Health: What's Covered and How to Access It
sections.content:
Medicare home health is a benefit that people underuse, partly because the eligibility rules aren't obvious. For ALS patients, it's one of the most valuable parts of the coverage.

To qualify for Medicare home health:
1. You must be homebound (leaving home requires considerable effort due to your condition)
2. You need skilled care — skilled nursing, physical therapy, speech-language pathology, or occupational therapy
3. Your doctor must certify the need and create a care plan
4. The home health agency must be Medicare-certified

For ALS patients, the homebound requirement and need for skilled care are almost always met as the disease progresses. The tricky part is that homebound doesn't mean you can never leave home — you can go to medical appointments, religious services, adult day programs, or even occasional outings. But leaving must require significant effort.

What home health covers:
- Skilled nursing visits (medication management, wound care, ventilator management, tube feeding management)
- Physical therapy
- Occupational therapy
- Speech-language pathology
- Medical social worker services
- Home health aide services (personal care, bathing, dressing) — but only when skilled care is also being provided

Critically: Medicare home health has no copayment for most services. Covered visits are free to the patient under original Medicare (no deductible, no coinsurance for skilled nursing and therapy). This is one of Medicare's most generous benefits.

The limitation: home health aide hours are typically limited to 28 hours per week, and Medicare doesn't cover 24-hour home care or custodial-only care (just help with daily activities without any skilled nursing component). As ALS progresses to needing 24/7 care, Medicare home health won't cover that — Medicaid (for those who qualify) or private pay become necessary for custodial care beyond what Medicare covers.

The ALS Association has state chapters with care services coordinators who can help navigate home health agency selection, Medicare appeals, and identifying additional community resources.

sections.heading: Hospice: What It Means for ALS and When to Consider It
sections.content:
ALS qualifies as a terminal illness, which means ALS patients can elect Medicare hospice benefits when a physician certifies they have a life expectancy of 6 months or less if the disease follows its expected course.

But here's the thing a lot of families don't know: hospice isn't giving up, and the 6-month window isn't a hard cutoff. Patients can remain on hospice for longer than 6 months if they continue to meet the criteria. And you can voluntarily leave hospice if circumstances change.

What Medicare hospice covers:
- Physician services for pain and symptom management
- Nursing care
- Medical equipment and supplies related to the terminal condition
- Drugs for comfort (palliative medications)
- Physical, occupational, and speech therapy
- Short-term inpatient care for acute symptom management
- Respite care (temporary inpatient care to give family caregivers a break — up to 5 consecutive days at a time)
- Social worker services
- Chaplain and counseling services
- Bereavement support for the family after death

For ALS patients and their families, the respite care and caregiver support components of hospice are significant. ALS caregiving is physically and emotionally demanding, and Medicare hospice's built-in support systems help caregivers stay functional.

The tradeoff with hospice: electing hospice means Medicare stops covering curative treatment. For ALS this is complex because there's no cure, but disease-modifying drugs like riluzole are technically aimed at slowing progression rather than palliation. Once on hospice, those drugs might not be covered. Discuss this specifically with your hospice team and neurologist before making the election decision.

Patients who choose hospice and then want to return to curative-oriented care can revoke hospice election at any time. The decision is reversible.

sections.heading: Filling the Gaps: Medigap, Medicaid, and VA Benefits
sections.content:
Standard Medicare has cost-sharing that adds up fast for ALS patients. The Part B 20% coinsurance on expensive DME is the big one — 20% of a $30,000 power wheelchair is $6,000. Part A deductible ($1,676 in 2026 per benefit period) can trigger multiple times with frequent hospitalizations.

A Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan covers most of this cost-sharing. Plan G, which covers the Part A deductible, all Part A coinsurance, and the Part B 20% coinsurance, is usually the right choice for ALS patients who can get it. The question is timing and eligibility.

If you're under 65 and receiving Medicare based on disability, you may have Medigap rights in your state — but many states don't require guaranteed issue for under-65 Medicare recipients. Some do: California, New York, and a handful of others require insurers to sell Medigap to under-65 Medicare beneficiaries. If you're in one of those states, get a Medigap plan. If not, you may face medical underwriting, and with an ALS diagnosis you may be denied by private Medigap insurers.

Medicaid can function as secondary insurance for ALS patients with limited income. Medicaid typically covers what Medicare doesn't, including the 20% coinsurance. The interaction between Medicare (primary) and Medicaid (secondary) is called dual eligibility, and it essentially makes most medical care free for people who qualify for both.

For veterans with ALS: the VA classifies ALS as a service-connected condition presumptively — meaning any veteran with ALS is automatically entitled to full VA healthcare and disability benefits without needing to prove the connection to military service. This is an enormous benefit. VA healthcare plus Medicare creates very comprehensive, nearly zero-cost coverage for veteran ALS patients. If the person with ALS has any military service history, VA enrollment should be an immediate priority.

faq.question: How quickly does Medicare start for someone with ALS?
faq.answer: Medicare starts the same month SSDI cash benefits begin — no waiting period. ALS is exempt from both the standard 5-month SSDI waiting period and the 24-month Medicare waiting period that applies to most other disabilities. This exemption was fully enacted in 2020. The key is getting SSDI applied for and approved as quickly as possible, since Medicare coverage can't start before SSDI benefits do.

faq.question: Does Medicare cover a ventilator for ALS?
faq.answer: Yes. Medicare Part B covers ventilators as durable medical equipment when medically necessary. Both non-invasive ventilators (like BiPAP machines) and invasive ventilators are covered as rental equipment, with Medicare paying ongoing monthly rental as long as the equipment is needed. This coverage has no time limit — Medicare keeps paying for as long as the machine is medically necessary.

faq.question: Does Medicare cover speech-generating devices for ALS?
faq.answer: Yes. Speech-generating devices (SGDs) are covered as DME under Medicare Part B. These can be sophisticated computer systems that use eye-tracking or other interfaces — some costing $10,000-$20,000. Coverage requires documentation of speech impairment and a prescription from a physician, typically supported by a speech-language pathology evaluation. Medicare pays 80% of the approved cost; a Medigap plan can cover the remaining 20%.

faq.question: What's covered under Medicare home health for ALS patients?
faq.answer: Medicare covers skilled nursing, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, medical social work, and home health aide services (when skilled care is also being provided). For ALS patients who are homebound and need skilled care, these services have no copayment. The limitation is that Medicare doesn't cover 24-hour custodial care — just skilled care on a part-time or intermittent basis.

faq.question: Can an ALS patient get Medigap insurance?
faq.answer: It depends on the state. People under 65 on Medicare due to disability face varied state rules — some states (California, New York, others) require guaranteed issue for under-65 Medicare recipients, meaning insurers must sell Medigap regardless of health status. Other states allow medical underwriting for under-65 enrollees, and an ALS diagnosis would likely result in denial. Check your state's rules specifically. At 65, the standard guaranteed issue rules apply during your open enrollment period.

faq.question: Are ALS veterans automatically eligible for VA benefits?
faq.answer: Yes. The VA presumes a service connection for all veterans diagnosed with ALS, regardless of their military occupation or deployment history. This means any veteran with ALS can receive full VA healthcare, VA disability compensation, and potentially VA caregiver support benefits without needing to prove the disease is related to their service. Enrolling in VA healthcare should be done immediately after diagnosis for any veteran with ALS — it stacks with Medicare and can eliminate most out-of-pocket costs.

keyTakeaways: ALS patients skip both the 5-month SSDI waiting period and the 24-month Medicare waiting period — Medicare coverage starts the same month SSDI benefits begin.
Filing for SSDI as quickly as possible after ALS diagnosis is critical because Medicare cannot start before SSDI does — ALS is a compassionate allowances condition that fast-tracks SSA review.
Medicare Part B covers ventilators as ongoing rental equipment with no time limit, power wheelchairs, speech-generating devices, and other essential ALS equipment — paying 80% of approved costs.
Medicare home health services including skilled nursing, therapies, and aide services are available with no copayment for homebound ALS patients with documented skilled care needs.
Veterans with ALS qualify for full VA benefits presumptively — no proof of service connection required — which combined with Medicare creates near-comprehensive coverage.

Hospice election is reversible and doesn't imply imminent death — the 6-month prognosis requirement can be recertified, and patients who stabilize can revoke hospice and return to curative-oriented care.

h1: Catastrophic Health Insurance: Is It Right for You?
metaDescription: Catastrophic plans have $10,600 deductibles and very low premiums. In 2026, CMS opened them to more people than just those under 30. Here's who should actually consider one — and who definitely shouldn't.
summary: Catastrophic health insurance sits in a weird spot.

sections.heading: The Plan Almost Nobody Talks About Seriously
sections.content:
Catastrophic health insurance sits in a weird spot. It's an ACA plan — it's sold on the marketplace, it covers essential health benefits, it's legit — but most health insurance guides treat it like a footnote. Something you mention for 22-year-olds and move on.

That's always been slightly unfair, and in 2026 it got more unfair. CMS changed the rules. Catastrophic plans — which used to be limited to people under 30 and a narrow set of hardship exemptions — are now available to a much larger population. Specifically: anyone with income above 250% of the federal poverty level who isn't eligible for meaningful premium subsidies.

That's a lot of people who've been stuck paying through the nose for bronze or silver plans. Now they have another option.

But catastrophic plans are genuinely not right for everyone. The $10,600 deductible in 2026 is real, and it applies to almost everything. The monthly premium is low. The financial protection before that deductible is nearly nonexistent.

So let's actually work through who this plan is designed for, how the math works, and when it makes sense to pay almost nothing per month in exchange for absorbing the first $10,600 of medical costs yourself.

sections.heading: How Catastrophic Plans Work in 2026
sections.content:
Catastrophic health insurance is its own metal tier — below bronze. The structure is simple almost to a fault:

Monthly premiums are very low. In 2026, a 25-year-old in most markets pays $60-$130/month. A 45-year-old (with the expanded eligibility) might pay $180-$280/month depending on state. These numbers are before any subsidy, and catastrophic plans currently cannot be paired with premium tax credits — which is actually a significant limitation we'll address.

The deductible equals the out-of-pocket maximum. For 2026, that's $10,600 for an individual. This is not a coincidence — by design, the deductible IS the maximum. Once you've paid $10,600 in covered medical costs in a calendar year, insurance kicks in at 100% and covers everything for the rest of the year.

Before you hit that $10,600 threshold: you pay the full cost of almost everything. Prescriptions, specialist visits, imaging, minor procedures — all out of pocket at the negotiated rate.

The three exceptions baked into the plan:
1. Three primary care visits per year are covered before the deductible. You pay nothing (or a very small copay) for those three visits.
2. All ACA-required preventive services are covered at 100% before the deductible. Annual physicals, screenings, vaccines, contraception — zero cost-sharing.
3. Emergency services are covered (though you'll still be applying costs to that deductible until you hit the cap).

That's it. There's no coinsurance structure. No tiers. You either haven't hit the deductible (you're paying everything) or you have (insurance pays everything). It's the bluntest possible financial instrument.

sections.heading: Who Was Eligible Before 2026 vs. Who Can Enroll Now
sections.content:
The old rules: catastrophic plans were available to two groups only.

Group one: people under 30. That's it — no income test, no other requirements. If you were 29 years and 364 days old, you could buy a catastrophic plan. The day you turned 30, you aged out.

Group two: people of any age who qualified for a hardship exemption. These exemptions included things like homelessness, recent domestic violence, bankruptcy, death of a family member causing financial hardship, and a few others. They were narrow, required documentation, and the process of obtaining one was cumbersome enough that many eligible people didn't bother.

The 2026 expansion: CMS broadened the hardship exemption substantially. Now, anyone with income above 250% of the federal poverty level (FPL) who isn't receiving premium tax credits automatically qualifies for a hardship exemption and can purchase a catastrophic plan.

Why 250% FPL? That's roughly where the enhanced ACA subsidies that were introduced in 2021 started to thin out — and with those enhanced subsidies potentially expiring or reducing after 2025, CMS was concerned about affordability for middle-income people who earn too much for significant subsidies but still find standard ACA plans expensive.

In 2026, 250% FPL for an individual is approximately $37,650. For a family of four, around $77,500. People above those thresholds who find bronze plans unaffordable now have access to catastrophic plans.

The under-30 rule still applies — anyone under 30 can still enroll in a catastrophic plan without any income test or exemption process. And people who qualify through the original narrow hardship exemptions (homelessness, bankruptcy, etc.) still qualify regardless of income or age.

sections.heading: The Math: When Low Premium Beats Low Deductible
sections.content:
Let's run some real numbers because the catastrophic plan decision is entirely a math problem.

Scenario: a 35-year-old who earns $55,000/year (above 250% FPL, now eligible for catastrophic). In their state, their 2026 options are roughly:

Bronze plan: $380/month premium, $7,500 deductible, $9,200 out-of-pocket max
Catastrophic plan: $195/month premium, $10,600 deductible = OOP max

Premium difference: $185/month, or $2,220/year.

If this person is healthy and uses virtually no medical care: they save $2,220 by choosing catastrophic. They get their annual preventive care for free and their three primary care visits, which is probably all they'll use.

If they have one significant health event — say, a broken arm requiring imaging, ER visit, and follow-up — they might hit $3,000-$5,000 in costs. Under bronze, after the deductible they'd have coinsurance kicking in. Under catastrophic, they're paying that out of pocket regardless. But they saved $2,220 in premiums to help offset it.

If they have a truly catastrophic year — cancer diagnosis, major surgery, extended hospitalization — the bronze plan's $9,200 OOP max is better than catastrophic's $10,600. The difference is $1,400 in maximum exposure, but the catastrophic plan saved $2,220 in premiums. Net: catastrophic plan comes out $820 ahead even in a catastrophic year.

The arithmetic genuinely favors catastrophic plans for healthy people who don't use much medical care. The hidden risk is the zone between routine and catastrophic — the $3,000-$9,000 expense range where bronze coinsurance starts helping but you haven't hit your catastrophic deductible yet.

But here's the real comparison you need to make: bronze WITH an ACA premium subsidy versus catastrophic WITHOUT one. If you qualify for premium tax credits, a subsidized bronze plan's effective cost can drop to $100-$150/month with a $7,500 deductible — which changes the math completely. Catastrophic plans are not eligible for premium tax credits. If you qualify for meaningful subsidies, bronze or silver almost certainly beats catastrophic.

sections.heading: Who Should Seriously Consider a Catastrophic Plan
sections.content:
Let's be specific about the profile.

The ideal catastrophic plan candidate: healthy, mid-30s to early 50s, earns $50,000-$120,000 as a self-employed person or has employer coverage that doesn't cover them (contractor work, gig economy), doesn't qualify for meaningful ACA subsidies, doesn't take ongoing prescription medications, and wants to minimize monthly cash outflow while maintaining coverage against financial ruin.

Freelancers and self-employed people who aren't eligible for meaningful subsidies are probably the biggest beneficiary of the 2026 expansion. A freelance graphic designer making $80,000 faces the frustrating ACA cliff where they make too much for significant help but not enough to easily afford $450-$600/month bronze premiums. Catastrophic at $250/month protects against a catastrophic health event while keeping monthly costs manageable.

Young adults who've aged out of parental coverage (under 26) and are starting their first jobs without employer health insurance are classic catastrophic candidates. Under 30, no income test required.

The catastrophic plan also pairs well with a Health Savings Account (HSA) — or would, if the plan were HSA-eligible. This is a significant limitation in 2026: most catastrophic plans are NOT HSA-qualified because their deductible structure doesn't meet HDHP requirements. This means you can't use pre-tax HSA contributions to offset catastrophic plan costs the way you could with a high-deductible bronze or silver plan. Check specific plan details in your market, as some catastrophic plans do qualify.

People with chronic conditions, expensive medications, or expected medical needs in the coming year should almost certainly look elsewhere. The $10,600 deductible on a catastrophic plan with diabetes, asthma, or any condition requiring regular specialist care will almost always cost more than a higher-premium, lower-deductible plan.

sections.heading: The Subsidy Problem: Why Catastrophic Isn't Always the Cheap Option
sections.content:
This deserves its own section because it's the part that trips people up most.

Catastrophic plans cannot receive ACA premium tax credits. Ever. Under current law, you either have a catastrophic plan or you have subsidies — not both.

This matters a lot for people whose income fluctuates. If you're self-employed and had a $90,000 year in 2025 but expect a $45,000 year in 2026, buying catastrophic in 2026 means forgoing potentially significant premium subsidies. At $45,000 for a 40-year-old, an ACA silver plan might cost $150-$250/month after subsidies — better coverage, better drug benefits, lower deductible.

The 2026 expansion specifically targets people above 250% FPL who aren't getting subsidies. But income eligibility for subsidies is fuzzy for self-employed and gig workers whose income varies. Before choosing catastrophic, model both scenarios: your realistic income estimate with subsidies on a bronze plan versus catastrophic without subsidies. The actual break-even income point depends on your age, state, and what plans are available locally.

Also worth noting: catastrophic plans do cover essential health benefits. Mental health services, substance abuse treatment, maternity, prescription drugs — all required under the ACA. But most services are subject to the full $10,600 deductible before coverage kicks in. The free preventive care and three primary care visits are the only meaningful above-deductible benefits.

State-based marketplace plans vary. Some states have added benefits or modifications to catastrophic plans. California, Massachusetts, and New York sometimes have state-specific requirements that affect catastrophic plan features. Always look at the actual Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC) for any plan you're considering.

sections.heading: Enrolling: When You Can Buy a Catastrophic Plan
sections.content:
Open Enrollment for 2026 Marketplace plans ran November 1 through January 15, 2026 in most states (some state-based exchanges have different windows). If you missed it, you need a qualifying life event to trigger a Special Enrollment Period.

Qualifying events that trigger a 60-day SEP: losing employer health coverage (job loss, end of COBRA), getting married, having or adopting a child, moving to a new coverage area, losing Medicaid or CHIP eligibility, and a few others.

For the hardship exemption qualifying catastrophic plan eligibility: the 2026 rule change should be automatically applied through healthcare.gov and state exchanges. If you're shopping the marketplace and your income is above 250% FPL, you should see catastrophic plans as an available option. If the exchange isn't showing them, use the HealthCare.gov chat support or call the marketplace directly.

Under-30 enrollment is straightforward — age is verified, no exemption documentation needed, catastrophic plans show up as an option during shopping.

One practical note: catastrophic plan networks can be narrower than other metal tiers in some markets. Before enrolling, verify that your primary care physician and any specialists you see regularly are in-network. The low premium doesn't help if you end up paying full out-of-network rates because your doctor isn't in the plan.

For people on the fence: the worst case of buying catastrophic and having a bad health year is hitting the $10,600 out-of-pocket max. That's a significant expense but not financial ruin for most people with modest savings or access to credit. The worst case of not having any health insurance is... considerably worse. Catastrophic plans exist because some coverage against catastrophe genuinely is better than no coverage.

faq.question: Who qualifies for a catastrophic health plan in 2026?
faq.answer: Three groups: people under 30 (no income test required), people of any age with income above 250% of the federal poverty level (~$37,650 for an individual) who aren't receiving premium tax credits, and people who qualify through a hardship or affordability exemption (homelessness, bankruptcy, domestic violence survivor, etc.). The under-30 and income-above-250%-FPL rules are the most commonly applicable in 2026.

faq.question: What is the deductible for a catastrophic health plan in 2026?
faq.answer: The 2026 out-of-pocket maximum — which equals the deductible on catastrophic plans — is $10,600 for an individual. Everything except the three free primary care visits and ACA preventive services counts toward this number. Once you've paid $10,600, the plan covers 100% of costs for the remainder of the calendar year.

faq.question: Can I use an HSA with a catastrophic health plan?
faq.answer: Generally no. Most catastrophic plans don't meet the IRS definition of a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP) required for HSA eligibility, primarily because they cover primary care visits before the deductible. Some catastrophic plans in some markets may qualify — you need to check the specific plan details. If HSA contributions are important to your financial strategy, a high-deductible bronze plan that does qualify is usually a better fit.

faq.question: Do catastrophic plans cover prescription drugs?
faq.answer: Yes — catastrophic plans cover prescription drugs as an essential health benefit. However, almost all prescription costs count toward your $10,600 deductible before coverage begins. There are no free drug tiers or first-dollar coverage for medications. If you take regular maintenance medications, run the math: your annual drug costs alone might exceed what you'd save in premiums versus a bronze or silver plan.

faq.question: Why can't I use ACA subsidies with a catastrophic plan?
faq.answer: Federal law explicitly excludes catastrophic plans from premium tax credit eligibility. The ACA's subsidies apply to bronze, silver, gold, and platinum tiers — not catastrophic. This makes catastrophic plans most beneficial for people who don't qualify for meaningful subsidies anyway, typically those with income above 400% FPL or the newly eligible group above 250% FPL in 2026.

faq.question: Is a catastrophic plan better than going uninsured?
faq.answer: For almost everyone, yes. The $10,600 annual out-of-pocket maximum protects against financial catastrophe from a serious illness, accident, or hospitalization. A single hospital admission for appendicitis, a broken leg from a car accident, or cancer diagnosis can generate $50,000-$500,000 in medical bills. With catastrophic coverage, your maximum exposure is $10,600. Without insurance, you're exposed to the full amount.

keyTakeaways: In 2026, CMS expanded catastrophic plan eligibility beyond under-30 to include anyone with income above 250% FPL (~$37,650 individual) who isn't receiving ACA subsidies.
The 2026 catastrophic deductible is $10,600 for individuals — which also equals the out-of-pocket maximum, meaning insurance covers 100% of costs after that threshold.
Three primary care visits and all ACA preventive services are covered before the deductible with no cost-sharing.
Catastrophic plans cannot be paired with ACA premium tax credits — people who qualify for meaningful subsidies should compare subsidized bronze or silver plans before choosing catastrophic.
The math favors catastrophic plans for healthy people who rarely use medical care, particularly self-employed individuals earning too much for significant subsidies.
Most catastrophic plans are NOT HSA-eligible — check plan-specific details if HSA contributions are part of your financial strategy.

h1: Best Telehealth Health Insurance Plans 2026
metaDescription: Some health plans now offer $0 virtual visits around the clock. Others charge you like it's an in-person appointment. Here's which carriers are doing telehealth right in 2026 — and when virtual care actually replaces going in person.
summary: Three years ago, telehealth was a pandemic-era convenience.

sections.heading: Telehealth Changed. Most Insurance Advice Hasn't.
sections.content:
Three years ago, telehealth was a pandemic-era convenience. You talked to a doctor on your phone because you couldn't go in person. The visits were sometimes free, sometimes not, sometimes covered like a regular visit, sometimes weird billing gray zones nobody understood.

In 2026, that's over. Telehealth is a first-class benefit that genuinely shapes which insurance plan you should buy. Some plans have built their entire care model around it. Others still treat virtual visits like a novelty add-on and charge you accordingly.

The stakes are real: $0 versus $40-$75 per virtual visit adds up to hundreds of dollars a year if you're accessing care regularly. And if you live in a rural area, have a chronic condition that requires frequent check-ins, or just have a schedule that makes afternoon doctor appointments basically impossible — the right telehealth benefit isn't a nice-to-have. It's the difference between actually using your insurance and not.

Here's a current look at which carriers are doing this well in 2026, what the actual benefits look like, where telehealth genuinely works better than in-person, and where it falls short.

sections.heading: Oscar Health: Built for Virtual-First
sections.content:
Oscar has been more aggressive about virtual care than almost any major insurer, and in 2026 their telehealth benefits are genuinely best-in-class for an ACA marketplace plan.

The flagship feature: $0 virtual urgent care available 24/7 on most Oscar plans. Not a low copay. Zero. You open the Oscar app, request a virtual visit, and you're typically connected within minutes. For common conditions — respiratory infections, UTIs, allergies, minor injuries, medication questions, skin issues — this is faster and cheaper than any other option.

On their Virtual Primary Care plans (available in select markets), members get $0 primary care visits (both virtual and in-person), $0 preferred generic drugs, and dedicated care team with a specific doctor who knows your history. The care team model is Oscar's real differentiator — you're not seeing a random provider on a video call, you have an assigned physician and care team that manages your ongoing health.

In specific markets for 2026 — including Florida and Georgia — Oscar Silver plans include $0 primary care visits 24/7 alongside $0 virtual urgent care. These plans are available at competitive silver premiums and represent probably the strongest combination of telehealth access and in-person coverage in the ACA marketplace.

Limitations: Oscar is not available in every state. Its network outside of major metros can be thin — if you're in a rural area, the virtual care is great but finding in-person specialists in-network might be harder. Check availability and network in your specific area before enrolling. Oscar also uses a proprietary app-based care model that some people love and some find overly tech-dependent.

sections.heading: Blue Cross Blue Shield: Breadth Over Innovation
sections.content:
BCBS is the largest health insurer in the country by membership, operating through 35+ independent licensee plans across all states. Saying "BCBS has good telehealth" is slightly complicated because each BCBS plan is run independently — what Anthem BCBS offers in California differs from what BCBS of North Carolina offers.

The common thread: virtually every BCBS plan includes telehealth through either their own virtual visit platform or partnerships with vendors like Teladoc, MDLive, or Doctor on Demand. The cost varies by plan — some BCBS plans offer $0 virtual urgent care visits, others charge the same copay as an in-person primary care visit.

Where BCBS consistently outperforms: breadth. The BlueCard network means BCBS members traveling anywhere in the country (or world) can access in-network care. For people who travel frequently or split time between states, having BCBS means you're never truly out-of-network in the US. That breadth extends to virtual care — BCBS telehealth platforms are accessible from anywhere, not just your home state.

For Medicare Advantage, BCBS has been one of the more aggressive MA carriers on expanding telehealth coverage. Many BCBS MA plans include $0 telehealth visits for primary care and mental health — benefits beyond what Original Medicare provides.

The recommendation: if telehealth is your primary concern, BCBS isn't the leading edge carrier. But if you want solid telehealth combined with the largest provider network and the ability to use your insurance wherever you are, BCBS is hard to beat. Especially for people who are heavy travelers or have family in multiple states.

sections.heading: Kaiser Permanente: The Virtual-First Model Done Right
sections.content:
Kaiser operates differently than every other health system on this list. They're not just an insurer — they own their hospitals, employ their doctors, and run their own health system in the markets they serve (California, the Pacific Northwest, Colorado, Georgia, Hawaii, Maryland/DC, and Virginia).

This integrated model makes telehealth work better at Kaiser than anywhere else because the doctors doing your virtual visit are the same physicians managing your in-person care. Your records are in the same system. There's no care coordination gap between your video visit and your lab results or referrals.

Kaiser's virtual care in 2026 is extensive: video visits, phone consultations, e-visits (asynchronous messaging with a physician), virtual pharmacy consultations, and mental health video appointments. Many of these are $0 or have lower copays than equivalent in-person visits.

The specific benefit: Kaiser's electronic health record system is among the best in the industry. When you have a virtual visit, the doctor can see your entire care history, order labs that get drawn at a Kaiser lab near you, send prescriptions to a Kaiser pharmacy, and coordinate any necessary in-person follow-up — all without you making multiple calls or managing your own records.

The limitation is geographic: Kaiser is only available in about a dozen markets. If you're not in California, Colorado, Georgia, or their other service areas, Kaiser isn't an option. And within their markets, Kaiser's network is essentially only Kaiser — which is fine if a Kaiser facility is nearby, limiting if it's not.

For people in Kaiser service areas who are okay with an integrated system approach: Kaiser consistently ranks among the highest for member satisfaction and telehealth quality. The virtual-first model works because the entire care system is designed around it.

sections.heading: Medicare Advantage Plans: The Best Telehealth in Medicare
sections.content:
Original Medicare's telehealth coverage is limited and has historically required patients to be in rural areas or at approved healthcare facilities to access virtual care. Congress has extended pandemic-era expansions through 2027 — so through the next open enrollment cycle, Original Medicare covers telehealth more broadly — but it still has restrictions.

Medicare Advantage plans have gone much further. Many MA plans offer telehealth benefits that far exceed Original Medicare, including:

$0 virtual primary care visits. A growing number of MA plans — especially from UnitedHealthcare, Humana, and BCBS — offer zero-cost virtual visits with primary care physicians as a standard plan benefit.

$0 mental health telehealth. Mental health access is a significant challenge for Medicare beneficiaries, and MA plans have been among the most aggressive at expanding virtual mental health benefits. Some plans offer unlimited virtual therapy sessions with no copay.

In-home health monitoring telehealth: certain MA plans now cover remote monitoring of chronic conditions — blood pressure, glucose levels, heart rate — with data transmitted to care teams who can schedule virtual check-ins before problems escalate.

UnitedHealthcare specifically announced extended telehealth benefits for MA members through 2026 covering both medical and behavioral health services. Humana and BCBS MA plans in competitive markets have followed with similar expansions.

The caveat: MA telehealth benefits vary significantly by plan and geography. The same carrier (say, UnitedHealthcare) might offer $0 telehealth in one county and charge copays in another, depending on the plan's local market competition and benefit structure. Always review the specific plan's Summary of Benefits during AEP, not just the carrier's marketing materials.

For Medicare beneficiaries who use telehealth regularly, shopping MA plans specifically for telehealth benefits during Annual Enrollment Period (October 15 - December 7) can meaningfully reduce costs.

sections.heading: When Telehealth Genuinely Replaces In-Person Care
sections.content:
This is the part most insurance comparison articles skip: the clinical reality of when telehealth actually works.

Telehealth is genuinely as good as or better than in-person for:

Common infections and minor illness. Sinus infections, UTIs, strep throat (for adults with typical symptoms), pink eye, ear infections — an experienced physician can diagnose and treat these based on symptoms alone. The visual exam through a camera adds enough information to prescribe appropriately without physical examination in most cases.

Medication management and follow-ups. Refills, dosage adjustments for stable conditions, reviewing lab results, managing blood pressure or cholesterol between annual visits — these don't require a physical exam. They require physician attention, which telehealth delivers efficiently.

Mental health. The research is actually pretty convincing that telehealth therapy is as effective as in-person therapy for most conditions including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and OCD. Removing transportation barriers and scheduling friction increases access to care in ways that improve outcomes. The $0 mental health telehealth benefits in some plans are legitimately undervalued.

Chronic disease monitoring. Check-ins between appointments for diabetes management, heart failure follow-ups, COPD monitoring — when combined with at-home monitoring tools, telehealth can catch deterioration faster than waiting for quarterly appointments.

Dermatology. High-quality photos submitted through asynchronous telehealth platforms are often sufficient for diagnosing and treating common skin conditions. Some MA plans cover asynchronous dermatology visits.

Telehealth is not adequate for:

Anything requiring physical examination. Abdominal pain, chest pain, suspected fractures, skin lesions requiring biopsy, heart murmurs — you need to be in a room with a doctor. Don't try to manage these virtually.

New complex diagnoses. If you've never been seen for a significant condition, the initial workup usually requires labs, imaging, and physical examination that telehealth can't do.

Procedures. Obviously. Anything requiring a physical intervention needs an in-person setting.

Pediatric visits for young children. Examining a toddler via video is genuinely challenging even for experienced pediatricians. Most well-child visits, vaccinations, and ear infections in very young children are better handled in person.

sections.heading: Telehealth Limitations: What the Insurance Ads Don't Say
sections.content:
A few things worth knowing before you buy a plan specifically for its telehealth benefits.

State-crossing restrictions. Medical licenses are state-specific. If you're in Texas, your telehealth provider needs a Texas medical license. If you travel to a different state, the telehealth visits you make there may be subject to different rules. Some plans have addressed this through interstate compact agreements and multi-state licensed providers — but if you split time between states, ask specifically how your plan handles telehealth in your secondary state.

Prescribing limitations. Controlled substances (stimulants for ADHD, benzodiazepines, opioids) have strict federal rules about prescribing via telehealth. Since 2023, the DEA has been working through rules that would restrict telehealth prescribing of controlled substances after pandemic-era flexibilities expire. As of 2026, some prescribers and platforms still operate under extensions, but this is a legally evolving area. If ADHD medication or other controlled substance management is a primary use case for telehealth, verify what your specific plan and its telehealth vendors can prescribe in your state.

Quality variation. $0 urgent care telehealth through an insurance company's preferred platform is not all equally good. Some platforms connect you quickly with qualified physicians. Others cycle through physician assistants and NPs who may or may not be well-suited to your specific question. Read reviews for the specific telehealth platform your plan uses — not the insurance company's marketing materials.

The urgent care trap. Some people use telehealth urgent care for situations that actually need in-person ER evaluation. A telehealth provider who evaluates your "bad headache" virtually may advise you to go to the ER — which is appropriate but also means you've now paid both a telehealth visit and an ER visit. Know when to go straight to emergency care without attempting telehealth first.

Mental health provider availability. The $0 mental health telehealth benefit is real and meaningful, but finding an in-network mental health provider — even virtually — remains challenging in many markets. High demand and limited supply of providers means the actual availability of that benefit may be limited even when the benefit exists on paper.

sections.heading: How to Compare Plans Specifically for Telehealth
sections.content:
When shopping 2026 plans, here's what to actually look for beyond the marketing headline.

Step one: find the Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC) for any plan you're considering. The SBC lists exactly what you pay for telehealth visits — $0, a copay amount, or subject to deductible. Don't rely on plan brochures.

Step two: identify which telehealth platform or vendor the plan uses. Oscar uses its own app. UnitedHealthcare uses their app plus Teladoc for some services. BCBS plans use various vendors depending on the local plan. Google the telehealth platform name + reviews to understand the actual user experience.

Step three: check mental health telehealth specifically. Many plans that offer $0 medical telehealth still charge more for mental health visits, and vice versa. If mental health is a priority, find the exact cost-sharing for therapy and psychiatry visits.

Step four: if you use any telehealth for prescription management, verify what controlled substance prescribing the plan's telehealth network supports in your state.

Step five: for Medicare beneficiaries, check the Annual Notice of Change that plans send each fall. Telehealth benefits in MA plans can change year-to-year during AEP. If your plan reduced telehealth benefits for 2026, this is a legitimate reason to switch plans during AEP.

Finally: don't let $0 telehealth drive you to a plan with a bad in-person network or unaffordable out-of-pocket costs. Telehealth is a great benefit but it's not a substitute for needing real hospitals, real specialists, and real in-person care when something serious happens. The plan that has both good telehealth AND a strong in-person network is the right plan. Telehealth-only optimization is a mistake.

faq.question: Which health insurance has the best telehealth in 2026?
faq.answer: For ACA marketplace plans, Oscar Health leads on telehealth with $0 virtual urgent care 24/7 and $0 primary care visits on many plans. Kaiser Permanente has the best integrated virtual-first model in the markets where they operate. For Medicare Advantage, UnitedHealthcare, Humana, and BCBS MA plans in competitive markets have been most aggressive about $0 telehealth benefits. The "best" depends heavily on your specific state and market.

faq.question: Do all health insurance plans cover telehealth?
faq.answer: Virtually all ACA-compliant plans sold on the marketplace include some telehealth coverage in 2026. What varies is the cost: some plans charge the same copay as an in-person visit, others offer $0 telehealth, and others apply telehealth costs to your deductible. The difference between free virtual visits and visits that count toward your deductible can be hundreds of dollars annually.

faq.question: What conditions can be treated via telehealth?
faq.answer: Telehealth works well for common infections (UTIs, sinus infections, strep), medication refills and follow-ups, mental health therapy and psychiatry, chronic disease management check-ins, and many dermatology issues. It's not appropriate for anything requiring physical examination or procedures, new complex diagnoses that need workup, or emergencies. When in doubt about whether your situation needs in-person care, err on the side of going in.

faq.question: Can I get prescriptions through telehealth?
faq.answer: Yes, for most medications. Telehealth providers can prescribe antibiotics, common medications, and many maintenance drugs. Controlled substances (stimulants, benzodiazepines, opioids) face federal restrictions on telehealth prescribing that are in flux in 2026 — check with your specific plan's telehealth platform about what they can prescribe and under what circumstances in your state.

faq.question: Does Medicare cover telehealth?
faq.answer: Original Medicare covers telehealth more broadly in 2026 than pre-pandemic, with coverage extended through December 2027 by the Consolidated Appropriations Act signed in February 2026. Medicare Advantage plans typically go further — many offer $0 telehealth for primary care and mental health as supplemental benefits. Check your specific MA plan's benefits rather than assuming Original Medicare's rules apply.

faq.question: Are Lemonade health insurance telehealth benefits good?
faq.answer: Lemonade Health operates in select states and has built a modern, app-first insurance experience. Their telehealth integration has grown through 2025-2026 partnerships with virtual care platforms. For specific telehealth benefit details and availability in your state, check Lemonade's current plan SBC directly — telehealth benefits are improving rapidly and carrier-specific details change year-over-year in ways that make general assessments quickly outdated.

keyTakeaways: Oscar Health leads ACA marketplace telehealth with $0 virtual urgent care 24/7 and $0 primary care on many 2026 plans in select markets.
Kaiser Permanente's integrated model — same doctors, same records, same system — makes their telehealth the highest quality experience for members in their service areas.
Medicare telehealth coverage was extended through December 2027, and many Medicare Advantage plans offer $0 telehealth visits beyond what Original Medicare provides.
Telehealth works as well or better than in-person for common infections, mental health therapy, medication management, and chronic disease monitoring.
Telehealth is NOT appropriate for anything requiring physical examination, new complex diagnoses, or emergencies — don't let $0 virtual visit access delay necessary in-person care.
When comparing plans, look for the exact telehealth cost in the Summary of Benefits — not the marketing materials — and identify which third-party platform the plan uses before enrolling.

title: Medicare Advantage Plans with Low Copays
metaDescription: How Medicare Advantage copay structures actually work, which plans have $0 PCP copays, how specialist copays compare across major carriers, and drug tier breakdowns — so you pick the right plan before you actually need it.
